In article <sxd4b.5047$zK3.210424@twister.tampabay.rr.com>,
 "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote:

> "Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message
> news:joe_zitzelberger-E026FD.19150030082003@corp.supernews.com...
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

It does if you include the four or five companies that do more 
xactions/day than the one I'm with does.  And then all the little shops 
that do small volume, or banks that do their own.

I think a reasonable estimate for US credit card transaction volume 
might be around the 200 Million per day mark.  100Mish for Visa, 15Mish 
for MasterCard, 85Mish for Amex/DC/CarteBlanch/Discover/etc.

But there are also plenty of retail store cards, gas&oil cards, gift 
cards, frequent flyer mile accounts, auto club accounts and a few other 
type accounts that all of those processors provide.  I have no idea how 
to estimate the volume of those transactions, but they are large.

Not 2 Billion in a day, but not in a year either.  Perhaps a week.

If
> it's a debit card, are funds needing to be transferred? I could go on and

> information that banks and card companies require to backup the single
> swipe, especially if you happen to challenge that charge and say you
didn't

And they need ALL this information *online* for 8 months? ....ouch...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-09-01T13:05:00-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0309011205.144f6171@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com> <217e491a.0308291403.530033c4@posting.google.com> <1fh1lvs4r45ch5g25ifj2tktplpjieu76h@4ax.com> <Utf4b.451$rf4.185@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com>`

```
"Gonzo" <ckhamel1961@yahoo.com> wrote 

> It's been interesting reading all the remarks from folks scoffing at the
> numbers I tossed around here. I suppose that if one works enough years in
> I/T that one feels that he's seen just about everything. Then something
> comes around that makes you look twice and really wonder if its real.

Perhaps because the numbers appear to be require disk space than would
be more than in many of the top 20 largest computers in the world. 
Your original data would currently occupy a few tens of terabytes.  A
similar space would be required for the merge output and another set
of terabytes for the resulting DB/2 database (_no_one_ is going to
overwrite the original database with the new DB/2 load on an initial
attempt).

Total space required may be around 100 Terabytes.

IBM DB/2 has only been tested to 10 terabytes (from recent items).  

In sharp relief you specified a 3 hour requirement to sort (now
reduced to merge) for this enormous volume.

It is not the sizes alone but the juxtiposition of the size and time
requirement that suspends belief.

> People used to think the earth was flat... 

Not generally, no.  That was what the Church tried to indoctrinate
people with, but the shape and size of the Earth had been well
established by 450 BC.  Ptolemy's Geographica of 120AD (or so)
established the modern mechanism of spherical coordinates.

It seems that many Americans are taught that Columbus (a central
figure and hero in their history) proved he was right when all others
laughed at him and claimed he would 'fall off the edge'.  Actually
Columbus was completely wrong in most things he believed, he never
stopped believing in a 17000 mile Earth and that he had arrived in the
'Indies' having sailed past Japan and China.  His detractors knew that
the Earth was round and was 24000 miles and, by knowing it was 13000
miles eastwards to Japan, knew the trip that Columbus was planning to
China and the Spice Islands would be 11000 miles, not the 4000 he
claimed.

> people used to say we'd never get to the moon. 

Did they ?  Actually there are people who still say that 'we' never
got to the moon, and never will.

> Heck, if someone told me in the '70s that one day a computer called a
> laptop would have more processing and memory power than the IBM 360 I was
> working on at the time, I would have laughed them out of the building!

Well, silly you.

> As a customer we tend to think that a single swipe of a card
> initiates a single transaction, since we see just one charge on our
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> on, but I think you might get the picture. 

These would seem to be more likely required as _fields_ in a _single_
transaction rather than separate records scattered through an audit
trail.

Obviously there may be more data required for a _positive_ result on a
check for stolen or frozen card, but it would seem silly to scatter
data about several records just for, say, a flag of whether the PIN
was used.

> These are the types of
> information that banks and card companies require ...

And they are called 'fields' or 'attributes' not 'records'.
```

*still* a lot of folks that think we live on a pancake. Hence the
International Flat Earth Society.

> Did they ?  Actually there are people who still say that 'we' never
> got to the moon, and never will.

Touche! Good point.... was it just good matte paintings and a studio in the
Mojave Desert??????

>
> > Heck, if someone told me in the '70s that one day a computer called a
> > laptop would have more processing and memory power than the IBM 360 I
was
> > working on at the time, I would have laughed them out of the building!
>
> Well, silly you.

Yep. If I'd felt differently back then, I'd be wealthy and retired in New
Zealand by now.....  [grin]

>
> > As a customer we tend to think that a single swipe of a card
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> trail. <snip>

True. Of course, I remember back in COBOL 101 that my instructor said stuff
like "COBOL is a self-documenting programming language," and "COBOL is very
simple to read." (Come to think of it, he was probably a member of the
International Flat Earth Society.) With those kind of thoughts running
through my head, one of the first programs I had to fix professionally was
written by a former assembler programmer who was about as cryptic and
complex in his COBOL coding as I have ever seen since. (i.e. "DATE-OPENED"
would be something like "A5687," something along that line.) He was a
wonderful guy, wrote wonderful and efficient assember, but his COBOL was
greatly wanting in departments of "self-documenting" and "very simple."

I think it goes without saying that the way something *should* be coded is
not always the way it *is* coded. Also, because there is a better technique
of coding and file layout doesn't mean that folks are going to use it. I've
generally found that the axiom "if it's working, don't touch it" applies a
lot, especially the more complex and/or older the system is. That idea is
changing, fortunately, and I think we have Y2K (amongst other things) to
thank for that. Still, it's bad coding and/or new technology that keeps us
all in business..... and I say that a little tongue-in-cheek.

>
> Obviously there may be more data required for a _positive_ result on a
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> And they are called 'fields' or 'attributes' not 'records'.

Yep.... that's the way I would handle it today. Twenty years ago I might
have done something different. The client we're working with goes waaaaaaay
back, and it wouldn't surprise me to find they've interpreted this
differently. It's very possible that this conversion is trying to migrate
towards a more efficient way of doing things.

That's the dichotomy of conversions.... the client wants the new technology,
but they want it to work just like the old system.

Gonzo
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-09-03T12:09:43+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f55323a_7@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com> <217e491a.0308291403.530033c4@posting.google.com> <1fh1lvs4r45ch5g25ifj2tktplpjieu76h@4ax.com> <Utf4b.451$rf4.185@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com> <217e491a.0309011205.144f6171@posting.google.com> <pR35b.364$jm7.45@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com>`

```

"Gonzo" <ckhamel1961@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:pR35b.364$jm7.45@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com...
>
> "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Touche! Good point.... was it just good matte paintings and a studio in
the
> Mojave Desert??????
>

I remember the 20th July, 1969 as if it was yesterday. It was a beautiful
sunny day in Auckland, New Zealand. Our punch card department had windows
that looked out over the harbour and the moon was visible in the daytime
sky.

We had transistor radios listening to the exchange between the Eagle and
Houston.

Around 3 in the afternoon, everybody (programmers, management, punch girls,
data entry clerks...) drifted towards the windows and we stood looking at
the moon as we listened to the famous exchange. I don't believe anyone there
was not touched by it. When we watched those fuzzy pictures of Armstrong and
Aldrin bouncing round the moon on our TVs in the evening, it didn't have the
same immediacy and impact that listening to it did in the afternoon.

I don't personally believe it was all a fake, but even if it was, it
enriched our lives and made us think about higher things than the daily
grind of office work.

> >
> > > Heck, if someone told me in the '70s that one day a computer called a
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Zealand by now.....  [grin]

LOL! Gonzo, I remember saying: "In 25 years they'll have all this on a
desktop." That was in the late 60s. One of my colleagues remarked: "Nah,
Pete, not in our lifetime..."

I am now semi-retired in New Zealand (though far from wealthy <G>). Maybe if
I'd had the foresight to predict laptops, I'd be fully retired, and
loaded...<G>.

> >
> > > As a customer we tend to think that a single swipe of a card
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> True. Of course, I remember back in COBOL 101 that my instructor said
stuff
> like "COBOL is a self-documenting programming language," and "COBOL is
very
> simple to read." (Come to think of it, he was probably a member of the
> International Flat Earth Society.) With those kind of thoughts running
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You have to try and understand that many of the concepts we now take for
granted simply weren't around then. Assembler programmers never really
considered documentation. It was only after having to maintain some of my
own assembler, months later, that I decided it might be a good idea to
comment every line...(To this day, when coding in any assembler, I comment
every line...)

Many of us found COBOL to be "imprecise" in the sense that you couldn't do
things with it like you could with assembler. Having developed some
favourite routines with logical operations (like clearing fields by XORing
with themselves, and swapping fields without an intermediate work area...
<G>) it was a bit disappointing to find that COBOL just wasn't up to it.

Nowadays, I'd not even consider the extra overheads in swapping fields in
COBOL, but in those days we had 10K available to work in (the Operating
System took 6K; 4K if we reconfigured it, and we did for some "special"
jobs...).

I guess my point is that the World has moved on. It probably isn't fair to
whip historical cats; things were different then.

> I think it goes without saying that the way something *should* be coded is
> not always the way it *is* coded. Also, because there is a better
technique
> of coding and file layout doesn't mean that folks are going to use it.
I've
> generally found that the axiom "if it's working, don't touch it" applies a
> lot, especially the more complex and/or older the system is. That idea is
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
A fair point. However, there is a question mark over how much longer bad
coding and new technology can continue to keep COBOL programmers in
business... Coding is getting better. The need for it may be diminishing.
Technology is getting "smarter"... I reckon the writing's on the wall.

> >
> > Obviously there may be more data required for a _positive_ result on a
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> have done something different. The client we're working with goes
waaaaaaay
> back, and it wouldn't surprise me to find they've interpreted this
> differently. It's very possible that this conversion is trying to migrate
> towards a more efficient way of doing things.
>
> That's the dichotomy of conversions.... the client wants the new
technology,
> but they want it to work just like the old system.
>
Amen! Heard that so many times. And in adopting that position, they tie the
new technology's hands behind its back, then grizzle that the new technology
isn't that great after all... The amazing thing is that, very often, even
though they hogtied it, the new technology is able to implement their
requirements. As they slowly untie it and become more familiar with it,
things improve, and then you'll hear the same people who said they wanted it
to be exactly like the "old" system, saying how much they embrace the new
technology and how they always advocated the use of it....<G>

I usually congratulate them on being so forward thinking...

Just in finishing, Gonzo, I was browsing through Richard Feynman's "Six Easy
Pieces" which sits on my coffee table with other similar works by Michio
Kaku, John Gribben, Stephen Hawking, et al.

In a lecture to Freshmen at Caltech in 1987 (the supernova of '87 had just
been discovered and Feynman was very excited about it). He said:
"Tycho Brae had his supernova and Kepler had his. Then there weren't any for
400 years. Now I have mine. "
The class fell silent, and Feynman continued on.
"There are 10**11 stars in the Galaxy. That used to be a HUGE number. But
it's only 100 billion. It's less than the National deficit! We used to call
them astronomical numbers. Now we should call them economical numbers."
The class dissolved in laughter, and Feynman, having captured his audience,
went on with his lecture.

I guess in this context your 400 billion records is only 4 Galaxies of data
<G>.

I reckon you will need the capabilities of Deep Thought  (see "Hitchhiker's
Guide") to process it in three hours, but don't exceed 42 trillion....<G>

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2003-09-01T13:58:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0309011258.28ceec99@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Bottom posting

"Gonzo" <ckhamel1961@yahoo.com> wrote in message news:<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com>...
> I am assisting a colleague of mine in trying to design a way of sorting a
> whole bunch of records. We are talking over 400 billion... yes, you read
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks in advance for your thoughts and ideas

I think this is an interesting topic.  Having read many of the
responses, I don't think you should be discouraged from posting here
even though your task details are rather too skimpy.  After all
casting the net for ideaa as well as asking your own colleagues is a
good approach to a demanding task, as some of us might already have
done something similar.

My own feeling is why does it all have to be in one table?  Wouldn't
monthly or weekly tables be a better approach?  They could be joined
as required and would make it much easier to archive data more than a
few months or weeks old.  You would just leave the older tables out of
the queries and add new ones as time progresses.  Multiple tables
would reduce the amount of sorting to be done at one time and would
minimise potential wastage due to job aborts due to contention, system
glitches, etc.  Breaking the job up into smaller parts would also be
less disruptive to other activities and might also facilitate running
more than one such job at a time if your processor(s) make that
practical.

Robert
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

- **From:** "Gonzo" <ckhamel1961@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-02T16:48:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pR35b.363$jm7.84@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com> <fcd09c56.0309011258.28ceec99@posting.google.com>`

```

"Robert Jones" <robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:

> I think this is an interesting topic.  Having read many of the
> responses, I don't think you should be discouraged from posting here
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> done something similar.

Skimpy? Yes... I think that was pretty obvious. That's all I had at the
time, and when I get more I'll try to be back and post some more. It was a
general question, and I was/am looking for general answers. I forget
sometimes that using newsgroups is like those wierd dreams we have when
you're naked and and you walk into a room shouting, "Can someone tell me
where I can get some clothes please?" Then the strangers walk up to you and
say, "Did you know you're naked?" or "You have earwax in your navel" or "I
have a sock in my pocket, and I'll give it to you for money." There's always
a risk when asking for help/guidance/counsel.

> My own feeling is why does it all have to be in one table?  <etc....snip>

I should explain that our conversion business focuses on just the
conversion, and not the actual import or export to the systems. We leave the
actual import/export to our client who is the most familiar with their
applications. We help facilitate the transformation between systems by
engaging the old system and new system experts, then we code and test to
what we cooperatively develop. We're no different than just hiring any sort
of consultants to do the same thing, other than we use and maintain a
specialized set of tools for the migration process, have a very specific
methodology on how to do our conversions, and we deliver on a fixed price
for the client. I'm absolutely sure this is not the *only* way to approach
conversions out there, but it's worked for us for over 10 years, it's still
working for us, and I can't complain about the year-end bonuses.

So, in a round-about way of answering your question about one table, joined
tables, etc., it's the way the client wants the data "at the moment." I've
*usually* found that if we can tangibly show there's a better way to handle
the data or find that's it's not feasible to merge 400 billion records
within our allocated window, then they'll change to accommodate the
improvements or limitations. You definitely have some good insights, and
they will be passed along.

Gonzo
```

Werner

- **From:** weberm@polaris.net (Ubiquitous)

- Of the 50-odd Muslim-dominated nations, two are putative democracies:

satphone connected to European satellites, Japanese camcorder, Finninsh
watch, Chinese knock-off of a Russian AK-47 propped against the wall. It may
be that his sandals are domestic.

Sitting atop 70% of the world's deliverable energy, the Arab/Muslim world
has no other excuse than lack of will to do right by its populace.

chooses to devote its gaze, backward, to the unatainable.

On  5-Sep-2003, "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:

> Ah, right, about Indonesia. But the rest are monarchies (Kuwait, Jordan),
> Tyrannies (Lybia), Theocracies (Iran), Oligarchies (Egypt, Syria), or
> out-and-out anarchies (Sudan, Somalia). And the type of the government
> matters. Democracies don't start wars or sanction terrorism.

Democracies do start wars.   Heck, the U.S. had a civil war, with both sides
democracies.   We can claim that we were forced to start the war in Iraq - but
we can't claim that we didn't start it.

Rebels to democracies do do terrorism (Oklahoma City).   Rich and powerful
states do not sanction terrorism - using their definitions.   Dropping a bomb
from an airplane is what we do - carrying a bomb on one's back is what poorer
people do.   I can't see that the poor bastard who is blown up cares whether it
was a terrorist or a civilized warrior who does the deed.

The big tough guy calls the little guy a coward for not standing toe-to-toe and
duking it out like a civilized fighter.   The Philistines must have thought
David cheated against Goliath.
```

>>
>>> - One is hard-pressed to name a single human advancement originating
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

While it might be true that the Muslim world hasn't done much in the
last 100 years, they certainly did more than their share earlier in
our history.  Maybe more than any other group of people in the areas
of science, math, medicine and art.  It doesn't make sense to condemn
or belittle a whole group of people because they haven't done anything
extraordinary in recent times.

>>
>>>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Funny you should try to use Bernard Lewis to make your point.  He is
someone I've read a lot of in recent years.  He has said the following
in regards to the reasons some Muslims hate the West:

"Islam is one of the world's great religions. Let me be explicit about
what I, as a historian of Islam who is not a Muslim, mean by that.
Islam has brought comfort and peace of mind to countless millions of
men and women. It has given dignity and meaning to drab and
impoverished lives. It has taught people of different races to live in
brotherhood and people of different creeds to live side by side in
reasonable tolerance. It inspired a great civilization in which others
besides Muslims lived creative and useful lives and which, by its
achievement, enriched the whole world. But Islam, like other
religions, has also known periods when it inspired in some of its
followers a mood of hatred and violence. It is our misfortune that
part, though by no means all or even most, of the Muslim world is now
going through such a period, and that much, though again not all, of
that hatred is directed against us."

We should not exaggerate the dimensions of the problem. The Muslim
world is far from unanimous in its rejection of the West, nor have the
Muslim regions of the Third World been the most passionate and the
most extreme in their hostility. There are still significant numbers,
in some quarters perhaps a majority, of Muslims with whom we share
certain basic cultural and moral, social and political, beliefs and
aspirations; there is still an imposing Western presence -- cultural,
economic, diplomatic -- in Muslim lands, some of which are Western
allies. Certainly nowhere in the Muslim world, in the Middle East or
elsewhere, has American policy suffered disasters or encountered
problems comparable to those in Southeast Asia or Central America.
There is no Cuba, no Vietnam, in the Muslim world, and no place where
American forces are involved as combatants or even as "advisers." But
there is a Libya, an Iran, and a Lebanon, and a surge of hatred that
distresses, alarms, and above all baffles Americans.

(If you really wish to educate yourself about hese issues, read his
whole two part essay on the subject at:

 http://www.theatlantic.com/issues/90sep/rage.htm

>>
>>> Unfortunately for us westerners, and the Islamic people, their
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Sharia is the body of religious law governing the Sunni and Shia
branches of Islam.  It is derived from  two major and two lesser
sources. The first major source is specific guidance laid down in the
Qur'an, and the second source is the Sunnah, literally the 'Way', i.e.
the way that Muhammad (the Prophet of Islam) lived his life. (The
compilation of all what Muhammad said, did, or approved of is called
the Hadith.) A lesser source of authority is Qiyas, which is the
extension by analogy of existing Sharia law to new situations.

How much different is that from our own rule of law in the West?  Is
there not an arguement being made in Alabama right now that our laws
are based on the Ten Commandments?  If you say yes, then aren't we
just as guilty of looking backwards by your way of thinking?  

Like Jewish law and Christian canon law, Islamic law has no one, set
meaning for all time and places. In the hands of moderates, religious
law can be moderate, even liberal. In the hands of post-Englightenment
readers of philosophy, religious law is relegated to ritual (as
opposed to law in a civil sense), or even to just being history. In
the hands of zealots, it becomes legally enforced against all people
of a faith, and even against all people that come under their control.
Islamic law to American Muslims in Boston is a very different thing
than Islamic law to religious Muslims in Saudi Arabia, Gaza, or
Pakistan. Both are following Islamic law, yet it varies as much as
individual Muslims vary. (As is true for Jews and Christians, etc.)

Regards,

This might be an opportune time to quote Herr Bormann and his public 
pronouncement that "National Socialism and Christianity are 
irreconcilable".

I don't wish to sound as if I am defending Christians or Nazis, (as an 
angry, apathetic, agnostic libertarian I would never do either) but 
there is a need for accuracy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: {FLAME} Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Tux <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-09-06T08:02:03+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
JerryMouse wrote:

>>> ISLAM: Winning the hearts and minds of the world, one bomb at a time.
> 
> Am I missing something? What's short-sighted, idiotic, or dumb-ass about
> the statement?

Yes, you probably are missing something: like _why_ some Islamists, and
Palestinians, think that retribution is their only option left against
American Imperialism.
 
> Islamic extremism is the problem.

Only to Americam Imperialism.
 
> - Excluding oil, the exports of the Arab countries plus Iran, combined,
> total less than Finland.

1700 years ago or so the measure of a country was how much they could send
back to Imperial Rome to make the life of the Romans more comfortable and
more splendid.  You seem to be making a similar measurement.

> - Spain translates more books from English into Spanish each year than the
> entire Muslim world translates non-native books into Arabic, Farsi, etc.

Why would they want 'non-native' books ?  Presumably you think that _your_
books are somehow superior and should be force fed to Arabs to indoctrinate
them with 'American values'.

> - One is hard-pressed to name a single human advancement originating in
> Muslim lands for the last 100 years: Music, Art, Medicine, Science,
> Literature, anything. 

But that may be because you are ignorant of what they do actually achieve,
your perception is based entirely on what is presented to you by your press
and media.

> One only has to watch a video of Ben Laden:
> Norwegian satphone connected to European satellites, Japanese camcorder,
> Finninsh watch, Chinese knock-off of a Russian AK-47 propped against the
> wall. It may be that his sandals are domestic.

Yes, it is the products of _western_ technology that allow freedom fighters 
to exist.  Just as pushing native American Indians off their land was no
problem at all until they got hold of guns.
 
> Sitting atop 70% of the world's deliverable energy, 

'Deliverable' to America, no doubt.

> the Arab/Muslim world
> has no other excuse than lack of will to do right by its populace.
 
> Unfortunately for us westerners, and the Islamic people, their society
> chooses to devote its gaze, backward, to the unatainable.

That is correct, the aim of every people must be to become 'American'.
```

Tux wrote:

> That is correct, the aim of every people must be to become 'American'.

The aim of every people must be to become FREE.  America (for all its 
problems) is a great example of that.  Once you taste freedom, you don't 
want anything else.  Someone raised in oppression doesn't even know 
enough sometimes to know exactly what it is they want, only that they 
don't want to be oppressed.

I personally LOVE Ubiquitous's tag line...  :)  When this murderer who 
was just executed originally killed that abortion doctor, mainstream 
Christians throughout America decried his actions as contrary to the 
Bible (which they were).  However, we've heard no such denunciation of 
9/11 or some of these other things from the "mainstream Islamic 
community" - they're too caught up in being angry at America because of 
our freedom.

If making them American makes them free, then by all means, long live 
American Imperialism.  (Although, I must ask - apart from Alaska and 
Hawaii, what outside of the mainland US is part of our empire?)
```

Australia. I'd give it 20 years... We are already getting Oz TV channels.
It's not that I'm anti-Oz... It's just that I believe NZ offers values and a
way of life that is unique. (Whether it is good or not, I won't debate here
<G>).

Pete.

> We know that NZ will become a world financial leader.

We lead by being first everyday.

(as I write this it is almost 8am Monday while Sydney is 5:50am,
London is Sunday  evening and New York is Sunday afternoon).
```

On Fri, 05 Sep 2003 18:32:43 -0500, LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
enlightened us:

>Tux wrote:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>Hawaii, what outside of the mainland US is part of our empire?)

It has nothing to do with a hatred of "our" freedom.  And there was
much condemnation from the Arab world following the 9/11 attack had
one been able to hear it over the din of revenge and retribution that
was going on.

The Arab and Islamic reaction to Al-Qaeda's attack and to the initial
US military response also highlights the degree to which the US has
become estranged from these societies. Although many Arab and Islamic
leaders were quick to condemn the attack and to reject Bin Laden's
call for a new jihad, Arab and Muslim opinion remains sharply critical
of US policy in the Middle East. These attitudes make it more
difficult for moderate Arab governments to support the war against
terrorism and make it more likely that the US campaign against
Al-Qaeda will create new sympathizers.

An extremely good essay on 9/11, terrorism and the U.S. involvement in
it can be found at
http://www.mindef.gov.sg/safti/pointer/Vol28_4/6.html

Take some time and read it.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Public Restroom Graffiti:



MEN'S RULE OF THUMB: IF IT HAS TITS OR WHEELS, 
YOU'RE GOING TO HAVE TROUBLE WITH IT. 
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve

- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com> <bj7nja$5ll$2@news.utelfla.com> <ibW5b.10899$Mb2.400506@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <8cmdnY7z0sF4HMWiXTWJjw@giganews.com> <bjaq4l$qrg$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <vli78tdj0vt6f1@corp.supernews.com> <e1sjlv8g3uarimdpq7g8okvi0as05bv1n9@4ax.com>`

```
SkippyPB wrote:

>> If making them American makes them free, then by all means, long live
>> American Imperialism.  (Although, I must ask - apart from Alaska and
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> was going on.

This is a bald-faced, unmitigated, and flagrant lie. There was virtually
zero condemnation from the Arab world directed to the 9/11 attackers, and
almost none from the Muslim world at large. In the days following 9/11, not
one Arab head of government repudiated the attack. Not one high-ranking Arab
religious leader expressed allegiance toward stopping terrorism. Not one.

>
> The Arab and Islamic reaction to Al-Qaeda's attack and to the initial
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> of US policy in the Middle East.

Another egregious lie that doesn't pass the giggle test. "Many Arab ...
leaders were quick to condemn the attack and to reject..." is so brazen in
its falsity as to go down in history as a monumental scam. Of the "Many," I
defy anyone to name a few. There are 50-odd governmental leaders to pick
from and several hundred accepted religious leaders. Show me something less
than unanimity, support, and fealty toward terrorism (excepting, maybe,
Pakistan).

Dancing in the streets of Iraq hardly qualifies as "sharply critical."

> An extremely good essay on 9/11, terrorism and the U.S. involvement in
> it can be found at
> http://www.mindef.gov.sg/safti/pointer/Vol28_4/6.html
>
> Take some time and read it.

I read it. Written by a mid-level serving officer in the Singapore army. His
credentials are zero or closely adjacent, his observations myopic, and his
conclusions irrelevant. Of the tens of thousands of discourses on the
subject, urging consideration of something written by the equivalent of a
Boy Scout leader is pitiful.
```

|There was virtually

not

Arab
| religious leader expressed allegiance toward stopping terrorism. Not one.

BULL SHIT.

http://www.arabicnews.com/ansub/Daily/Day/010912/2001091229.html

Mubarak denounces terror attacks in America
Egypt-USA, Politics, 9/12/2001

Egyptian President Hosni Mubarak condemned terrorism under any
circumstances, terming the terrorist attacks that took place in the United
States yesterday as "heinous and unimaginable."

President Mubarak was giving statements following an expanded cabinet
meeting yesterday at the presidential palace in Heliopolis.

The meeting discussed only one issue: the deplorable unexpected incident in
the United States, said the president, referring to those who fell victims
to this incident.

http://www.cnn.com/2003/WORLD/meast/08/17/saudi.clerics.ap/index.html

Saudi clerics condemn terrorists
Sunday, August 17, 2003 Posted: 11:44 AM EDT (1544 GMT)

RIYADH, Saudi Arabia (AP) -- Saudi Arabia's highest religious body has
condemned violence by Islamic militants and deemed helping terrorists "one
of the greatest sins."

The statement from the Council of Senior Clerics came a day after Saudi
authorities arrested at least 11 suspected militants and seized a large
weapons cache in the southern Jazan province.

The Council of Senior Clerics said in a statement carried by the official
Saudi Press Agency that participating in terrorist acts is "a dangerous
criminal act ... punishable by Islamic law."

Terrorism, "is an act of sabotage, and an absolute aberration. (Those behind
recent attacks and plots) shouldn't have been moved by corrupt statements
and slogans that cause division and corrupt the nation," the statement said,
adding that justifications for violence have "no religious grounds."

http://groups.colgate.edu/aarislam/response.htm#Expressions%20of%20grief%20a
nd%20sympathy%20in%20the%20Arab%20and%20Muslim%20world:

 The terrorist act was strongly condemned by every single Palestinian
organization including Fatah, the Democratic Front for the Liberation of
Palestine, the Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine, Hamas, Workers
Unions and Committees, Human Right organizations (AlHaq, Law, Palestine
Center for Human Rights), student associations, municipalities, mosques and
churches, etc.

-In Iran, Tehran's main soccer stadium observed an unprecedented minute's
silence in sympathy with the victims.

-Iran's Ayatollah Imami Kashani spoke of a catastrophic act of terrorism
which could only be condemned by all Muslims, adding the whole world should
mobilise against terrorism.

http://www.centerpeace.org/MEWIR/Volume%201%5C2001-10-05.htm

The Center for Middle East Peace and Economic Cooperation is pleased to
announce the first issue of its weekly Middle East Week in Review.  In the
three weeks since the terrorist attacks, the Middle East and the need to
include the region in an international coalition against terrorism have
become the center of attention.  All governments within the region, with the
exception of Iraq, have condemned the attack on the U.S.  King Abdullah of
Jordan, FM Ahmed Maher of Egypt, and FM Ismail Cem of Turkey have visited
Washington to meet with President Bush and Secretary Powell.  A European
Union delegation has gone to Pakistan, Iran, Saudi Arabia, Egypt, Lebanon
and Syria to garner support for the effort against terrorism.  Given these
developments, we believe knowledge of and reporting on the Middle East and
North Africa is more important than ever; hence this weekly report.  We
welcome your comments and feedback.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: {FLAME} Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-08T09:43:06-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-356658.09430608092003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com> <bj7nja$5ll$2@news.utelfla.com> <ibW5b.10899$Mb2.400506@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <8cmdnY7z0sF4HMWiXTWJjw@giganews.com> <bjaq4l$qrg$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <vli78tdj0vt6f1@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <vli78tdj0vt6f1@corp.supernews.com>,
 LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote:

> Tux wrote:
> 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Hawaii, what outside of the mainland US is part of our empire?)

We acquired Guam, the P.I., Cuba and Puerto Rico in a war to keep 
Spanish Imperialism out of our hemisphere.  Cube was converted to a free 
nation almost immediately, P.I. a few decades later.  To date neither 
Guam or PR have seriously asked for anything but continued commonwealth.

The problem, as I see it, is that our greatest detractors are, in fact, 
very free.  But they are free because of US military might.  Dependance 
breeds resentment.

France is the worst example of this -- for a century they have existed 
only because of US military intervention.  Yet as those years progressed 
they became worse and worse about undermining US efforts to keep them 
free and safe.

Richard is a fine example, on a much smaller scale.  He seems convinced 
that everything from Coral Sea through SEATO through the presence of the 
Pacific Fleet throughout the cold war were just some twisted conspiracy 
to force him to drink more Coca-Cola.

<newsgroup_psychoanalysis>
It is awfully easy to stand behind a larger sibling and throw taunts at 
the schoolyard bully.  But somewhere, in the back of their minds they 
know that they cannot throw those taunts without the larger sibling.  
The resentment the feel toward the bully, or their own weakness 
gradually erodes their self-esteem and is transfered to the larger 
sibling.  

Thus, the strength of the larger sibling becomes a symbol and constant 
reminder of their own weakness and inadequacy.
</newsgroup_psychoanalysis>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: {FLAME} Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-09-08T12:11:21-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0309081111.8fa0f0e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com> <bj7nja$5ll$2@news.utelfla.com> <ibW5b.10899$Mb2.400506@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <8cmdnY7z0sF4HMWiXTWJjw@giganews.com> <bjaq4l$qrg$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <vli78tdj0vt6f1@corp.supernews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-356658.09430608092003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote 

> Richard is a fine example, on a much smaller scale.  He seems convinced 
> that everything from Coral Sea through SEATO through the presence of the 
> Pacific Fleet throughout the cold war were just some twisted conspiracy 
> to force him to drink more Coca-Cola.

Not at all.  Mostly it is to ensure that the 'next war' is not fought
on American soil.

For most of WWI America stood aside and sold arms to both sides.  They
only intervened when it was obvious that neither side would be able to
recover for decades if left to continue to fight the stalemate.  That
is to say, the armament makers saw their european markets evaporating
and lobbied to ensure that domestic sales took up the slack    ;-)

Of course this is entirely understandable when the make up of the
Americans is looked into.  They kicked out most of the French and the
English and took as immegrants large numbers of Germans and Irish. If
there had been a vote they may have supported the Germans.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: {FLAME} Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-09T09:32:26-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-DCDC90.09322309092003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com> <bj7nja$5ll$2@news.utelfla.com> <ibW5b.10899$Mb2.400506@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <8cmdnY7z0sF4HMWiXTWJjw@giganews.com> <bjaq4l$qrg$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <vli78tdj0vt6f1@corp.supernews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-356658.09430608092003@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0309081111.8fa0f0e@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <217e491a.0309081111.8fa0f0e@posting.google.com>,
 riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote 
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> on American soil.

No, the two oceans and a very competent navy do that for us.  

We did it to keep you from living in a Soviet slave state.  Often at 
great expense to ourselves and with little gratitude from the 
beneficiaries.


> For most of WWI America stood aside and sold arms to both sides.  They
> only intervened when it was obvious that neither side would be able to
> recover for decades if left to continue to fight the stalemate.  That
> is to say, the armament makers saw their european markets evaporating
> and lobbied to ensure that domestic sales took up the slack    ;-)

You seem to imply that a then small, young country with little to offer 
had some moral responsibility to arbitrate, by force possibly, a dispute 
between six major old world powers.  But now, the same country, the only  
major world power, should mind its own business and leave everyone 
alone.  What was so terrible about our early neutrality?  We had no 
stake in what the parties were waring about.

Interesting that you view everything through corporate financial 
glasses.  But such a view in some cases ignores key trigger events that 
had nothing to do with arms trading.  France was near collapse in 1916 
while Germany was not.  England forged an inflamatory telegraph and 
"let" us intercept it; German sank a passenger ship; et al.


> Of course this is entirely understandable when the make up of the
> Americans is looked into.  They kicked out most of the French and the
> English and took as immigrants large numbers of Germans and Irish. If
> there had been a vote they may have supported the Germans.

We kicked out the French and English?  When?  We tossed the king and 
that silly royalty thing (at least until that Diana woman showed up).  
But we never evicted anyone from either country.  Huge parts of our 
population are English and French.

Our 1910 census (data at  
http://fisher.lib.virginia.edu/cgi-local/censusbin/census/cen.pl) shows 
a total population of 91.6M with 13.3M foreign-born white people leaving 
us with 90% native born.  Of the remaining 10% 2.4M were German and 1.3M 
were Irish.  Perhaps 25% of the total immigrant population.  Those 
numbers are on par with what we see from the other side of the war -- 
England, France, Italy and Russia had almost 4M.  Austria adds an extra 
1.7M to the scale on the German/Irish side.  You are giving a great deal 
of weight to about 1M extra German/Irish/Austrian immigrants in a 
country that was very suspicious of foreigners and quite racist.

France and England had supported the then-recent US rebellion by selling 
ships and arms to them -- something most of the people of the time would 
remember well.  (How kindly disposed do you think England would be if we 
started selling the IRA Nuclear subs?  Or Russia if we supplied Chechnya 
with F-16's?)

FWIW we should have supported the Germans or left them to win on their 
own, as was likely in 1916.  A world without France and possibly without 
the Soviet Union would likely have been the result -- and I cannot think 
of any good arguments against such an outcome.
```

peace in the 1930s and her subsequent refusal to honor her commitment to 
Poland.  I'm talking about her shabby treatment of NATO troops and their 
efforts.  I'm talking about her strange efforts in southeast asia after 
the war and its related destabilaztion.  But I certainly am not talking 
about needing France for anything recent, they have no military use.

shipping?  That was more a case of France wanting to take what England 
had -- or at very least shove a nasty thorn a bit further into her paw 
to distract her from other French aims.

> Bush has alienated a good portion of the world, and now he's getting paid

> Pearl Harbor.

I'm not sure why the U.S. would have -- the treaty of Versailles put 
France and England on the hook to go to war with Germany if she invaded 
Poland.  The U.S. had no such responsibility to do so -- and could not 
have moved significant troops to Europe in time anyway.  

France on the other hand had 100ish divisions sitting on the western 
border of Germany in September 1939.  She "declared" war and then went 
out for wine and cheese, leaving Poland in the lurch.


> | > The French could argue that they supported the Colonies during the
> American
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> But would the American Revolution have succeeded without France?

It is a good question.  It is quite likely that the AR would not have 
been successful without overt French help.  Although the non-overt, 
incidental, French assistance of keeping large numbers of English troops 
occupied in other parts of the world was also a great help.


> | > Bush has alienated a good portion of the world, and now he's getting
> paid
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> How many thousands of troops have they sent?

Poland, Spain, Ukraine, Latvia, Mongolia and about a dozen others have 
committed about a division collectively.

Quite a bit more than France has committed to anything in a very long 
time.

The rest of the NATO countries were pretty well behind the Iraq thing.  
Why is it that not getting buy-in from Germany and France somehow 
equates to 'alienating a good portion of the world'?  Why do those two 
countries matter so much?

http://www.startribune.com/stories/1576/2245655.html

Gunmen open fire on French peacekeepers in Kabul
Steve Gutkin

Published April 20, 2002 KABU21

KABUL, Afghanistan - Gunmen opened fire on French peacekeepers patrolling
the Afghan capital of Kabul, slightly injuring one soldier, a peacekeeper
said Saturday.

The French soldiers returned fire during the attack near the Kabul airport
Friday evening, shooting 70 to 80 rounds, said Capt. Serge Khun, spokesman
for the 18-nation, 4,500-strong international peacekeeping force responsible
for security in Kabul.

| The rest of the NATO countries were pretty well behind the Iraq thing.

NATO is split on Iraq.

| Why is it that not getting buy-in from Germany and France somehow
| equates to 'alienating a good portion of the world'?  Why do those two
| countries matter so much?

They are members of NATO, and they could be considered to senior members.

As for alienating the world:
Try doing a Google search for Bush & arrogance

Bush did tie with Saddam in this poll.

http://www.ananova.com/news/story/sm_753735.html

Bush 'as great a threat to peace as Saddam'

A survey has found people consider George W Bush as great a threat to world
peace as Saddam Hussein.

The online poll compiled by YouGov found that when asked who was the biggest
threat to peace, 45% said the US President - exactly that same number as
named the Iraqi dictator.

The poll found 47% regarded America as a bully, and 27% thought it was a
force for good.

Just 34% of people questioned said they admired the US, against 54% who did
not.

The poll also showed support for Tony Blair continuing to slump amid the
preparations for war.

It put Labour on 35%, two points ahead of the Tories on 33%, with the
Liberal Democrats on 25%.

The poll, commissioned by the Sunday Times, involved 2,032 adults.

September 11, 2003 08:43:05

Blair, BBC Stung By WMD Inquiry

The row over the way Blair made the case for war has eroded his once solid
popularity. Some polls indicate fewer than one in four Britons trusts the
prime minister.

http://www.theledger.com/apps/pbcs.dll/article?AID=/20030911/NEWS/309110355/
1036
Published Thursday, September 11, 2003

And President Bush's popularity has been dropping because of the failure to
turn up weapons of mass destruction in Iraq -- the principal justification
given for the pre-emptive strike on Iraq. British Prime Minister Tony Blair,
who was our principal ally in Iraq, is in serious political trouble for the
same reason.

- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com> <bj7nja$5ll$2@news.utelfla.com> <ibW5b.10899$Mb2.400506@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <8cmdnY7z0sF4HMWiXTWJjw@giganews.com> <bjaq4l$qrg$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <vli78tdj0vt6f1@corp.supernews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-356658.09430608092003@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0309081111.8fa0f0e@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-DCDC90.09322309092003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote 

> We did it to keep you from living in a Soviet slave state.  Often at 
> great expense to ourselves and with little gratitude from the 
> beneficiaries.

Soviet slave states do not make for a good customer base.

> You seem to imply that a then small, young country with little to offer 
> had some moral responsibility to arbitrate, by force possibly, a dispute 
> between six major old world powers. 

Did I imply that ?  I was merely indicating that it was in America's
best commercial interest (or that of the arms manufacturers) to
intervene. I didn't know that the issue of 'moral' was even close.

> But now, the same country, the only  
> major world power, should mind its own business and leave everyone 
> alone.  

Did I imply that ?

> What was so terrible about our early neutrality?  We had no 
> stake in what the parties were waring about.

Not in _what_, everyone had forgotten what that was anyway, but had a
major stake in the fact they were waring.

> Interesting that you view everything through corporate financial 
> glasses.  

Not everything, no, just the US foreign policy which seems to be
driven by lobbiests.

As for the other key triggers, those were fodder for the lobbiests to
change the arms buying from the europeans who could no longer afford
them to the US government who could.

> We kicked out the French and English?  When?  We tossed the king and 
> that silly royalty thing (at least until that Diana woman showed up).  

and the english army, and the Canadian english and french who invaded
from the north.

> But we never evicted anyone from either country.  Huge parts of our 
> population are English and French.

Ex- English and French.

> You are giving a great deal 
> of weight to about 1M extra German/Irish/Austrian immigrants in a 
> country that was very suspicious of foreigners and quite racist.

No more weight than the political leaders gave to them.
 
> France and England had supported the then-recent US rebellion 

Do you mean the fight for freedom from the tyranny of the North ?

> (How kindly disposed do you think England would be if we 
> started selling the IRA Nuclear subs?  Or Russia if we supplied Chechnya 
> with F-16's?)
 
> FWIW we should have supported the Germans or left them to win on their 
> own, as was likely in 1916.  

No, it was not likely that anyone would 'win'.  It was a complete
stalemate and likely to remain so until _everyone_ just gave up and
went home - which is what the French tried to do and the Russians
eventually did do, but it was of no consequence by that time as the
Germans were too.

> A world without France and possibly without 
> the Soviet Union would likely have been the result 

It was Czarist Russia at that time.

> -- and I cannot think 
> of any good arguments against such an outcome.

The World would be perfect if only we could get rid of the _______
(fill in blanks as required).
```

In article <217e491a.0309091601.4270a038@posting.google.com>,
 riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote 
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Soviet slave states do not make for a good customer base.

On the contrary, they loved our products.  There are those that claim 
that Levis, Rock 'n' Roll and McDonalds are more responsible for Soviet 
reform/breakup than M1s & MXs.

Of course, we would never defend anyones freedom unless there was 
something in it for us.


> > You seem to imply that a then small, young country with little to offer 
> > had some moral responsibility to arbitrate, by force possibly, a dispute 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Did I imply that ?

Well, yes, you did.  I must admit that you might not have in that 
specific post, but I've been reading CLC for years (and mostly manage to 
lurk without getting sucked into non-Cobol discussions).  You 
occasionally tell us how you really feel, e.g:

Richard Plinston (riplin@Azonic.co.nz) wrote@2002-08-29 01:38:35 PST: 
=> I don't think that America wants to rule the world, 
=> they just want the rest of the world to be free 
=> (as in freedom).  Free to be exactly like America,
=> free to drink Coke and eat McDonalds, free to 
=> benefit from America's political and legal system
=> by adopting US laws, free to buy american products.
=>
=> And if they don't want that 'freedom' then America
=> will bomb them. 

It is impossible not to consider the content of historic posts when 
reading current ones on similar subjects.  You seem to have a very low 
regard for 'commercial interests'. 

Looked at in that light it sounds like you are finding fault with the 
arms manufacturers and other 'commercial interests' selling to long 
established and recognized governments involved in a war in which their 
country is neutral.


> > Interesting that you view everything through corporate financial 
> > glasses.  
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> them to the US government who could.

Man, I thought I was cynical...

How is it that all the other governments of the world have managed to 
remain uninfluenced by the omnipotent arms manufacturers?  Or are all 
those other governments under the protection of the omnipotent black 
helicopter manufacturers?


> > France and England had supported the then-recent US rebellion 
> 
> Do you mean the fight for freedom from the tyranny of the North ?

Around here it is usually called the "War of Northern Aggression", but 
yes, that one.


> > (How kindly disposed do you think England would be if we 
> > started selling the IRA Nuclear subs?  Or Russia if we supplied Chechnya 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Germans were too.

Interesting, but highly questionable.  With the collapse of the eastern 
front in early 1917 Germany was in really good shape.  France was doomed 
without American help.


> > A world without France and possibly without 
> > the Soviet Union would likely have been the result 
> 
> It was Czarist Russia at that time.

That fell in early 1917 -- mostly do to the leadership efforts of the 
Czar.  Lenin made a separate peace with the Germans (Breast-Litovsk 
Treaty) that would have been absolute death to the Bolshevik revolution 
within a few short years.

Not only would all of the Soviet slave states west of Russia been under 
the protection of Imperial Germany, but the White Russians would have 
had a sympathetic, anti-communist, neighbor.  While Russia would have 
been deprived of Ukrainian resources it desperately needed.

Instead of being the most brutal, bloody government in the history of 
mankind the USSR would have been a quaint little footnote in history 
texts that never quite got off the ground. 

Of course, the treaty of Versailles revoked the Breast-Litovsk treaty, 
much to the regret of many eastern europeans subsequently invited to 
live in the new workers paradise.


> > -- and I cannot think 
> > of any good arguments against such an outcome.
> 
> The World would be perfect if only we could get rid of the _______
> (fill in blanks as required).

I never said a France-less world would be perfect, just a nicer place...
```

"Joe Zitzelberger" wrote:
> She is cute, you may have just identified the one useful cultural item
> that France has created in the past few hundred years...

Joe, I'm sure (hoping) your tongue is firmly in cheek!

More fuel from...

http://www.michaelmoore.com/words/message/index.php?messageDate=2003-03-17

6. Finally, we love France. Yes, they have pulled some royal screw-ups. Yes,
some of them can be pretty damn annoying. But have you forgotten we wouldn't
even have this country known as America if it weren't for the French? That
it was their help in the Revolutionary War that won it for us? That our
greatest thinkers and founding fathers -- Thomas Jefferson, Ben Franklin,
etc. -- spent many years in Paris where they refined the concepts that lead
to our Declaration of Independence and our Constitution? That it was France
who gave us our Statue of Liberty, a Frenchman who built the Chevrolet, and
a pair of French brothers who invented the movies? And now they are doing
what only a good friend can do -- tell you the truth about yourself,
straight, no b.s. Quit pissing on the French and thank them for getting it
right for once. You know, you really should have traveled more (like once)
before you took over. Your ignorance of the world has not only made you look
stupid, it has painted you into a corner you can't get out of.

Gary.
```

```
I had promised myself to stay out of this thread but, as the Doc would say,
"This one I CANNOT resist..." <G>

"Gary" <IDontThink@so.com.so> wrote in message
news:yZO9b.274638$2x.76063@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net...
> "Joe Zitzelberger" wrote:
> > She is cute, you may have just identified the one useful cultural item
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Now, I have a high regard for most of what Michael Moore does, but this time
he's just plain wrong...

> 6. Finally, we love France.

You speak for yourself, Michael...

> Yes, they have pulled some royal screw-ups.

Sure thing. I won't list them because it just makes me sad and angry...

>Yes,
> some of them can be pretty damn annoying.

As a Nation, consistently. My faith in Humanity makes me believe there HAVE
to be SOME decent French people and I am invariably polite to French people
I meet in my own country (or anywhere except France, actually. As I never go
there, this is not a problem.)

> But have you forgotten we wouldn't
> even have this country known as America if it weren't for the French?

Well, Michael, even though I have great respect and regard for America,
those of us who weren't born there might suggest that the jury's still out
on whether the French did the World a favour in this department.

I don't personally believe there wouldn't be a USA if it weren't for the
French. Washington had the British licked with or without French support,
because the fervour of the people and the rampant injustice imposed by King
George would eventually bring about the Birth of a Nation as being the only
acceptable solution. It might have been a DIFFERENT America, but we will
never know whether it would have been a BETTER or WORSE America.


>That
> it was their help in the Revolutionary War that won it for us?

While they were instrumental in SOME important victories, it is just rubbish
to suggest they won the American War of Independence. (Mind you, I'm sure
there are numerous French History texts that will support this view...<G>)

>That our
> greatest thinkers and founding fathers -- Thomas Jefferson, Ben Franklin,
> etc. -- spent many years in Paris where they refined the concepts that
lead
> to our Declaration of Independence and our Constitution?

There were many reasons to visit Paris in those days....

Ben Franklin also spent a lot of time in England (he was a visitor to the
Hellfire Club founded by Sir Francis Dashwood, and his portrait hangs in the
family manor at West Wycombe in Buckinghamshire. In fact, Dashwood gave him
money to help fund the revolution, even though he was also a supporter of
King George, who was a member of the club...). I notice that Michael Moore
is not claiming any British influence on the thinking of the founding
fathers, yet all of them I believe spent time in England, and it was the
English who first established (in Western Europe) the principle of the right
of the governed to participate in the governing (Magna Carta 1215).

> That it was France
> who gave us our Statue of Liberty,

Like, you couldn't build one yourselves <G>?

> a Frenchman who built the Chevrolet,

But Ford who got it mass produced...

> and
> a pair of French brothers who invented the movies?

OK, as a keen photographer, I have to admit they deserve credit for that.
However,  silver based photography is rapidly becoming an outmoded
technology. Within one century it has outlived its usefulness. And who says
someone else wouldn't have invented photography if the French hadn't?

>And now they are doing
> what only a good friend can do -- tell you the truth about yourself,

They wouldn't know "truth" if it bit them in the arse. They invented the
word "chauvinism". They are simply justifying their own political stance
which is based on their own political interests. Instead of supporting the
allies as they should have (for Christ's Sake, America (mainly) liberated
their country twice in the last century; isn't that worth anything? If they
disagreed with Bush's policy they could have expressed that in many much
more positive ways (Tony Blair did),  without attempting to disrupt the
European Union for the sake of their interests in Iraq. Instead, they
encouraged the Germans (who really should know better) to take a crack at
joint control of the EU in memory of former glory days. It was pathetic and
disgusting. )

> straight, no b.s. Quit pissing on the French and thank them for getting it
> right for once.

I wouldn't piss on the French if they were on fire. As for getting it right,
when that remarkable event occurs, I'll be first to comment.


> You know, you really should have traveled more (like once)
> before you took over. Your ignorance of the world has not only made you
look
> stupid, it has painted you into a corner you can't get out of.
>

That may well be true, but the ignorance of "Dubbyer" is not the ignorance
of America.  You can ALWAYS get out of a corner as long as you are prepared
to leave footmarks in the paint... (Sometimes the cost of getting out is the
cost of repairing the paintwork.)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: {FLAME} Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-19T09:48:32-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-A6F492.09483219092003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com> <ibW5b.10899$Mb2.400506@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <8cmdnY7z0sF4HMWiXTWJjw@giganews.com> <bjaq4l$qrg$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <vli78tdj0vt6f1@corp.supernews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-356658.09430608092003@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0309081111.8fa0f0e@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-DCDC90.09322309092003@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0309091601.4270a038@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-6EA98F.21063110092003@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0309110035.3bd9125a@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-C2168E.09065015092003@corp.supernews.com> <yZO9b.274638$2x.76063@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net>`

```
In article <yZO9b.274638$2x.76063@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net>,
 "Gary" <IDontThink@so.com.so> wrote:

> "Joe Zitzelberger" wrote:
> > She is cute, you may have just identified the one useful cultural item
> > that France has created in the past few hundred years...
> 
> Joe, I'm sure (hoping) your tongue is firmly in cheek!

Nope.  I couldn't be more serious.


> More fuel from...
> 
> http://www.michaelmoore.com/words/message/index.php?messageDate=2003-03-17

Yea.  Mike Moore is funny, but I don't take his foreign policy advice 
any more seriously that that which I get from the Franken & Davis show.


> 6. Finally, we love France. Yes, they have pulled some royal screw-ups. Yes,
> some of them can be pretty damn annoying. But have you forgotten we wouldn't
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> stupid, it has painted you into a corner you can't get out of.

No, we hate France and it is time to admit it.

This thread started on American Imperialism and American Bullying.  Lets 
hold those accusations up to a French light and see what shines through:

In 1918 the American Expeditionary Force rescued France from the Brink 
of defeat at the hands of the German Spring Offensive.  Some do not 
believe this, but in 1918 Henri Petain and Lloyd George both made 
statements to the effect of 'I think we have lost this puppy.'.  Did 
America use this opportunity to annex France and make her part of the 
growing American empire?  No. 

In 1919, as peace talks proceeded, America laid out a kinder, gentler, 
peace plan.  France wanted to humiliate her former foe.  Did American 
bully her into accepting a peace with honor?  No.

As a result, in 1944, American troops once again had to rescue France 
from her former foe.  This time they invaded and conquered a France that 
was defended, not by cheese-eating surrender monkeys, but by a real 
army.  Again, America declined to add France to her Empire, instead 
control was handed back to the French people.  American did not even 
force her to purchase to goods and services that supposedly make up 
Americas unique commercial imperialism.  On the contrary, American 
poured billions of dollars into rebuilding French infrastructure and 
industry.  Imperialism?  No.

American then offered to secure French safety in the future under this 
plan called NATO.  France halted the process until America agreed to not 
complain about French re-assertion in southeast asia.  American 
bullying?  Or French?

Did America bully her when France dissed that military alliance, knowing 
that she was geographically protected anyway?

Did America bully her when France put American troops at risk by denying 
over-flight rights?  ***

(***as a fun aside France is now complaining that Quadaffi did not pay 
out as must to French bombing victims as he did to American plane 
bombing victims.  I have taken great satisfaction in their whining of 
late.  I guess that response was effective.)

Has America every been a bully to France?  No.

Has America ever seized territory from France?  No.

Has America ever forced France to participate in American commercial 
activity?  No.

Has America ever been anything but a good friend to France?  This has 
become an abusive relationship, and it is time for America to kick 
France to the curb.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Designing CoBOL from scratch

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-01T06:23:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EBB4b.8139$zK3.399555@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<bifpoa$3h6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <bifrb0$k0j$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <X2X2b.5928$O36.348139@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <3F4E24B4.2127CAB1@sbcglobal.net> <bip7bd$mkk$1@news.btv.ibm.com> <3F525C55.68D6EDF0@sbcglobal.net>`

```
"gary drummond" <gdrumm@sbcglobal.net> wrote in message
news:3F525C55.68D6EDF0@sbcglobal.net...
> > "gary drummond" <gdrumm@sbcglobal.net> wrote in message
> > news:3F4E24B4.2127CAB1@sbcglobal.net...
> > > jce wrote:
> > > > > COBOL remains the best language for doing what it was designed to
do -
> > > > > processing business rules.
> > This is often said.  I don't even think that this holds true as a
> > statement
> > > > anymore.  More and more "business rules" are becoming decision and
rules
> > > > engines with complex data dynamic data structures and management....
> > > > As systems have matured they have
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> > What does this have to do with what I said? This isn't anything but a
linear
> > system - I hardly see this as a complex dynamic data system.   I hardly
see where using Cobol would be advantageous over
> > other language.
> > JCE

> You haven't done anything but spout buzz words
I said 'How would you manage a system that could manage simultaneous
transactions using an object based rating, customers and service models
using real time dynamically
changing rates.'  I also mentioned decision trees and rules engines....
The buzzwords here are what exactly? Object ? Dynamic?  Tree?

> and imply that you know the answers to all the programming problems we
will ever see.
In fact I said 'As business rules matured they have left behind COBOL.'
This does not in any way refer to all programming problems we will ever see.
Perhaps I should rephrase what I said for the literal.
'Many more recent business rules are premised on an object behaviour within
a real time, dynamic environment.  Many of these rules are more
appropriately solutioned using methods other than COBOL.  From this
statement I derive the following: "COBOL is not necessarily the best
language for processing all business rules".

AFAICS, the implication is non-existent. The inference is all yours.

> Do you also sell clothes to the Emperor?
No, because I don't assume everyone is stupid and incompetent.

> To quote you-"The recent thread on UNSTRING for example highlights
> the difficulties with even simple expression parsing.  COBOL does
> not lend itself to this very well at all."
As I recall - this thread lead to lengthy discussion both using unstring,
and byte level checking to do what I would contend should be a fairly simple
exercise.  I will accept that collectively the group provided several simple
solutions - but I doubt that all languages would have had such serious
debate on the issue.  Perhaps the issue is less of ability to do it and more
the fact that COBOL language libraries are often less shared and publicly
disclosed for legal reasons (or maybe it is more difficult).

> What I said to you, was to pick your language, or environment, and do it
> faster than the language you said " does not lend itself to this very well
at
> all ".
> Prove what you say! Spend a maximum of 2 hours using something else to do
at least what I did.
Again, let me re-iterate. This [problem you provide] isn't anything but a
linear system   I don't believe your challenge is comparable.  It's a *very*
linear problem - you sort the records, you do simple parsing, you create
output.  in fact, this is an example of the type of problem that COBOL
handles *very* well.  I am sorry that you started with C and wasted time -
strange for someone defending COBOL so heartily, but maybe this was the
experience taught you the value of COBOL.
The metaphorical equivalent of your response is that I've stated that
flippers are not good for running a marathon. (I neglected to mention
specifics such as 'Clogs are better', 'boots are better still', and 'running
shoes are best').  You've retorted with - I can swim this fast with
flippers. I don't believe you can swim, so show me you can do better with
any other footwear.

> We COBOL programmers seem to make it (expression parsing) work, but we
> prove it every day. You haven't proved anything except you can pass buzz
words
> in > a response, and knock COBOL for not being very good at parsing.
Given that I am in CLC it is a safe assumption that I would fall into 1 or
more of the following (and in fact it's all three):
(a) I use COBOL
(b) like COBOL
(c) value COBOL.
(d) prove every so often that I can do expression parsing

Given that I am of reasonable intelligence it is a safe assumption that
would fall into 1 or more of the following (and in fact it's all three):
(a) I use languages other than COBOL
(b) like languages other than COBOL
(c) Value languages other than COBOL

> Show us something else that does, and prove it by doing it in less than 2
hours.
The newer implementations or requirements for the "legacy" function of
BILLING is no longer what I consider to be COBOL friendly- it's dynamic and
changing and flexible in a way that I think is more than COBOL can
reasonably handle.  When the problem domain is determined up front to be
object based - procedural COBOL is no longer suited as the preferred
solution.  I just think that the idea of "legacy services" of yesteryear is
coming to an end.  Do a search on the web on convergent billing, real time
billing, or CMR and they are not built on the same principles of yore.
Tell me how a COBOL programmer would most efficiently solution a real time,
event based, flexible decision based rule engine.
I am trying to do this for various political and environmental (and simple
co-existence) reasons as we speak....in COBOL.  I'm sure that if I was
building a house and not an extension to an existing mansion I would not be
using this language.  It's manageable but not as easy as I would like.
That's my evidence right there...it's good enough for me...

> You could open a clothing store I guess...
I believe that anyone selling the emperor clothes wouldn't be stupid enough
to open a store.

You should read the thread on Fortress Mentality and quit being so
defensive.
I'm not spelling the end for COBOL like others, I'm just saying it's not the
be all end all solution for all "business rules".  I think business rules
themselves are migrating towards a different model.

John
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Designing CoBOL from scratch

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** gary drummond <gdrumm@sbcglobal.net>
