> A couple of simple ideas to add to the collection of excellent responses you
> already have. 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> have easy solutions.

No this is all done on a mainframe, so the problem isn't a tab contra
space thing.  Nice suggestion though.  10 years ago I did a conversion
from COBOL (77?) on a DEC to COBOL 85 on a VAX, and that was one of
the problems that we experienced.  Back then I programmed the editor
to reformat the tabs to spaces line by line (and a few other similar
problems), and it worked like a charm, costing less time that I'd have
used to make a program to do the same.  Had the volume of programs
been +factor 10, it would have been better to code a batch program to
take care of the problem.


> 2) The other idea is to just write a simple fixer rather than a perfect fixer.

In that case it would be better to just leave the code as it is.  At
least now we aren't seriously misled by the indentation. When it's to
the right, it's not trustworthy.
 
> If you cannot find ready tools also orient yourself in a divide and conquer
> looking at the Data Division as a different problem than the Procedure
> Division.

Good suggestion, but I forgot to mention that the problem is almost
exclusivly in the procedure division.


> If you are in an IBM ISPF environment there is 
> some chance that EDIT Change All commands can partially repair sever
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> mean to imply otherwise).  

I'm in no way a ISPF shark, even though I've meant to spend more time
studying the editor.  Maybe in the future.

Any manual solution would be too time consuming at the present - I'm
not even certain that the boss would give us time to figure out how to
use an automated process.

> Best Wishes,
> Bob Rayhawk
> RKRayhawk@aol.com

Thanks!!
- J.B.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Indent Blues - the woe of auto. conversions to COBOL from 4th gen. lang.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Jussi Jumppanen <jussij@zeusedit.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-02T09:42:11+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F53D953.7973@zeusedit.com>`
- **References:** `<uEL3b.275$EW1.232@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net> <20030829155131.20965.00000062@mb-m21.aol.com> <fed16812.0309010751.7646c96f@posting.google.com>`

```
J.B. Martin wrote:

> No this is all done on a mainframe, so the problem isn't a tab contra
> space thing.  

For what it is worth, the Zeus programmers editor has built in macro 
engine that might be of some help. Basically you start the macro 
engine recording, show Zeus what you want done to the text, stop 
the recording and then just play it back as many times as you like.

The one problem would be Zeus only runs on Windows, but as Zeus 
also support seamless ftp editing onto mainframe servers, it is 
not totally impossible to edit the files in Zeus.

The other good think is Zeus is shareware and the shareware version 
is fully functional for 60 days:

  http://www.zeusedit.com/download.html

So it will not cost you a cent :) Just a thought.

Jussi Jumppanen
Author of: Zeus for Windows (All new version 3.90 out now)
"The C/C++, Cobol, Java, HTML, Python, PHP, Perl programmer's editor"
Home Page: http://www.zeusedit.com
```

## Thread: Redefines in COBOL — 2 this month / 13 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

_Thread started [2003-08](2003-08.md#t-8b7d7b8b70)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Redefines in COBOL

- **From:** "Bill J." <bjacaruso<NO~~~SPAM>@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-02T14:35:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OU15b.454$wr3.322@newssvr24.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<lD43b.346$CH4.166@newssvr22.news.prodigy.com> <_U43b.353$NR4.88@newssvr22.news.prodigy.com> <Kpadne4PhvVZrdCiXTWJig@comcast.com>`

```
I have requested information from Acucorp suport on the file dispatch
library.

Also after some research I have found that Acucorp supplies a C$ Routine
called C$REDIRECT, it allows us to point our IO calls to a different COBOL
application, whihc in our case can then access the additional file system.

However, my problem is that the example application that Acucorp supplies is
full of copy books and GUI calls.  I would really like to see a sample that
is a little more clear.

Does anyone have experience using ths routine?  If so I would love to talk
to you about your experience.

Bill J.
Austin.


"JJ" <jj@nospam.com> wrote in message
news:Kpadne4PhvVZrdCiXTWJig@comcast.com...
> Bill,
>
> Have you talked to Acucobol support?
>
> I am fairly certain that they provide the ability to hook into all I/O
calls
> to allow alternate indexed file handlers.  For example, they can use
C-ISAM
> or RMS (on VMS) in place of their indexed file system.
>
> If you look in the filetbl.c file in the Lib directory where your runtime
is
> installed, that has a C struct where you install dispatch functions for
> other file handlers.  It doesn't give any information on what functions
you
> must provide in your dispatch handler, but you can probably get that from
> support.  I remember reading a post on their user forum a few months ago
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > We use the Acucorp environment and we have been unable to figure out a
way
> > to "hook" the calls or directives.
> >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > In other words I can override a function name, so instead of calling
the
> > > orginal function, it call my function instead.
> > >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > My COBOL knowledge is limited to maintence work on existing code, but
we
> > > would like to use some different IO routines instead of the native
stuff
> > > that happens when we call OPEN, READ, WRITE etc.
> > >
> > > Is there a way to "redefine" these routines so the OPEN call and its
> > > parameters are directed to our custom routines without having to
change
> > the
> > > source where the OPEN call is made?
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

(our file system does not suport all COBOL types).

The C$REDIRECT hadoes pass informaiton in with the call from the original IO
call but I am not sure what soime of it is.

Bill J.

## Thread: Net Express compiler keeps adding ".CPY" suffix — 3 this month / 13 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [2003-08](2003-08.md#t-2a93582101)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Net Express compiler keeps adding ".CPY" suffix

- **From:** renfrewthejust@yahoo.com (Renfrew)
- **Date:** 2003-09-02T08:52:00-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d6e1fab7.0309020752.3763bec8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<d6e1fab7.0308261200.1e90887a@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0308261918.193ec27d@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote in message news:<217e491a.0308261918.193ec27d@posting.google.com>...
> renfrewthejust@yahoo.com (Renfrew) wrote 
> 
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> specifier and do actually work ?


Yes, we do this kind of thing in a number of places and they all have
worked except this one.

In the interest of expediency (not to mention staying employed) I have
since been forced to impose a somewhat less-than-ideal solution to
this problem:  I copied the contents of NUMSECHK.WS over to another
file within the same area as the rest of the copy procs.

I do not like this solution because if there are ever changes to be
made to the original NUMSECHK.WS then said changes will now also have
to be migrated over to the duplicated version.  Oh well, sometimes we
just gotta do whatever it takes to move on.  If I ever do find the
more ideal solution for this I will certainly post it here.

My sincerest thanks to everyone for the suggestions.  

DaveM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Net Express compiler keeps adding ".CPY" suffix

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-02T20:26:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_175b.22661$Om1.8694@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<d6e1fab7.0308261200.1e90887a@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0308261918.193ec27d@posting.google.com> <d6e1fab7.0309020752.3763bec8@posting.google.com>`

```
To avoid the future maintenance problem, why don't you change the existing
source code in

  NUMSECHK.WS

to be ONLY a "COPY  newfile.cpy" statement.

That way existing code and new code would ALL look at your "copied" over
location for the source code.

Note: For those not familiar with NetExpress, it does support nested COPY
statements.
```

YES!  That did the trick.  Definitely a much better solution than mine
because now I don't have to worry about maintaining more than one
version of this proc.

It remains a mystery why the compiler won't deal with having the
server/volume reference in the main CBL for this particular proc, but
I can live with that.

A thousand thanks, Bill.  

Dave Miner

ortho@trouble[~]> runcbl -version
ACUCOBOL-85 runtime version 2.1.1
Vision version 3 file system
Copyright (c) 1985 - 1992, Acucobol, Inc.

brian

```
On Wed, 03 Sep 2003 18:17:21 +0100, Frederico Fonseca
<real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com> wrote:

>On 3 Sep 2003 00:01:05 -0700, brian@brie.com (Brian Lavender) wrote:
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>You will at least the configuration file if it is on /etc.

Here is the error I saw today, and I have seen before. /dev/ttya03 is
where patients can check themselves in. I have seen the error at the
terminal where the receptionist works.

remove SRTSCHa03? Dating the CheckinCRT /dev/ttya03 **remove SRTSCHa03

I type 'yes'and then I get the following error.

File error 92 on PATFILE.DAT
COBOL error at 008084 in ODECK1
Called from 0005B0 in ODRIVE

I had a previous error on the datafile DECKFILE.DAT where the data
became corrupted.

File error 98,01 on DECKFILE.DAT
COBOL error at 0080AF in ODECK1
Called from 0005B0 in ODRIVE

I replaced DECKFILE.DAT from the original tar file created to transfer
this app which was just transferred over. This restored file was out
of sync with the rest of the application. This was before I found out
about the vutil command. I thought maybe the app would pick up the
inconsistencies, or the inconsistencies would become immediately
evident. I think things might still be broken.

I think that I may have messed up when bringing the app from the
original server to the newly built one. I tarred the directory
containing the data, cobol engine, and application while users were
using it. I was thinking that it would be ok to do this, but after
thinking about it, I realized that the app probably had various data
files open during the transfer process, which may cause problems. Does
this sound likely. I tested this app before transferring it, and
things worked well with the data set used for testing. 

brian
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SCO resources for AcuCobol-85?

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-10T08:48:56+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<udltlvk51krihk9cm507o1gb0r99psn8o5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<53fb18f9.0309022301.2aad5247@posting.google.com> <qd8clvoomsq09le33hsm79afempk3dviog@4ax.com> <2mctlvk9bth9ddih23dnlh0414q7hdochr@4ax.com>`

```
On Tue, 09 Sep 2003 22:26:59 -0700, Brian Lavender <brian@brie.com>
wrote:

>On Wed, 03 Sep 2003 18:17:21 +0100, Frederico Fonseca
><real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com> wrote:
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>remove SRTSCHa03? Dating the CheckinCRT /dev/ttya03 **remove SRTSCHa03
The above is part of the application. You should check the code to see
where this particular message is displayed and why.
>
>I type 'yes'and then I get the following error.
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>things worked well with the data set used for testing. 

NEVER NEVER NEVER create a backup with users using the files.

If this new machine has already became the prodution one then you may
already have lost vital information, and you will need to speak with
whoever created the application for them to go there and run
verification programs on all files.

If not then create a backup of the original files when NO ONE is using
them (e.g. quick everyone out and keep them out!!), and then restore
them to the new machine.

If at all feasible run vutil on the new machine, one file at the time,
and teste the application then.

regards

Frederico


Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

brian

## Thread: Sending Mail From Cobol — 7 this month / 9 total

### Sending Mail From Cobol

- **From:** dipu <member37866@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-03T04:27:48-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3323031.1062577668@dbforums.com>`

```

Is there some standard IBM supplied API that can be used to send email
from a MAINFRAME COBOL PROGRAM ?
```

#### ↳ Re: Sending Mail From Cobol

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-03T12:02:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PLk5b.124085$0v4.9016260@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3323031.1062577668@dbforums.com>`

```

"dipu" <member37866@dbforums.com> wrote in message
news:3323031.1062577668@dbforums.com...
|
| Is there some standard IBM supplied API that can be used to send email
| from a MAINFRAME COBOL PROGRAM ?
|

If you're on an IBM OS/390 or z/OS, scan your JCL, PROC, and control
libraries for SMTP.

You might also scan the control library for @, to look for e-mail addresses.

Also check SDSF for anything starting with SMTP.

If it's already being used, you should be able to figure out how to use it.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sending Mail From Cobol

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-03T08:03:39-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-A32C03.08033903092003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3323031.1062577668@dbforums.com>`

```
In article <3323031.1062577668@dbforums.com>,
 dipu <member37866@dbforums.com> wrote:

> Is there some standard IBM supplied API that can be used to send email
> from a MAINFRAME COBOL PROGRAM ?
> 

Depends on the way your shop has SMTP configured.  You can usually spool 
mail message to the SMTP started task and everything else is automagick.

You will need:

   - the name of the started task for the SMTP you wish to use.  By 
default this is SMTP.

   - the output class that your sysprogs have defined.  By default this 
is class B.

   - permission to use SMTP if RACF or allow/deny have been configured 
for SMTP.

Your output DD statement will look like:

   //YOURMAIL  DD  SYSOUT=(x,yyyyyyyy)  

where x = output class and yyyyyyyy = started task name for SMTP.

Just write your email to the YOURMAIL dd in the standard email style and 
you are done, e.g.:

   HELO jes-node-name
   RCPT TO  you@yoursite.com
   DATA
   Test
   .

You can do the same from CICS using the SPOOL OPEN/WRITE/CLOSE.  Use an 
output descriptor on the SPOOL OPEN command of (B,SMTP) and your mails 
will be sent.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sending Mail From Cobol

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)

- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0309040500.3a34b97e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3323031.1062577668@dbforums.com> <3328249.1062650973@dbforums.com>`

```
dipu <member37866@dbforums.com> wrote in message news:<3328249.1062650973@dbforums.com>...
> OS Version is OS/390.I also want to add attachement.

Which VERSION of OS/390?  This determines the level of default unix
serives installed as either "Open Edition", "USS" or maybe even LINUX
- and will help determine what is available to you.  Hopefully the
other messages on your thread have given you the answer you seek.

Curious though - what are you going to attach?  What could possibly
exist on the mainframe that you can attach that would be of use on the
PC?  There's a whole EBCDIC/ASCII issue to consider especially if you
get into MIME binary attachments.
```

## Thread: Building a wall for division... — 82 msgs

### Building a wall for division...

- **From:** KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM (KELLIE FITTON)
- **Date:** 2003-09-03T08:50:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello everyone,

I would like to know if you agree with me on this one, do you think the
programmers who invented the OO programming language have decided to use
this FANCY terminology so they can build a wall between themselves and
everybody else? Afer all, the term Encapsulate simply means package some
codes into a small function, Polymorphism is just a simple message that
has multi-form displayed text, Method is just a sub-routine that is called
by the fancy word "invoke" which is another word for the "CALL" statement.
your unbiased point of view will be appreciated, thank you. Kellie.

***
```

#### ↳ Re: Building a wall for division...

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-03T16:09:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bj53n7$74g$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com>`

```

On  3-Sep-2003, KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM (KELLIE FITTON) wrote:

> I would like to know if you agree with me on this one, do you think the
> programmers who invented the OO programming language have decided to use
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> your unbiased point of view will be appreciated, thank you. Kellie.

Not in the way you imply.   In marketing a product (in this case a book or
consulting service), it is common to come up with new names, especially if the
author doesn't want people to confuse the old term with his new way of using it.
  Sometimes the term comes later (The West Coast Offense in the NFL).

Some terms have become so widely used that we need to define it each time we use
it with a different person.   What is a database?   It depends on who is
talking.

It is quite possible to use all of your above tools and not have an OO design.
```

Sounds like a record layout. Record-Layout Oriented Programming!

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Building a wall for division...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-04T20:41:28-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vlfn4l5ir2da7c@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com> <CW2dnfew2cei_MuiXTWQlg@giganews.com> <vlcuqrl1k9am85@corp.supernews.com> <1X6dnVrCwPvULMqiXTWQkQ@giganews.com>`

```

Anon <NoSpam@Anon.ORG> wrote in message
news:1X6dnVrCwPvULMqiXTWQkQ@giganews.com...
> > "Objects are useful, but they are not new. Ever since Cobol has
> > had structures; ever since Pascal has had records; ever since
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> So Object = data and its format = some kind of file ?

I probably should have said, "Object -- Data conforming
to its definition, structured data."

The object definition (its format) may be a record layout,
structure definition, or data definitions in a class. A file is
a collection of objects.

The simple use of objects *might* be termed "Record
Oriented Programming"; but this adds nothing to the
terminology already used.

While objects are easily understood (they are, simply, data);
the more useful form in OOP is the class, an object definition
together with its implementation. It is in the notion of class
that the other OO terms apply.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Building a wall for division...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-05T01:33:29-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vlg88phc43a5ac@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com> <CW2dnfew2cei_MuiXTWQlg@giganews.com> <vlcuqrl1k9am85@corp.supernews.com> <1X6dnVrCwPvULMqiXTWQkQ@giganews.com> <vlfn4l5ir2da7c@corp.supernews.com>`

```

Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:vlfn4l5ir2da7c@corp.supernews.com...
[snip all]

I find that after checking a few more reference, I must withdraw
my statements.

When the context is strictly OO, object appears to be synonomous
with class (aka, object defintion) and the data is an object
instance. This is something I knew at one point but have not
referenced in a few years.

Lately, I have been considering object in a non-OO context where
object as data makes more sense. It is the data that is acted on;
not its definition.

Working Draft for C99, "3.15 Object
A region of data storage in the execution environment, the
contents of which can represent values. Except for bit-fields,
objects are composed of contiguous sequences of one or
more bytes, the number, order, and encoding of which are
either explicitly specified or implementation-defined. When
referenced, an object may be interpreted as having a
particular type; see 6.2.2.1."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Building a wall for division...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-09-05T20:14:36+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f5846df_6@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com> <CW2dnfew2cei_MuiXTWQlg@giganews.com> <vlcuqrl1k9am85@corp.supernews.com> <1X6dnVrCwPvULMqiXTWQkQ@giganews.com> <vlfn4l5ir2da7c@corp.supernews.com> <vlg88phc43a5ac@corp.supernews.com>`

```
What about Object Methods, Rick?

(I object to the object being just data <G>)

You can argue that only the object data is instantiated, but this is system
dependent. There is no guarantee that on all systems a single re-entrant,
re-usable set of Object Methods will be used to process all object
instances. Some systems may instantiate a copy of the methods at the same
time as the data. (early versions of Unix used to create copies of
application code and data for each terminal, for example, I don't know what
it does today but I shouldn't think it does this...)

It is important that the concept of an object includes the behaviour of the
object. How this is actually implemented is less important.

Pete.

"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:vlg88phc43a5ac@corp.supernews.com...
>
> Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Building a wall for division...

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-05T13:54:04-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vlhjkn8kd3h467@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com> <CW2dnfew2cei_MuiXTWQlg@giganews.com> <vlcuqrl1k9am85@corp.supernews.com> <1X6dnVrCwPvULMqiXTWQkQ@giganews.com> <vlfn4l5ir2da7c@corp.supernews.com> <vlg88phc43a5ac@corp.supernews.com> <3f5846df_6@news.athenanews.com>`

```

Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3f5846df_6@news.athenanews.com...
> What about Object Methods, Rick?

Methods appear to belong to Object Oriented; not, for example,
Object Based.

> (I object to the object being just data <G>)
>
> You can argue that only the object data is instantiated, but this is
system
> dependent. There is no guarantee that on all systems a single re-entrant,
> re-usable set of Object Methods will be used to process all object
> instances. [...]

Consider that a class (OO object) may define only data, only
methods, or both. I may then argue that, in OO, 'object' cannot
*be* "[a] unit consisting of data and the methods that act upon
that data" since some 'objects' may not include one of the
required elements.

> It is important that the concept of an object includes the behaviour of
the
> object. How this is actually implemented is less important.

For me, a concept is important if it improves my understanding.
Object as data, method as procedure, class as a combination
of data and procedure works well for me; but is in conflict with
the literature. My use of these terms may be confusing to others
and that is why I must withdraw.

--- going off-topic ---

Besides, I am rather busy. I took 'a position contrary to
Treasury regulations' when I filed my Income Tax; listing a
Constitutional issue as the basis. Should the IRS decide to
prosecute, my defense will include three Constitutional and
one Legislative issue. The first is based on errors of
Judicial construction concerning Article I, Section 8. The
second seeks to respond to a statement made by Justice
O'Conner concerning 'express delegation'. The third relies
on a dissenting position written by Chief Justice Rehnquist
concerning the Tenth Amendment. The fourth is a Legislative
issue concerning the reasonableness of a requirement for
certain evidence under some conditions.

The testimony that I would give for the fourth issue includes
the notion that some acts of government are so offensive to
liberty that the Court must deny access even when legal
standing is available to the government. [I see some support
for denial in 'Federalist 81'.]

As grand as all that may sound, there will be a couple meetings
with the IRS before their decision. They may *reasonably*
decide in my favor on the fourth issue without my appearing in
court and without all the testimony that would have been
presented.

"When you're up to your ass in alligators its difficult to
remember that your initial objective was to drain the swamp."
I would rather be programming!

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Building a wall for division...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-09-06T11:33:17+12:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Building a wall for division...

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-09-05T14:30:02-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0E46b.3619$I_2.554688@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com> <CW2dnfew2cei_MuiXTWQlg@giganews.com> <vlcuqrl1k9am85@corp.supernews.com> <1X6dnVrCwPvULMqiXTWQkQ@giganews.com> <vlfn4l5ir2da7c@corp.supernews.com> <vlg88phc43a5ac@corp.supernews.com> <3f5846df_6@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3f5846df_6@news.athenanews.com...
> What about Object Methods, Rick?
>
> (I object to the object being just data <G>)
>

Peter this invoke/call difference is getting my goat. Let me see if I can
compare the two in a way that means something to the average Cobol
programmer, and let you comment on it.

If we took a standard Cobol subroutine, and specified it as intitial, then
added several entry points to it, we would have an "object(in general
terms)" with several methods (also in general terms).

The key to understanding it in OOP terms is that the first "call", in Cobol,
does the oop equivalent of creating the "instance" automatically.
Similarly, the first "cancel" does the equivalent of destroying that
instance. You CANNOT have more than one instance of the routine, unless you
make a second copy of the source, then give it a new subroutine name.

So if we wanted to do OOP-like things, with only a single instance of a
specific object, we could call the subroutine, thereby creating it and
intializing it at the same time.  We could then call it several more times,
and cobol being what it is, would be using various procedural methods on the
data contained in the subroutine  Then we cancel the subroutine, and
everything in it is destroyed.

The prime difference with OOP is that we are not confined to a single
instance of that routine, and a single instance of data at the same time.
We create and destroy MULTIPLE instances of the same rountine.  EACH remains
in effect until the object is destroyed.

Not sure if that will make sense to people or not, but it seems to me one
way of looking at it that demonstrates the difference.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Difference between INVOKE and CALL (was Re: Building a wall for division...)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-09-06T13:40:15+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f593bff_2@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com> <CW2dnfew2cei_MuiXTWQlg@giganews.com> <vlcuqrl1k9am85@corp.supernews.com> <1X6dnVrCwPvULMqiXTWQkQ@giganews.com> <vlfn4l5ir2da7c@corp.supernews.com> <vlg88phc43a5ac@corp.supernews.com> <3f5846df_6@news.athenanews.com> <0E46b.3619$I_2.554688@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
"Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:0E46b.3619$I_2.554688@news20.bellglobal.com...
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
> news:3f5846df_6@news.athenanews.com...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Don't worry about it. The differences are subtle and we've all made numerous
attempts to clarify. If people are not going to use OO anyway, there's not
much point in making the effort...

Nevertheless, let's have another look at it.

> If we took a standard Cobol subroutine, and specified it as intitial, then
> added several entry points to it, we would have an "object(in general
> terms)" with several methods (also in general terms).
>

Yes, that's not bad...

> The key to understanding it in OOP terms is that the first "call", in
Cobol,
> does the oop equivalent of creating the "instance" automatically.
> Similarly, the first "cancel" does the equivalent of destroying that
> instance. You CANNOT have more than one instance of the routine, unless
you
> make a second copy of the source, then give it a new subroutine name.
>
...Or run it in another region on a mainframe or as a separate thread in
Windows...
>
> So if we wanted to do OOP-like things, with only a single instance of a
> specific object, we could call the subroutine, thereby creating it and
> intializing it at the same time.  We could then call it several more
times,
> and cobol being what it is, would be using various procedural methods on
the
> data contained in the subroutine  Then we cancel the subroutine, and
> everything in it is destroyed.
>

Pretty fair description of "OO like" processing.


> The prime difference with OOP is that we are not confined to a single
> instance of that routine, and a single instance of data at the same time.
> We create and destroy MULTIPLE instances of the same rountine.  EACH
remains
> in effect until the object is destroyed.
>

Yes, that is certainly one of the main differences, but there are others
that are just as important.

> Not sure if that will make sense to people or not, but it seems to me one
> way of looking at it that demonstrates the difference.
>
It is a good attempt to do something that is not easy.

I think the problem arises because we try to look at something via the
mechanics of it.

To understand the difference between CALLed subroutines and INVOKED Object
Methods it is necessary to look BEYOND what the computer does when it
encounters these verbs.(In actuality, it may do exactly the same processing,
except that one is pointer based and the other isn't...This is why trying to
describe it in terms of the bottom level is confusing and, ultimately,
fruitless.)

To recognise why INVOKE is different to CALL you must recognise why an
Object Method is different from a subroutine or module. That involves a
conceptual understanding.

I posted something on this in response to a question from JerryMouse. In
case you missed it, and, given that it isn't too long, and is apropos this
topic, I've reproduced it here.

================== start quote ========================
>
> OK, I'll byte. Just how does a Method differ from a Subroutine, aside from
> "subroutine" having more vowels?
>
Jerry,

there are serious differences between subroutines, or even called modules,
and the Methods of an Object.

Without getting into the whole scholarly pedantics of it (which I find about
as entertaining as watching paint dry), the simple answers, based on
empirical experience, are these:

1. A subroutine requires some controlling logic to make it happen. The
subroutine is often part of the program which contains this controlling
logic. A Method is free standing encapsulated code. It doesn't require
controlling logic to make it happen.(see next point)

2. A called module is closer to a Method but still not the same. A called
module is activated by being called; a Method is activated by an event. (The
event can be the receipt of a message telling it to wake up, or it can be a
real time event like the click of a button.) This is where we get to the
subtle difference between CALL and INVOKE.

INVOKE LOOKS the same as CALL (see my response to Kellie elsewhere) but it
is conceptually causing a message to activate the Method to be generated.
(The mechanics may be identical to those used with CALL, but the concepts
are not, and this is important.). INVOKE is an OO COBOL Programmer's way of
raising an event that the Object will respond to, using a specific Method.
The system can INVOKE the Method by raising other events. This is,
obviously, not the same as handling called modules.

3, The Methods of an Object, considered collectively, represent the
"behaviour" of the Object. Called modules are not part of an entity like an
Object so they cannot be considered as "behaviour".

You can argue that this isn't important (I thought so, when I first
encountered it...) but as time goes by and I have gained a lot more
experience of using Objects, I have come to the realization that concepts
can be just as important as mechanics, particularly when you need to
conceptualize the design of a system. The behaviour of an Object (which, in
my case, will become a component) is significant when deciding the
interfaces it will require.

Part of the problem here is that there are many different definitions and
usages  within the OO and Component worlds. In a way this is good because it
means that each user can define the terms for his context and, as long as it
is clear how he intends to use the terminology, no harm is done. There is an
excellent paper on Component Based Design by Kirby McInnis that covers this:

http://www.cbd-hq.com/articles/1999/990715_cbdandreuse.asp

Quoting from the paper...

"Although most of the academic and industrial realm understands some
definition of a component, there tends to be some variance in what each
individual's definition of a component is. It is not always necessary that
everyone agrees on a definition, but it is necessary to understand the
author's use of the words."

======================= end quote ====================

Summarising:

1. Object Methods represent  the behaviour of an Object and are (arguably)
more "coherent" than Called modules.

2. Object Methods are independent, encapsulated, functionality and do not
require "control logic" to activate them, the way a called module or
subroutine does.

3. Object Methods are designed to be event driven. (this is why Objects are
taking the programming world by storm; the networks require event driven
code.)

3. INVOKE  (conceptually) raises an event against an Object that is
represented by a pointer.(The pointer is created when the first instance of
the Object is created (instantiated)). There can be many pointers
representing many Objects simultaneously executing.

4. CALL activates a single instance of a module.

I really don't know whether the above helps or simply muddies the water
further.

Although they may LOOK the same, an albino carrot is NOT a parsnip.

Neither is a called module or sub-routine an Object Method.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Building a wall for division...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-05T02:42:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RKS5b.33849$Om1.22210@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com> <CW2dnfew2cei_MuiXTWQlg@giganews.com> <vlcuqrl1k9am85@corp.supernews.com> <1X6dnVrCwPvULMqiXTWQkQ@giganews.com>`

```
(I haven't finished reading this thread, so I hope this reply is NOT a
duplicate)

To *ME* - an "object" (in OO terminology) must include both data and methods
for acting upon that data.  For an example of an "object" see

  "E.17 Object oriented concepts"

starting on page 768 of the 2002 COBOL Standard.  There is an example of a
"checking account" object including its "deposit" and "withdrawal" methods
starting on page 769 - and this same example is "expanded and modified" on
the following pages.
```

```

"Anon" <NoSpam@Anon.ORG> wrote in message
news:CW2dnfew2cei_MuiXTWQlg@giganews.com...
> > I would like to know if you agree with me on this one, do you think the
> > programmers who invented the OO programming language have decided to use
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > has multi-form displayed text, Method is just a sub-routine that is
called
> > by the fancy word "invoke" which is another word for the "CALL"
statement.
> > your unbiased point of view will be appreciated, thank you. Kellie.


I think this "fancy" terminology is just a lexicon for a different system.
Given that procedural doesn't really have these things they don't have the
equivalent "easier" names.

1) Encapsulate - To encase in or as if in a capsule.  (Basic Level English)
Does not mean what you say. It's more access protection and implementation
hiding.

2) Polymorphism:  (Basic Level Greek but not so hard)
Also - this is approximately 36 years old...so the perceived wall is not
new...
 <theory, programming> A concept first identified by
Christopher Strachey (1967)
Does not mean what you say.

3) Method - A means or manner of procedure, especially a regular and
systematic way of accomplishing something
Does not mean what you say. It's not a sub-routine as such.

4) Invoke - To call on, to Summon (Basic Level English)

I suggest you try and think of some easier words that describe what these
things are..I doubt you will find many, and if you do you're a few years too
late to change any minds.

> Personally I agree with you.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> and are not acceptable.
"An object is a discrete item that can be manipulated and/or can manipulate
other discrete items."

based on the equivalent:

"a grammatical constituent that is acted"

I don't understand the point of describing an OO entity without OO
lingo....not sure what this proves.


JCE
```

cons. It saves having to build detailed repositories but it does incur a
small runtime overhead. Most importantly, it provides extreme flexibility.

invoke that I haven't seen done using subroutines.   Of course I often cursed
those subroutines for hiding what they were doing and making debugging much

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Building a wall for division...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-09-05T12:01:54+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f57d369_2@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com> <CW2dnfew2cei_MuiXTWQlg@giganews.com> <7GA5b.4243$y_6.264072@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <F5ScneY14r8xpsqiXTWJkw@giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:F5ScneY14r8xpsqiXTWJkw@giganews.com...
> jce wrote:
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Jerry,

there are serious differences between subroutines, or even called modules,
and the Methods of an Object.

Without getting into the whole scholarly pedantics of it (which I find about
as entertaining as watching paint dry), the simple answers, based on
empirical experience, are these:

1. A subroutine requires some controlling logic to make it happen. The
subroutine is often part of the program which contains this controlling
logic. A Method is free standing encapsulated code. It doesn't require
controlling logic to make it happen.(see next point)

2. A called module is closer to a Method but still not the same. A called
module is activated by being called; a Method is activated by an event. (The
event can be the receipt of a message telling it to wake up, or it can be a
real time event like the click of a button.) This is where we get to the
subtle difference between CALL and INVOKE.

INVOKE LOOKS the same as CALL (see my response to Kellie elsewhere) but it
is conceptually causing a message to activate the Method to be generated.
(The mechanics may be identical to those used with CALL, but the concepts
are not, and this is important.). INVOKE is an OO COBOL Programmer's way of
raising an event that the Object will respond to, using a specific Method.
The system can INVOKE the Method by raising other events. This is,
obviously, not the same as handling called modules.

3, The Methods of an Object, considered collectively, represent the
"behaviour" of the Object. Called modules are not part of an entity like an
Object so they cannot be considered as "behaviour".

You can argue that this isn't important (I thought so, when I first
encountered it...) but as time goes by and I have gained a lot more
experience of using Objects, I have come to the realization that concepts
can be just as important as mechanics, particularly when you need to
conceptualize the design of a system. The behaviour of an Object (which, in
my case, will become a component) is significant when deciding the
interfaces it will require.

Part of the problem here is that there are many different definitions and
usages  within the OO and Component worlds. In a way this is good because it
means that each user can define the terms for his context and, as long as it
is clear how he intends to use the terminology, no harm is done. There is an
excellent paper on Component Based Design by Kirby McInnis that covers this:

http://www.cbd-hq.com/articles/1999/990715_cbdandreuse.asp

Quoting from the paper...

"Although most of the academic and industrial realm understands some
definition of a component, there tends to be some variance in what each
individual's definition of a component is. It is not always necessary that
everyone agrees on a definition, but it is necessary to understand the
author's use of the words."

Hope this helps,

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Building a wall for division...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-05T02:36:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WES5b.33842$Om1.33758@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com> <CW2dnfew2cei_MuiXTWQlg@giganews.com> <7GA5b.4243$y_6.264072@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <F5ScneY14r8xpsqiXTWJkw@giganews.com> <3f57d369_2@news.athenanews.com>`

```
An interesting (well interesting to me) related topic to how Peter
distinguishes METHODS from (called) subroutines is the question of
"asynchronous" processing.  Several existing COBOL environments already
support this (as an extension) and MANY non-COBOL environments already
support it.  However, the "standards" committees are currently looking at
what SHOULD be added to COBOL to "better" handle this in a "portable" way.

In case it isn't clear, one can have OO applications without asynchronous
processing and you can have modular (non-OO) applications that rely on it -
but I do see (from what is stated below) at least SOME justification for
"including" (improving?) asynchronous support in an OO COBOL environment
(i.e. send a "message" to an "object" and let it process that message while
your "main" logic goes happily on its way).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Building a wall for division...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-09-05T18:22:35+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f582ca2_4@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com> <CW2dnfew2cei_MuiXTWQlg@giganews.com> <7GA5b.4243$y_6.264072@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <F5ScneY14r8xpsqiXTWJkw@giganews.com> <3f57d369_2@news.athenanews.com> <WES5b.33842$Om1.33758@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`

```
Good point, Bill.

In an OO environment, on the occasions when I need asynchronous processes
(like Agents...), I simply run them under Windows sub-tasking using a timer
component to start them off  every so often. On other occasions, when I
don't want to wait for completion of an invoked component, I use the
PowerCOBOL "OpenForm" Method which starts the component in a modeless window
(which can be invisible if necessary - it is just a vehicle for the async.
process). I'm not sure that COBOL actually needs asynchronous processing,
because it relies to a large extent on the environment rather than the
Language.

I remember getting conversational mode 3270s to update in real time under
IMS/DC (which was supposed to be impossible), by running asynchronous
threads and using common mailboxes. (I "cheated" by dropping the terminals
out of Conversational mode while their screens were updated, then putting
them back into it immediately the update was complete. The trick was knowing
WHEN to do this; it gave me a crash course in the concepts of asynchronous
processing...<G>)

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:F5ScneY14r8xpsqiXTWJkw@giganews.com...
> jce wrote:
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Invoking a method is roughly analogous to calling a subroutine in a specific
instance of a specific object. I can create multiple objects, and invoke the
same method in each of them.  There is really no equivalent in subroutine
style words ... that is part of the problem, you cannot see the advantages
until you understand how it works, and that means writting code.

Donald
```

```
> "An object is a discrete item that can be manipulated and/or can manipulate
> other discrete items."

This is no different than "anything you want it to be" which is the kind of
answer I ALWAYS get when I ask WHAT THE HECK IS AN OBJECT?
A discrete item? You mean a bit? byte? field? record? file? sub-routine?
program? job-stream? on-line transaction? WHAT? How am I supposed to be
oriented to this object if don't even know what it is and there is no
definition of it? In the real world an object is a thing, a fork, a door,
a broom etc. In the programming world I can see, at least on the screen,
a program, a file, a record, a sub-routine. Show me an object. Type one
in. Someone must have a small OO system out there. Copy one your "objects"
so we can see it.

> based on the equivalent:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> lingo....not sure what this proves.

Because if you don't understand the lingo to start with, describing an
OO entity with this lingo results in "me no understand what you are
talking about". That's what it proves.
```

"Anon" <NoSpam@Anon.ORG> wrote in message
news:_ZWdndhxeuUSJcqiXTWQlg@giganews.com...
> > "An object is a discrete item that can be manipulated and/or can
manipulate
> > other discrete items."
>
> This is no different than "anything you want it to be" which is the kind
of
> answer I ALWAYS get when I ask WHAT THE HECK IS AN OBJECT?
It *IS* whatever you want it to be.

> definition of it? In the real world an object is a thing, a fork, a door,
> a broom etc. In the programming world I can see, at least on the screen,
> a program, a file, a record, a sub-routine. Show me an object. Type one
> in. Someone must have a small OO system out there. Copy one your "objects"
> so we can see it.
You just described a real world object as a "thing".  Then you gave examples
of "things".
An OO object is a modelled thing - a fork, a door, a broom if you happen to
be writing a system that is modelling or solutioning a robot living a
mundane suburban home life.
At then end of the day it is whatever you want it to be.  It conforms to a
set of ideas and design principles in general that have been discussed in
other posts to this thread.
Each language is a different in their exact implementation:

Java Classes can have single inheritance

C++ Classes can have multiple inheritance

C++ lets friends touch it's private parts,.whereas Java has no friends.

> > I don't understand the point of describing an OO entity without OO
> > lingo....not sure what this proves.
> Because if you don't understand the lingo to start with, describing an
> OO entity with this lingo results in "me no understand what you are
> talking about". That's what it proves.

I looked in my dictionary for the following:
BURNING: To cause to undergo combustion
So we lookup the following

COMBUSTION: The process of burning.

The point is that if I look closer there are further examples of each word
used in a context that help to build an understanding(even then you probably
wouldn't understand burning until you saw it)..  You use OO to describe OO
because that is easiest - but it also assumes some effort has been made to
understand the subject  Same thing here...if you don't understand the
language, then read around a build an understanding....When much of the
language is built on existing english language, I have no idea how you would
get stuck.  I'm sure you know what the following word is, but would you from
the definition?

FLAME: The zone of burning gases and fine suspended matter associated with
rapid combustion; a hot, glowing mass of burning gas or vapor.

JCE.
```

fork, a door,

the screen,

object. Type one

your "objects"
> so we can see it.

I've not provided the implementation, but here is the
interface:

  TGenericBitmap = class(TObject)
  private
    FBitmap       : TGenericBitmapInstance;
    FMask         : TGenericBitmapInstance;
    FMaskType     : TBitmapMaskType;
    FMaskColor    : TColor;
    FBackColor    : TColor;
    FColorSpace   : TBitmapColorSpace;
    FCompression  : TBitmapCompressionMethod;
    FTicks        : Cardinal;
    FWarnings     : Cardinal;
    procedure CreateMask();
    procedure CreateAlphaMask();
    procedure CreateBitMask();
    procedure DestroyMask();
    function  GetDepth(): Byte;
    function  GetHeight(): Integer;
    function  GetPaletteColor(const ColorIndex: Byte): TColor;
    procedure SetPaletteColor(const ColorIndex: Byte; const
Color: TColor);
    function  GetWidth(): Integer;
    procedure Render_Mask(const DestDevice: THandle; const
Dest, Source: TRect);
    procedure Render_AlphaChannel(const DestDevice: THandle;
const Dest, Source: TRect);
    procedure Render_AlphaMask(const DestDevice: THandle; const
Dest, Source: TRect);
    procedure Render_BitMask(const DestDevice: THandle; const
Dest, Source: TRect);
    procedure Render_MaskColor(const DestDevice: THandle; const
Dest, Source: TRect);
  public
    constructor Create(const Info: TMaxBitmapInfo; const
MaskType: TBitmapMaskType; const ColorSpace: TBitmapColorSpace;
const CompressionMethod: TBitmapCompressionMethod);
    destructor Destroy(); override;
    property BackColor   : TColor read FBackColor write
FBackColor;
    property BitmapData  : Pointer read FBitmap.Data;
    property ColorSpace  : TBitmapColorSpace read FColorSpace;
    property Compression : TBitmapCompressionMethod read
FCompression;
    property Depth       : Byte read GetDepth;
    property Handle      : THandle read FBitmap.Handle;
    property Height      : Integer read GetHeight;
    property MaskColor   : TColor read FMaskColor write
FMaskColor;
    property MaskData    : Pointer read FMask.Data;
    property MaskType    : TBitmapMaskType read FMaskType;
    property Palette     : THandle read FBitmap.Palette;
    property PaletteColor[const ColorIndex: Byte]: TColor read
GetPaletteColor write SetPaletteColor;
    property Ticks       : Cardinal read FTicks write FTicks;
    property Warnings    : Cardinal read FWarnings write
FWarnings;
    property Width       : Integer read GetWidth;
    procedure Relenquish(var Bitmap, Mask:
TGenericBitmapInstance);
    procedure Render(const DestDevice: THandle; const Dest,
Source: TRect; const Layer: TBitmapLayer = bmpComposite);
    function ScanLineWidth() : Integer;
    function BytesPerPixel() : Integer;
    function PixelsPerByte() : Integer;
  end;

Objects created from this class are used to manipulate
raster-type graphics.  Out of context, I'm unsure how
illustrative this will be, but it is a classic example of
encapsulating data and procedures that operate on that data.

> Because if you don't understand the lingo to start with,
describing an
> OO entity with this lingo results in "me no understand what
you are
> talking about". That's what it proves.

Objects are pretty abstract, so I see the difficulty you
describe.  It is an oddly-constrained request however.  An
analagous request would be to define a linking verb without
using any grammar lingo.  I
```

Anon wrote:
>>If you want me to respond further to your posts (here or privately) please
>>mail me privately with your identity.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> I am trying to cut the spam. I guess I need to come up with a better way.

Get Netscape 7.1.  Their junk mail filter is great - you train it for 
the first few weeks, then tell it to move all junk to the "Junk Mail" 
folder, then tell it after x days to delete mail from the junk folder. 
I trained mine for around 3 weeks, then after I turned on the filter, 
I've had maybe 4 or 5 messages in 2 months that didn't get caught, and 
only 1 legit message that got flagged incorrectly.  (The filter 
continues learning based on what you manually flag or unflag - I've only 
had 1 piece of junk mail that didn't get flagged in the last month.)

I've been pleasantly surprised with how well it's worked - I expected it 
  to be a big pain, but it hasn't been...
```

Everybody has.

We need a new e-mail system that verifies that senders are either - who they say
they are, or anonymous.   Trouble is, we will filter the latter out.  Programs
that gather names for spammers are smarter and smarter.


The feds, should require that all e-mail that they get after 2004 will be using
a protocol that verifies sender.   I wish I had enough money to pay of a
lobbyist who could persuade them to do this - if I had that much money, I'd be
playing golf right now.

have a couple

The difference, though slight in description, is profound.  The
procedures are specifically scoped to the data it operates
upon.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Building a wall for division...

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-09-05T12:44:45+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f57dd70_7@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com> <CW2dnfew2cei_MuiXTWQlg@giganews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-6D34C9.18595204092003@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message
news:joe_zitzelberger-6D34C9.18595204092003@corp.supernews.com...
<snip> >
> > CHALLENGE to those who disagree. Without using OO lingo,
> > in a clear cut straightforward manner: DEFINE "OBJECT".
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> upon that data, packaged together.

That's good, Joe.

I don't normally respond to anonymous posts, but a challenge is a
challenge...<G>

Here's a longer one that attempts to NOT use ANY OO terms (not even
"Method")...Plain English, "in a clear cut straightforward manner" as
requested...<G>

"An object, in the OOP sense, is a representation in computer code of a real
world (or imaginary) entity. It contains the properties and behaviours of
the entity it "models".  A "dog" Object would have data fields to represent
legs, a tail, hairy coat, and whatever other attributes of real life dogs
were considered pertinent. It would have code to simulate the behaviours of
a real world dog. We could expect it to be able to "bark" and "piss on
trees". It might also "fetch", "lie down", and "beg".

Most importantly, our computer "dog" object is capable of reproducing
itself. (OK, I won't go there...<G>)

It can have many copies of itself, all independent of each other, barking,
pissing on trees, fetching, in fact, we can create a whole pack of dogs from
this one object."

As you can see, OO can be fun...  <G>

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Building a wall for division...

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-09-04T12:54:02+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f568e1d_9@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com>`

```

"KELLIE FITTON" <KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM> wrote in message
news:41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com...
> Hello everyone,
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> ***

Kellie, some questions for you...(some people believe that answering a
question with a question is no answer at all; you must decide for yourself.)

Why would a set of programmers want to alienate or separate themselves from
the mainstream <G>?

Why would you care whether anyone agrees with you on this?

Why is OO terminology FANCY, but standard COBOL isn't, in your eyes?

Why would you already decide you understand OO terminology when your post
demonstrates that you don't?

Now some advice...

1. It is very easy when confronted with new ideas to relate them to things
you already know and say: "Well, that's just a fancy way of saying
(whatever)..."

Most of us fall into this trap because relating new information to
information we already know is part of the learning process.
Just because something LOOKS like something else, or shares some of the
behaviours of something else, doesn't mean it IS something else, or even
that it is the same as something else in all respects. Cultivate careful
observation and investigation skills. (I used to do a party trick of
handling a certain insect that was known to have a painful sting. The
"trick" was that it wasn't the insect everyone thought it was...it was, in
fact, a harmless relative that had adopted the camouflage of its more
dangerous cousin. Only very careful inspection could reveal the difference,
and most people would give anything that looked like it a wide berth...)

My advice: Reserve judgement until you have done more research.

2. OO is the best thing that has happened to COBOL since it gained the
ability to access disk files...<G>

But, depending on your requirements, you may never need to use it. If you
intend to write Batch Processes, (sadly, this is all that most College
courses are teaching with regard to COBOL) then standard COBOL is more than
adequate. If you don't intend to get into component development and the
distribution of objects across the network, then standard COBOL is, again,
more than up to the task. If you intend to avoid the Internet and Web based
applications, standard COBOL is fine. (You CAN write CGI code in standard
COBOL, but you CAN'T plug CALLed modules into a Web Page as you can with
INVOKEd components. You already have the reference to my article which deals
with this...)

However, the rest of the (non-COBOL) world is going with objects.

There have been some very good responses to your post.

My advice: Learn, use, and write COBOL. But don't stop there...("Everything
I want to do, I can do in COBOL...") Learn Java. (This will expand your
horizons and help you to understand the differences between OO and non-OO.
It will also increase your value and make you more marketable. Decide
whether you want to be a computer consultant who deals with all things
computer, or a COBOL programmer (they are both honourable aspirations; the
only difference is in the relative projected life span.). When you have
Java, learn HTML, XML, JavaScript, ASP...and keep going!

Good Luck!

Pete.
```

KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM (KELLIE FITTON) wrote 

> I would like to know if you agree with me on this one, do you think the
> programmers who invented the OO programming language have decided to use
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> your unbiased point of view will be appreciated, thank you. Kellie.

OO and the terminology isn't something 'fancy' that was dreamt up, the
terms derive from the jargon of the particular discipline that
implemented OO first - simulation. Their jargon is just not the same
as your the jargon that you are used to.

It seems to me that it is you that is trying to build the wall (around
yourself) by trying to rephase what simulation people have used for
decades, and OO for nearly as long, into jargon that you are familar
with - that of obsolete procedural languages.

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>

- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0309032049.6526cc62@posting.google.com>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0309032049.6526cc62@posting.google.com...
| KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM (KELLIE FITTON) wrote
|
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
| > has multi-form displayed text, Method is just a sub-routine that is
called
| > by the fancy word "invoke" which is another word for the "CALL"
statement.
| > your unbiased point of view will be appreciated, thank you. Kellie.
|
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
| as your the jargon that you are used to.

If memory serves me well, the first object-oriented programming language was
developed at the Xerox Palo Alto lab.
I checked, it was in 1972.
http://www.parc.com/about/history/

When was OO first used for simulation?

From:
http://www.netlingo.com/lookup.cfm?term=Xerox%20PARC

In 1970, Xerox Corporation gathered together a team of world-class
researchers and gave them the mission of creating "the architecture of
information." The scientists of the Xerox Palo Alto Research Center (PARC)
lived up to this challenge by inventing personal distributed computing,
graphical user interfaces (GUI), the first commercial mouse, bitmapped
displays, Ethernet, client/server architecture, object-oriented programming,
laser printing, and many of the basic protocols of the Internet.

Another one:
http://www.atp.nist.gov/eao/gcr02-834/b-8.htm

PARC was founded in 1970 with a mission to create the "architecture of
information," and over the past 30 years has invented graphical user
interfaces, Ethernet networking, the computer mouse, object-oriented
programming, and many other path-breaking technologies.

What about this:

Xerox's goal was to change the nature of component-based software
development by incorporating a new model of software development,
aspect-oriented programming, into software tools.  These tools would
separate the semantic details (functionalities) of a component from the
implementation details to increase the plug-and-play compatibility of
components.

 Xerox developed technology that fully accomplished the goals of the
ATP-funded project.  Their tool AspectJ embodies this technology.  This tool
operates within object-oriented programming and Java programming
environments.  AspectJ allows more components to be plugable into a software
program, which greatly reduces the cost and time of installing components
and increases the selection of available components that a software
developer can use within its production process.

 AspectJ    Free download from the Internet
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Building a wall for division...

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-09-05T00:52:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030904205225.28554.00000366@mb-m03.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<NaP5b.128758$3o3.9070195@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Harley" dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net 
Date: 9/4/03 5:39 PM EST
Message-id: <NaP5b.128758$3o3.9070195@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>

posted in this thread commenting on Xerox PARC, and their pivotal role in the
evoloution of modern technologies.

I, like others surely, enjoyed those comments.

Object Oriented programming did not start in Palo Alto, IMHO, and definitely
traces its roots back much further than 1972.  The poster mentions simulation
and object oriented programming, you have to look at work on Simula if you want
to understand the ground breaking inventions (and much of it happen off-shore
from North America, although lots of funding came from here).

The poster waxes ultimately into brief mention of
AspectJ with comments hinting to the effect that the ultimate of ultimate
object oriented programming is Aspect Oriented Programming. 

Well I don't think so, it is highly esoteric and seems not to be the
mainstream.  But more to 
the point, I don't believe that PARC of the 1970s is the source of 'Aspects,'
such notions are more recent. 

Infact they are a revisionist notion trying to deal with the fact that if
things are not built into the Classes and Objects from the top down, it is hard
with the current technology to force the functionality into the application.

What is missing from object oriented programming is management. As OOP takes a
greater and greater share of the mainstream work, now suddenly folks have it in
mind to force software to do things that communication monitores like CICS and
IMS have always done.

And if I might take a moment to comment on the original bigger picture in the
thread about the fancy teminlogy. It is worth noting that objects have multiple
methods. So it is not quite right to compare an object to a module. Although a
method maybe is a function maybe is a module maybe is a program, and there
isn't a need to be fancy.  But an object is not a module.

The idea of object oriented programming is to get your stuff together before
you code. That is the quest of all evolving structured programming technologies
and methodologies. So an object is a gathering of the design ideas for related
data and programs into a single named item. When you want to mess with that
gathered data, only its methods will be allowed to do the work.  So the data in
an object is definitely distinct from a flat lander 'record' (since anyone can
get to a record/struct, and not so an object's data). Encapsulation is both the
gathering of the data and the functionality as well as the protection of the
data (a 'wall' the original poster would say, is placed around an objects
data).

That wall makes the data inside an object much different from a record or
struct in working storage or linkage. Most traditional data in COBOL is shared
data, which makes it very vulnerable. Even a COBOL mainline Linkage back up
into the executive software's data makes the executive software vulnerable (you
can crash CICS and IMS from your COBOL program).  Data within Objects is
distributed data (you have to go through the methods to get the values). 
Static data of a Class allows the new technologies to revert some specific
fields back to common shared access. 

These notions are not just fancy, they are highly valuable distinctions.
Nothing in _traditional_ COBOL compares to polymorphism, except jump tables
like WHENEVER error handlers in DB2 and CICS, which many shops discourage.
And as far as I know nothing  is present in the traditional (older) COBOL
compilers
to compare to operator overloading  It is difficult to even thing of such
things in COBOLese.

But in the end you software is better if you plan it out in some detail before
you code, and OOP is just one collection of concepts that can help you do that.

Best Wishes to All
Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

> greater and greater share of the mainstream work, now suddenly folks have
it in
> mind to force software to do things that communication monitores like CICS
and
> IMS have always done.
>
> And if I might take a moment to comment on the original bigger picture in
the
> thread about the fancy teminlogy. It is worth noting that objects have
multiple
> methods. So it is not quite right to compare an object to a module.
Although a
> method maybe is a function maybe is a module maybe is a program, and there
> isn't a need to be fancy.  But an object is not a module.
>
> The idea of object oriented programming is to get your stuff together
before
> you code. That is the quest of all evolving structured programming
technologies
> and methodologies. So an object is a gathering of the design ideas for
related
> data and programs into a single named item. When you want to mess with
that
> gathered data, only its methods will be allowed to do the work.  So the
data in
> an object is definitely distinct from a flat lander 'record' (since anyone
can
> get to a record/struct, and not so an object's data). Encapsulation is
both the
> gathering of the data and the functionality as well as the protection of
the
> data (a 'wall' the original poster would say, is placed around an objects
> data).
>
> That wall makes the data inside an object much different from a record or
> struct in working storage or linkage. Most traditional data in COBOL is
shared
> data, which makes it very vulnerable. Even a COBOL mainline Linkage back
up
> into the executive software's data makes the executive software vulnerable
(you
> can crash CICS and IMS from your COBOL program).  Data within Objects is
> distributed data (you have to go through the methods to get the values).
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Nothing in _traditional_ COBOL compares to polymorphism, except jump
tables
> like WHENEVER error handlers in DB2 and CICS, which many shops discourage.
> And as far as I know nothing  is present in the traditional (older) COBOL
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> But in the end you software is better if you plan it out in some detail
before
> you code, and OOP is just one collection of concepts that can help you do
that.
>
> Best Wishes to All
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

I ran across Simula after I posted my message, sorry for being premature.
And, you are correct that the development of Object-Oriented concepts
pre-dated the development of Smalltalk at Palo Alto.
I did run across one link that stated that Simula had a Notion of
Class/Instance.
I guess it's similar to the arguement that VB isn't really OO because it
lacks Inheritance.

The real point is that OO has been around for quite some time, and it, like
many other things, evolved over time.

|
| The poster waxes ultimately into brief mention of
| AspectJ with comments hinting to the effect that the ultimate of ultimate
| object oriented programming is Aspect Oriented Programming.

I had never even heard of Aspect Oriented Programming until I ran across the
article.
I figured a GOV web site might carry some weight.

| Well I don't think so, it is highly esoteric and seems not to be the
| mainstream.  But more to
| the point, I don't believe that PARC of the 1970s is the source of
'Aspects,'
| such notions are more recent.

You are correct, AOP is more recent.
I don't know if AOP is will be the next HOT buzz word, remember OOP didn't
catch on immediately. It was around many years before it came into vogue.
Smalltalk was developed in the early 70s, and released in the early 80s, yet
it seems that OO didn't catch on until the 90s.

| Infact they are a revisionist notion trying to deal with the fact that if
| things are not built into the Classes and Objects from the top down, it is
hard
| with the current technology to force the functionality into the
application.

See comment at bottom.

|

takes a

and

can

If the data is written, anyone may be able get to it, and modify it.
It is a violaton of OO concepts, but without some sort of security built
into the application, encapsulation is a theory, not a reality.

|Encapsulation is both the
| gathering of the data and the functionality as well as the protection of
the

| data).

up

tables
| like WHENEVER error handlers in DB2 and CICS, which many shops discourage.
| And as far as I know nothing  is present in the traditional (older) COBOL
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
| But in the end you software is better if you plan it out in some detail
before
| you code, and OOP is just one collection of concepts that can help you do
that.

IMHO, the "problem" with OO is in how the designer visualizes the machine.
In other words, is the cigarette lighter part of the car, or is the car part
of the cigarette lighter?
The answer may appear to be obvious, but it depends on whether you build
cars, or cigarette lighters.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Building a wall for division...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-09-06T01:55:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030905215536.21879.00000611@mb-m04.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<et26b.130121$3o3.9141416@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

 "Harley" dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net 
Date: 9/5/03 11:03 AM EST
Message-id: <et26b.130121$3o3.9141416@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>

has again posted interesting comments, adding the following quoted notion at
the end of his posting:

<<
IMHO, the "problem" with OO is in how the designer visualizes the machine.
In other words, is the cigarette lighter part of the car, or is the car part
of the cigarette lighter?
The answer may appear to be obvious, but it depends on whether you build
cars, or cigarette lighters.

>>

I am rather more neutral. Considering neither the older procedural programming
paradigms wrong or right, nor the more recent paradigms such as OOP wrong or
right. But they are somewhat inverse of one another, there we agree.

Encapsulation does to some extent mean turn your application organization
outside in.

In terms of your last expression, it actually can make a difference if you are
trying to drive your car or drive your cigarette lighter. And it makes a
difference if you are trying to ignite something with the car (which I have
seen happen) or ignite something with a lighter.

The ideas we have and the words we use do have a correspondence. And the
bindings between OOP terms and the software management concepts is real. For
some software applications the newer ideas can easily be a better fit for the
effort to organize the underlying information.

In the rest of my comments here I use the word 'you' in the royal sense not as
an address to the named poster or to anyone in particular. 

Yet the fact is that you can get things inside of an object in a way that is
hard to do in flat COBOL. I think that the real answer to the original posters
question to the effect: isn't this just a lot of fancy smancy terms? is no.

As many posters are illustrating in this thread with their good ideas is that
you _can_ do things with the flat procedural model of the environment. Like you
can write initializers.
With transaction monitors like IMS or CICS you can get atleast multiple copies
of your program 
simultaneously; procedures and data replicated.
You can get data somehow down inside of the called program where some outlander
can't get to it. Lots of good stuff _could_ be done. But that is not the
question.

The question is more like _will_ you do it. Object Oriented technologies
basically just automate the process of encapsulating the data inside of the
inner thing we call an object. The OOP technologies just automate the
generation of virtual pointer arrays and add considerable discipline to the
regulation of code references to 
methods hanging out on that array. 

You _can_ do that with COBOL, atleast now with the availability of  USAGE IS
PROCEDURE-POINTER syntax along with the permissible combination of the OCCURS
clause and permitted REDEFINEs of groups gathering one or more USAGE IS
PROCEDURE-POINTER. But this is new COBOL and it will be a major chore to do it.
But it is not an unreasonable claim that you could write a JVM with modern
COBOL even short of the actual Object Oriented syntax enhancements. (You
_could_).

In contrast to the old flat model, now in
OOP, including the objects that you can code directly with some new COBOLs, all
of that is automated. So part of the problem here is that we are replacing
programmers with machines, and some of them don't quite get it.

So you _can_ code object oriented things in basic COBOL, but _would_ you do it?
Without automation such as is brought forth with CLASS, REPOSITORY, OBJECT you
would be slow to deliver the results. Your approach, no matter how superior
would not be as easily 
recognized as the automatically generated standard approach and would be more
expensive to maintain, and ,,,, and would ulitmately outlive you and become one
of the grand legacy relics until someone somewhere found the plug.

The error handling jump tables of CICS and DB2 are shunned. And the computed
GOTO,
which actually supported in source code syntax a nascent kind of indexing into
a list or addresses  has been removed from COBOL. 

In effect Simula got there too early, there was not enough of a mass market.
The same actually explains why computed GOTO got removed, it is actually a fine
idea and utterly structured. (This poster would not claim that ALTER GOTO is
structured, in fact that may have been the original destructor.)

An important weakness in the things now available for COBOL (keeping all the
actual object syntax to the side), is that  PROCEDURE-POINTER and
FUNCTION-POINTER can not point to paragraph names.  So you are stuck with only
the ability to grab what are effectively exported names from the Linkage Editor
namespace - that is definitely a constraint ... if you wanted to use the modern
-POINTERs you would simply have to greatly modularize. That is not trivial.

If you did not use the new world COBOL -POINTERS I am not sure I would want to
maintain the system that you create. To approach a full set of OOP features you
have to get references to executable code into an array and index it. That is
very hard in older COBOL.

Still human determination can not be stopped. I would agree that because a
number of participants in this newsgroup are quite skilled you could do this
kind of stuff, but frankly the issue is _would_ we do it with old COBOL coding
constructs? Is there anyway that that is as cost effective and reliable as the
automatically generated function arrays and internalized and protected inner
data?

The machines are replacing  us. I hate when that happens.


Anyway, good tidings to all, and to all a good night.

Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

COBOLs, all
