do it?

(I work on the IBM mainframe where OOP hasn't quite caught on yet.)
I use nested programs in new development, it sometimes starts discussions on
OO programming concepts.
I can, to some extent, get a form of Encapsulation, and crude
Inheritance/Polymorphism (code reuse).
I think you have to crawl before you can walk.
The main selling point is productivity. It's easier, and faster to re-work a
compact program, rather than to take a monolith, and extract what you need.

| Without automation such as is brought forth with CLASS, REPOSITORY, OBJECT
you
| would be slow to deliver the results. Your approach, no matter how
superior
| would not be as easily
| recognized as the automatically generated standard approach and would be
more
| expensive to maintain, and ,,,, and would ulitmately outlive you and
become one
| of the grand legacy relics until someone somewhere found the plug.
|
| The error handling jump tables of CICS and DB2 are shunned.

Part of the problem was performance, there is some overhead in using the
Jump Tables.
"Real Programmers" used "return codes" and tested them.
I doubt that there are any real performance issues, especially with the
greater CPU power now available.

|And the computed
| GOTO,
| which actually supported in source code syntax a nascent kind of indexing
into
| a list or addresses  has been removed from COBOL.

Not yet, I just checked the IBM z/OS COBOL LRM, and GO TO DEPENDING ON is
still supported.
(The last time I used it, someone asked if I could use a vector table, I
said OK, no problem. Others in the group looked at me like I had 3 heads.)

| In effect Simula got there too early, there was not enough of a mass
market.
| The same actually explains why computed GOTO got removed, it is actually a
fine
| idea and utterly structured. (This poster would not claim that ALTER GOTO
is
| structured, in fact that may have been the original destructor.)
|
| An important weakness in the things now available for COBOL (keeping all
the
| actual object syntax to the side), is that  PROCEDURE-POINTER and
| FUNCTION-POINTER can not point to paragraph names.  So you are stuck with
only
| the ability to grab what are effectively exported names from the Linkage
Editor
| namespace - that is definitely a constraint ... if you wanted to use the
modern
| -POINTERs you would simply have to greatly modularize. That is not
trivial.

Not sure.
You might be able to do it with a bunch of ENTRY points.
(It's still not trivial.)
I haven't tried it yet, but you MIGHT be able to set self-referencing
pointers to ENTRY points in the program that would normally be Paragraph
names.

| If you did not use the new world COBOL -POINTERS I am not sure I would
want to
| maintain the system that you create. To approach a full set of OOP
features you
| have to get references to executable code into an array and index it. That
is
| very hard in older COBOL.

inner

|
| The machines are replacing  us. I hate when that happens.

The machines will replace some of us, but there are opportunities other than
OOP available.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **Message-ID:** `<20030906143858.19404.00000461@mb-m20.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<jLj6b.128171$0v4.9320626@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

In this thread I had stated that the computed  GOTO, has been removed from
COBOL.

Harley" dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net 
Date: 9/6/03 6:43 AM EST
Message-id: <jLj6b.128171$0v4.9320626@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>

posted a correcting note for which I am grateful

<lots of snips>

<<...
Not yet, I just checked the IBM z/OS COBOL LRM, and GO TO DEPENDING ON is
still supported.
(The last time I used it, someone asked if I could use a vector table, I
said OK, no problem. Others in the group looked at me like I had 3 heads.)

>>

Well, surely you are a person with triple brain power and your posts are above
average.

Thanks.

It may be as hard to define a pin-headed manager as it is to corner OOP
terminology, but 
you can spot a triple coned programmer a mile away. 

So maybe the real issue is that OOP is exactly the unleashing of this kind of
critter and permissing it to wander about in shops where business data
processing abounds.

The common goal has got to be to keep building down the walls of terminology.
The methods of an object then are a table of 
procedures (vector, addresses of procedures).
You can code this kind of thing manually even in COBOL, as well as other
popular languages. 
Yet it is much better to let automating
tools do it for us in an economically recognizable fashion.

The more recent additions to the arsenal of 
OOP approaches, such as verifiers and theorem provers are also excellent
quality assurance devices, and do assist in protection 
of executable environments against error and
malice.


Best Wishes to All, single-minded or not,
Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Building a wall for division...

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-09-08T00:33:37-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0309072333.49fa6801@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0309032049.6526cc62@posting.google.com> <NaP5b.128758$3o3.9070195@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote 

> When was OO first used for simulation?

I don't know, but it was certainly in Simula-67 (that's the 1967
version of Simula language).

The basis of objects in simulation is that each is programmed with a
certain set of behaviours and then instances of them are invoked at
intervals (eg randomly) and then they run autonomously until they
'die' or exit.

Whether this mechanism should be applied to business depends on how
much the application can be distorted to fit it.

I quite often find OO documents which seem to be completely
inappropriate, for example from an IBM document:

""" the Employee objects bob and jane both have a .salary  and a
.room_number, but not the same room and salary as each other."""

more to the point from JScript tutorial:

"""Let's create an instance of the Employee class, and set its name
and address:

var Bob : Employee = new Employee("Bob Smith");
Bob.address = "2010 University Avenue, Palo Alto, CA 94301";

The command Bob.printMailingLabel() will yield the following output:

Bob Smith
2010 University Avenue, Palo Alto, CA 94301

"""

How can this possibly relate to any real application ?  If the Company
employs someone new, say Jane, do we have to change the program to
also have:

var Jane : Employee = new Employee("...

OO programming is ideal for managing multiple instances of objects. 
Business applications often don't do this, they do iteration and only
require one instance which is serially reused. In a file the record
buffer is reused, in an SQL cursor the fetch sets the data items.
```

I can process many rows in a single SQL statement.

While OO does have multiple instances of objects, I would be interested in
hearing about multiple instances of a business object (not gui stuff) being
used in a real world situation.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Building a wall for division...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-09-08T08:41:46-04:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Building a wall for division...

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-09T14:41:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bjkor7$9im$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0309032049.6526cc62@posting.google.com> <NaP5b.128758$3o3.9070195@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0309072333.49fa6801@posting.google.com> <Um_6b.133513$3o3.9453989@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <7P_6b.1234$fC5.312295@news20.bellglobal.com> <wj87b.131457$0v4.9603205@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

On  8-Sep-2003, "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

> I can see that an OO program may be less error prone, and easier to test.

Depends.  I can also see the opposite.

OO requires that you have confidence that your invoked objects work correctly.


In mainframe shops, it is not uncommon at all to require full user testing for
any change.  If a program is called by 100 other programs, often the choice is
"it's too expensive to test this - let's not fix it".    Part of implementing an
OO environment is persuading the users that they need different testing
standards.

Also, when you hide the workings of an object, you can hide the problems.   I
have seen a similar problem with a standard that says "when you hit an abort
condition, call this program that does our standard abort".   The trouble with
this, is that the dump is of the called abort program - which is pretty
worthless.

Objects get triggered, instead of called.   At the most basic level, these are
the same thing.   But at the level where this is useful, it can make testing
more difficult - how do you make sure you test each and every way it can get
triggered?   Often you can't.

OO isn't all upside.  Like any choice, there are costs as well.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Building a wall for division...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-09-08T17:30:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F5CBD87.C9CE357E@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0309032049.6526cc62@posting.google.com> <NaP5b.128758$3o3.9070195@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0309072333.49fa6801@posting.google.com> <Um_6b.133513$3o3.9453989@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```


Harley wrote:

> While OO does have multiple instances of objects, I would be interested in
> hearing about multiple instances of a business object (not gui stuff) being
> used in a real world situation.

'Not GUI stuff' - four classes :-

1. Class - IsamFile (implies a fixed PrimeKey length - say 20 or 50 chars)
2. Class - RelativeFile
3. Class - SequerntialFile
4. Class - LineSequentialFile

Each is based on 'Record varying from "a" to "b" depending on "c"'. In the case
of ISAM, "a" must be at least the PrimeKey Length. Pass the file pathname and
"actual" record length ( the value for "c"), for each 'instance' created.

Each of the above invokes a 'common' file status 'FileErrors' class.

As regards ISAM, the necessity of 'several' keys can be obviated by association
with  SortedCollections.

Above can be used for a mixture of 10 or 50 files etc.

Can't do above with DBs - but an SQL CURSOR SELECT can also be used with a
SortedCollection.

There again - you're on a mainframe - so you may not see the necessity as the
OS so often provides its own file handling routines.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Building a wall for division...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-09-09T00:45:21+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f5c7ad8_4@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0309032049.6526cc62@posting.google.com> <NaP5b.128758$3o3.9070195@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0309072333.49fa6801@posting.google.com>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0309072333.49fa6801@posting.google.com...
> "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
LOL! Pretty risque for IBM...

> more to the point from JScript tutorial:
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> How can this possibly relate to any real application ?

Ask Bob Smith at 2010 University Ave in Palo Alto...<G>

Seriously, the fact that poor examples are available (and I agree with you
the above are seriously bad...<G>) doesn't mean that OO is poor...

> If the Company
> employs someone new, say Jane, do we have to change the program to
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> buffer is reused, in an SQL cursor the fetch sets the data items.

This is a very valid point. Serially reusable iteration is certainly the
basis of batch processing. (And we used it for on-line processing with
IMS/DC and TaskMaster, but that's another story...).

I think the reason this is so is because THAT's how we currently design
Business computer systems.

If (just an example...) we were to use a customer OBJECT, instead of a
customer FILE, we would gain little advantage. (We'd need an instance of the
Object for each customer (because, presumably, the Object would contain the
customer data as well as the methods to operate on it, and this would mean
loading the instance from somewhere (RDB?)) and you could make a very good
argument that if we are processing customers in bulk, all these object
instances are just extra overhead. That's why we use files for sequential
processing.

But what happens when the customer base has to be distributed across a
network and any  given customer needs to be available from anywhere,
anytime?

Now, the Transactional nature of the Object instance comes into its own. We
don't need to open the whole file (even if we could); we need only the
desired instance. It can be flashed around the network to anywhere it is
needed, in an instant.
Code does not have to be available on multiple servers (as it would be if
you did this with record instances) and duplicated across the network.
Instead the customer Object's Methods can be remotely invoked from a single
instance. Not only that, but it can interact with other objects in the
system just as easily. This is even before we consider the other benefits of
OOD/OOP (Class inheritance, interfaces, and the buzzwords...). However, to
gain these benefits requires a fundamental shift in the way we view and
design commercial computer systems. (That seems to be what is holding OO
back at present, certainly as far as COBOL sites are concerned...)

The fact is that OO designs model real life (which tends for the most part
to be "transactional" - certainly, from our individual perspectives <G>)
better than the file model does, IN MOST CASES.
(Not when it comes to Batch Processing...although an OO design does not
preclude batch processing, it is unlikely to be as efficient as a
traditional file based system.)

If you see things in terms of individuals, then Objects are better, if you
see things in terms of groups or crowds, then they're not  (necessarily)
<G>.

Pete.
```

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote

> This is a very valid point. Serially reusable iteration is certainly the
> basis of batch processing. (And we used it for on-line processing with

It is also the basis of interactive systems.  You only work with one
Customer order at a time, one order line, one product, and then
seriall reuse for the next.  There may be a case for having all the
order lines in an array, or held on disk and reread as required.

In fact if each line is an object instance then the object handles
still need to be in an array or list or similar.

> If (just an example...) we were to use a customer OBJECT, instead of a
> customer FILE, 

Instead of ?

> But what happens when the customer base has to be distributed across a
> network and any  given customer needs to be available from anywhere,
> anytime?

Now _access_ to the customer data has to be distributed accross the
network, but that doesn't need the customer records (or objects) to be
'distibuted'.  In fact it would not normally be sensible to do so. 
There is a need to back up the data to cater for hardware or
procedural failure, there is always a need to have some central
repository so that _anyone_ can access any of them at any time.

> Now, the Transactional nature of the Object instance comes into its own. We
> don't need to open the whole file (even if we could); 

What do you mean by 'opening the whole file' ?.  To find where an
object actually is located there needs to be some mechanism - an
index, register, or directory.  Or did you have some form of magic ?

> we need only the desired instance. 

Yes it must be magic.

> It can be flashed around the network to anywhere it is
> needed, in an instant.

Is this a copy that is 'flashed around' or is it deleted from the
original place ?  Is the index or directory updated to indicate where
it is held now ? What is actually 'flashed around' ?  The data items
or all the code methods as well ?

You are long on marketing doubletalk and short on practicalities.

> Code does not have to be available on multiple servers (as it would be if
> you did this with record instances) and duplicated across the network.
> Instead the customer Object's Methods can be remotely invoked from a single
> instance. 

So, in the end the object is _not_ 'flashed around' the network, it is
just accessed as required on the server (in this case an object
server).  It is just another redistribution of client-server
functionality, perhaps more granular.

> The fact is that OO designs model real life (which tends for the most part
> to be "transactional" - certainly, from our individual perspectives <G>)
> better than the file model does, IN MOST CASES.

        Bob = new Employee()     it is then.

> (Not when it comes to Batch Processing...although an OO design does not
> preclude batch processing, it is unlikely to be as efficient as a
> traditional file based system.)

I don't know why you bang on about batch processing.
 
> If you see things in terms of individuals, then Objects are better, if you
> see things in terms of groups or crowds, then they're not  (necessarily)

Now I can see that you said this as relating to batch processing =
crowds, but in fact it is completely inverted.

It you only need to deal with one thing at a time then serial reuse is
best, if you need to deal with multiple instances (crowds) the objects
may be better.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Building a wall for division...

- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0309032049.6526cc62@posting.google.com> <NaP5b.128758$3o3.9070195@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0309072333.49fa6801@posting.google.com> <3f5c7ad8_4@news.athenanews.com> <217e491a.0309082334.7743404d@posting.google.com> <3f5da6f6_1@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote 

> > In fact if each line is an object instance then the object handles
> > still need to be in an array or list or similar.
> >
> As an OO programmer you don't care. You COULD maintain an array of object
> references, or not, depending on how you design it.

You make it sound as if you can casually ignore them and then
magically reuse them from nowhere.

> Sorry, I have become accustomed to remotely invoking component methods where
> there is only one instance on the whole Network, but it APPEARS as if it has
> been sent to where you want to use it.

What it appears to do is a result of your imagination.
 
> The "magic" is in the RMI.

That ain't magic.
 
> As I'm not trying to sell you or anyone else anything, Richard, your hostile
> comment is unwarranted. and adds nothing to your argument. Neither does it
> encourage me to continue this dialogue. I responded to your post in good
> faith because you raised a valid point. I was trying to explain how I see
> this, and have a civised discussion on it.

Hostile ?
 
> As for practicalities, my systems work. (This is not to imply that yours
> don't, before you get started...<G>)

They may well work, I never doubted this, but the terms you used such
as 'flashed around the network to whereever needed' doesn't describe
_how_ they work, in fact the actuality is the opposite.  The objects
don't move at all, the client program goes out and seeks them.

> Well...yes. But the effect is different than it would be with a file. If the
> data has to be displayed somewhere miles away, it IS flashed around the
> network. The point is that with objects they can all be in different
> locations on different servers and still interact as if they were "local".
> This is difficult (though not impossible) to do with files.

But is no different than could happen with file servers or RDMS
servers.  Generally these are accessed by several interactions to
establish connection, access data and then close connection.  The RMI
may be done with a single interaction of with several.  Granted the
object may be more intelligent.

In the end the data still _is_ held in files or RDMS (or object
database).  Invokiong an object may _still_ result in a file being
opened and the data read from it.  There is just another level of
abstraction plastered over the top.
```

#### ↳ Re: Building a wall for division...

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-04T18:12:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vlfhmt2an7k7e2@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com>`

```
KELLIE FITTON wrote:
> Hello everyone,
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> your unbiased point of view will be appreciated, thank you. Kellie.

Who know why they chose the terminologies they did - but, they are 
actually pretty meaningful.  Polymorphism is more than multi-form 
displayed text.  Encapsulation is packing code, but it's much more than 
that - when you take into account public methods versus private methods, 
properties, etc.  It's not just the code that's packaged, it's data too.

Invoke is a bit different than Call.  A call executes a predefined 
subroutine, whether it's statically linked or dynamically linked. 
Invoking a method isn't the same as calling a class - it's telling that 
particular -instance- of an object that you want it to do something.

Subtle, yes - and, if you're trying to learn OO, comparing the OO 
terminology to the structured terminology is often a great way to take 
some of the mystery away from OO.  However, there are subtle differences 
that make OO different, and these terms help emphasize that.

Personally, I'm more a fan of component-based software engineering 
(CBSE).  With OO, you generally have lots of objects, and if one needs 
to be changed, you change the object.  In the course of changing this 
object, it's quite easy to break something else.  CBSE applies the same 
processes as most other engineering disciplines, such as civil 
engineering.  When you build a bridge, you don't go design, develop, and 
stress test a great big concrete arch - you buy bricks, which do very 
little in and of themselves, but do what they do very well.  You'll also 
buy the mortar to hold them together, and the rebar to support it.

In CBSE, the bricks are your components, and the mortar is a small, 
structured program that ties the components together.  It uses the 
object model from OO (while removing a lot of the complex portions of 
it), and uses structured programming for it's strength, which is a 
top-down (or bottom-up) design.

Of course, this is biased by my life experiences and reading I've done 
over the years...  But, I have no financial stake in whether you use OO 
or CBSE.  :)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Building a wall for division...

"LX-i" <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message
news:vlfhmt2an7k7e2@corp.supernews.com...

> Invoking a method isn't the same as calling a class - it's telling that
> particular -instance- of an object that you want it to do something.
>
> Subtle, yes - and, if you're trying to learn OO, comparing the OO

This is the most suncinct explanation I have read yet.
Tap, Tap, as we say in the pool ng.

Donald
```

##### ↳ ↳ [OT bit I didn't start it:-)] Re: Building a wall for division...

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-05T05:49:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<stV5b.10693$Mb2.399288@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com> <vlfhmt2an7k7e2@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"LX-i" <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message
news:vlfhmt2an7k7e2@corp.supernews.com...
> KELLIE FITTON wrote:
> > Hello everyone,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> object, it's quite easy to break something else.
The common thing that everyone says about components is that they are
extremely well designed and thoroughly thought out such that no modification
is necessary. The interaction is extremely well defined and blah blah
blah....Why assume that the same level of care isn't taken in an object?
What's the big deal about wrapping an object - saying it is designed well
and cannot change - and calling it a component?

> processes as most other engineering disciplines, such as civil
> engineering.  When you build a bridge, you don't go design, develop, and
> stress test a great big concrete arch - you buy bricks, which do very
> little in and of themselves, but do what they do very well.  You'll also
> buy the mortar to hold them together, and the rebar to support it.
Actually I believe they have simulation software and models.  If this whole
component thing was as great as you say then all bridges would look the
same.  We would have one or two bridge companies that would buy bridge
components and spend time only building bridges.  First architects would be
laid off because bridges could be designed by politicians iteratively
describing the scene....yes, there's water...and boats...yes big boats...and
we have trains....and it's go to be no higher than 400 ft high...hmmm big
lorries with 18 wheels and we have a depressed metropolis and suicides are
not good for my career...and then *poof* ... they would be out of business
because the bridges that their smart bridge making software designed would
last forever and be unimprovable because of the high quality bricks and
mortar that was chosen..

Furthermore, to quote someone else:
"Unlike modules, which vary depending on who wrote them and the
circumstances under which they were written, components MUST conform to a
standard interface that is UNIVERSAL. This is called COM or ActiveX in the
MicroSoft environment, and is a subset of the great Online Linking and
Embedding technology called OLE."

What is this magical standard UNIVERSAL interface that has a particular
Microsoft implementation?!?

Not that I think components are the devil or anything - but is this group
comp.lang.component ?

JCE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT bit I didn't start it:-)] Re: Building a wall for division...

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-09-05T19:46:07+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f584032_4@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com> <vlfhmt2an7k7e2@corp.supernews.com> <stV5b.10693$Mb2.399288@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
"jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:stV5b.10693$Mb2.399288@twister.tampabay.rr.com...
>
> "LX-i" <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > object, it's quite easy to break something else.

> The common thing that everyone says about components is that they are
> extremely well designed and thoroughly thought out such that no
modification
> is necessary. The interaction is extremely well defined and blah blah
> blah....Why assume that the same level of care isn't taken in an object?

I don't think "everyone" has been saying that, John. It was mainly me, and
the reason I have been saying it is because nobody else was...<G>. I
certainly didn't mean to imply that the same levels of care cannot be, or
are not, taken in other forms of programming.

> What's the big deal about wrapping an object - saying it is designed well
> and cannot change - and calling it a component?
>
It isn't a big deal once you recognise the opportunity and use it. It IS a
big deal the first time you realise the full implications of being able to
do this.

(They go far beyond anything I have posted here... Consider global access
(as in from anywhere on the Planet), for the cost of a local phone call,
with distributed data across all kinds of platforms, just as a start.... The
first time I saw the potential for this, I was stunned... but that's maybe
just me. It was certainly a big deal to me. Nowadays, I am blase about it.
There are many other advantages of components but I can see from your post
you're in no mood to discuss them <G>).

After your post describing the imminent death of components that people
can't understand I ran some searches... Try inputting "Component based
design" into Google - I got 4.4 million responses... 298 responses to a
search looking for College courses in the same. It isn't JUST me...<G>


> > processes as most other engineering disciplines, such as civil
> > engineering.  When you build a bridge, you don't go design, develop, and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Actually I believe they have simulation software and models.  If this
whole
> component thing was as great as you say then all bridges would look the
> same.

That really isn't fair. Would you say that all brick houses look the same?
Daniel was using an analogy. It maybe isn't perfect (I've used it myself)
but it isn't bad.

>We would have one or two bridge companies that would buy bridge
> components and spend time only building bridges.

Precisely that is happening with office block construction, but, again, it
is only an analogy. Because components can be likened to system building
blocks, doesn't mean that whatever shortcomings you see with bricks are
necessarily ascribable to them. (It might be true or it might not - it is an
analogy.)

> First architects would be
> laid off because bridges could be designed by politicians iteratively
> describing the scene....yes, there's water...and boats...yes big
boats...and
> we have trains....and it's go to be no higher than 400 ft high...hmmm big
> lorries with 18 wheels and we have a depressed metropolis and suicides are
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
LOL! Good stuff...<G>

> Furthermore, to quote someone else:
> "Unlike modules, which vary depending on who wrote them and the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Embedding technology called OLE."

That was me again. I stand by it.
>
> What is this magical standard UNIVERSAL interface that has a particular
> Microsoft implementation?!?
>

Its universal because CORBA now supports ActiveX, and Java has a special
bean processor that wraps beans as ActiveX. So, your ActiveX components can
run on non MicroSoft platforms and non-ActiveX components (suitably wrapped)
can run in MicroSoft environments. If you will grant that MS and non-MS
covers the "universe" then the statements I made are fair.

> Not that I think components are the devil or anything - but is this group
> comp.lang.component ?
>
OK, I plead guilty to this. You are right. It is a COBOL group and it is
very wrong of me to discuss components here. (Even though I DO write them
(mainly) in COBOL, and live in the (apparently, forlorn) hope that some
others here might be encouraged to do so also.)

Of course, it is OK to discuss Gun Laws, Religion, the American Political
system, and the shape of bloody flapjacks, not to mention the origins of
language, (at extreme length), the internal physics of microwaved coffee,
and so on, ad infinitum...

 But you resent me posting on components.

Guess I better stop.

(Yeah...right <G>)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT bit I didn't start it:-)] Re: Building a wall for division...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-09-05T13:27:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0309051227.685fb3a9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com> <vlfhmt2an7k7e2@corp.supernews.com> <stV5b.10693$Mb2.399288@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <3f584032_4@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote

> Its universal because CORBA now supports ActiveX, 

It supports ActiveX on Intel _Windows_ machines.

> So, your ActiveX components can run on non MicroSoft platforms 

Non-Microsoft platforms would not want to run ActiveX executables even
if they could, they are Intel binaries and so would not run on, say,
PowerPC, and they rely on the underlying Windows environment.

> If you will grant that MS and non-MS
> covers the "universe" then the statements I made are fair.

Microsoft may think that your view of the 'universe' is fair.
```

I'll take this on board in any future articles I produce on the subject.

Pete.

- **Message-ID:** `<EBf6b.8728$y_6.531328@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com> <vlfhmt2an7k7e2@corp.supernews.com> <stV5b.10693$Mb2.399288@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <3f584032_4@news.athenanews.com>`

```

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3f584032_4@news.athenanews.com...
> OK, I plead guilty to this. You are right. It is a COBOL group and it is
> very wrong of me to discuss components here. (Even though I DO write them
> (mainly) in COBOL, and live in the (apparently, forlorn) hope that some
> others here might be encouraged to do so also.)
Not really.  It's perfectly fine...in clc you have a group seniority level
that allows you to do this ;-)
I have the ability to stop reading...I guess I was in a bad mood.

> Of course, it is OK to discuss Gun Laws, Religion, the American Political
> system, and the shape of bloody flapjacks, not to mention the origins of
> language, (at extreme length), the internal physics of microwaved coffee,
> and so on, ad infinitum...
You left off answering questions with questions... and answers in panzers.

>  But you resent me posting on components.
Not really.
The first time the police call for money you listen and are polite.
The second time the police call for money you listen and are less polite.
The third time the police call for money you listen to get to the end and
tell them you're not interested.
The fourth time the police call for money you tell them no.
The fifth time the police call to tell you they've found your stolen car you
didn't pick up.

You have very valid salient points; however you head straight to the goose
that lays the golden egg.  When  you do this it sells you short.  ....go to
a Ford salesman and ask them about Dodge....Go to a priest and ask him about
buddhism...  Doesn't mean that Dodges don't go (their cars might not) and
that buddhists are hell bound.
There is no one ubiquitous solution for anything that is based on opinions
and interpretations

Components depend on a model...there are multiple models (COM/J2EE/CORBA).
In theory they are important; in practice there are hindrances - number one
being the number of people you can convert :-)   You always talk about the
same ActiveX/COM - where is Java in this? Isn't a javabean a component? How
does that relate to ActiveX?

There are other things far more important to the future of businesses (which
is primarily your arguments' domain) and these are rarely discussed with
such fervor - CMM, for example, which is pervasive in business and the
leading indicator of your job future more than whether you use components,
It's also far more common in COBOL shops than Java shops.  Unfortunately
your premise is that "coders will be non existent".  This makes most
argument and discussions short and simple for you.  Evolution or
Creationism - well, once you decide the latter the argument on the first is
moot....

> Guess I better stop.
> (Yeah...right <G>)
You mean I cannot stop you ? ;-)

Remember - water off a duck's back.  :-)))

JCE
```

written. I use Java beans (well, 2 of them that I wrote just to try out that
environment) and have ALWAYS included them in discussions on Components. I
also made it clear that the environment I am working in is primarily MS and
so I use ActiveX. I am also not seeking to "convert" people to any
particular approach. I'll be very happy if they just look at it.

> There are other things far more important to the future of businesses
(which
> is primarily your arguments' domain)

My "argument" is not about the future of business. It is about the future of
business software and IT methods. I am also concerned about the future of
COBOL programmers, having been one for all of my working life. You have
missed the point entirely.

> and these are rarely discussed with
> such fervor - CMM, for example, which is pervasive in business and the

forum.

I have advocated an evolutionary approach to system development so you have
it the wrong way round. I do certainly believe that "coders will be
non-existent" but not this week...

> You mean I cannot stop you ? ;-)
>
Governments couldn't stoop me when I have something I want to say...

> Remember - water off a duck's back.  :-)))

Quack...quack.

However, I won't be responding further until I see some evidence that you
have actually read my position, and are capable of presenting a fair case
against it, if that is what you want to do.

Pete.


>
> JCE

>

"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:vlksmfqib6eq04@corp.supernews.com...
>
> Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, it made me smile too when I saw it after posting...<G> Maybe a Freudian
slip...

The allusion is to a controversy that raged here around pirate radio back in
the early 60s. Finding all the usual avenues barred, (thanks to covert
activity by Government and vested interests) some friends and I simply
organised public meetings (nowadays we'd use the Internet as well).  Within
6 months the groundswell of public opinion was such that it could not be
resisted and the Government caved and granted licenses. I would hasten to
add, that my own part in this was miniscule, compared to the efforts of some
others, nevertheless, I learned that if you have something to say and you
feel seriously about it, you should not allow anything to stop you...

(I was a lot younger then and nothing like the gentle, mellow,  calm,
personality you read now...<G>)

> Government has been 'stooping' me for almost 30 years; but
> I have only recently found the words that may stop it.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The Doc, for the most part, can only be a GOOD influence on all of us...<G>

Pete.
```

No Sir, merely influence; not cause.

[I have to learn to talk like that to show proper deference while
suggestting, to one "bound ... to support this Constitution", that
Article VI, Clause 3, requires adherrence to the idea of 'express
delegation'; that failure 'to support this Constitution' (uphold and
preserve an order given by the people) in the manner of 'express
delegation' is literally to commit an 'illegal' act against the people.
In 1987, I understood 'support' to mean 'literal interpretation'. It was
only about three weeks ago that I substituted 'express delegation'.
This aspect of the clause has not been the subject of judicial
interpretation.]

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT bit I didn't start it:-)] Re: Building a wall for division...

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-05T06:49:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vlgu1e764hnm61@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<stV5b.10693$Mb2.399288@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309030750.26448ae6@posting.google.com> <vlfhmt2an7k7e2@corp.supernews.com> <stV5b.10693$Mb2.399288@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
(How is this off-topic?)

jce wrote:

> "LX-i" <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message
> news:vlfhmt2an7k7e2@corp.supernews.com...
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> and cannot change - and calling it a component?

There isn't - that's the beauty of components.  (In fact, I think PECD 
actually uses OO COBOL in many of his components.)  Nothing says that 
CBSE precludes OO - in fact, OO can be a viable player in CBSE, because 
there are certain things that are better suited for OO.

>>processes as most other engineering disciplines, such as civil
>>engineering.  When you build a bridge, you don't go design, develop, and
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> mortar that was chosen..

No, all bridges wouldn't look the same.  And, there are parts where the 
analogy breaks down, as physical things exposed to the elements and 
stress will naturally break down, whereas 1s and 0s will not.  Bricks 
are bricks, but there are many types of bricks - they vary in color, 
shape, and size, and durability.  You choose the type you need based on 
what you're wanting to do.  We shouldn't expect that componentization 
should equal uniformity.  Do all information systems do the same thing 
(or look the same) since most of them use a database?   :)

There's also another point of components - they're very narrowly 
defined.  If you'll look in the archives at aboutlegacycoding.com, 
there's an article by PECD where he has a component for download that 
takes a string and validates it as a number, then formats it in 
different ways.  This component could be plugged into a large number of 
applications, from online banking to bowling score tracking to a 
shopping cart application.  They won't all look the same, and the 
component is narrow (generic) enough to work.  Now, if you need to 
validate whether that number is in a given range, you can either check 
it within code (< and >, in some form, are pretty much standard in most 
programming languages), or you can write a small "range checking" 
component, which takes 3 parameters, and tells you whether the 1st is 
between the 2nd and the 3rd.  Again, something you could use in any 
number of applications that look differently.

> Furthermore, to quote someone else:
> "Unlike modules, which vary depending on who wrote them and the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Microsoft implementation?!?

It doesn't have to be - what he was saying is that wrapping these 
components means you can call them from any language that supports this 
interface.  For example, you can call a COBOL component, a FORTRAN 
component, and a Quick BASIC component from a C++ program.  Instead of 
trying to link them all together in one executable, your C++ program is 
tying the components together, and it doesn't matter what host language 
the components are in because all they expose are an OLE or 
COM-compliant interface.

> Not that I think components are the devil or anything - but is this group
> comp.lang.component ?

No, but it's very relevant to COBOL professionals - it's a way for us to 
extend the life of this language, use it for its strengths, and include 
other components that utilize the strengths of some other languages as well.
```

merge some QSAM files into one without doing any sorting? Thx.
```

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

> Could anyone tell me, what's the difference between them if I just want to
> merge some QSAM files into one without doing any sorting? Thx.

Merge or concatenate?
```

#### ↳ Re: REPRO VS DFSORT

- **From:** "Gonzo" <ckhamel1961@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-03T21:25:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<p%s5b.1081$o57.424@newssvr24.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3f561e2d$1@shknews01>`

```

"KK" <KK@KK.com> wrote:
> Could anyone tell me, what's the difference between them if I just want to
> merge some QSAM files into one without doing any sorting? Thx.

Strictly speaking, REPRO does not perform a "merge" process that I am aware
of. Merge would be a SORT function.

If you want to "concatenate" a bunch of QSAM files into a single file, then
I suppose either would do. My own personal preference is SORT, but that's
only because I have occasion to use it more. I don't have anything against
REPRO. Others might have some other preferences, or qualms about
performance.

Gonzo
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: REPRO VS DFSORT

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Frank Yaeger <yaeger@us.ibm.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-15T09:10:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F65E48F.2090902@us.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<3f561e2d$1@shknews01> <p%s5b.1081$o57.424@newssvr24.news.prodigy.com> <hBt5b.18416$Ih1.7258087@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <QXS5b.9822$Mb2.383258@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <JR%5b.129990$3o3.9130571@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Harley wrote:

> I think it's the other way around: IEBGENER invokes ICEGENER.

No.  DFSORT's ICEGENER invokes IEBGENER when DFSORT copy can't be used.
For more information on DFSORT's ICEGENER, see:

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ICECA109/9.2.18?SHELF=&DT=20020722140254
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: REPRO VS DFSORT

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jREMOVEcCAPSewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 2003-09-20T05:46:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SQRab.10735$BS5.9876@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3F65E48F.2090902@us.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<3f561e2d$1@shknews01> <p%s5b.1081$o57.424@newssvr24.news.prodigy.com> <hBt5b.18416$Ih1.7258087@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <QXS5b.9822$Mb2.383258@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <JR%5b.129990$3o3.9130571@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3F65E48F.2090902@us.ibm.com>`

```
Frank Yaeger wrote:
> Harley wrote:
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Perhaps merely semantic confusion.

Assuming it's similar to Syncsort interfaces, a JCL reference to 
IEBGENER really invokes a "sort" module, which decides whether the real 
IBM IEBGENER must be used, and if so transfers control by some means to 
the "real" IEBGENER module/CSECT.  The crucial point from the 
application programmers viewpoint is that the JCL does still reference 
PGM=IEBGENER, so that use of the sort program for improved Copy 
performance is transparent to the JCL writer.  So, perhaps both answers 
are correct.  Invoking IEBGENER in JCL invokes a program in linklist 
known as IEBGENER, which really invokes some "sort" code, which 
conditionally transfers to the code previously (without sort in the 
picture) known as IEBGENER.

On our system IEBGENER is an alias for Syncsort's BetterGener module and 
is in a linklist library concatenated ahead of SYS1.LINKLIST, so it is 
invoked by default by JCL references to IEBGENER.  If necessary it calls 
IEBGENR, which is an alias for IBM's IEBGENER in SYS1.LINKLIST.
```

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

```

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: REPRO VS DFSORT

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Frank Yaeger <yaeger@us.ibm.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-22T13:19:28-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F6F5950.7070605@us.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<3f561e2d$1@shknews01> <p%s5b.1081$o57.424@newssvr24.news.prodigy.com> <hBt5b.18416$Ih1.7258087@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <QXS5b.9822$Mb2.383258@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <JR%5b.129990$3o3.9130571@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3F65E48F.2090902@us.ibm.com> <SQRab.10735$BS5.9876@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
Joel C. Ewing wrote:
> Perhaps merely semantic confusion.
> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> IEBGENR, which is an alias for IBM's IEBGENER in SYS1.LINKLIST.

Yes, just semantic confusion.  For DFSORT, ICEGENER is called first 
either directly by PGM=ICEGENER, or by PGM=IEBGENER if the site has 
installed ICEGENER as a "replacement" for IEBGENER.  ICEGENER then usess 
DFSORT copy if possible, otherwise it calls IEBGENR which is an alias 
for the real IEBGENER.

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team  (IBM) - yaeger@us.ibm.com
Specialties: ICETOOL, OUTFIL, Symbols, Migration
=> DFSORT/MVS is on the Web at http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort
```

#### ↳ Re: hardware requirements and compile ProCobol

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-04T15:20:37+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ecielvg3tnnv6a4pjdo53l584kuopkqopf@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3329633.1062683805@dbforums.com>`

```
On Thu, 04 Sep 2003 09:56:45 -0400, PRE <member38102@dbforums.com>
wrote:

>
>hello
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I can't help with 1, but 2.
Oracle has some makefiles supplied with the database, and these are
installed with PRO*COBOL.
look at $ORACLE_HOME/precomp/* structure for makefiles for cobol.
You should have a procob and a procob2 directories both with examples
of makefiles.

It is advised by Oracle to use these makefiles as base for your own,
so do look at them.

IF you do not have the above folders available then you may need to
install PRO*COBOL as this is not installed by default.




Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: hardware requirements and compile ProCobol

- **From:** "Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-04T18:04:21+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bj7s6b$6km$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<3329633.1062683805@dbforums.com> <ecielvg3tnnv6a4pjdo53l584kuopkqopf@4ax.com>`

```
> I can't help with 1, but 2.
> Oracle has some makefiles supplied with the database, and these are
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> install PRO*COBOL as this is not installed by default.

If you're working on UNIX :

In addition to what Frederico has said above, you need to have your COBOL
environment set up in order to get the option to install Pro*COBOL on UNIX.

On both UNIX and Win32 :

If having done that you rerun the Oracle installer, select a Custom install,
and then Oracle Developer, by expanding the tree you should see Pro*COBOL
available for installation. As part of the install on UNIX, it will recreate
a new run-time including the Oracle support libraries (rtsora, under
$ORACLE_HOME/bin).

Under $ORACLE_HOME/precomp/demo, you will find two subdirectories, procob --
for use with the Oracle 1.8.xx precompiler -- and procob2 -- for Oracle
8.x.xx or 9.x.xx. Those directories include sample programs, and makefiles
(for UNIX) and a batch file (for Win32), which should point you in the right
direction for the correct build command(s) to use for your application.

Hope this helps.

SimonT.
```

### Net Express 3.0 help

- **From:** "Steve" <steve533@charter.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-04T16:43:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vlfcfmgnctjvb9@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Hi COBOL people,

    I'm in a COBOL class at school and we are using MF Net Express 3.0.  I
bought the program off a buddy of mine who took the class last semester (The
orriginal, not some bootleg).  Anyway, recently my registry got really
screwed up due to a virus that my little bro, bless his heart, downloaded.
I recoved to an earlier point and now the program won't work.  Keeps telling
me "FATAL ERROR -- Cannot access message file"  When ever I go to compile.
My teacher said it sounds like I'm missing specific registry entries.  Well
I could reinstall and that should fix it, well turns out my roommate let one
of his buddies borrow my CD and also turns out that was a month ago, and no
one knows where it is.  So What I'm getting at is, does anyone know which
registry entries would fix this, or does anyone have like a program update
that would fix it, anything.  Or even if anyone has, or knows where to find,
the program downloadable online, any help would be much appreciated.

Thanks,
Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: Net Express 3.0 help

- **From:** Doug Scott <dwscott@ieee.org>
- **Date:** 2003-09-05T00:02:06+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VA.000001f1.037230ad@ieee.org>`
- **References:** `<vlfcfmgnctjvb9@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Steve,

> the program

Presumably you mean the Cobol compiler?

You're stuffed, as far as I can see. Without knowing which version of which 
compiler nobody can help you, and personally I don't think many of us get 
into the details of the registry entries for specific compilers. The 
registry is set up and maintained by the compiler itself.

If you had a registry editor and knew the publisher of the compiler, you 
could go looking for that publisher's name and (after backing up the 
registry) removing entries there. But you'd still have to do a re-install.

Over to anybody else, but I think you're going to have to recover that CD. 
And let it be a lesson to you about "lending" software :-)

---

Doug

dwscott@ieee.org
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Net Express 3.0 help

- **From:** "Steve" <steve533@charter.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-04T19:13:31-05:00

#### ↳ Re: Net Express 3.0 help

- **From:** "Gary" <IDontThink@so.com.so>
- **Date:** 2003-09-05T00:43:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<i%Q5b.354280$YN5.240985@sccrnsc01>`
- **References:** `<vlfcfmgnctjvb9@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Steve" <steve533@charter.net> wrote:
> I recoved to an earlier point and now the program won't work.  Keeps
telling
> me "FATAL ERROR -- Cannot access message file"  When ever I go to compile.

Hmmm, don't think there's much you can do here Steve. It looks like you
recovered your registry to a point prior to the NX install. Even if you
could restore the NX entries manually, I doubt you'd get much further
because the license key check will kick in.

Since you have rights to an original copy of NX, you might try Micro Focus
support to see if they can relay a copy of the software and your license key
to you.

Gary.
```

Trying this again... sorry for any dups that may appear...

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" wrote:
> Can you embed your called  standard COBOL routines into a Web page (Client
> OR Server) and invoke them remotely from anywhere on Earth? Can you then
> have the same code run on a desktop (without re-compiling or changes of
any
> kind)?

I may have misunderstood what you mean by "embed", but a (procedural) COBOL
program deployed as a CGI (that uses stdin/stdout -- i.e. accept/display)
could be invoked from anywhere and also called directly on a desktop?

Gary.

<a id="t-38b72314a8"></a>
## Thread: Cobol Compiler for Red Hat Linux 8.0. — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Cobol Compiler for Red Hat Linux 8.0.

- **From:** thecobolguy <thecobolguy@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-06T13:43:35-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3338774.1062870215@dbforums.com>`

```

Friends, I have all my applications developed in MF Cobol/2 and am
compiling them with a COBOL-85 compiler compatible with MF syntax on an
Intel 486 system running AT&T Unix SVR 4.2. I can create statically
linked executables that do not need a runtime system. (RTS).



           I want to move all my applications to a new machine (Intel
           P-4) running Red Hat Linux 8.0 Professional. Which is the
           most appropriate and cheapest Cobol compiler that is
           available for this platform which can compile my application
           and create statically linked executables that I can run
           without an RTS without having to make any changes in the
           source codes?



           An early reply would be highly appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Compiler for Red Hat Linux 8.0.

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-06T19:28:19+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<um9klvomksa9bvti3m9i0kp4rpv0kvq2mg@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3338774.1062870215@dbforums.com>`

```
On Sat, 06 Sep 2003 13:43:35 -0400, thecobolguy
<thecobolguy@yahoo.com> wrote:

>
>Friends, I have all my applications developed in MF Cobol/2 and am
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
As far as I know it's either 
www.microfocus.com
or
www.adtools.com

As far as I  know no other COBOL vendor will allow you to create
executables on those platforms.

Whether Fujitsu will allow you to run your programs withouth ANY
change it's another story.





Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Compiler for Red Hat Linux 8.0.

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-09-06T14:19:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0309061319.61dad6bf@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3338774.1062870215@dbforums.com> <um9klvomksa9bvti3m9i0kp4rpv0kvq2mg@4ax.com>`

```
Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com> wrote 

> >           I want to move all my applications to a new machine (Intel
> >           P-4) running Red Hat Linux 8.0 Professional. Which is the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >           source codes?

> As far as I know it's either 
> www.microfocus.com

MF requires RTS Application Server which is not cheap.  Actually you
_can_ make static binaries, but it is not easy.

> www.adtools.com

> Whether Fujitsu will allow you to run your programs withouth ANY
> change it's another story.

It is extremely unlikely that Fujitsu NetCobol 7 for Linux would be
suitable.  It has no SCREEN SECTION or ADIS capability.  It does do
ANSI ACCEPT/DISPLAY but does not implement ACCEPT/DISPLAY AT and
requires that some other user interfacing be used, such as SP/2, CGI,
or perhaps some form of CICS.

Also be aware that these may be supported on RedHat 7.2 but may not be
on 8.0 - I haven't checked recently.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Compiler for Red Hat Linux 8.0.

- **From:** Joe Klemmer <spam@x-nc.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-07T16:47:13-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pan.2003.09.07.20.47.13.702528@x-nc.net>`
- **References:** `<3338774.1062870215@dbforums.com>`

```
On Sat, 06 Sep 2003 13:43:35 -0400, thecobolguy wrote:

> 
> Friends, I have all my applications developed in MF Cobol/2 and am
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>            An early reply would be highly appreciated.

You should check out Kobol from theKompany.  It will build native
executables for both Linux and WinXX (under cygwin).  I have been playing
with it for a while and it's quite good.  http://www.thekompany.com and
look for Kobol.

Joe
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Compiler for Red Hat Linux 8.0.

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-09-07T20:58:38-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0309071958.34fbe528@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3338774.1062870215@dbforums.com> <pan.2003.09.07.20.47.13.702528@x-nc.net>`

```
Joe Klemmer <spam@x-nc.net> wrote 

> You should check out Kobol from theKompany.  It will build native
> executables for both Linux and WinXX (under cygwin).  I have been playing
> with it for a while and it's quite good.  http://www.thekompany.com and
> look for Kobol.

Last time I looked there was no multi-user facilities.  ie no file
sharing with record locking.  I think LOCK MODE is supported by the
syntax, but is not operitive.

They will be adding SQL access which will cater for multi-user, but
that doesn't help existing INDEXED file users.

Also the ADIS facilities are a subset of MF's (AFAIK).
```

<a id="t-8571616d41"></a>
## Thread: Routine Needed — 4 this month / 56 total

_Thread started [2003-08](2003-08.md#t-8571616d41)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Routine Needed

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-09-07T14:36:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0309071336.b0b74f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0308091504.77154c29@posting.google.com> <qeKcncGKTcM38auiXTWJig@giganews.com>`

```
Let's get back and answer the question at hand.

There is an open source library called ZLIB - of which there is an
ActiveX control which you can pass a string and get a zip (deflate)
compressed string in return.  Search for "Zlib" "activeX" on the
internet - there's at least one free one out there.

RE: the file sizes - I think you are mistaken about the current sizes
- though I admit I too am perplexed by the seq vs indexed limitation
re: reorganizing a corrupt file.  Best not to corrupt files I guess
<Grin>.

From the Fujitsu Readme for v7 (Notice the "About"):

  C) Maximum file sizes
     In addition to the "normal version" of the COBOL file system
     installed by default, a "large version" that allows you to use
     larger-sized files is provided.
     The "normal version" is F3BIFRM.DLL (installed by default).
     The maximum size of files is as follows:
     - line sequential, record sequential, relative file:
                     about 1.0G bytes.
     - indexed file: about 1.7G bytes.

     The "large version" of F3BIFRM.DLL (provided on the CD-ROM), is
     not installed by the installer. The maximum sizes of files are
     as follows when using the "large version":
     - line sequential, record sequential, relative file:
                     about 2.0G bytes.
     - indexed file: about 3.4G bytes.

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message news:<qeKcncGKTcM38auiXTWJig@giganews.com>...
> Thane Hubbell wrote:
> >
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Mind you, I haven't tried to reorg a large ISAM file yet, just asking.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Routine Needed

- **From:** Kyle Kittinger <kittingr@vvm.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-06T15:52:35-07:00

- **References:** `<87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com> <bh8chd$nq9$1@kostnix.uvt.nl>`

```

One simple routine would be to take two charater and convert them to
their numeric value and make it a six digit number , 1 character taken
the upper halve and that 2nd the lower half. This will leave you with
a six digit number equal to two characters that you would need to
store. This will cut down the space requirement by 1/2.





On Mon, 11 Aug 2003 17:27:43 +0200, "R. Hendriks"
<R.Hendriks_1@uvt.nl> wrote:

>Perhaps that the PKWARE Data Compression Library is of any use?
>(http://www.pkware.com/products/developers/dcl/)
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

On  8-Sep-2003, "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

> I think I've got it now.  The weapons of mass destruction could not be
> found, so now the rational is to attack anybody mean to women.  Astonishing
> how oil or money has nothing to do with it ... just pure idealism and a love
> of humanity in all it's purity.

I don't think oil or money are the primary reason.  I think the primary reason
to conquer Iraq was to show that we are the biggest and baddest wolf in the pack
- and that if anybody messes with us, someone will pay.

And I am not convinced that this won't be more effective than waiting to punish
only the guilty, sadly.
```

On  8-Sep-2003, "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

> Well Howard, if you want to prove you are the biggest and baddest, why all
> the bitching and moaning when people tell you to FOAD?.

Some hypocrisy - and sometimes the person being big and bad isn't the person
bitching and moaning.

Also, when we think it may be necessary to be the bully - we expect our allies
to come to the same belief at the same time.   That's human nature - we ALL
expect others to become enlightened when we do (or when we think we are).   
Nothing like a reformed sinner (or, for that matter - a fallen angel).

> What gets my goat is being told over and over and over that you are the
> kindest, most humane, nicest country on the face of the earth, with a form
> of government that is based on pure idealism. I can handle being told to get
> out of the way.  Being told I have to worship the bullies while they act it
> out is a bit much.

Not as bad as being lumped in with those bullies.   I would MUCH rather have my
enemies, or even my companions act objectionable than have my side do so.   My
side should be above this.
```

children in the house.)  Just because weapons have yet to be found 
doesn't mean they weren't developed and hidden very well - Iraq is a bit 
bigger than your house, and I know we've all had trouble finding things 
that -we- put somewhere.  Don't rule out WMDs yet...

And yes, it is possible to do things to liberate people with no ulterior 
motives...  But, those who have ulterior motives themselves are often 
suspicious of everyone else.  Why should Hussein and his royal few 
profit from Iraq's huge oil supplies?  Why not sell the oil and let the 
Iraqi people get in on the $$$ to be made?  How is that a bad thing?

destroyed in mid-1991.

When the inspectors left in 1998 they, the UN weapons inspectors, and 
many of the current nay-sayers were convinced that SH still had WMD's 
stashed and WMD development programs.

I am not so innocent as to believe that SH just decided one day to burn 
everything and stop pursuing the WMDs he had been developing for over 20 
years.

suppose all the troops that found and destroyed the various WMD are in 
on the conspiracy as well?

Do you truly think Iraq just destroyed all their WMD after the weapons 
inspectors left in 1998 and with no international pressure on them at 
all?

off the site mentioned under Cobol Humour:

URGENT ASSISTANCE - FROM USA
IMMEDIATE ATTENTION NEEDED: HIGHLY CONFIDENTIAL
FROM: GEORGE WALKER BUSH 202.456.1414 / 202.456.1111 FAX: 202.456.2461

DEAR SIR / MADAM,

I AM GEORGE WALKER BUSH, SON OF THE FORMER PRESIDENT OF THE UNITED 
STATES OF AMERICA GEORGE HERBERT WALKER BUSH, AND CURRENTLY SERVING AS 
PRESIDENT OF THE UNITED STATES OF AMERICA. THIS LETTER MIGHT SURPRISE 
YOU BECAUSE WE HAVE NOT MET NEITHER IN PERSON NOR BY CORRESPONDENCE. I 
CAME TO KNOW OF YOU IN MY SEARCH FOR A RELIABLE AND REPUTABLE PERSON TO 
HANDLE A VERY CONFIDENTIAL BUSINESS TRANSACTION, WHICH INVOLVES THE
TRANSFER OF A HUGE SUM OF MONEY TO AN ACCOUNT REQUIRING MAXIMUM 
CONFIDENCE.

I AM WRITING YOU IN ABSOLUTE CONFIDENCE PRIMARILY TO SEEK YOUR 
ASSISTANCE IN ACQUIRING OIL FUNDS THAT ARE PRESENTLY TRAPPED IN THE 
REPUBLIC OF IRAQ.  MY PARTNERS AND I SOLICIT YOUR ASSISTANCE IN 
COMPLETING A TRANSACTION BEGUN BY MY FATHER, WHO HAS LONG BEEN ACTIVELY
ENGAGED IN THE EXTRACTION OF PETROLEUM IN THE UNITED STATES OF 
AMERICA,AND BRAVELY SERVED HIS COUNTRY AS DIRECTOR OF THE UNITED STATES 
CENTRAL INTELLIGENCE AGENCY.IN THE DECADE OF THE NINETEEN-EIGHTIES, MY
FATHER, THEN VICE-PRESIDENT OF THE UNITED STATES OF AMERICA, SOUGHT TO 
WORK WITH THE GOOD OFFICES OF THE PRESIDENT OF THE REPUBLIC OF IRAQ TO 
REGAIN LOST OIL REVENUE SOURCES IN THE NEIGHBORING ISLAMIC REPUBLIC OF
IRAN. THIS UNSUCCESSFUL VENTURE WAS SOON FOLLOWED BY A FALLING-OUT WITH 
HIS IRAQI PARTNER, WHO SOUGHT TO ACQUIRE ADDITIONAL OIL REVENUE SOURCES 
IN THE NEIGHBORING EMIRATE OF KUWAIT, A WHOLLY-OWNED U.S.-BRITISH
SUBSIDIARY.

MY FATHER RE-SECURED THE PETROLEUM ASSETS OF KUWAIT IN 1991 AT A COST OF 
SIXTY-ONE BILLION U.S. DOLLARS $61,000,000,000). OUT OF THAT 
COST,THIRTY-SIX BILLION DOLLARS ($36,000,000,000) WERE SUPPLIED BY HIS 
PARTNERS IN THE KINGDOM OF SAUDI ARABIA AND OTHER PERSIAN GULF
MONARCHIES, AND SIXTEEN BILLION DOLLARS ($16,000,000,000) BY GERMAN AND 
JAPANESE PARTNERS. BUT MY FATHER'S FORMER IRAQI BUSINESS PARTNER 
REMAINED IN CONTROL OF THE REPUBLIC OF IRAQ AND ITS PETROLEUM RESERVES.

MY FAMILY IS CALLING FOR YOUR URGENT ASSISTANCE IN FUNDING THE REMOVAL 
OF THE PRESIDENT OF THE REPUBLIC OF IRAQ AND ACQUIRING THE PETROLEUM 
ASSETS OF HIS COUNTRY, AS COMPENSATION FOR THE COSTS OF REMOVING HIM 
FROM POWER.  UNFORTUNATELY, OUR PARTNERS FROM 1991 ARE NOT WILLING TO 
SHOULDER THE BURDEN OF THIS NEW VENTURE, WHICH IN ITS UPCOMING PHASE MAY 
COST THE SUM OF 100 BILLION TO 200 BILLION DOLLARS ($100,000,000,000 
-$200,000,000,000), BOTH IN THE INITIAL ACQUISITION AND IN LONG-TERM 
MANAGEMENT.  WITHOUT THE FUNDS FROM OUR 1991 PARTNERS, WE WOULD NOT BE 
ABLE TO ACQUIRE THE OIL REVENUE TRAPPED WITHIN IRAQ. THAT IS WHY MY
FAMILY AND OUR COLLEAGUES ARE URGENTLY SEEKING YOUR GRACIOUS ASSISTANCE. 
OUR DISTINGUISHED COLLEAGUES IN THIS BUSINESS TRANSACTION INCLUDE THE 
SITTING VICE-PRESIDENT OF THE UNITED STATES OF AMERICA, RICHARD 
CHENEY,WHO IS AN ORIGINAL PARTNER IN THE IRAQ VENTURE AND FORMER
HEAD OF THE HALLIBURTON OIL COMPANY, AND CONDOLEEZA RICE, WHOSE 
PROFESSIONAL DEDICATION TO THE VENTURE WAS DEMONSTRATED IN THE NAMING OF 
A CHEVRON OIL TANKER AFTER HER.  I WOULD BESEECH YOU TO TRANSFER A SUM
EQUALING TEN TO TWENTY-FIVE PERCENT (10-25 %) OF YOUR YEARLY INCOME TO 
OUR ACCOUNT TO AID IN THIS IMPORTANT VENTURE. THE INTERNAL REVENUE 
SERVICE OF THE UNITED STATES OF AMERICA WILL FUNCTION AS OUR TRUSTED
INTERMEDIARY. I PROPOSE THAT YOU MAKE THIS TRANSFER BEFORE THE FIFTEENTH 
(15TH) OF THE MONTH OF APRIL.  I KNOW THAT A TRANSACTION OF THIS 
MAGNITUDE WOULD MAKE ANYONE APPREHENSIVE AND WORRIED. BUT I AM ASSURING 
YOU THAT ALL WILL BE WELL AT THE END OF THE DAY. A BOLD STEP TAKEN SHALL 
NOT BE REGRETTED, I ASSURE YOU. PLEASE DO BE INFORMED THAT THIS BUSINESS 
TRANSACTION IS 100% LEGAL. IF YOU DO NOT WISH TO CO-OPERATE IN THIS 
TRANSACTION,PLEASE CONTACT OUR INTERMEDIARY REPRESENTATIVES TO FURTHER
DISCUSS THE MATTER.  I PRAY THAT YOU UNDERSTAND OUR PLIGHT. MY FAMILY 
AND OUR COLLEAGUES WILL BE FOREVER GRATEFUL.

PLEASE REPLY IN STRICT CONFIDENCE TO THE CONTACT NUMBERS BELOW.

SINCERELY WITH WARM REGARDS, GEORGE WALKER BUSH

Switchboard: 202.456.1414 Comments: 202.456.1111 Fax: 202.456.2461
Email: president@whitehouse.gov
```

Chemical weapons for which there was no effective delivery system
beyond the local area.

_Potential_ WMD.  Planning and development towards WMD.  Not actual
WMD.

> When the inspectors left in 1998 they, the UN weapons inspectors, and

> and WMD development programs.

Yes.

No.
 
> Do you truly think Iraq just destroyed all their WMD after the weapons 
> inspectors left in 1998 and with no international pressure on them at 
> all?

You said it yourself:

"Iraqi made WMD was found and destroyed in mid-1991."

"all the troops that found and destroyed the various WMD"

The longer range developed SCUDs were also destroyed.

There may be WMD development that is well hidden, but the claim that
there was deliverable actual weapons that could be deployed and that
they posed an actual threat has not been substanciated.

"LX-i" <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message
news:vlpe3idi1mrgfa@corp.supernews.com...
| Donald Tees wrote:
| > "LX-i" <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
| > found, so now the rational is to attack anybody mean to women.
Astonishing
| > how oil or money has nothing to do with it ... just pure idealism and a
love
| > of humanity in all it's purity.
|
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
| that -we- put somewhere.  Don't rule out WMDs yet...

It wasn't WMDs, it was that they were going to give them to a terrorist.

They won't find the WMDs or the terrorist link.

Before the war started, Bush started to shift from protecting America to
liberating the Iraqi people.
He KNEW there were no WMDs, and he wasn't concerned about a terrorist
getting their hands on them.

Remember we had the al Qaeda guy who was going to build a dirty bomb in
custody.
We also knew the location of Iraq's nuclear facility.
Yet, there was no effort to secure the location as quickly as possible, and
Iraqis looted the facility.
Nobody was chastised for not securing the nuclear material.
It should have been a priority, if the Administration was concerned about
terrorists obtaining the makings for a dirty bomb.

How many heads rolled because of this major blunder?
None.

Why?

| And yes, it is possible to do things to liberate people with no ulterior
| motives...  But, those who have ulterior motives themselves are often
| suspicious of everyone else.  Why should Hussein and his royal few
| profit from Iraq's huge oil supplies?  Why not sell the oil and let the
| Iraqi people get in on the $$$ to be made?  How is that a bad thing?

The Iraqi people aren't going to get the money.
It's going to American companies for the rebuilding effort.

Bush is seeking investments in Iraq.
Maybe we'll invest in their electronics industry.

A capitalist wouldn't seek investment when he could reinvest profits, and
keep the money in his own pockets.
In other words a rapid increase in Iraqi oil production isn't necessary.
It would probably drive the price of oil down, which would be
counter-productive from an Iraqi perspective, and beneficial from an
American perspective.
```

<a id="t-7dd6a2575e"></a>
## Thread: Pro*COBOL and Micro Focus "Free" source format — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Pro*COBOL and Micro Focus "Free" source format

- **From:** matthew.james@shinetech.com (Matthew James)
- **Date:** 2003-09-07T17:27:54-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1f426830.0309071627.6865596a@posting.google.com>`

```
There seems to be a problem with using Oracle Pro*COBOL with the Micro
Focus "Free" source format. The code generated by Pro*COBOL is not
'valid' free format COBOL.  Specifically, Pro*COBOL generates a number
of long 'strings' in working storage - these strings are generated
using line continuation characters that are only valid for fixed
format source.  Therefore the Micro Focus COBOL compiler barfs.

There are a number of ways around this - essentially involving an
intermediate step (between the Pro*COBOL pre-compile and the Micro
Focus compile) that cleans up this working storage so that it will
compile.

This is fine for a makefile type build approach.  However we are
trying to move to NetExpress 4 which is much more interactive.  My
goal is to have single step compilation in NetExpress ...

My questions are:
- Does anybody else have a solution for this ?
- Is there a way to tell Pro*COBOL to generate free format compliant
code ?
- Has anybode else looked into building a Micro Focus integrated
pre-compiler to fix this (ie chain it after Pro*COBOL and have it fix
the generated statements for the COBOL compiler) ?

Thanks
Matthew James
```

#### ↳ Re: Pro*COBOL and Micro Focus "Free" source format

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-08T09:36:17+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hbfolv0k90ariqjc4ht5dr628eb1efhm8e@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1f426830.0309071627.6865596a@posting.google.com>`

```
On 7 Sep 2003 17:27:54 -0700, matthew.james@shinetech.com (Matthew
James) wrote:

>There seems to be a problem with using Oracle Pro*COBOL with the Micro
>Focus "Free" source format. The code generated by Pro*COBOL is not
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>Matthew James

try the precompiler option "format=terminal".




Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

## Thread: refactoring COBOL — 3 msgs

### refactoring COBOL

- **From:** Sven Brueggemann <SBrueggemann@gmx.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-08T11:54:27+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gckolvcgd93c6nlk4s6tbn7nmo123o6m61@4ax.com>`

```
Hello,

are there any good (online) ressources on refactoring techniques for
COBOL?

Kind regards

Sven
```

#### ↳ Re: refactoring COBOL

- **From:** "Ira Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-08T15:59:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f5cec06$1@giga.realtime.net>`
- **References:** `<gckolvcgd93c6nlk4s6tbn7nmo123o6m61@4ax.com>`

```

"Sven Brueggemann" <SBrueggemann@gmx.net> wrote in message
news:gckolvcgd93c6nlk4s6tbn7nmo123o6m61@4ax.com...
> are there any good (online) ressources on refactoring techniques for
COBOL?

If you are looking for heuristics for *when* (e.g., what circumstances) to
refactor, I have only a little bit to offer.
We've found that many COBOL programs (well, in fact, most programs
regardless
of language) are 10-20%, i.e., contains removable cloned code.    Given that
it costs about $1/line/year to have code around, finding and removing
typically
15% of a 100K SLOC application can make a visible dent in your budget every
year.
You can find out about clones at our website.

Others can probably offer other advice on when to refactor.

If you are looking for *how* to refactor, you can
1) do it by hand with an editor
2) do it with automated tools.
We offer the capability to do automated refactoring in the form of the
DMS Software Reengineering Toolkit.
DMS is generalized compiler technology, and can be obtained
with a COBOL front, and can parse/analyze/transform/prettyprint.
It is the basic machinery needed to carry out refactoring.
See http://www.semdesigns.com/Products/DMS/DMSToolkit.html.
```

#### ↳ Re: refactoring COBOL

- **From:** "Deborah Torrekens" <deborah@phidani.be>
- **Date:** 2003-09-09T14:37:00+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f5dc992$0$1129$6c56d894@feed0.news.be.easynet.net>`
- **References:** `<gckolvcgd93c6nlk4s6tbn7nmo123o6m61@4ax.com>`

```
Hi Sven,

RainCode, a Belgian corporation specialized in compiler techniques and
source code manipulations, launched a COBOL refactoring service online.

Go to: http://www.raincode.com/online

Evaluation is free:
You just need to upload the sources you want to refactor, and launch the
job.
After a week or so, you will be notified that you can come back to the site
to look at the results, and you get a firm quote.
There are no up-front costs; it's worth a trial.

Please let me know if you need further info.

Best regards,
Deborah

>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Sven
```

- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com> <bj7nja$5ll$2@news.utelfla.com> <ibW5b.10899$Mb2.400506@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <8cmdnY7z0sF4HMWiXTWJjw@giganews.com> <43lhlvc0ber0vlbdddo3f6gt3c0emk5i3r@4ax.com> <d8qcnQRgW52OcMWiXTWJjg@giganews.com> <bjasmf$2un$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <srqjlvsh8cs2o8saofqleo3ff7pkec31rn@4ax.com> <vlnb3o35ei3ef7@corp.supernews.com> <eliolvonacgt7st88ksf6n5makt34g8a9t@4ax.com> <vloul2pu2lhmb1@corp.supernews.com>`
