```
On  8-Sep-2003, LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote:

> > Democracy:
> > 1. Government by the people, excersised either directly or through
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> representatives.  It is rule of the masses.

Who's "they"?    Dictionary writers look around to see how words are used and
report on them.  Occasionally such popular definitions aren't applicable
(programmers have their own meaning for "database", and scientists have their
own meaning for "theory").    But certainly they are accurate in reporting the
most common usage of "democracy".

How many people use the primary meaning of "republic" as "a state without a
king"?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: {FLAME} Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-08T12:20:40-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vlpejr6g8b9ed@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<bjib0m$3s0$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com> <bj7nja$5ll$2@news.utelfla.com> <ibW5b.10899$Mb2.400506@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <8cmdnY7z0sF4HMWiXTWJjw@giganews.com> <43lhlvc0ber0vlbdddo3f6gt3c0emk5i3r@4ax.com> <d8qcnQRgW52OcMWiXTWJjg@giganews.com> <bjasmf$2un$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <srqjlvsh8cs2o8saofqleo3ff7pkec31rn@4ax.com> <vlnb3o35ei3ef7@corp.supernews.com> <eliolvonacgt7st88ksf6n5makt34g8a9t@4ax.com> <vloul2pu2lhmb1@corp.supernews.com> <bjib0m$3s0$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> On  8-Sep-2003, LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote:
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> report on them.

Exactly - that's the problems.  Words -must- have fixed definitions - 
there must be a standard for a society that wishes to remain great. 
Imagine railing against segregation, only to find in 20 years it's been 
redefined to mean "separation of church and state".  Democracy is mob 
rule - 50% + 1.  Our founding fathers went out of their way to ensure 
that they did not create a democracy, but a republic.  A republic may 
have appointed representatives, or democratically-elected ones (as we 
do).  We originally had a mix of each, as the House was elected and the 
Senate was appointed.  (I think we'd be a lot better off if we got back 
to that model.)

> Occasionally such popular definitions aren't applicable
> (programmers have their own meaning for "database", and scientists have their
> own meaning for "theory").    But certainly they are accurate in reporting the
> most common usage of "democracy".

And the most common usage is -wrong-.  This is often the case - most 
people thought that Y2K was the beginning of the new millennium, and 
that the time between 11:59 am and 12:01 pm is 12:00 pm (it's actually 
12:00 m - the "m" means "meridian", which is noon).  I argued this at 
the last Professional Military Education (PME) class I attended, and it 
wasn't any more popular there than it has been here - doesn't mean it 
isn't right, though.

> How many people use the primary meaning of "republic" as "a state without a
> king"?

I have no idea.  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: {FLAME} Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-08T19:34:10+00:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: {FLAME} Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-09T06:44:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vlrf92ocue2o66@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<bjkb79$2gc$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com> <vloul2pu2lhmb1@corp.supernews.com> <bjib0m$3s0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <vlpejr6g8b9ed@corp.supernews.com> <bjkb79$2gc$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> In article <vlpejr6g8b9ed@corp.supernews.com>,
> LX-i  <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> wishes to remain great so space and time -must- have fixed definitions'.

No, not a "stagnant" language.  Language is but one facet in the set of 
absolutes that must be present.  Without generation after generation 
adhering to the same absolutes, it's only a matter of time before the 
society begins to crumble.  Think of it like the foundation of a house - 
if it's wishy-washy, the house will fall.

Absolutes don't mean that -nothing- changes - but it does mean that the 
underlying principles do not.  Our own country is beginning to feel the 
effects of this.
```

## Thread: Using win32 API's to emulate OO programming... — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### Using win32 API's to emulate OO programming...

- **From:** KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM (KELLIE FITTON)
- **Date:** 2003-09-08T08:54:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41758a6b.0309080754.52c45ee@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello everyone,

can the use of win32 API's enables a procedural cobol code to
either emulate or behave & function as an OO programming?
looking forward to read your comments, Kellie.

***
```

#### ↳ Re: Using win32 API's to emulate OO programming...

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-08T17:26:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WY27b.1$ev2.270@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309080754.52c45ee@posting.google.com>`

```
"KELLIE FITTON" <KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM> wrote in message
news:41758a6b.0309080754.52c45ee@posting.google.com...
> Hello everyone,
>
> can the use of win32 API's enables a procedural cobol code to
> either emulate or behave & function as an OO programming?
> looking forward to read your comments, Kellie.

Not really, as "OO" is a function of the compiler, not the platform API.

The 'use of Win32 APIs' implies you are inserting lines like..

  CALL windows-function USING xxxxx, xxxx, xxxxx, xxxxx RETURNING yyyy

.. into your procedural code.  Still looks pretty procedural, huh?

While you "can" access some (much?) of the Windows API through the Component
Object Model (COM) interface used in "OO" programming, your source code
remains procedural. (And you'd best be _real_  comfy using the COBOL POINTER
and PROCEDURE-POINTER data types to even give this a shot.. because the
basic COM interface works by returning pointers to procedure-pointers).

If you want "OO programming " on your desktop,  you are best off using a
compiler designed to support "OO" development.
```

```
> Not really, as "OO" is a function of the compiler, not the platform API.
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> compiler designed to support "OO" development.


I admit to not knowing much about this but couldn't you develop an OO
COBOL app and "INVOKE windows-function USING xxxx xxxx RETURNING yyyy.
I mean really... what's the difference?
```

<a id="t-4b4b3c28bb"></a>
## Thread: Building Common Solutions — 2 msgs

### Building Common Solutions

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-09-08T22:08:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030908180803.16646.00000655@mb-m02.aol.com>`

```
I hereby abolish the thread "Re: Building a wall for division..."
And instantiate this new gizmo instead.

The idea is to discuss how COBOL and OOP, most probably Java, are going to
cohabit our business world.

There is a call for examples. Well they are hard to conjure only if you are
still in denial.

Take inventory systems in a major corporation that has a number of product
lines.There is politics in the divisions of the corp, and an inertia to the
distinct traditions of the markets the several divisions service.  

So certain things about basic inventory records are common, but some are unique
to specific divisions.  The common stuff goes in an inheritable super Class the
inheritors can be specific by adding entirely new methods and fields or doing
some overriding by same name.

Order processing may well reflect some of those division to division
differences, but Order processing can have its own way of making life mixed up
too. Some products, lets say, by company policy can be ordered as a single
order, others must be itemized distinctly, .. still others may allow for
distinct request dates within a single item (whereas that might be forbidden on
other product lines).  'Order' is common to all, 'Item' common to several, and
'date-request' applicable only to things in that 'class' (which would be called
final in oopese..

The examples simply abound. Just begin to look at all the stuff you already
know. If there is anything like a relational database or a hierarchical
database being used for what you know about. then class structures can
gravitate towards the same arrangement.

It is a certainty the folks who speak oopese are going to flood into the
business shops because that is the only thing the academic institutions are
'building.' This might well have been a gradual phenomemnon if we had had
continuous growth, but the slow sustained slow down means we are probably going
to have aburst of culture shock sometime soon. 

More generally, if there is anything in an app that you know that has data
arranged
1-to-n or n-to-m, it could be reasonable to apply OOD to future soultions for
those systems. 

The point of OOP tools is that they make the generation of programs efficient,
and infact
displace a tedious programming effort. It would be a shame to see the baby go
out with
the bath water.  OO tools are definitely an automation of the computer system
construction task.  It matters not if one resists.  Here it comes.

You want examples, we got examples. A bank has customers.  You only need one
set of manipulators for Customer.  The ATM account is a distinct class. The
Demand Deposit Account is a distinct class.  You'll have arguments trying to
inherit one form the other. You will have a food fight over single inheritance
versus multiple inheritance. Then someone will say Aspect Programming.  But you
are still in there flailing away withh your flow chart template to satiate you
flatlander COBOLese tendencies, and your personal hard copy JVM instruction set
list in the other to placate your urge to keep up with the Jones; being the
guru that you are, you know it will work ... and then someone steps up and says
"internet access to customer data and accounts, including actual fiduciaries
(transfers let's say) and establishment of repetitive payment authorizations".
Yeah, you say, we can inherit what we need, that's none of that silly GUI
stuff, we are just talking about the account methods and the customer methods.
We can fit that together!
Surely we don't want to rewrite all the account code, etc. We must be able to
glue it all together.

The best thing to build towards is an ability to THINK OBJECT.  You can not do
that
if you resist. If you are professional, an access point for you if you still
see this dimly, is that it is a very good idea to automate data processing. The
issue is not the efficiency of 
the machine, the issue is the efficiency of staff. 

Among the religions that are necessary to work in business DP are a belief in
structured code, and a belief in re-usable code. OO tools tend to force
structure into code, although it may actually tend to modularize too much.
Balance should be the goal. OO tools get people to think about code re-use from
the very start, which may well lead to more success in achieving the goal of
cost containment through code re-use. 

Best Wishes,
Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Building Common Solutions

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-09T12:25:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TEj7b.132091$0v4.9661965@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<20030908180803.16646.00000655@mb-m02.aol.com>`

```
I've read it, and I'll probably have more comments later (I agree with most
of it), but there is one issue I'd like to address now.

"RKRayhawk" <rkrayhawk@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20030908180803.16646.00000655@mb-m02.aol.com...
| I hereby abolish the thread "Re: Building a wall for division..."
| And instantiate this new gizmo instead.
<<sniped some "must read" comments>>

| The best thing to build towards is an ability to THINK OBJECT.  You can
not do
| that
| if you resist. If you are professional, an access point for you if you
still
| see this dimly, is that it is a very good idea to automate data
processing. The
| issue is not the efficiency of
| the machine, the issue is the efficiency of staff.

I agree in part. Programmer efficiency is a critical issue.
(My feeling is that we will move to system modeling tools that will generate
the source coding for us. In the mean time, I will design with code reuse in
mind. My definition of components differs from Mr. Dashwood's, but I do
build components which are easily plugged into new programs. They are not
OO.)

Efficiency of the machine is still important, and will be for at least a few
more years.

The newer machines are faster, and larger, and inefficiency can sometimes be
corrected by getting a faster, bigger machine.
But, there are still mission critical systems that must run as quickly as
possible.
Seconds aren't important, but minutes are.

When I do address efficiency problems, it is usually an I/O issue.
I expect that the I/O bottleneck will become less of an issue when we have
mammoth machines which keep frequently/recently accessed data in memory.
(IMHO. With more machines moving to 64 bit addressing, the mammoths are
coming to an installation near you soon. )
```

---

```
interested in the SHARE presentation available online at:


http://www.ibm.com/software/awdtools/cobol/demo/
```

#### ↳ Re: XML and (IBM) Enterprise COBOL

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-09-09T18:10:01+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f5d6fb7_6@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<HJa7b.1$_F6.0@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`

```
Fascinating, Bill.

It was impressive. First time I've seen the WebSphere development
environment. Good stuff.

One of the things on my "list" was to write an xml enabler that would do
exactly what this does.  The idea was to analyse COBOL Source and then
generate XML. (I already have tools that analyse COBOL Source and generate
Relational Database tables; XML is just another step...). I gave it away
when I found components that can do it, even though they are not COBOL
oriented.

It's a really good demo. Thanks for the link.

Pete.

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:HJa7b.1$_F6.0@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net...
> If you are interested in XML and an IBM z/OS COBOL environment, you might
be
> interested in the SHARE presentation available online at:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
> Fascinating, Bill.
>
> It was impressive. First time I've seen the WebSphere development
> environment. Good stuff.
It's based on eclipse which is a free download if you're interested.  I know
that there is an initiative to get a COBOL environment set up and Fujitsu
donated a lot of their code.   I don't know the status because I haven't
actively followed it. www.eclipse.org/cobol but I am more than willing to
take advantage of a free tool once it's there :-)

I've been lobbying to get this (WSED) setup at my office (setbacks include
the setup on the z/os side where they are unfamiliar with client side
development tools) and the fact that the client WSED is very heavy (512MB
required - which is still not enough, unfortunately).  We have machines on a
lease basis....*sigh*

The environment (WSED) is excellent though as you can develop your
java/xml/dads/ejbs/cobol/pli apps in one ide.  Eclipse is awesome as far as
free cross platform ide's go - and it's the base for IBM and Rational (which
is now IBM I guess) so it's pretty industry tested.

JCE
```

```
Tarun.
```

```
Also, if using a currently supported COBOL compiler on OS/390 (or z/OS), you
can tell "nesting" levels by looking at the LISTING - and use that (with
some multiplier, e.g. 2 or 4) and figure out how to indent "logically"
related lines.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL indentation - OS/VS Cobol

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-11T20:46:49-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vacnbtaub4WuPyiXTWQlg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3346333.1063083600@dbforums.com>`

```
If your shop happens to have the old IBM OS/VS Cobol compiler
lying around it had compile options to do just exactly what
your wanting. In fact this compiler as obsolete as it is had
a lot of useful stuff that later compilers do not.

If you use compile option FDECK along with LSTONLY or LSTCOMP
it will put to DD SYSPUNCH a reformatted source "deck" that
indents under the IF's , lines up the ELSE's, picture clauses,
level numbers and stuff, puts one verb on a line and so on.
It was a very handy little thing.

There are also options STATE and FLOW. These write debugging
information to DD SYSDBOUT. STATE prints the line of code that
abended and FLOW prints a trace of up to the previous 99
paragraphs names prior to the abend. Also, a handy little
thing.

This is probably useless information unless you have a really
old program. It likely would do nothing if it encountered
a "function" or an "end-if". Nevertheless, these were nice
features that I could still really use. I wish IBM would have
included them new compilers.
```

```
is in it?

 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

<a id="t-1919f9d15f"></a>
## Thread: Somewhat OT: Cross border data processing considerations. — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### Somewhat OT: Cross border data processing considerations.

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-09-09T11:58:12-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F5DEA84.AA895C3A@istar.ca>`

```
I originally posted this message to ibm-main@bama.ua.edu (can bee read
as newsgroup bit.listserv.ibm-main) in response to a thread on
outsourcing to India and other countries.  The only response I got was
from Canada agreeing with me and noting that the privacy laws are
already in effect for financial institutions.  Since this is an
international forum for application programmers, I would be interested
in comments on these issues, especially the one about test data and the
whole issue of confidentiality.

As a United States citizen living and contracting in Canada, in one
sense I would be on both sides of the fence since I might be
undercutting my fellow United States citizens while in turn being
undercut by those from other countries.  Rather than going into the
those issues there are some other issues that long term are of great
importance and being overlooked in the debate I have seen thus far. 
There are issues of balancing market and production, differing laws on
privacy, on intellectual rights and on contract rights.  The concerns
apply whether we are talking about work being outsourced from the United
States to India or India to the United States.  In the area of privacy,
European Union citizens might well be concerned about outsourcing to
United States because of the lack of privacy protection.  This
discussion is meant to stimulate thought and the examples will be given
in terms of United States and Canadian legal framework purely because
those are the areas with which I am the least unfamiliar.  

For people to decry IBM or Microsoft setting up major centers of
production in India is to ignore that India is a major market for both
companies and there is a corporate need and responsibility to have a
local presence.  In addition companies that broaden their base by
integrating various cultural backgrounds are increasing their abilities
to expand intellectually.  

When I note that the legal system in a foreign country is different and
that there may be unpleasant surprises, this is to note the obvious. 
People in the United States have been unpleasantly surprised by the
differences between the states.  The majority of the states of the US
and provinces of Canada have legal systems based on British common law
but at least one state - Louisiana and 1 province - Quebec have laws
based on the French legal system.  The interaction between national and
regional laws can be a minefield in many federal countries.  Of special
concern is the protection of intellectual rights.  This can be a
minefield in many countries including the United States.  While various
toy companies that outsourced their production to China have complained
about their products being copied, I suspect that this is not just a
first world/third world issue.  In addition, many courts are aggressive
about claiming jurisdiction over actions in foreign countries when
citizens or companies within the court's jurisdiction are involved.  The
United States and Spain both come to mind.  Even without this factor
agreement on what rules apply can be tricky.  In addition, with data or
information, it may be an unpleasant surprise in any country to find out
what information the government has access to.  This is equally true of
the United States exacerbated by the Patriot Acts as it is about any of
the other countries. 

Privacy may actually become murkier with call centers outsourced across
borders even if the data bases reside within the outsourcing country. 
Which country's privacy laws apply?  Does the country of the call center
have the right to access the data to enforce their laws?  Given the
cross border scams that are reputed to originate in Canada aimed at the
United States as reported by the Canadian Broadcasting Corporation and
the Toronto, Ontario Canada newspaper Globe and Mail, this again is not
just a first world/third world issue.  If software maintenance is
outsourced, how is the test data created?  If there is a production
problem is the relevant data anonymized before being given to fixing
company?  Can a system that deals with sensitive data be maintained
without the maintainers having access to sensitive data?  I remember an
excerpt from the hilarious appendix to the Westinghouse Fast Dump
Restore manual about their problem determination section getting a large
(then paper) dump of an abend which contained data regarding the sending
company's secret project on fillet of old gnu.  While the example was
stated that way to show the principle rather than identify specific
companies, the problem is real.  Abends may involve or expose sensitive
information.  The problem exists even if both the outsourcing company
and the company doing the work are in the same jurisdiction.  It gets
trickier when state or provincial borders are crossed and even more so
when national borders are involved.  Of course, if the data is for a
company that is operating in both jurisdictions anyway and is accessed
in those
jurisdictions (Visa cards anyone?), then this would be a non-issue. 
Note this may be a case where your data is safer in Canada or the
European Union.  A privacy activist is reported to have purchased the
social security numbers (default national identifiers in the United
States) of key legislators on a California legislative committee.  He
then published parts of those numbers (4 digits for each number if I
recall correctly) to point out the problem and make the point about lack
of protection to these legislators in a way that hit home.  The
legislators' response apparently is to have him investigated for trying
to intimidate the committee.  There will be privacy laws in effect
January 1, 2004 in Canada.  The safeguard and requirements are better
than those in effect in the United States.  However exceptions for
government access carved out by laws enacted in response to the New York
World Trade Center attacks makes data as vulnerable to quiet government
access as it would be in the United States. 

I believe that much of the outsourcing that has been done may have
exposed companies to unanticipated risks even if cross border issues
were not involved.  The risks are far less when you are just purchasing
hardware and software because you are not exposing company information
to outside parties. Of course with the call home capability in much of
today's hardware and software, there are potential security issues. 
Should the military establishment of India trust hardware and software
produced and maintained in the United States?  Given the history of the
relations between the two countries, I would be leery if I were in their
shoes and if I were a member of the United States military, I would have
an equal suspicion of software and hardware from India.

Outsourcing of anything increases risk because control is not as great. 
Doing it across borders increases the risk because the legal systems
differ and the responsibilities to governments differ.  Both parties
have a greater risk.  Just read the Wall Street Journal (major United
States business newspaper) for the perceived risks to business in some
states because of the court system.  For those who own shares in
companies, it might be worthwhile asking pointed questions of your
managements about these issues.  My personal belief is that much IT
outsourcing is because the communication between IT departments and even
between say systems and applications was poor or stilted anyway.  Of
course outsourcing in general may in part be due to many businesses
being divided up into little fiefdoms that don't work together well
anyway.
```

## Thread: Dead Horse(s) — 1 msgs

### Dead Horse(s)

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-09T15:13:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xfadnbk8CrPMqcOiXTWJhA@giganews.com>`

```
(From another newsgroup - "tx.guns")

Sometimes I feel like "Dead Horse"....

The tribal wisdom of the Dakota Indians, passed on from generation to
generation, says that when you discover that you are riding a dead horse,
the best strategy is to dismount.

Modern organizations (education, government, military, corporations, etc)
often employ a wide range of far more advanced strategies, such as:

1. Buying a stronger whip

2. Changing riders.

3. Threatening the horse with termination.

4. Appointing a committee to study the horse.

5. Arranging to visit other countries to see how others ride dead horses.

6. Lowering the standards so that dead horses can be included.

7. Reclassifying the dead horse as "living impaired."

8. Hiring outside contractors to ride the dead horse.

9. Harnessing several dead horses together to increase the speed.

10. Providing additional funding and/or training to increase the dead
horse's performance.

11. Doing a productivity study to see if lighter riders would improve the
dead horse's performance [this sounds like the place for dead riders].

12. Declaring that because the dead horse does not have to be fed, it is
less costly, carries lower overhead, and therefore contributes substantially
more to the bottom line than do some other horses.

13. Harnessing the dead horse in a six-horse team, thereby raising the dead
horse's average output to equal the other horses in the team.

14. Re-write the expected performance requirements for all horses

15. Promoting the dead horse to a supervisory position.
```

## Thread: IBM public "Product positioning" for IMS — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### IBM public "Product positioning" for IMS

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-10T03:56:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nhx7b.1114$ca3.227@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`

```
I thought the following excerpt from:
    http://www.ibm.com/isource/cgi-bin/goto?it=usa_annred&on=203-216

might be of interest to those in comp.lang.cobol and IBM-MAIN,

"Product positioning

IMS V9, exclusive to the z/OS platform, is complementary with DB2 and
WebSphere servers for database and transaction management. All of these are
strategic and continue to be enhanced.

IMS Database Manager utilizes hierarchical organization technology, whereas
DB2, Oracle, and desktop systems utilize relational technology. Hierarchical
and relational databases have each continued to grow with their specific
characteristics and different roles to play. Hierarchical is best used for
mission-critical work and for work that requires the utmost in performance.
Relational is best used for decision support.

Hierarchical databases can offer a significant performance edge over
relational databases when queries are known before hand. Query optimization
for relational databases ensures good performance where the query is not
known in advance. Each type is best at what it does. The products supporting
these technologies are being enhanced to address the different application
requirements and are continuing to more and more overlap in their
capabilities. However, the product originally designed for that capability
will inherently be the best.

Relational and hierarchical technology can work together for optimum
solutions. Users can efficiently store operational data in hierarchical
form, which can be accessed easily by their favorite relational decision
support tools, with minimal impact on the production hierarchical data. IMS
data can be accessed directly or propagated and replicated with relational
data for summarizing, enhancing, and mining. IBM provides standard
application interfaces for accessing IMS as well as other data. Both
relational and hierarchical IMS data can be most efficiently accessed,
together or independently, using the IMS Transaction Manager and WebSphere
servers.

IBM continues to invest in providing these complementary solutions. Note
that most IMS customers also have DB2.
IMS Transaction Manager and WebSphere Servers are both strategic application
managers and are being enhanced to take advantage of each other. They each
have inherently different characteristics. IMS is more efficient in
application management, data storage, and data access, but applies strict
rules for this access. WebSphere make it easier to serve the Web and
integrate data that may have been less defined in advance. Thus, they play
different roles in the enterprise. Customers are using both application
managers - WebSphere for newer Web-based applications, and IMS for more
mission-critical high performance/availability and low-cost/transaction
applications and data.

IMS and WebSphere products together have been providing tools to make this
combination an optimum environment. Using IMS Connect/Connector for Java,
WebSphere development tooling can develop Web applications that can serve
the Web and easily access existing/new mission-critical IMS applications.
Using JDBC and IMS Open Database Access, WebSphere applications can also
access IMS DB data directly.

IBM demonstrates its commitments to you by continuing to enhance IMS.
Current investments have:
    Substantially improved database availability
    Established open access to IMS transactions
    Extended Parallel Sysplex� systems support

IMS, the recognized database manager and transaction server in the industry,
is critical to your business."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu MySql connectivety

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-10T11:26:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vluk54scctqh93@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3f5f4480$0$64725@hades.is.co.za>`
- **References:** `<3f5eeb12$0$64720@hades.is.co.za> <3352310.1063195463@dbforums.com> <3f5f4480$0$64725@hades.is.co.za>`

```
Hopeful wrote:

> I will try your idea off storing the graphics elsewhere.
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> as this is the other place I used the BLOB type.

Look at the "text" datatype in MySQL.

http://www.mysql.com/doc/en/Column_types.html

You can also do this with multi-row inserts.  Add a "sequence number" 
field to your table, and store the text 255 characters at a time, in 
sequence.  It's a little more code to break it out and put it together, 
but it works great - we're doing something similar (except we're 
breaking on 6,000 characters, supporting a max of 100,000).

This may actually be more efficient than a large item anyway, as there 
is some significant overhead associated with the larger data types such 
as BLOB and TEXT.  And, you know that you won't be storing any more than 
necessary, within 255 characters.  (And, if you define them as VarChar, 
you won't be storing -any- extra data.)

Hope that helps...
```

```
>Hi
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>on this list may have already crossed this bridge.

I have not tried it, but I think you need to use the following
construct

INVOKE "Item"(a) OF "Fields" OF "Recordset" OF ad2 "AppendChunk" USING
Data

Step by step.
create ADO obj (name as ad2)
1- On the code select ad2 , right click for properties, select
recordset.
2- Select the result from 1, right click for properties, select fields
3- select the result from 2, right click for properties, select item
4- select the result from 3, righ click for methods, select
appendchunk

Hope it is clear.



>
>I also seem to be having difficulty figuring out how to populate a
>powercobol table control with data received from an ODBC select statement.
>Any pointers greatly appreciated.
Se on the ADO examples supplied with the compiler. There are 4 of
those.

>
>Nik

>



Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

### Extract alphanumeric value from Comp-3 filed

- **From:** binoy_nair <member30209@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-10T13:10:41-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3353863.1063213841@dbforums.com>`

```

Hi,

  I am using MVS cobol/390

  I have a field

        Var-a     pic s9(5)v99 comp-3

  getting value from  IMS DB

  This field contains both numeric and

  alphanumeric value like 'BR' 'SV'

  I need to check this field using INSPECT to

  find if it contains 'BR' or 'SV'

  But iam not able to move

  Var-a to a  PIC X(7)field

  (as inspect does not work properly on comp

   Field)

  Till now i have tried redefining var-a

  with var-a-x pic x(4) and then using inspect

  but inspect fails to work properly as var-a-x

  does not contain the complete extracted comp

  value.

  Thanks for any suggestion.

Regards

Brij
```

#### ↳ Re: Extract alphanumeric value from Comp-3 filed

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2003-09-10T18:54:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030910145402.03265.00000548@mb-m11.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3353863.1063213841@dbforums.com>`

```
Brij writes ...

>
>Hi,
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>  alphanumeric value like 'BR' 'SV'

If the field is defined as you say,
then you _cannot_ get alphanumerics
into it; so, var-a does _not_ contain
the alphanumerics, the field as it
comes from IMS may contain 
alphanumerics, but then it must not
be defined as packed-decimal.

Something doesn't add up in your
description.

>
>  I need to check this field using INSPECT to
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>  value.

No. inspect does not work because just
re-defining a field does not do any conversion.
The data is still in packed decimal.
>
>  Thanks for any suggestion.
>

OK. I suggest you start by being more careful
and precise in your specification.

If you are truly getting a field from IMS that
contains alphanumerics, then it must be
defined as such. Let's say you define the
field from IMS as

Var-a-x  pic x(7).

Then you can do inspect if Var-a-x to see
if contains 'BR' or 'SV' (are there any other
possibilities? what about upper- lower-case
issues?) If you have alpha characters, are
they always in the same location in the field?
The define the field with sub-fields.

Then you can move the numeric portion
to your packed decimal field.

That's about the best I can do without
getting a more accurate picture of what
you're doing.

Kind regards,



-Steve Comstock
800-993-9716
303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: Extract alphanumeric value from Comp-3 filed

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-10T22:12:50+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uetulvo82qcibfpvlhla6a7llonik9meoe@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3353863.1063213841@dbforums.com>`

```
On Wed, 10 Sep 2003 13:10:41 -0400 binoy_nair <member30209@dbforums.com>
wrote:

:>  I am using MVS cobol/390

:>  I have a field

:>        Var-a     pic s9(5)v99 comp-3

:>  getting value from  IMS DB

:>  This field contains both numeric and

:>  alphanumeric value like 'BR' 'SV'

:>  I need to check this field using INSPECT to

:>  find if it contains 'BR' or 'SV'

:>  But iam not able to move

:>  Var-a to a  PIC X(7)field

:>  (as inspect does not work properly on comp

:>   Field)

:>  Till now i have tried redefining var-a

:>  with var-a-x pic x(4) and then using inspect

:>  but inspect fails to work properly as var-a-x

:>  does not contain the complete extracted comp

:>  value.

If it is truly numeric, then it will never contain these strings.

If it did contain such strings, then the PIC X(4) overlay would have been the
correct solution - and it would contain the entire string as it is the exact
same size as the COMP-3 numeric item.

I suggest going back to whomever described the data to you and get a better
explanation of what they want.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Extract alphanumeric value from Comp-3 filed

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2003-09-11T18:04:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0309111704.7be88277@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3353863.1063213841@dbforums.com> <uetulvo82qcibfpvlhla6a7llonik9meoe@4ax.com>`

```
You can get invalid data into a comp-3 field (or any other) if the
data was moved or read into a field of a higher level that contains
the comp-3 field, e.g.:

01 garbage pic x(n) value 'QPND...123ACRS...@#...'
01 hi-lev.
   05 cmp-3-fld pic S9(n) comp-3.

move garbage to hi-lev

Check to verify that your data and the data definitions correspond.
You may have the wrong file or the wrong layout.
 
To move the comp-3 field without changing the format, you can redefine
it as pic x(n) then move the redefining field.

You can move the higher level as above: move hi-lev...

For completeness, if your numeric variable is in DISPLAY format you
can use reference modification in the move to avoid conversion. E.g.
move var-d(1:5)...

Regards, Jack. 

Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message news:<uetulvo82qcibfpvlhla6a7llonik9meoe@4ax.com>...
> On Wed, 10 Sep 2003 13:10:41 -0400 binoy_nair <member30209@dbforums.com>
> wrote:
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> explanation of what they want.
```

<a id="t-889b0a345c"></a>
## Thread: Marriage for convenience... — 11 msgs

### Marriage for convenience...

- **From:** KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM (KELLIE FITTON)
- **Date:** 2003-09-10T13:08:59-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41758a6b.0309101208.1e4907e6@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello everyone,

in OO programming, why multiple re-useability of code in the
superclass (parent-class) is only through the USE of inheritance?
thanks a lot for your help, Kellie.

***
```

#### ↳ Re: Marriage for convenience...

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-09-11T02:03:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030910220343.07744.00000532@mb-m17.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309101208.1e4907e6@posting.google.com>`

```
KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM  (KELLIE FITTON)
Date: 9/10/03 3:08 PM EST
Message-id: 

asks 
<<
in OO programming, why multiple re-useability of code in the
superclass (parent-class) is only through the USE of inheritance?
thanks a lot for your help, Kellie.
>> 

If there are those who have actually used COBOL object compilers, comments on
this would be great from the focus perspective of COBOL. Generally I think that
the answer is that there is no such limitation as the poster askes about..

You can get an object's features by inheritance or by aggregation. You do have
to be an object of class x to inherit that classes objects, but you can just
instantiate any object within your object.

You can 'use' code and data in these two fashions in object oriented
programming. 

The term code-reuse is fairly tired and worn out, but it relaly just means if
we plan ahead and design programs with good abstractions we can probably use
them in a number of places during the initial system deployment, and perhaps
use them again (invoke them as inherited features or aggregated features), in
new programs down the line in subsequent years.

Code re-use is one of the religions of modern data processing. It is not an
intrinsically OOD concept, it is more general.

The idea of the OOP tools is that they encourage analysts to plan ahead
somewhat differently than older tools do. The discussion can be about whether
that is true, and if so is it an improve manner of thinking.

Code re-use is hard. There are many factors that move against it, including
non-technical political issues in major corporations.

From the stand point of the programmer, inheritance is one way to get
capabilities of code that was previously written. Aggregation is another.
Importantly, as you seem to suggest in one of your prior posts, the flat
topology of APIs also is a way to get to previously written code.

That is, COBOL is an excellent model of how things will not be flatlander file
system OR object based systems. There will be a mix.  We will have flat files,
object files, and EXEC escaped APIs harnessed. It just keeps getting bigger, 
better AND badder.

We will have a mixture of object oriented programs and classically called user
code and
ever more APIs that become successful as part of middle ware products or OS
services.  

Inheritance is only part of the picture. Code re-use is a general goal to save
money in the long run by not re-writing code.  There is some religious belief
that if you design more abstractly the code is not only more likely to be
re-usable, but might even be more correct upon the initial system install. Even
if you believe in the virtue of
early abstraction in a project, it remains open to discussion as to how to use
the methodologies of OOD per se to make a real difference in the professional
work.


Kellie, do you use object orient technology at this time?


Best Wishes,
Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

```
No disrespect to anyone in clc.....however, I think that if you are
interested in OO and you want to learn about OO you are probably better off
going to a newsgroup that uses an OO
language...comp.lang.java/comp.lang.c++, I don't know much about
comp.object.  Not due to any level of competence, but due to a much larger
community interested in that subject.  You would probably get more out of
it.

If you are interested in OO as it relates to COBOL then this group would be
more useful.
If you are interested in OO as it relates to procedural code then this group
would be more useful.
If you are interested in OO as it relates to pancakes and microwaved coffee
then this group would be as useful as any other.

This group in general from what I can tell is much friendlier about
answering any questions that are generic like this.  I'm almost sure you
would get many rtfm replies in comp.lang.java and I think that if I were to
give advice it would be along the lines of....reading a book on OO.  If you
want to see how it done with Java go to the Sun tutorial...basically there
are many resources MUCH more effective than newsgroups when you are trying
to get to grips with a subject....I wouldn't learn history of Britain
1485-1603 by going to alt.history or alt.history.british and saying - what's
up with the Henry, why did he kill them chicks?  I might read a few books
first...

JCE
```

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-12T13:10:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iBj8b.136146$0v4.9964432@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309101208.1e4907e6@posting.google.com> <20030910220343.07744.00000532@mb-m17.aol.com>`

```

"RKRayhawk" <rkrayhawk@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20030910220343.07744.00000532@mb-m17.aol.com...
| KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM  (KELLIE FITTON)
| Date: 9/10/03 3:08 PM EST
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
| If there are those who have actually used COBOL object compilers, comments
on
| this would be great from the focus perspective of COBOL. Generally I think
that
| the answer is that there is no such limitation as the poster askes about..
|
| You can get an object's features by inheritance or by aggregation. You do
have
| to be an object of class x to inherit that classes objects, but you can
just
| instantiate any object within your object.
<<snip>>

Which brings up the issue of circular reference.
In another post, you mention the Bank/Customer relationship, and appear to
be using the Customer as the main object.
From what I have seen, most Bank processing is by Account Type (checking,
savings, loans, Credit Cards), and centralized Customer processing has been
an after thought.

I think that Objects developed from a Customer perspective may not be usable
from an Account Type perspective, and vice versa, due to the possibility of
creating a circular reference. i.e. Account Type includes Customer which
includes Account Type.

IMHO, Primitive Objects, those which contain no other Business Objects can
probably be used safely, but Compound Objects need to have the same
perspective.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Marriage for convenience...

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-09-13T01:27:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030912212756.06150.00001117@mb-m10.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<iBj8b.136146$0v4.9964432@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
It is best to keep turning the posts back towards the language COBOL, in
deference to the many viewers who want relevance. 

I know some COBOL, but my OOP experience is not COBOL.

I had the privilege of posting in several threads about object oriented
understanding of business requirements.  I think examples are everywhere, when
we relax and look around. The bank example were meant as common experience
examples.  Other posters around town call for examples that are not GUI, which
is legit, we need to go beyond kindergarden.

But I am not trying to post perfect examples totally thought out. There is not
room and, hey, maybe I am not the guy. It is just that there are many available
examplary applications of object thinking to common business requirements.

In "Harley" dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net 
Date: 9/12/03 8:10 AM EST
Message-id: <iBj8b.136146$0v4.9964432@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>

The always polite poster
was kind enought to comment 
<<

IMHO, Primitive Objects, those which contain no other Business Objects can
probably be used safely, but Compound Objects need to have the same
perspective.

>>

Well, we do disagree then.  I think that a compound object of a 'double entry'
in a journaling object is a good generic example that ought to apeal to many
viewers. 

This generalizes beyond the simplistic bank example where we may want a
customer, plus an account and sometimes two accounts if we are going to do a
transfer fiduciary.

In the general ledger we have not only two sided transactions all the time in
double entry book keeping, but there is really a list on each side.

Asset increase:
  add to equipment  18,000
  add to cash              2,000

Liability increase:
   add to loans we gotta pay  20,000

A single object, the balancedEntry might be abstracted.  This kind of thing
_might_ have object thinking applied to it.

The resulting journal could still be written as a flatlander sequential file
(where we are in append mode), or a SQL file. The incoming transactions,
likewise could be flat file technology; or online one at a time transactions
acquired by APIs in major software like CICS, IMS, or a socket kind of thing
(via an API off the shelf).  We can still apply object thinking to the core
process.. It is a choice.

If object thinking improves the design, lets use it. If the available OOP tools
help generate code economically, then lets use that.

Examples abound, really!  The only thing that makes them hard to see is
resistance. When we move from them to us, it gets easier.

The objectives are strictly practical. Save money! by reducing development cost
or increasing reliability of deployed systems.

We can abstract the idea of account. We can abstract the idea of working
several accounts at once (in a fiduciary sense) in a combined transaction (a
transfer, or a multi element 'double entry' journal action). We use the object
think if it helps clarify.  The goal in the begining of design is to figure it
all out as best we can before we code. OOD does help, according to some
practitioners.

Once we have design, some people believe that the automation of the data
processing development activity through OOP tools is a major cost containment:
their conventions might lead them to try to instatiate that notion in our minds
 as a singularity majorCostContainment.

You can like OOD, or not. And you can like OOP tools or not. But I am relaxed.
The examples are simply everywhere.

What will happen as the economy finally expands is that the vast majority of
folks coming through the front door will speak oopese. If we resist their
thinking we will probably miss opportunities to save money.

But it is all open to discussion, and everyone ought to be relaxed. 

Best Wishes,
Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Marriage for convenience...

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-13T16:17:46-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vm72c9od2591c7@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<iBj8b.136146$0v4.9964432@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309101208.1e4907e6@posting.google.com> <20030910220343.07744.00000532@mb-m17.aol.com> <iBj8b.136146$0v4.9964432@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Harley wrote:

> I think that Objects developed from a Customer perspective may not be usable
> from an Account Type perspective, and vice versa, due to the possibility of
> creating a circular reference. i.e. Account Type includes Customer which
> includes Account Type.

Who says you have to pick?  Have each instance of the customer object 
have an array of account object identifiers as properties, and each 
instance of the account object have a customer identifier as a property? 
  You're still object-oriented, but you've got a cross-reference via 
properties.  This makes it easy to process by account number OR by 
SSN/EIN (for example).
```

- **References:** `<3357002.1063275019@dbforums.com> <3358237.1063295505@dbforums.com>`

```
On Thu, 11 Sep 2003 11:51:45 -0400, H.Mezdar
<member22969@dbforums.com> wrote:

>
>my program open 17 files & when i run it more than 16 times
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>pls help me.
If it works for the root user then is most likely to do with
permissions.

Have a look at all directories and files used by the program, and make
sure that they have both "rw" for the files and "rwx" for the
directories that are part of the files path.




Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: unixware+cobol micorficus v3.1

- **From:** "Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-12T08:12:36+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bjrsmi$cnu$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<3357002.1063275019@dbforums.com> <3358237.1063295505@dbforums.com>`

```
You may need to check either your ulimit settings, or the kernel
configuration for the number of files you can open simultaneously. The root
login normally has any file limits set to 'unlimited', whereas regular login
accounts would be set to a fixed value.

I'd suggest referring to the OS documentation for more details on how to
configure this -- it's been a while since I did this on UnixWare.

Simon.
```

#### ↳ Re: unixware+cobol micorficus v3.1

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-09-11T11:52:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0309111052.2b785cb4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3357002.1063275019@dbforums.com>`

```
H.Mezdar <member22969@dbforums.com> wrote 

> have file status 9/41 on unixware,

9/41 is corrupt index file.  The file index needs to be rebuilt.
 
> the same program work well n openserver v5.0.5

Unixware and OpenServer are different product lines.  OpenServer is
derived from Xenix (originally Microsoft Xenix) which was a Unix
edition 7 (ie pre System III) licenced version.  UnixWare is AT&T Unix
via Novell to SCO.  While there has been convergence there are still
differences.

It may be that the locking mechanisms are different such that the
OpenServer run-time fails to lock records correctly for index updates
allowing two programs to mutually corrupt the file.

Or perhaps you copied the file while it was being used resulting in
corruptions.  That is when you copied the first part of the file the
last part was being updated and when it got to the last part the first
bit had been changed which was not reflected in your copy.
 
> can't open more than 286 files

Are you trying to open more than 286 ?
```

## Thread: DB2 to COBOL translation — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### DB2 to COBOL translation

- **From:** "Gonzo" <ckhamel1961.nospam@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-11T16:31:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%r18b.88$iT5.27@newssvr24.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Hi folks,

I was given a DB2 layout without the benefit of a DCLGEN translation, and
I've got field definition of DECIMAL(22,7). How would you change this to a
COBOL PIC? I'm working with COBOL OS/390, and according to the doc I have,
the rule is supposed to be:

DECIMAL(p,s)      ->     PIC S9(p-s)V9(s) COMP-3.

As I plug it in, I obviously get PIC S9(15)V9(7) COMP-3. My maximum length
for COMP-3 fields is 18 digit positions, and I bust that by 4 positions.

I was just going to pad the first two bytes as filler and handle the excess
in the procedure division, but I was hoping someone might have another idea
at hand.
```

#### ↳ Re: DB2 to COBOL translation

- **From:** "Don Leahy" <dleahy@rogers.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-12T16:19:20-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1Tp8b.4160$Nx6.626737@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<%r18b.88$iT5.27@newssvr24.news.prodigy.com>`

```
You could try SELECTing the column with the DIGITS() function, which will
convert it to character.  Then you can fetch the column into a PIC
X(whatever) field.

EXEC SQL
   SELECT DIGITS(DECIMAL_COL)
        FROM some_table
        INTO  :WS-CHAR-FIELD
END-EXEC

"Gonzo" <ckhamel1961.nospam@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:%r18b.88$iT5.27@newssvr24.news.prodigy.com...

excess

idea
> at hand.

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DB2 to COBOL translation

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-13T05:13:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0Ix8b.53489$Mb2.1745073@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<%r18b.88$iT5.27@newssvr24.news.prodigy.com> <1Tp8b.4160$Nx6.626737@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Ask an obvious question....does the data in the table actually reflect the
data definition?
If the fields are all 15,5 then you could do similar and try casting to a
DECIMAL(15,5) in the same manner as this DIGITS select only it leaves the
same value.

Of course if you are doing math with the numbers and they are big (ie.
adding) there is always the chance that you will still exceed the internal
storage anyway.  In this case the DIGITS scalar f. works well because you
can store the lod and rod separately....

It wouldn't be the first time that a DBA and application guy didn't talk.

JCE
```

```
EXEC SQL



SELECT DISTINCT dup_codigo,
```

## Thread: Fujitsu Cobol F3BIPRCT.DLL — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu Cobol F3BIPRCT.DLL

- **From:** johnfdutcher@yahoo.com (John Dutcher)
- **Date:** 2003-09-11T18:58:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9f50fd4d.0309111758.6930f38c@posting.google.com>`

```
Several responses have been made to the issue of receiving an error
message when trying to 'execute' a compiled and linked program using
the Fujitsu V3.0
included with Thane Hubbel's 'Teach Yourself Cobol in 24 Hours'. The
message notes the inability to find the F3BIPRCT.DLL and the
frequently suggested solution is to 'fix up' the PATH in autoexec.bat.
But what if the PATH is just exactly as it should be after the install
(mine is) ? If I use the Windows 'Start', 'Find' function...it finds
F3BIPRCT.DLL right where it is supposed to be....and right where the
PATH points (C:\FSC\PCOBOL32).
Is there any fix short of placing all source/obj code in the PCOBOL32
folder
which does let the execute take place ?
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol F3BIPRCT.DLL

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-09-14T11:22:48-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0309141022.268daca2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9f50fd4d.0309111758.6930f38c@posting.google.com>`

```
John,

I'll email you privately to get your autoexec.bat.  The problem with
the v3 installer is that it often times mangles the path beyond repair
- inserting a strange new line and line break.  Manually this can be
fixed.  The other issue I have seen is path entries with "spaces" and
extra semicolons.

Watch for my email.

johnfdutcher@yahoo.com (John Dutcher) wrote in message news:<9f50fd4d.0309111758.6930f38c@posting.google.com>...
> Several responses have been made to the issue of receiving an error
> message when trying to 'execute' a compiled and linked program using
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> which does let the execute take place ?
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol F3BIPRCT.DLL

- **From:** "N. Shamsundar" <shamsundar@uh.edu>
- **Date:** 2003-09-15T19:28:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bk5lh0$38mo$1@masala.cc.uh.edu>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<9f50fd4d.0309111758.6930f38c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9f50fd4d.0309111758.6930f38c@posting.google.com>`

```
I have had no problems running Fujitsu Cobol from the command line. When 
I wish to start a command line session working on a Cobol program, I run 
the following batch file (in W2K or WXP).

	S:\>type e:\lang\Cobol\PCOBOL32\pth.bat
	path e:\lang\cobol\pcobol32;%path%
	set lib=e:\lang\cobol\pcobol32;%lib%

N. Shamsundar
University of Houston
```

## Thread: Question: File Status - IBM QSAM — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Question: File Status - IBM QSAM

- **From:** "William Bub" <fathafluff@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-12T03:36:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sbb8b.52295$yG2.33756@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
   I've been programming in COBOL on an IBM Mainframe for about 5 years. I
have seen various ways of handling file errors for QSAM files (i.e. flat
files; sequential files), and I have been told various things regarding
possible errors.
   One thing that I have been told is that you should always check File
Status after an OPEN. I agree with that, since various IO or JCL errors can
cause a file to fail to open.
   But what about READ, WRITE, and CLOSE? I've seen programs which check
Status for all of these, and others which do not. I am told that it is not
necessary, since, if the file was opened successfully, any further error
except End of File (IO error, out of space, etc.) will abend the program,
and any check of Status will never occur.
   Again, I am *only* talking about QSAM files, reading or writing
sequentially. Any comments?
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2Ak8b.202$Aq2.40@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<sbb8b.52295$yG2.33756@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <dsh2mv878l6l45n2udlv7mthuqtnghqvuh@4ax.com> <3F61722E.E1D82F74@worldnet.att.net>`

```
It seems to me that the "basic question" is whether you want to place logic
in your program to "handle" situations that might occur for QSAM files that
cannot be checked by a <not> AT END or not.

If you WANT to "recover" in your own logic for other "errors" (conditions),
then use a file status key and/or a declarative to handle all "non-normal"
situations.  If you only want to ABEND when such situations arise, then do
NOT use any file status field or any declarative.

P.S.  Also make certain that you understand and set correctly the CBLQDA
run-time option.
```

#### ↳ Re: Question: File Status - IBM QSAM

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-09-12T08:16:53-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F61AB25.417C46E4@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<sbb8b.52295$yG2.33756@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
In response to the posting below, either specify the FILE STATUS option
on the SELECT statement and check all I-O because the run-time is
deferring handling to the program or don't specify FILE STATUS and let
the run-time abend the program on serious errors.  If you specify FILE
STATUS and don't check the result of a write, upon an out of space
condition the WRITE will fail and the program will continue normally.  A
lot of data can be put to the bit bucket this way without any
indication.

Clark Morris

William Bub wrote:
> 
>    I've been programming in COBOL on an IBM Mainframe for about 5 years. I
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> sequentially. Any comments?
```

#### ↳ Re: Question: File Status - IBM QSAM

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-12T11:27:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%4i8b.138901$3o3.9879824@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<sbb8b.52295$yG2.33756@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```

"William Bub" <fathafluff@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:sbb8b.52295$yG2.33756@twister.nyroc.rr.com...
|    I've been programming in COBOL on an IBM Mainframe for about 5 years. I
| have seen various ways of handling file errors for QSAM files (i.e. flat
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
| Status after an OPEN. I agree with that, since various IO or JCL errors
can
| cause a file to fail to open.

Once you include checking file status, you disable system error checking.
Leave the file status out, if you want the system to handle the errors.

|    But what about READ, WRITE, and CLOSE? I've seen programs which check
| Status for all of these, and others which do not.

Again, once file status is used, you MUST use it.

|I am told that it is not
| necessary, since, if the file was opened successfully, any further error
| except End of File (IO error, out of space, etc.) will abend the program,
| and any check of Status will never occur.

Not true, if you specify file status, the system will still recognize AT
END, and INVALID KEY if they are coded.

|    Again, I am *only* talking about QSAM files, reading or writing
| sequentially. Any comments?

The best thing to do is leave the file status out, and let the system handle
all errors.
```

#### ↳ Re: Question: File Status - IBM QSAM

- **From:** "Vince Coen" <Vince_Coen@f609.n257.z2.fidonet.org>
- **Date:** 2003-09-12T14:51:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1063378035@f609.n257.z2.fidonet.ftn>`
- **References:** `<sbb8b.52295$yG2.33756@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
Hello William!

12 Sep 03 03:36, William Bub wrote to All:

 WB>    One thing that I have been told is that you should always check
 WB> File Status after an OPEN. I agree with that, since various IO or JCL
 WB> errors can cause a file to fail to open.
 WB>    But what about READ, WRITE, and CLOSE? I've seen programs which
 WB> check Status for all of these, and others which do not. I am told that
 WB> it is not necessary, since, if the file was opened successfully, any
 WB> further error except End of File (IO error, out of space, etc.) will
 WB> abend the program, and any check of Status will never occur.
 WB>    Again, I am *only* talking about QSAM files, reading or writing
 WB> sequentially. Any comments?

I have always written checks for all IO. That way I control what the program 
or procedure will do and not the operating system.

There are instances where you can get a IO failure and need to perform a 
specific action and not a abend.

Vince
```

#### ↳ Re: Question: File Status - IBM QSAM

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-13T03:27:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<M8w8b.53326$Mb2.1727646@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<sbb8b.52295$yG2.33756@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
I always use an error declarative section for exception on input and output.

I give each file a file status and in the generic exception message handler
I write a list of each of the
filenames and the current file status.

I prefer this because you can set the return code to be a nice OPC catchable
number like 99 which flags in a job scheduler.  A true abend general freaks
out system people and creates an unecessary dump in any report management
system.

You can get the same information by not handling it but it looks amateurish
to just let it abend when it's simple enough to not allow it.

I would also follow any local standards....we had a contractor add FILE
STATUS at a time when they were not used to each of the programs they had to
update (don't know why)...unfortunately they didn't always check it and it
would abend in some strange places when the read would fail and not be
caught.

I have often had the same thoughts with a DB2 Close Cursor.  Typically this
is the last action and catching it makes no difference....what do you do
with an exception if you're just closing up the program anyway.

JCE




"William Bub" <fathafluff@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:sbb8b.52295$yG2.33756@twister.nyroc.rr.com...
>    I've been programming in COBOL on an IBM Mainframe for about 5 years. I
> have seen various ways of handling file errors for QSAM files (i.e. flat
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Status after an OPEN. I agree with that, since various IO or JCL errors
can
> cause a file to fail to open.
>    But what about READ, WRITE, and CLOSE? I've seen programs which check
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
"jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:M8w8b.53326$Mb2.1727646@twister.tampabay.rr.com...
| I always use an error declarative section for exception on input and
output.
|
| I give each file a file status and in the generic exception message
handler
| I write a list of each of the
| filenames and the current file status.
|
| I prefer this because you can set the return code to be a nice OPC
catchable
| number like 99 which flags in a job scheduler.  A true abend general
freaks
| out system people and creates an unecessary dump in any report management
| system.

Sometimes I WANT to freak them out.

|
| You can get the same information by not handling it but it looks
amateurish
| to just let it abend when it's simple enough to not allow it.

It's a matter of preference.
I prefer having the system check, at least it will handle things
consistently.
An untrapped error could be harder to fix, since the end result could be
erroneous data getting into the system.

|
| I would also follow any local standards....we had a contractor add FILE
| STATUS at a time when they were not used to each of the programs they had
to
| update (don't know why)...unfortunately they didn't always check it and it
| would abend in some strange places when the read would fail and not be
| caught.
|
| I have often had the same thoughts with a DB2 Close Cursor.  Typically
this
| is the last action and catching it makes no difference....what do you do
| with an exception if you're just closing up the program anyway.

Times change, now I can send an e-mail to have someone look into the problem
before it becomes something serious.
```

## Thread: Cobol humors... — 6 msgs

### Cobol humors...

- **From:** KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM (KELLIE FITTON)
- **Date:** 2003-09-12T07:54:51-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41758a6b.0309120654.d4b6c01@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello,

these humor were taken from another website for c++, i took the
liberty to replace c++ with OO.

"Cobol lets you shoot yourself in the foot rather easily. OO cobol
allows you to reuse the bullet!".

"Cobol gives you enough rope to hang yourself. OO cobol also gives you
the tree object to tie it to".

"Before OO cobol we had to code all of bugs by hand; now we inherit
them".
 
Q: How many OO cobol programmers does it take to change a lightbulb? 
A: None. You just send a change bulb message to the socket object! 

Q: How many OO cobol programmers does it take to change a light bulb? 
A: You're still thinking procedurally! A properly designed light bulb
object would inherit a change method from a generic light bulb class!

A surgeon, a civil engineer and a software engineer were chatting at a
bar. The discussion rolled around to whose profession was the oldest.
The surgeon said that his was, since in the book of Genesis, God
created Eve from one of Adam's ribs, and surly that involved surgery.
The civil engineer countered by saying that before God created man, he
created the heavens and the Earth from chaos, surly a feat of civil
engineering. The software engineer just smiled and said
"Where do you think the chaos came from?"

***
```

```
KELLIE FITTON wrote:
> 
> Hello,
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…

How about...

            THE COMMON BUSINESS ORIENTED GOLDILOCKS
            --- ------ -------- -------- ----------

IDENTIFICATION DIVISON.
PROGRAM ID.           A COBOL FABLE.
SECURITY.             INSECURE.
PROGRAMMER-ID.        ARTHUR SHAPIRO.
REMARKS.              SLIGHTLY MORE MANGLED VERSION OF ONE IN JAN., 1968
               DATAMATION.
DATE WRITTEN.         ONCE UPON A TIME.

ENVIRONMENT DIVISON.
CONFIGURATION SECTION.
OBJECT COMPUTER.      ANY MUSIC BOX, MEMORY SIZE 8X64 BYTES,
                      19 TAPE DRIVES, 11 DISK DRIVES, 1 GOLDILOCKS, 3 BEARS.
INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
FILE-CONTROL.
    SELECT TAPE DRIVES, ASSIGN THEM TO CREDITOR.
    SELECT DISK DRIVES.
    SELECT GOLDILOCKS, SELECT BEARS, ASSIGN TO ONE COTTAGE.
I-O CONTROL.
    APPLY RED TAPE TO TAPE DRIVES, APPLY BRAHMS RECORD TO DISK DRIVE,
    APPLY GOLDI, BEARS TO COTTAGE.
    DATA DIVISON.
FD GOLDI.
    LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
    VALUE OF IDENTIFACTION IS "GOLDILOCKS"
    DATA RECORD IS GOLDILOCKS.
01  GOLDILOCKS.
    02    HGT   SIZE IS 62 INS.
    02    WGT   SIZE IS 110 LBS.
    02    VITAL-STATS.
          03    B     38.
          03    W     24.
          03    H     36.
    02    RATING      100%.
FD  THREE-BEARS.
    LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
    VALUE OF IDENTIFICATION IS "BEARS"
    DATA RECORDS ARE DADDY-BEAR, MUMMY-BEAR, BABY-BEAR.
01  DADDY-BEAR.
    02    HGT   70 INS.
    02    WGT   750 LBS.
    02    COLOR-OF-EYES   BLOODSHOT.
    02    DISPOSITION     UNBEARABLE.
01  MUMMY-BEAR.
    02    HGT   65 INS.
    02    WGT   700 LBS.
    02    COLOR-OF-EYES   BLUE.
    02    DISPOSITION     BEARABLE.
01  BABY-BEAR.
    02    HGT   40 INS.
    02    DISPOSITION     INFANTILE.
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01 COTTAGE      PICTURE IS COZY.
    02    KITCHEN.
          03    TABLE     SIZE IS LARGE, VALUE IS 1.
          03    CHAIRS    SIZE IS MEDIUM, VALUE IS 3.
    02    PORRIDGE.
          03    KING-SIZE    OCCURS 1 TIME.
          03    QUEEN-SIZE   OCCURS 1 TIME.
          03    PRINCE-SIZE  OCCURS 1 TIME.
    02    DOOR  SIZE IS USUAL, VALUE IS OPEN.
    02    BEDROOM.
          03    BED.
                04 LARGE     OCCURS 1 TIME.
                04 MEDIUM    OCCURS 1 TIME.
                04 SMALL     OCCURS 1 TIME.
          03    WINDOW    SIZE IS SMALL, VALUE IS OPEN.
01  CORRECT-COTTAGE REDEFINES COTTAGE, VALUE IS SAME.
77 KING-SIZE-BED-SLEPT-IN    SIZE IS BIG, VALUE IS ROCK-BOTTOM.
77 QUEEN-SIZE-BED-SLEPT-IN   SIZE IS MEDIUM, VALUE IS DEPRESSED.
77 NO-PORRIDGE               SIZE IS SMALL, VALUE IS ZERO.
77 SIP                       SIZE IS LITTLE, VALUE IS "SSSLURP".
77 SLUMBERLAND               SIZE IS UNLIMITED, VALUE IS ZZZZZZZZZ.
CONSTANT SECTION.
01 COMMENT1     SIZE IS 36, VALUE IS "SOMEBODY HAS BEEN EATING MY PORRIDGE".
01 COMMENT2     SIZE IS 36, VALUE IS "SOMEBODY HAS BEEN SLEEPING IN MY BED".
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
FOREST SECTION.
START-OF-TALE.
    OPEN STORY. READ FOLLOWING.
FIRST-MOVE.
    MOVE GOLDILOCKS TO COTTAGE.
    IF DOOR IS CLOSED OR BEARS ARE GREATER THAN ZERO ALTER ENTER-GO3
          PROCEED TO HASTY-RETREAT.
ENTER-GOLDILOCKS.
    GO TO KITCHEN-SCENE.
KITCHEN-SCENE.
    IF PORRIDGE IS KING-SIZE, PERFORM TASTE-ROUTINE VARYING PORRIDGE-
          KING-SIZE BY 1 UNTIL PORRIDGE EQUALS PRINCE-SIZE
          OTHERWISE COMPUTE IF COTTAGE = CORRECT-COTTAGE GO TO BEDROOM-SCENE.
TASTE-ROUTINE.
    SUBTRACT SIP FROM PORRIDGE(KING-SIZE).
    SUBTRACT SIP FROM PORRIDGE(QUEEN-SIZE).
    SUBTRACT SIP FROM PORRIDGE(PRINCE-SIZE) GIVING NO-PORRIDGE.
BEDROOM-SCENE.
    MOVE GOLDILOCKS TO BEDROOM.
    ADD GOLDILOCKS TO BED(LARGE). DISPLAY "IT IS TOO HARD".
    SUBTRACT GOLDILOCKS FROM BED(LARGE) GIVING KING-SIZE-BED-SLEPT-IN.
    MOVE GOLDILOCKS TO BED(MEDIUM). DISPLAY "IT IS TOO SOFT".
    SUBTRACT GOLDILOCKS FROM BED(MEDIUM) GIVING OUEEN-SIZE-BED-SLEPT-IN.
    MOVE GOLDILOCKS TO BED(SMALL). DISPLAY "IT IS JUST RIGHT".
    ADD GOLDILOCKS TO SLUMBERLAND.
BEARS-RETURN.
    MOVE DADDY-BEAR, MUMMY-BEAR, BABY-BEAR TO KITCHEN.
    MOVE CORRESPONDING BEARS TO PORRIDGE.
    DISPLAY "DADDY BEAR ", COMMENT1.
    DISPLAY "MUMMY BEAR ", COMMENT1.
    DISPLAY "BABY BEAR ", COMMENT1, " AND EATEN IT ALL UP".
    MOVE BEARS TO BEDROOM.
BEARS-IN-BEDROOM.
    EXAMINE BEDS, REPLACING ALL GOLDILOCKS WITH BEARS.
    DISPLAY "DADDY BEAR ", COMMENT2.
    DISPLAY "MUMMY BEAR ", COMMENT2.
    DISPLAY "BABY BEAR ", COMMENT2, " AND HERE SHE IS".
HASTY-RETREAT.
    IF WINDOW IS OPEN EXIT GOLDILOCKS OTHERWISE MOVE GOLDILOCKS TO DOOR.
END-OF-TALE.
    CLOSE STORY, DISPLAY "WOULD YOU BELIEVE CINDERELLA IN PL/I?".
    STOP RUN.
```

```
      EXEC SQL

01 INPUT-VARIABLE       PIC S9(4) COMP-5.

01 OUTPUT-VARIABLE    PIC S9(4) COMP-5.

EXEC SQL END DECLARE SECTION END-EXEC.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.

     EXEC SQL CONNECT TO DEFAULT END-EXEC.

     MOVE 100 TO INPUT-VARIABLE.

     EXEC SQL

      CALL PROC (:INPUT-VARIABLE, :OUTPUT-VARIABLE)

    END-EXEC.

Carlos Lages
```

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-13T16:21:19-05:00

## Thread: REDEFINES confusion — 24 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### REDEFINES confusion

- **From:** clvrmnky <clvrmnky@coldmail.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2003-09-12T12:50:41-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BPm8b.69623$PD3.4724069@nnrp1.uunet.ca>`

```
I'm a little confused on the uses of REDEFINES.  I think I have a handle 
on what it does, but I'm unclear on where one would use it.  I know I 
have some code that might benefit it, as I have a record made up of 
aggregate similar information.

I've gone through Murach's and done a few web searches without finding 
an example that speaks to me.  Does anyone have a good reference or 
starting point for more typical uses of the REDEFINES clause, and how it 
might better my life?
```

#### ↳ Re: REDEFINES confusion

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-09-12T14:59:42-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mJo8b.6840$fC5.1362045@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<BPm8b.69623$PD3.4724069@nnrp1.uunet.ca>`

```
"clvrmnky" <clvrmnky@coldmail.com.invalid> wrote in message
news:BPm8b.69623$PD3.4724069@nnrp1.uunet.ca...
> I'm a little confused on the uses of REDEFINES.  I think I have a handle
> on what it does, but I'm unclear on where one would use it.  I know I
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It is really quite simple ... it allows you to define the same data in two
different layouts.

example .. I have two record layouts, completely differnt, that depend on
the first character of the record.  I describe it as so:

    01 record-layout.
        02 record-type            picture x.
            88 type-one            value 1.
            88 type-two            value 2.
        02 rest-of-record.
            03 data-name-one    picture x.
            03 data-name-two    picture x(50).
            03 data-name-three   picture x(10).
        02 second-definition redefines rest-of-record.
            03 data-name-four    picture x(25).
            03 data-name-five     picture x(25).
            03 data-name-six      picture x(11).

You will notice both definitions are the same size, though the second *may*
be shorter, it cannot be any longer.

You would place a record into "record-layout", then reference either the
first set of data names or the second, depending on the value of
record-type.

Donald
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: REDEFINES confusion

- **From:** clvrmnky <clvrmnky@coldmail.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2003-09-12T16:53:17-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1nq8b.69721$PD3.4725069@nnrp1.uunet.ca>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<mJo8b.6840$fC5.1362045@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<BPm8b.69623$PD3.4724069@nnrp1.uunet.ca> <mJo8b.6840$fC5.1362045@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
[example snipped]

I think I have a handle on the syntax, but so far all I've seen are 
contrived examples, except for one that shows a date stored as a long 
string, or redefined as month/day/year strings.

My main query is that I'm just not sure where I would use such a device 
in my own code.  I think I have a few likely candidates, but was hoping 
for further examples.  That is, not how, but why.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: REDEFINES confusion

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-12T21:04:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Wxq8b.2831$NM1.522@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<BPm8b.69623$PD3.4724069@nnrp1.uunet.ca> <mJo8b.6840$fC5.1362045@news20.bellglobal.com> <1nq8b.69721$PD3.4725069@nnrp1.uunet.ca>`

```
The WHY is when you want to "look at" storage in multiple ways.  Some
examples (that you may or may not think of as "contrived" - but which are
fairly COMMON in real-world "business" applications).

Example 1:
  Same field may be alphanumeric or numeric

  05  Alpha-Field   Pic X(5).
  05 Num-field redefines Alpha-Field   Pic 999V99.

Example 2
   Some "monetary" field is looked at as number of cents or dollars

  05 Dollar-field   Pic  9999V99.
  05 Cents-Field redefines Dollar-Field  Pic  9(6).

Example 3:
  You want to look at one-byte of a binary field

 05  Full-Binary   Pic S9(04) Binary.
 05  Filler redefines Full-Binary.
     10  Byte-One   Pic X.
     10  Byte-Two  Pic X.
```

```
constraints we had in the past.
```
