```
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. MANHAT.

       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
      * Arguments
       01  WS-TEST-COUNT            PIC 9999999       VALUE
100000.
       01  WS-INIT-YEAR             PIC 9999          VALUE
1626.
       01  WS-INIT-PRINCIPLE        PIC 9(12)V9999    VALUE
12.00.
       01  WS-INTEREST-RATE         PIC 9V9999        VALUE
0.04.
      * Computed initial conditions
       01  WS-MULTIPLIER            PIC 9V9999        VALUE
ZEROS.
      * State variables
       01  WS-TEST                  PIC 9999999       VALUE
ZEROS.
       01  WS-PRINCIPLE             PIC 9(12)V9999    VALUE
ZEROS.
       01  WS-YEAR                  PIC 9999          VALUE
ZEROS.
      * Statistics
       01  WS-ZERO-DATETIME.
           05  WS-ZERO-YEAR         PIC 9999          VALUE
ZEROS.
           05  WS-ZERO-MONTH        PIC 99            VALUE
ZEROS.
           05  WS-ZERO-DAY          PIC 99            VALUE
ZEROS.
           05  WS-ZERO-HOUR         PIC 99            VALUE
ZEROS.
           05  WS-ZERO-MINUTE       PIC 99            VALUE
ZEROS.
           05  WS-ZERO-SECOND       PIC 99            VALUE
ZEROS.
           05  WS-ZERO-HS           PIC 99            VALUE
ZEROS.
           05  WS-ZERO-ZONE         PIC XXXXX.
       01  WS-FINAL-DATETIME.
           05  WS-FINAL-YEAR        PIC 9999          VALUE
ZEROS.
           05  WS-FINAL-MONTH       PIC 99            VALUE
ZEROS.
           05  WS-FINAL-DAY         PIC 99            VALUE
ZEROS.
           05  WS-FINAL-HOUR        PIC 99            VALUE
ZEROS.
           05  WS-FINAL-MINUTE      PIC 99            VALUE
ZEROS.
           05  WS-FINAL-SECOND      PIC 99            VALUE
ZEROS.
           05  WS-FINAL-HS          PIC 99            VALUE
ZEROS.
           05  WS-FINAL-ZONE        PIC XXXXX.
       01  WS-ELAPSED               PIC S9(9)         VALUE
ZEROS.
      * Scratch variables
       01  WS-TIME-DIFF             PIC S9(9)         VALUE
ZEROS.
       01  WS-SOME-DATE             PIC 9(8).

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.

       MAIN.
      * Compute some initial conditions
           ADD 1.0 TO WS-INTEREST-RATE GIVING WS-MULTIPLIER.

      * Output initial conditions
           DISPLAY " ".
           DISPLAY "The Manhattan Benchmark".
           DISPLAY "  Test Count:          " WS-TEST-COUNT.
           DISPLAY "Init -------------------------".
           DISPLAY "  Year:                   " WS-INIT-YEAR.
           DISPLAY "  Principle: "      WS-INIT-PRINCIPLE.
           DISPLAY "  Rate:                 "
WS-INTEREST-RATE.

      * Start the clock
           MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE TO WS-ZERO-DATETIME.

           PERFORM ITERATE-TEST
               VARYING WS-TEST FROM 1 BY 1
               UNTIL WS-TEST > WS-TEST-COUNT.

      * Stop the clock
           MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE TO WS-FINAL-DATETIME.
           PERFORM COMPUTE-ELAPSED.

      * Kick results
           DISPLAY "Results ----------------------".
           DISPLAY "  Principle: "      WS-PRINCIPLE.
           DISPLAY "  Elapsed:           "      WS-ELAPSED.

           STOP RUN.

       ITERATE-TEST.
      * Run a test
           MOVE WS-INIT-PRINCIPLE TO WS-PRINCIPLE.
           PERFORM COMPOUND-INTEREST
               VARYING WS-YEAR FROM WS-INIT-YEAR BY 1
               UNTIL WS-YEAR >= WS-ZERO-YEAR.

       COMPOUND-INTEREST.
           MULTIPLY WS-MULTIPLIER BY WS-PRINCIPLE.
      *    DISPLAY WS-YEAR "  " WS-PRINCIPLE.

       COMPUTE-ELAPSED.
      * Determine elapsed time (in milliseconds)
           MOVE ZEROS TO WS-ELAPSED.

           MOVE WS-FINAL-DATETIME(1:8) TO WS-SOME-DATE.
           MOVE FUNCTION INTEGER-OF-DATE(WS-SOME-DATE)
               TO WS-TIME-DIFF.
           MOVE WS-TIME-DIFF TO WS-ELAPSED.

           MOVE WS-ZERO-DATETIME(1:8) TO WS-SOME-DATE.
           MOVE FUNCTION INTEGER-OF-DATE(WS-SOME-DATE)
               TO WS-TIME-DIFF.
           SUBTRACT WS-TIME-DIFF FROM WS-ELAPSED.
           MULTIPLY 86400000 BY WS-ELAPSED.

           SUBTRACT WS-ZERO-HOUR FROM WS-FINAL-HOUR
               GIVING WS-TIME-DIFF.
           MULTIPLY 3600000 BY WS-TIME-DIFF.
           ADD WS-TIME-DIFF TO WS-ELAPSED.

           SUBTRACT WS-ZERO-MINUTE FROM WS-FINAL-MINUTE
               GIVING WS-TIME-DIFF.
           MULTIPLY 60000 BY WS-TIME-DIFF.
           ADD WS-TIME-DIFF TO WS-ELAPSED.

           SUBTRACT WS-ZERO-SECOND FROM WS-FINAL-SECOND
               GIVING WS-TIME-DIFF.
           MULTIPLY 1000 BY WS-TIME-DIFF.
           ADD WS-TIME-DIFF TO WS-ELAPSED.

           SUBTRACT WS-ZERO-HS FROM WS-FINAL-HS
               GIVING WS-TIME-DIFF.
           MULTIPLY 10 BY WS-TIME-DIFF.
           ADD WS-TIME-DIFF TO WS-ELAPSED.

       END-OF-SOURCE.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Manhattan Benchmark (Visual Basic 6.0)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T13:42:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bl21a901ie@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<bkf4f3$ps$04$1@news.t-online.com> <20030919201153.22634.00001298@mb-m23.aol.com> <bkhcju$td3$06$1@news.t-online.com> <a1Xab.1038$Hd6.816465@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <MPG.19dd6bc111d08ff598969b@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <bl1v2702pjj@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```
This code (compiled) comes in at about 3.7 seconds:

Private Sub Main()
  'arguments
  Dim iTestCount As Long
  Dim iInitYear As Long
  Dim cInitPrinciple As Currency
  Dim dInterestRate As Double
  'computed initial conditions
  Dim dMultiplier As Double
  Dim iThisYear As Long
  'state variables
  Dim iTest As Long
  Dim cPrinciple As Currency
  Dim iYear As Long
  'statistics
  Dim fElapsed As Single

  'initial conditions
  iTestCount = 100000
  iInitYear = 1626
  cInitPrinciple = 12
  dInterestRate = 0.04

  'compute some initial conditions
  dMultiplier = 1 + dInterestRate
  iThisYear = Year(Now)

  'start the clock
  fElapsed = Timer()

  For iTest = 1 To iTestCount
    cPrinciple = cInitPrinciple
    For iYear = iInitYear To iThisYear - 1
      cPrinciple = cPrinciple * dMultiplier
    Next iYear
  Next iTest

  'stop the clock
  fElapsed = Timer() - fElapsed
  If fElapsed < 0 Then
    fElapsed = fElapsed + 86400
  End If

  'kick results
  MsgBox "Test Count: " & iTestCount & vbCrLf _
    & "Init --------------" & vbCrLf _
    & "Year: " & iInitYear & vbCrLf _
    & "Principle: " & Format$(cInitPrinciple, "0.0000") &
vbCrLf _
    & "Rate: " & Format$(dInterestRate, "0.0000") & vbCrLf _
    & "Results --------------" & vbCrLf _
    & "Principle: " & Format$(cPrinciple, "0.0000") & vbCrLf _
    & "Elapsed: " & Format$(1000 * fElapsed, "0") & vbCrLf, _
    vbInformation, "Manhattan Benchmark"
End Sub
```

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:P4ecnddLcbs9T-miXTWJhg@giganews.com...
> Grinder wrote:
> > "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in
message

> >> I am NOT great at understanding financial calculations,
but how
> >> about the solution discussed at:
> >

problem

> > environments.  Financial functions would be a better
solution

> > attention span holds up, I'll compare those as well.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> "Benchmark language "X" vs "Y" in processing 10000 records.
But each 80-byte
> record must be typed rather than read from a file in order to
remove the
> built-in file efficiency of language "X."

Sure, for given problem, there may be more efficent means
unique to each environment.  For compound interest
calculations, in fact, there are differing sets financial
functions for each of these four platforms that are undoubtedly
faster and better than the test code.

I'm not really sure (or if) you're saying something beyond
that.  Do you feel like this bit of code unfairly compares mbp
COBOL?

Although it's not very useful in predicting the efficiencies of
any application, this type of comparsion can show relative
rates for basic operations like looping, adding and such.
Processing 10000 records sounds like a good comparison in it's
own right.

Maybe it would make the tests better represent their intent by
just summing a number, without the pretense of calculating this
the return on an investment?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Benchmark Java vs. COBOL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-21T14:18:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Bqibb.152328$3o3.10887264@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<bkf4f3$ps$04$1@news.t-online.com> <20030919201153.22634.00001298@mb-m23.aol.com> <bkhcju$td3$06$1@news.t-online.com>`

```

"Gerd Nachtsheim" <gerdn@users.sourceforge.net> wrote in message
news:bkhcju$td3$06$1@news.t-online.com...
| RKRayhawk wrote:
|
|
| > But the real point is to build _down_ the walls of division. Meaningful
| > comparisons will have to move towards measuring interoperable
environments as
| > well. These issue are major. But comparison work is most useful if it
help
| > guide folks on how to use these wonderful tools _together_.
|
| Excellent point. If a simple proof-by-benchmarking says 'there is no
benefit
| performancewise in porting things from COBOL to Java'(or vice versa, who
knows?)
| everybody gets back on the track and stays focussed on the real issues of
an
| integration project.

As RKRayhawk pointed out in another post, machine performance may be a moot
point soon. Single iteration performance is often unmeasurable, and
meaningless, "batch" performance may have a measurable performance
difference, but is it significant enough to make a determination of
superiority (probably not).

You may want to focus your benchmark on Programmer Productivity (initial
development, and maintenance/enhancement), as that is where you will
probably find a cost/benefit justification.
```

#### ↳ Re: Benchmark Java vs. COBOL

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-19T21:36:23-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GL2cnZWStOW4IPaiU-KYjA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<bkeeqc$vgi$00$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Gerd Nachtsheim wrote:
> Dear COBOL community,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> has not been conducted yet.

Why do you ask this question and what decisions depend on the answer?

Visual Basic is probably faster than either COBOL or Java on string
operations and FORTRAN is probably faster in math (try computing the
hyperbolic arctangent of an angle in Java (or COBOL)). All four are,
however, generally left trying to button their fly when compared to
assembly.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Benchmark Java vs. COBOL

- **From:** Gerd Nachtsheim <gerdn@users.sourceforge.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-20T12:44:00+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bkhb1h$9ib$01$1@news.t-online.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<GL2cnZWStOW4IPaiU-KYjA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<bkeeqc$vgi$00$1@news.t-online.com> <GL2cnZWStOW4IPaiU-KYjA@giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:

> Gerd Nachtsheim wrote:
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>>and string operations?

> Why do you ask this question and what decisions depend on the answer?

Why do you ask this question and what decisions depend on the answer? ;)

There are too many flames and rants rather than facts crusing around the 
internet that let many discussions among developers drift into religious wars 
about programming languages. I *think* that there is not much difference between 
a Java programm and a COBOL program in terms of performance in areas like string 
operations and simple math. Yet I have to find visible evidence based on a 
serious benchmark.

In other words: if a decission 'integration vs. rewrite' is heavily influenced 
by performance considerations, don't you feel much better if you know that your 
decission is based on facts rather than on myths|legends|rants(choose your 
favourite one)?

Does that answer your question?

And: ...are you willing to answer mine as well? ;)

Gerd
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Benchmark Java vs. COBOL

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-09-20T20:06:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030920160608.07383.00001730@mb-m27.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<GL2cnZWStOW4IPaiU-KYjA@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" nospam@bisusa.com 
Date: 9/19/03 9:36 PM EST
Message-id: <GL2cnZWStOW4IPaiU-KYjA@giganews.com>

commented

<<
Visual Basic is probably faster than either COBOL or Java on string
operations and FORTRAN is probably faster in math (try computing the
hyperbolic arctangent of an angle in Java (or COBOL)). All four are,
however, generally left trying to button their fly when compared to
assembly.

>>

You can find many who would agree with your good statement. Yet there are some
bones to pick over there.  

Focusing tightly on Java v COBOL, I would think that any application that at
its core has atleast a two dimmensional array, might do better in COBOL than
Java, and the comparison progresses to better and better for COBOL as the
dimmensions of the arrays increase. 

So actuarial tables investment decision support apps, might find COBOL doing
better than Java.

Furthermore, an app that actually just wants to move a lot of text around, oh
you know, like a credit card app that wants to vary text for accept and denial
letters, might do better if it was an 8 bit code (EBCDIC or ASCII) instead of
the 16-bit unicode intrinsic to Java which is where some Java coders might take
it (they do have Byte, but I am not sure they have all the same methods for
Byte as for Char). 

I enjoy assembler, and think you can do many things fast in assembler, and so I
am basically in agreement with the poster as far as it goes, but then there is
that broken record of mine: the issue is not the efficiency of the machine, the
issue is the efficiency of the work force. Java and COBOL definitely automate
the programmer more than assembler. So efficiency must be defined in the terms
of the units you are trying to optimize. 

"JerryMouse" and the original poster Gerd Nachtsheim, are, I think, focused
mostly on the hardware. Which I feel has become so cheap that the entire notion
of a benchmark needs review!  But that's my stuck record getting in there.

On atanh I would comment that COBOL is acquiring more FUNCTIONs now and the
guts of one popular hyperbolic arc tan algorithm is a log function, if I
understand correctly (and being a business major this really thin ice, I invite
correcting comments on that ;-).
And can you not get atleast that much coded right off in modern COBOL, flat or
object? (the log function)


atan is already an available function in COBOL. If atanh were added, is that
the good?

Concerning COBOL FUNCTIONS, by the way ....

FUNCTIONS automate the programmers task, they do not have to write that much of
the code. So if you needed cos or atan you just use it (of course there is some
work; you have to lay down the data correctly in interface to it.  And if my
guess work is close, should you need atanh, maybe you could get there in just a
few statements, ... or relatively few because the log function is available.  

The point is we do not have to pay folks to code up the atan function. The
available functions are perhaps reliable, and hopefully somewhat optimized by
the vendors.

So are functions the good?

We could write atan, log, atanh, cos, min, max, 
factorial, annuity,.... the whole lot in our own 'procedural' code. For years
some inverstment firms did exactly that.  But now we have automated that much
of the work.

Should we take some posters' point of view (and I am going beyond the valued
expressions of the two folks mentioned so far), and just do everything with
procedural code, because as a matter of religious conviction that is the good?

I can take this a step further, and all of this is meant as sharing, to seek a
common ground of understanding, to build _down_ walls.

SORT is a function delivered since the early days as a verb.  It is overloaded.
 You can invoke it with just the SD named, you can invoke it with input and
outpur FDs. You can invoke it with procedure names (If this were Java, we would
call Sort a method, and the arguments in the case where we name paragraphs or
sections would be objects replete with input and output methods). 

Since SORT really is an object, and objects are not really useful, would it
then be best in some shops to disallow internal sorts? I mean after all we
could just do that with procedural code!

Isn't MERGE just a relative of SORT in a way that would be easy to explain in
OOP terms. Should the object like aspects of these nice language facilities
lead us to stop using them, and just go back to procedural code where the
varieties of MERGE statements are implemented by custom code in each shop?

So the nature of this industry situation is that if a radical arrived and said
lets add a verb AWK to COBOL. The standards committees would take atleast ten
years to make an official considered opinion.  A Java programmer could add a
rudimentary awk class to your shop in about ten minutes.  If they had
experience with Unix and not the junk most universities use, they would without
reference to manuals be able to set up several useful varieties of awk that
would be recognizable to others.

It is not only the programmers who are being automated. I dare say that the
fundamental change made with ALGOL that allows coders, business oriented or
scientifically oriented, to determine the vocabulary on the lexical surface is
finally blossoming. The work of the standards committes are getting automated,
as well.

I would think that a good entry in the test suite that would compare COBOL to
Java, is a program that behaved like awk in several fashions.  First,
itsy-bitsy command sequences might sneak in from the command line. Second, an
input file with commands could be used. Third, declared text constants within
the program could control the exercise. 

The idea of this latter is to get the awk into the COBOL comparand. Then to
press the illustration as far as possible that we do not need a standards
committee to approve AWK, we can establish sample code that might do it in
several styles flat, object (and if you think that it is different think, then
also one as a function oriented encoding).


Gerd Nachtsheim gerdn@users.sourceforge.net 
Date: 9/20/03 6:10 AM EST
Message-id: <bkhcju$td3$06$1@news.t-online.com>


wrote

<<
I'd like to get back to the simple things like implementing an everyday 
algorithm with a nested loop (with an array)or formatting strings. That is what

applications do most of the time in business logic (if they're not waiting for 
some I/O operation). Java folks often struggle with BCD vs. IEEE 7?? floating 
point issues. Another interesting area of comparison.

>>

Several of those notions are reasonably doable. 
As long as you are talking about display numerics you can arfully define a
comment target for COBOL and Java.  Once you have encoded it differently by
packing it or risking floating point representation, you get into the cultural
problems.  More than one person in 
'the valley' is brainless when it comes to decimal.  Funny how there ain't much
business code on the i chips ;-), but hey, those things are great with gui!

I have a question if any knowledgable reader happens to know the resource. IBM
added decimal(n,p) to is mainfram C.  Do they have decimal as instrinsic data
type in any of the mainframe Java tools?

If so, then Java/COBOL comparisons could be contemplated that involve bcd
representation on Big Blues platform(s).

Ultimately, if folks were to put serious time into establishing Java/COBOL
comparisons, IMHO you should consider modest SQL apps from the start, as part
of the suite. You are likely to find that much database technology as API
plug-ins for most of the development languages. And how well these important
facilities interoperate with a given language runtime is immediately relevant.

But I would keep the discussion broad, rather than permitting any too narrow
focus on the efficiency of the machine. The new tools allow managers to
marshall their resources differently, and if is hard to place a metric on this.
It has always been hard to measure alternate strategies as to the long-term
maintenance costs as well. The new tools purport to improve long-term
maintenance (yet it is not getting easier to be _objective_ about that
long-term assessment).

Benchmarks could remove some of the subjectivity to all this, and our I-M-H-O
keys could get a rest.

Best Wishes,
Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Benchmark Java vs. COBOL

- **From:** hebisch@math.uni.wroc.pl (Waldek Hebisch)
- **Date:** 2003-09-21T16:53:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bkkl23$alf$1@panorama.wcss.wroc.pl>`
- **References:** `<bkeeqc$vgi$00$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Gerd Nachtsheim (gerdn@users.sourceforge.net) wrote:
: Dear COBOL community,

: No, I am not anticipating, expecting anything. No flame, no rant, just a 
: question: I wonder if there is a benchmark that compares performance between 
: COBOL and Java in common areas like simple math and string operations?

: I googled and visited the 'usual suspect' COBOL sites but I didn't find anything 
: useful. I'd be surprised to hear that such benchmark has not been conducted yet.

I can not point you to "ready made" comparison. As other posters wrote, 
it is extremaly hard to write large "equivalent" programs in two different
languages. But one can gain a lot of insight from micro-benchmarks. 
You shold look at:

http://www.bagley.org/~doug/shootout

At that site Doug Bagley presents a number of simple benchmarks and
compares many different languages. Cobol is not included in comparison
(Java is) but it should be easy for a competent Cobol programmer to
implement most of them. After that you can compare results with Java 
version from Bagley's site. AFAIK few benchmarks require features absent
in Cobol (but I am not a Cobol programmer). 
In general Bagly's results show that on Linux Java was (the results are
almost two years old) slow. 

You can also look at:

http://www2.hursley.ibm.com/decimal/telco.html

where you will find a specification for a "bussines oriented" benchmark
and Java and C implemention (the C version is incomplete, it needs extra
library). If you are interested I can provide you with another 
hand-optimised C version for additional comparison.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Benchmark Java vs. COBOL

- **From:** Gerd Nachtsheim <gerdn@users.sourceforge.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-22T01:32:56+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bklbgq$lu4$01$1@news.t-online.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<bkkl23$alf$1@panorama.wcss.wroc.pl>`
- **References:** `<bkeeqc$vgi$00$1@news.t-online.com> <bkkl23$alf$1@panorama.wcss.wroc.pl>`

```
Waldek Hebisch wrote:

> I can not point you to "ready made" comparison. As other posters wrote, 
> it is extremaly hard to write large "equivalent" programs in two different
> languages. But one can gain a lot of insight from micro-benchmarks. 

I fully understand that there is no easy answer to this, however we find 
these benchmarks for other languages and I still see no reason why there 
shouldn't be a COBOL branch, too.

> http://www.bagley.org/~doug/shootout
> http://www2.hursley.ibm.com/decimal/telco.html

That is the kind of information I was looking for. I think I won't find 
any benchmark of the requested kind that has been released to the public.

It should not be too difficult to get COBOL equivalents to those tests.


Thank you very much, Waldek

Gerd
```

## Thread: CICS ADDRESSABILITY TO DFHEIBLK IN DYNMIC CALL (NOT LINKED) PGM — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### CICS ADDRESSABILITY TO DFHEIBLK IN DYNMIC CALL (NOT LINKED) PGM

- **From:** millsjay@aol.com (Jay Mills)
- **Date:** 2003-09-19T06:08:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5597693c.0309190508.60f4f5f1@posting.google.com>`

```
Is there a method of establishing addressability to the DFHEIBLK
without passing it in the CALL with USING DFHEIBLK.

Here is the problem

LEVEL 1 CICS

PROGRAM-A SEND A RECIEVE MAP

DYNAMICALLY CALLS PROGRAM-B WHICH DOES

MOVE DFHVALUE(UCTRAN) TO WS00-STATUS              
EXEC CICS                                         
     SET TERMINAL(EIBTRMID) UCTRANST(WS00-STATUS) 
END-EXEC

If I CALL PROGRAM-B USING DFHEIBLK there is no problem.  If I CALL
WITHOUT ANY USING I get the shown below.  However, due to vendor code
restrictions, I would like to CALL without the USING DFHEIBLK.  Is
there a solution (INQUIRE COMMAND DOES NOT WORK EITHER)using
ENTERPRISE COBOL?

 AEIK

    Explanation: TERMIDERR condition not handled.

    This is one of a number of abends issued by the EXEC interface
program.
    Because of their similar characteristics these abends are
described as a
    group.

    See the description of abend AEIA for further details.

    Module: DFHEIP
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS ADDRESSABILITY TO DFHEIBLK IN DYNMIC CALL (NOT LINKED) PGM

- **From:** godch01 <member30532@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-19T11:00:50-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3390905.1063983650@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<5597693c.0309190508.60f4f5f1@posting.google.com>`

```

Have you tried

EXEC CICS ADDRESS EIB(pointer-variable) END-EXEC

in the called program.
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS ADDRESSABILITY TO DFHEIBLK IN DYNMIC CALL (NOT LINKED) PGM

- **From:** millsjay@aol.com (Jay Mills)
- **Date:** 2003-09-19T13:03:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5597693c.0309191203.57e9e7e0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<5597693c.0309190508.60f4f5f1@posting.google.com>`

```
The answer is:

EXEC CICS ADDRESS EIB(ADDRESS OF DFHEIBLK) END-EXEC

millsjay@aol.com (Jay Mills) wrote in message news:<5597693c.0309190508.60f4f5f1@posting.google.com>...
> Is there a method of establishing addressability to the DFHEIBLK
> without passing it in the CALL with USING DFHEIBLK.
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>     Module: DFHEIP
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS ADDRESSABILITY TO DFHEIBLK IN DYNMIC CALL (NOT LINKED) PGM

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-19T22:07:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<U6Lab.39596$NM1.21303@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<5597693c.0309190508.60f4f5f1@posting.google.com>`

```
Thou SHALT pass the DFHEIBLK in a call to a separately compiled program.
See:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/3.1.1.4

which says, in part,

"If you are calling a separately compiled COBOL program that was processed
with either the separate CICS translator or the integrated CICS translator,
you must pass DFHEIBLK and DFHCOMMAREA as the first two parameters in the
CALL statement."
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS ADDRESSABILITY TO DFHEIBLK IN DYNMIC CALL (NOT LINKED) PGM

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-22T08:41:41-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-62DF9A.08414022092003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<5597693c.0309190508.60f4f5f1@posting.google.com>`

```
For your first statement in the CALLed program, address the EIBLK and 
COMMAREA, e.g:

   EXEC CICS Address
      EIBLK( Address of DFHEIBLK )
      COMM( Address of DFHCOMMAREA )
   END-EXEC

That will allow you to operate with either style of parameter list as 
you prefer.




In article <5597693c.0309190508.60f4f5f1@posting.google.com>,
 millsjay@aol.com (Jay Mills) wrote:

> Is there a method of establishing addressability to the DFHEIBLK
> without passing it in the CALL with USING DFHEIBLK.
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>     Module: DFHEIP
```

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Net Express and web programming

- **From:** Alain Feler <member15854@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-19T11:27:55-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3390919.1063985275@dbforums.com>`

```

My shop is beginning to use Net Express 3.1 for web programming (CGI).

The product is quite nice, but during the debugging of the CGI progs
with their HTML pages, we experience many aborts of Net Express itself.

We get messages like "Unexpected error - Access Violation at ... some
memory address", with the option Continue / Exit, and of Course Continue
won't work.

Once a week, we could endure it, but once an hour is a bit too much.

We are jut beginning with the product, so we probably make BIG mistakes,
but we would expect more idiotproofness of a product of that price...

Could anybody using Net Express 3.1 swear here that it will work as it
does - in web mode - in his own company ? It would put some balm on our
wounds ....

Thanks in advance.



PS. We can't use NE 4.0 because we need also Server Express.
```

_Thread started [2003-03](2003-03.md#t-7ab4e12491)._

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-22T18:40:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cmHbb.5822$ev2.2673636@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<YlHab.1893$Sv4.1428513@newsserver.ip.pt> <TvKdncjx1ZvDz_aiXTWJig@giganews.com> <217e491a.0309221031.42a1c313@posting.google.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0309221031.42a1c313@posting.google.com...
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Are you suggesting that he write his own ODBC driver in C ?

I think he's suggesting using an "FJ files API" rather than the ODBC API...

.. and why not? Not that I know of any such API, but it's not unreasonable
that one exists...

In fact, it MUST exist.. because it only makes sense that a "files access"
API is part of the FJ run time... the question is, is the FJ file access
run-time available as a standalone and separately-licensed product? (No, I
don't know).

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol Files & ODBC

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-09-22T15:37:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0309221437.3e7f87a8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<YlHab.1893$Sv4.1428513@newsserver.ip.pt> <TvKdncjx1ZvDz_aiXTWJig@giganews.com> <217e491a.0309221031.42a1c313@posting.google.com> <cmHbb.5822$ev2.2673636@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote 

> > Are you suggesting that he write his own ODBC driver in C ?
> 
> I think he's suggesting using an "FJ files API" rather than the ODBC API...

Which is only useful if he is writing the program that is to use the
access to the Fujitsu files.

If he is using an existing application that can use ODBC to access
data (and does not currently use the FJ API), such as Excel, then he
would have to rewrite that in order to use the 'FJ files API'.

So it seems to be: 'don't bother buying an ODBC driver, just write
your own version os Excel (or whatever)'.
```

```
Richard wrote:
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Are you suggesting that he write his own ODBC driver in C ?

No. It's apparant that while FJ's ISAM routines do involve a database, they
are neither "Open" nor "Connective." I assumed he wanted to read FJ's ISAM
from some other process and I was attempting to provide a starting place for
the underlying question.

He's since said he wants access from Crystal Reports and such. I'm afraid
he's out of luck.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol Files & ODBC

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-09-22T20:00:50-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0309221900.57f9d8e3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<YlHab.1893$Sv4.1428513@newsserver.ip.pt> <TvKdncjx1ZvDz_aiXTWJig@giganews.com> <217e491a.0309221031.42a1c313@posting.google.com> <vKGdnQRVb4WO8fKiU-KYiQ@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote

> He's since said he wants access from Crystal Reports and such. I'm afraid
> he's out of luck.

Not necessarily.  From the Version 4 'features.htm' file:

"""V4 include technology that gives ODBC access to COBOL indexed data
files, thus giving a route for Crystal Reports to access COBOL indexed
data files.

You can use the benefits of Crystal Reports to provide reports on your
COBOL indexed data files."""

Fujitsu COBOL Version 3 CD (but _not_ the free download) included
Crystal Reports Professional version 4.5 for both Windows 3.1 and
Windows 95.

Version 4 included Crystal Reports Professional version 6 for Win32.

It may depend on what version of Fujitsu he has.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol Files & ODBC

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-09-22T22:07:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0309222107.1985d4f4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<YlHab.1893$Sv4.1428513@newsserver.ip.pt> <TvKdncjx1ZvDz_aiXTWJig@giganews.com> <217e491a.0309221031.42a1c313@posting.google.com> <vKGdnQRVb4WO8fKiU-KYiQ@giganews.com> <217e491a.0309221900.57f9d8e3@posting.google.com>`

```
NOTE: V5 made the v4 file system obsolete - changes were made - and
the ODBC drivers you mention were never updated.  There's little
demand.  Since Fujitsu can use embedded SQL and ODBC itself - if you
have a NEED for ODBC accessible data - don't use the Fujitsu file
system, use ODBC via Fujitsu and use the RDB of your liking.
```

<a id="t-258a1b5e15"></a>
## Thread: Move Problem — 14 msgs

### Move Problem

- **From:** arnb <arnbRemoveThis@Arnb.org>
- **Date:** 2003-09-21T14:47:52-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bkkrop$2p16t$1@ID-128292.news.uni-berlin.de>`

```
Using IBM COBOL VSE I executed code below (simplified from the real program) 
and had 5 end up in byte 1 of Field2. Is this correct?

I thought it would truncate the data move to field 1 and leave field2 alone no 
matter what length I coded in the move. I know if the to length was not included 
it would truncate, however since I must subsequently string field1 and field2 
delimiting by high values and insert tab characters, this was not an option in 
this situation.

77 datalen        pic s9(8) comp.
01 data-fields.
     03  field1    pic x(4) value high-value.
     03  field2    pic x(10) value high-value.
	
01  From-edited   pic zzzzzz9
01  From-data redefines from-edited pic x(7).


move a-number(assume the value is 65) to from-edited

Initialize tally

Inspect from-data tallying tally for leading spaces

Compute datalen = length of from-data - tally  (datalen = 2)

Move from-data (tally + 1:) to field1 (4:datalen)
```

#### ↳ Re: Move Problem

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-21T20:43:01+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u2vrmvcp0om26el62i2ao455i1ummq59j2@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<bkkrop$2p16t$1@ID-128292.news.uni-berlin.de>`

```
On Sun, 21 Sep 2003 14:47:52 -0400, arnb <arnbRemoveThis@Arnb.org>
wrote:

>Using IBM COBOL VSE I executed code below (simplified from the real program) 
>and had 5 end up in byte 1 of Field2. Is this correct?
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>Move from-data (tally + 1:) to field1 (4:datalen)
I would say that the way you have it here you may have defined it
wrong, and should really raise an error on the move.

I think the above move should be
 Move from-data (tally + 1:) to field1 (1:datalen)


As for having a "5" this is correct as you used reference modification
to do the move, and as such the move was considered a "elementary
field" to "alphanumeric", which basically means that the field is
treated as "numeric" on this case, thus only the rightmost digit(s)
are moved to the destination field if this one has a smaller lenght
than the sending field.


search www.google.com for the following
group:comp.lang.cobol "Another contest"

for a probably better explanation of the above.





Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Move Problem

- **From:** arnb <arnbRemoveThis@Arnb.org>
- **Date:** 2003-09-22T00:17:08-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bklt45$35fl1$1@ID-128292.news.uni-berlin.de>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<u2vrmvcp0om26el62i2ao455i1ummq59j2@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<bkkrop$2p16t$1@ID-128292.news.uni-berlin.de> <u2vrmvcp0om26el62i2ao455i1ummq59j2@4ax.com>`

```
On or about 9/21/2003 3:43 PM, it came to pass that Frederico Fonseca wrote:


 >> Move from-data (tally + 1:) to field1 (4:datalen)
> I would say that the way you have it here you may have defined it
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>  Move from-data (tally + 1:) to field1 (1:datalen)
It was originally moved to byte 1, but the recipient, who defined the format and 
field lengths of the data, asked me to put some additional data in bytes 1-3. I 
did not notice field1 was too short since the resulting output, which is 
generated by a string delimiting each field by high values and is tab delimited, 
  has most of the fields defined larger than they need to be.
> 
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> than the sending field.
I don't agree with this, it was an alphanumeric to alphanumeric move that should 
have truncated.

 From the IBM manual regarding reference modification
length
Must be an arithmetic expression.
The sum of leftmost-character-position and length minus the value one must be 
less than or equal to the number of characters in data-name-1.
> 
> 
> search www.google.com for the following
> group:comp.lang.cobol "Another contest"
I guess this is saying it's a compiler, well actually in this case an execution 
error.

> 
> for a probably better explanation of the above.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Move Problem

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-22T11:36:51+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<16ktmvcgfar7clqp4srs3q9qj4ec46gdq3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<bkkrop$2p16t$1@ID-128292.news.uni-berlin.de> <u2vrmvcp0om26el62i2ao455i1ummq59j2@4ax.com> <bklt45$35fl1$1@ID-128292.news.uni-berlin.de>`

```
On Mon, 22 Sep 2003 00:17:08 -0400, arnb <arnbRemoveThis@Arnb.org>
wrote:

>On or about 9/21/2003 3:43 PM, it came to pass that Frederico Fonseca wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>  has most of the fields defined larger than they need to be.
Happens to all of us.

>> 
>> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>have truncated.
Yes I was wrong. I was thinking on something different.
But it is only an alpha to an alpha because the destination is a
alpha. Had it been an numeric field then it would be a move from a
numeric to a numeric, and the rules would apply as such.

>
> From the IBM manual regarding reference modification
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>less than or equal to the number of characters in data-name-1.
I didn't say this was wrong. The fact, as pointed out on another reply
is that you are trying to move 2 bytes to the (last position of a
field + 1). This should raise an error if you were using the correct
compiler options.
Because you aren't the compiler is just copying the second byte to the
next position available on memory. We saw this behaviour on another
thread related to occurs.

Just out of curiosity would you be able to test this move on a
variable that is defined as the last one on Working-storage, and
feedback the result?




Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-21T18:14:42-05:00

```
arnb wrote:
> Using IBM COBOL VSE I executed code below (simplified from the real
> program) and had 5 end up in byte 1 of Field2. Is this correct?
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> Move from-data (tally + 1:) to field1 (4:datalen)

----------

Given the example above, there are several possibilities for the symptom you
are seeing:
1. Magic,
2. Bit Rot,
3. A hit by a stray neutrino, or
4. Undiagnosed dyslexia.

Significantly less probable than the above is a blunder of ghastly
proportions, heretofore undiscovered, of the IBM compiler.

Move (anything) to (anywhere) does not put a "5" somewhere else.

I suspect something go to "field1" before you did.
```

#### ↳ Re: Move Problem

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2003-09-21T19:09:19-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0309211809.16edf0f0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bkkrop$2p16t$1@ID-128292.news.uni-berlin.de>`

```
Hi Arn,

One way that might happen is if the receiving field of your last move
is
"data-fields", the 01 level.

What you have should produce a run time error of some sort, because
you're trying to reference data outside its range, i.e. 4:2, bytes 4 &
5 of a 4 byte field. That's a no-no.

In the COBOLII and later versions running on an MVS class OS, the
SSRANGE compiler option could be used to trap the error and provide
some diagnostic help. Without SSRANGE I can't remember what the system
does.

Regards, Jack. 


arnb <arnbRemoveThis@Arnb.org> wrote in message news:<bkkrop$2p16t$1@ID-128292.news.uni-berlin.de>...
> Using IBM COBOL VSE I executed code below (simplified from the real program) 
> and had 5 end up in byte 1 of Field2. Is this correct?
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> Move from-data (tally + 1:) to field1 (4:datalen)
```

```
subscript out of range.

Does SSRANGE also monitor data reference modification errors?.

Arn
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Move Problem

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-22T04:18:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wKubb.51011$Aq2.38878@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<bkkrop$2p16t$1@ID-128292.news.uni-berlin.de> <8a2d426e.0309211809.16edf0f0@posting.google.com> <bklrtr$35dif$1@ID-128292.news.uni-berlin.de>`

```
"arnb" <arnbRemoveThis@Arnb.org> wrote in message news:bklrtr$35dif$1@ID-128292.news.uni-berlin.de...
> On or about 9/21/2003 10:09 PM, it came to pass that Jack Sleight wrote:
> 
<snip>
> You may be right, however, we run with SSRANGE off, that means no checking of 
> subscripts during execution, with it on an error message is produced on a 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

From:
  http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/2.4.48 

"Use SSRANGE to generate code that checks if subscripts (including ALL subscripts) or indexes try to reference an area outside the region of the table. Each subscript or index is not individually checked for validity; rather, the effective address is checked to ensure that it does not cause a reference outside the region of the table. Variable-length items will also be checked to ensure that the reference is within their maximum defined length. 
Reference modification expressions will be checked to ensure that: 


  a.. The reference modification starting position is greater than or equal to 1. 

  b.. The reference modification starting position is not greater than the current length of the subject data item. 

  c.. The reference modification length value (if specified) is greater than or equal to 1. 

  d.. The reference modification starting position and length value (if specified) do not reference an area beyond the end of the subject data item. "
```

```
> <http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/2.4.48 
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>       data item. "
I ran a test today with SSRANGE on during compile and CHECK(ON) during 
execution, the program was terminated when it attempted to move data beyond the 
first field boundary. Unfortunately, when running without both of these options, 
storage integrity is not guaranteed and data fields may be unintentionally 
changed.
```

```
On or about 9/24/2003 2:07 AM, it came to pass that Jack Sleight wrote:
> It may be helpful to tell us what you want to accomplish with the
> code, for example, "I want 65 to appear in positions 2 and 3 of
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Jack
I thought I did that on my original message and did not want to constantly 
repeat old information in the thread. I also greatly simplified the code for the 
sake of brevity and clarity. In reality the data being moved is totally variable 
in length and the problem I had was not with the data, but the fact that a 
referenced variable length move instruction spilled into a non-target field. 
I've since learned this is possible unless certain options are used with an IBM 
COBOL LE program.

In your earlier response, you clearly pointed out the use of SSRANGE and 
CHECK(ON). I thank you for making me aware this option applied to referenced moves.

Somewhere else in this thread it was suggested I run a test and report back. My 
last post contained my test results and conclusion.

Once again thank you and all for your help and assistance.

Arn
```

```
                             OMNI GROUP
                             tgugger@msn.com
                             419-380-8853


            COBOL-370 Analyst/Programmer IDMS ADSO experience

  If interested and qualified answer the five questions below and email
WITH a resume to tgugger@msn.com.

Due Date: Sept 23, 2003 at 1:00 P.M. EST
Location: Raleigh, NC
4 months Rate: $32hr 1099 (You pay your expenses)
Start Date: Oct 2003

Description:
Several experienced analyst/programmers are required to assist with the
conversion
of the University's student records systems to a new key. Some analysis will
be required to help determine files, programs and procedures impacted by the
change. A number of COBOL programs must be written or modified to convert
files and to modify reports. A number of IDMS screens must be modified to
display and edit the new key.  TM 448

    Email answers WITH a resume to tgugger@msn.com.

1. Cobol experience-----------yrs
2. 370 experience-------------yrs
3. IDMS experience------------yrs
4. ADSO experience------------yrs
5. Write a paragraph detailing like work that you have done
In the past. Please be specific.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fatal error

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-09-23T20:54:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030923165417.29142.00000072@mb-m24.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<bkpf28$nta$1@titan.btinternet.com>`

```
But isn't this just uninitialized data?

  77  77-int pic 9(12).

Dividing uninitialized data could cause a runtime error. That is not
impossible.

as in 

divide 1 into 77-int
             giving 77-int-part
             remainder 77-dec-part


Perhaps you have only presented a portion of the code. 

Your actual code presented is 
<<
 main section.
           divide 1 into 77-int
             giving 77-int-part
             remainder 77-dec-part
           stop run.
>>

Do not know if you are just sketching it to give a flavor of the frustration or
if this is failry exact.  You would be perhaps better off with a period at the
end of the arithmetic statement (divide) and before the stop run.

Arithmetic statements can have certain conditional clauses (on error, not on
error) and you may have found a weakness in the compiler that does not see the
stop run statement.

I am not trying to be picky, just helpful. If the posting is exact, then the
recommendation would be to init all numerics, and dress up the procedures with
any and all available  scope delimiters (like period).

If your code was meant as a sketch, sorry for my excess focus on detail. 

If there is anything after that stop run, like a paragraph that does I/O, you
could get a drop through that sends out spurious error messages 

Best Wishes,
Bob Rayhawk
```

- **References:** `<bknhsj$cc8$1@hercules.btinternet.com> <217e491a.0309222310.2177cdef@posting.google.com> <bkos4e$9td$1@hercules.btinternet.com> <bkpf28$nta$1@titan.btinternet.com> <PC4cb.1047$RW4.468@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <bksb2u$8t1$2@titan.btinternet.com>`

```
Steve Rainbird <steve.rainbird@nospam.mssint.com> wrote in message news:bksb2u$8t1$2@titan.btinternet.com...
> It could say,
>
> Problem at line nnnn contact technical support
>
> Then at least I would have had some idea where the problem was.

 When I encounter an issue such as this, I experiment,
one change at a time, until I isolate the cause of the problem.

I saw a number of things that I didn't like in the code sample.

However, those are really different issues.

Your main point, which I agree with, is that exception errors
are almost always handled in an inexcusably obtuse manner.

This holds true for IBM, Microsoft, Compuware and the vast
majority of applications programmers. who fail to realize
that, for example, the technically-oriented gibberish that they
just included in the online screen's message field does nothing
to advise the user that they need to add a customer to the
customer master file, or a part number to the part master file.

They justify this sort of thinking by 'rationalizing' that
"the part had to exist for the order to be booked in the first place,
 so if it is missing here then we have a serious problem".
They failed to anticipate that the part may be deleted after
the order was created, due to a programmer failing to insert
the necessary edits in a part master maintenance program,
or perhaps subsequent program maintenance created a path
which caused such an edit to be bypassed.

The technical data can and should be sent via email
to an applications support mailing list or group.
The message returned to the user, such as the compiler diagnostic,
should be in non-technical plain English, or the appropriate foreign language.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fatal error

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-24T21:24:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YXncb.2113$NX3.786@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<bknhsj$cc8$1@hercules.btinternet.com> <217e491a.0309222310.2177cdef@posting.google.com> <bkos4e$9td$1@hercules.btinternet.com> <bkpf28$nta$1@titan.btinternet.com> <PC4cb.1047$RW4.468@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <bksb2u$8t1$2@titan.btinternet.com> <DAkcb.157068$3o3.11227707@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
I may be mistaken, but it seems to me that the original example is similar
to IBM (mainframe) compilers getting a U-level message.  What is happened is
that INTERNALLY something "totally unexpected" has happened and that there
is no "associated" line causing the problem.  Often this is due to some "it
can never happen" size limitation has been exceeded or some COMBINATION of
restrictions have been violated.

If it truly turns out that the original message WAS caused by a single
"statement" (much less line) in the source code, then the vendor (Micro
Focus) should fix the message.  If, as I suspect, it was a combination of
"problems" then I still think this is the best possible message.
```

```
I don't believe the Identification Division is optional in any version 
of COBOL.  Yes, the compiler shouldn't crap out, but looking at your 
output...

> Micro Focus Net Express V4
> Version 4.0.38 Copyright (C) 1984-2003 Micro Focus International Ltd.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> ERROR: (9) Program indicated failure

Note that the code is found to contain no errors, and it appears it may 
be assigning the program name XX to it.  Don't know if that's the 
problem or not, but it's where I would start looking - the program's 
gotta have a name.  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fatal error

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T06:52:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CmRcb.4782$NX3.2632@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
Believe it or not, the Identification Division *IS* optional in Micro
Focus - where the LRM includes the following,

"Another way to identify the first source element defined in a file is with
the basename, which is derived from the filename of the file containing the
source element. "
```

<a id="t-3f04a9a89d"></a>
## Thread: What comes around, goes around... — 4 msgs

### What comes around, goes around...

- **From:** KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM (KELLIE FITTON)
- **Date:** 2003-09-22T13:31:21-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41758a6b.0309221231.d40e465@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello everyone,

is it possible to code a procedrual & gui cobol programs to perform an
automatic "Garbage Collection" on-the-fly? I would like to prevent any
memory leaks that could cause the system to become unstable. I am using
(large indexed files & sequential files & sort routines & sub-programs)
by microfocus Netexpress 3.1 compiler on windows 2000 platform.
thanks a lot for your kind help, Kellie.
```

#### ↳ Re: What comes around, goes around...

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-22T22:01:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yiKbb.5874$ev2.2721700@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309221231.d40e465@posting.google.com>`

```
"KELLIE FITTON" <KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM> wrote in message
news:41758a6b.0309221231.d40e465@posting.google.com...
> Hello everyone,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> thanks a lot for your kind help, Kellie.

Assuming you are talking about a Windows environment, no.

Each Windows process owns and *jealously* guards all resources allocated to
that process until explictly released or the process ends. That is, to a
Windows process there is no such thing as 'garbage'  to collect. (Or,
"Windows cannot know it's a leak: it assumes if the programmer allocated the
resource, the programmer wants the resource available")

If you do have memory leaks, it's because the program was not coded
correctly, whether that be your program or any third-party products used
(e.g., NetExpress runtime, print library, whatever).

If you can end the process and restart a new process 'where you left off'
that would do the trick, but far and away your best bet for reducing 'leaks'
is to code carefully to start with.
```

#### ↳ Re: What comes around, goes around...

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-09-22T23:38:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F6F88EE.2A4020B2@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309221231.d40e465@posting.google.com>`

```


KELLIE FITTON wrote:

> Hello everyone,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> thanks a lot for your kind help, Kellie.

Well at long last you have got specific and told us you are using N/E V 3.1
- why didn't you do that with your first message <G>. You write about
'GUIs' so I'm assuming you are talking about Dialog Editor (visually design
'dialogs' and use GUI classes) as opposed to Dialog System (which is a
separate module but which does invoke M/F classes as necessary).

A bit of background so you know where you are at with Garbage Collection.
The COBOL Standards Committee (J4) first approved OO as an addition to
COBOL back in '93 as a draft.. Only three took up the cudgel ,  Hitachi
(only sold in Japan), IBM and Micro Focus. (Fujitsu followed a while later
so they were able to incorporate some additional information subsequently
'blessed' by J4).

The 'Standard' as set, specified general class structures but did not spell
out  support for 'standard classes', GUIs or garbage collection. What you
have in N/E is the M/Fr own version of support classes subordinate to Base,
plus of course the classes for GUI support.

Where is J4 at the moment  ?  They approved a "Finalizer" document last
year which is the standard for getting rid of objects - garbage collection,
but as yet, to my knowledge is not part of any OO COBOL compiler. They are
also working currently on 'standard classes' (that's the 'bunch' you see in
N/E under Base, which also includes collections). Target date for J4
'Standard Classes' - last time I looked - 2006.

So for the moment without auto garbage collection, you have to go with the
N/E, "finalize", "deepfinalize", and "deepfinalizeWithDepth". Just as you
have your housekeeping routines to initiate classes or Dialogs, similarly I
have 'close down housekeeping'.

I'm working on the following at the moment, so let's use it as an example
:-

Class : Treevbus.cbl (TreeView Business Logic)
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
OBJECT-STORAGE SECTION.
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
01 os-CheeseDBI                    object reference value null.
01 os-CheeseFile                   object reference value null.
01 os-CountryFile                  object reference value null.
01 os-CurrentTvItem                object reference value null.
01 os-Parent                       object reference value null.
01 os-Resource                     object reference value null.
01 os-Treeview                     object reference value null.
01 os-TvAncestor                   object reference value null.
01 os-TvPopup                      object reference value null.

01 os-CheeseDialog                 object reference value null.
01 os-CountryDialog                object reference value null.
01 os-TvDialog                     object reference value null.

Note the os-Parent (Main Window) and os-Resource (Dialog Editor dll) are
NOT included in the following - the disappear with a 'STOP RUN'. :-

When I use 'PB-Exit" or "PB-Cancel" then I  :-

   invoke self "finalizeObjects"

*>-------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "finalizeObjects".
*>-------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==01 ls==.
01 ls-object                         object reference.
Procedure Division.

set ls-object to os-CheeseDBI
perform FINALIZE-SINGLE
set ls-object to os-CheeseFile
perform CLOSE-FILE
set ls-object to os-CountryFile
perform CLOSE-FILE
set ls-object to os-CurrentTvItem
perform FINALIZE-SINGLE
set ls-object to os-Treeview
perform FINALIZE-SINGLE
set ls-object to os-TvAncestor
perform FINALIZE-SINGLE
set ls-object to os-TvPopup
perform FINALIZE-CLASS
set ls-object to os-CheeseDialog
perform FINALIZE-DIALOG-MENU.
set ls-object to os-CountryDialog
perform FINALIZE-DIALOG-MENU.
set ls-object to os-TvDialog
perform FINALIZE-DIALOG-MENU.
invoke self "finalize" returning self

EXIT METHOD.

copy "finalize.cpy".

End Method "finalizeObjects".
*>-------------------------------------------------------

The copy file included above :-

--------------------- finalize.cpy -------------------------

CLOSE-FILE.

if ls-object <> null
   invoke ls-object "closeFile" returning ls-SqlResult
   perform FINALIZE-SINGLE
End-if
.
FINALIZE-CLASS.

  if ls-object <> null
     invoke ls-object "finalizeObjects" returning ls-object
  End-if
  .
FINALIZE-COLLECTIONS.

  if ls-object <> null
     invoke ls-object "deepfinalize" returning ls-object
  End-if
  .
FINALIZE-DIALOG-MENU.

  if ls-object <> null
     invoke ls-object "hide"
     invoke ls-object "finalizeObjects" returning ls-object
  End-if
  .

FINALIZE-SINGLE.

  if ls-object <> null
     invoke ls-object "finalize" returning ls-object
  End-if
  .

------------------------------------------------------------

Note that in some instances the 'finalize' is done within Treebus.cbl,
other times it is passed to the other classes which themselves have a
method "finalizeObjects", containing the 'finalize.cpy" file.

It isn't picture perfect, (leaving a few objects hanging around) but it
gets rid of the majority and avoids memory leaks.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **Message-ID:** `<vn7gfb7i8c2ca5@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3F6F88EE.2A4020B2@shaw.ca>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:

> A bit of background so you know where you are at with Garbage Collection.
> The COBOL Standards Committee (J4) first approved OO as an addition to
> COBOL back in '93 as a draft.. Only three took up the cudgel ,  Hitachi
> (only sold in Japan),

Actually, the Hitachi OO COBOL is part of the Unisys Programmer's 
Workbench, and has a companion processor on the mainframe called OCOB. 
:)  All before J4 approved the 2002 standard...
```

## Thread: Linking Fujitsu CBL routines — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Linking Fujitsu CBL routines

- **From:** "R. G. 'Stumpy' Marsh" <rmarsh@work>
- **Date:** 2003-09-23T11:32:25+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f6f877f$1@news.maxnet.co.nz>`

```
Greetings all,

I'm rather a newbie with Fujitsu's version 3 compiler. I'm converting an
application from 16 bit MS/MF Cobol v5 to Fujitsu v3 for the 32bitness and
smaller data file sizes.

I'm having trouble with linking CBL routines into the program. I've managed
to get smaller programs, linking in Flexus SP2, to work, but I get a call
failure on CBL routines linked in the same way.

I'm aware that there are version compatibility issues, but I've got the
version 3 library and dll off the Fujitsu site, so that shouldn't (?) be a
problem.

I know it's not much to go on, but does anyone have any ideas? I'd give more
detail, but I don't know what's likely to be relevant.

Stumpy.


---
Outgoing mail is certified Virus Free.
Checked by AVG anti-virus system (http://www.grisoft.com).
Version: 6.0.520 / Virus Database: 318 - Release Date: 18/09/03
```

#### ↳ Re: Linking Fujitsu CBL routines

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-09-22T22:02:48-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0309222102.53cfcb44@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3f6f877f$1@news.maxnet.co.nz>`

```
The v3 version you downloaded does not support CBL_ routines with the
"student" serial number.  If you find the compiler worthwhile - rest
assured in the "production" version of the compiler (currently 7.0)
the CBL_ routines work quite well.
```

```
"R. G. 'Stumpy' Marsh" <rmarsh@work> wrote 

> I'm rather a newbie with Fujitsu's version 3 compiler. I'm converting an
> application from 16 bit MS/MF Cobol v5 to Fujitsu v3 for the 32bitness and
> smaller data file sizes.

Read the licence agreement that you entered into when you downloaded
the free version 3 compiler: it is a student licence that is for
learning Cobol.  Applications may not be created and distributed with
this version, you should buy a current version if you intend to
distribute.

> I'm having trouble with linking CBL routines into the program. I've managed
> to get smaller programs, linking in Flexus SP2, to work, but I get a call
> failure on CBL routines linked in the same way.

It would be useful if you gave examples of what you mean by 'link CBL
routines' into the program.  Do you mean such things as 'CALL
"CBL_delete_file"'.  These are not supported by version 3.


> I'm aware that there are version compatibility issues, but I've got the
> version 3 library and dll off the Fujitsu site, so that shouldn't (?) be a
> problem.

What did the version 3 documentation tell you ?
 
> I know it's not much to go on, but does anyone have any ideas? I'd give more
> detail, but I don't know what's likely to be relevant.
```

```
is not distributable.
 
> That's what I mean. If CBL routines aren't supported by v3, how come

> than an old license?

Quite right, the version 3 has not support for CBL routines 'out of
the box'.  There is a separate DLL that does support _some_ CBL
routines.

> No mention whatsoever. 

They aren't in the documents because it is a later add-on it seems.

You can get the names of those that are supported from the .DLL, I
seem to recall there used to be a downloadable document for the
routines, or perhaps that was version 4.
```

```

Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0309232116.6169a03@posting.google.com...
> EMail me if you want a copy of the document 'CBL_Rout.PDF' from
> version 4 which should be similar to what you need for the routines.

I have a version 4 listing of routines, thanks. I have managed to get the
CBL routines to work... more-or-less. The sticking point seems to have been
the DLOAD compiler directive. I now have monolithic executables, but they
work.

Now that I have them going, I'm running into another problem. I have a
reliable intermittent (if that makes sense) problem with their execution. I
have two sets of calls to PC_SET_DRIVE and CBL_CHANGE_DIR:

SET-LOCAL-FOLDER SECTION.
   CALL "PC_SET_DRIVE"    USING LOCAL-DRIVE.
   CALL "CBL_CHANGE_DIR"  USING LOCAL-PATH.

SET-NET-FOLDER SECTION.
   CALL "PC_SET_DRIVE"    USING NET-DRIVE.
   CALL "CBL_CHANGE_DIR"  USING NET-PATH.

With data declared:
01 CONTAINER.
   03 IRRELEVANT                PIC 999.
   03 IRRELEVANT.
      05 IRRELEVANT               PIC 99.
      05 IRRELEVANT             PIC 99.
      05 IRRELEVANT               PIC 99.
   03 NETWORK-CONFIG.
      05 LOCAL-DRIVE            PIC X VALUE "C".
      05 NET-DRIVE                  PIC X.
      05 TERMINAL-NUMBER           PIC 99.

Not the way I'd choose to do it, but it's what I have to work with...

LOCAL-DRIVE and NET-DRIVE are both given sensible values (F and G) before
the offending code is executed.

As shown, both PC_SET_DRIVEs fail, returning -1 in PROGRAM-STATUS.

If I declare another variable:

01 DRIVE-LETTER    PIC X.

...move NET-DRIVE or LOCAL-DRIVE into it and call PC_SET_DRIVE on
DRIVE-LETTER it works.

If I move NET-DRIVE to DRIVE-LETTER and then move DRIVE-LETTER back into
NET-DRIVE and call PC_SET_DRIVE on NET-DRIVE it fails again. I've even tried
busting the data structure containing the DRIVE variables and filling them
separately instead of as part of a structure MOVE, nothing helps.

To make things worse, once I'd given up and included the intermediate step,
I discovered that while the CBL_CHANGE_DIR on NET-PATH works, the identical
call using LOCAL-PATH doesn't. They are declared:

       01 FILE-FOLDERS.
         03 NET-PATH                    PIC X(40) VALUE "\    ".
         03 LOCAL-PATH                  PIC X(40) VALUE "\DEBT".

...and aren't altered in the code before the use that causes the problem.

I've run this through the debugger looking at it from every angle I can
think of and it just makes no sense.

Any ideas?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Linking Fujitsu CBL routines

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T12:19:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0309261119.54b348e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3f6f877f$1@news.maxnet.co.nz> <217e491a.0309222211.b06ec4e@posting.google.com> <3f70b76c$1@news.maxnet.co.nz> <217e491a.0309232116.6169a03@posting.google.com> <3f73c5cc$1@news.maxnet.co.nz>`

```
"R. G. 'Stumpy' Marsh" <rmarsh@work> wrote

> With data declared:
> 01 CONTAINER.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> As shown, both PC_SET_DRIVEs fail, returning -1 in PROGRAM-STATUS.

It is possibly a problem with termination.  The routine may be using
characters until it finds a space or low-values.  When Local-Drive is
a single character embedded in a structure the next byte is whatever
Net-Drive contains and the routine takes the value being passed as
"CF".  When it is an 01 level this takes up 4 or 8 bytes and the value
is "C     " which the routine correctly identifies as a single drive
letter.  Similar with the path.

When a parameter is passed in Cobol only the start address is given to
the routine, it must decide how long it is from the definition or from
context.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Linking Fujitsu CBL routines

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** R. G. 'Stumpy' Marsh <rmarsh@xtra.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-09-27T21:43:41+12:00

- **Message-ID:** `<vcganv83nneuupj9shvm2d3l2jlnqnqafh@news.xtra.co.nz>`

```
In message <217e491a.0309261119.54b348e@posting.google.com>,
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>It is possibly a problem with termination.  The routine may be using

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
```

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Accessing a VSAM file outside of IBM mainframe

- **From:** "mg" <1@1.1>
- **Date:** 2003-09-23T03:03:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uKObb.141757$7G2.15635@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
Hi all.  I have a VSAM file that resides on an ESA/390 VSE/ESA V.2.4 and I
would like to access it remotely from within .net applications.  There are
third party software packages (VIA-SQL for one) that will do this but costs
$30,000.  I know version 2.5 comes with extra goodies that would help me but
I can't upgrade do to reasons I won't get into.  So saying all of this has
anyone been able access a VSAM file from outside of the IBM mainframe
environment?
```

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-23T22:05:24+00:00

```
Take a look at C-Cubed - they not only allow file access, but subroutine
access from the PC as well!  They also do file transfers to/from, interface
with IBM IDCAMS, as well as provide various dataset functions (allocate,
rename, delete, catalog lists, etc).  All of their utilities are Microsoft
COM (ActiveX) technology based, which allow easy access from .NET, Visual
Basic, VBA (Excel, Word), C++, PowerBuilder, etc..  Check out the MVS Direct
API product on their web-site - for $250 per run-time license you can't beat
the price!

They also have other API's available into various MVS utilities:  CA-7,
CA-Endevor, IOF, SDSF, CA-View (SAR), Infoman, NDM, Strobe, etc..

Web-site is www.c-cubed.net

HTH - TL
```

<a id="t-f37b4e5ab4"></a>
## Thread: New, free tech paper: Unicode — 1 msgs

### New, free tech paper: Unicode

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2003-09-23T12:48:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030923084818.17694.00000038@mb-m02.aol.com>`

```
Hi,

I just wanted to point out a new technical article available from our website.
No cost or obligation [it is a bit nerdy, though].

From time to time we create course material as part of our research and course
development process that ends up not actually working for the course.

The information is good and interesting but, for some reason or other doesn't
fit into the flow of the course.

Precisely because the content has some general value, we like to make these
materials available for free reference from our website.

A new techie paper is available on our website today:

"Introduction to Unicode".  One section discussing the concepts and role of
Unicode in the world of the web - and on the mainframe; a second section
detailing the mappings between the three formats of Unicode, UTF-8, UTF-16, and
UTF-32.

Maybe you know people in your organization who would be interested in this
content, so I'm just asking for you to pass it on.

To get to this article, follow this link:
http://www.trainersfriend.com/Book_site.htm

I'd appreciate hearing about any errors you may find there.


Kind regards,

-Steve Comstock
800-993-9716
303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

### Re: Need Help

- **From:** "Simeon Nevel" <snevel@sonic.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-23T14:17:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jCYbb.23634$dk4.756571@typhoon.sonic.net>`
- **References:** `<ktbvmv4g4j5or9s9lqp5kdeoq1v20skv53@4ax.com>`

```

"Dee Mike" <disconcertinglyRATS_morbid@yahoo.comRATS> wrote:

> Hi.  I'm taking two programming classes this semester, one in COBOL
> and one in JCL.  And I'm totally lost.  I've been looking at different
> COBOL books at Barnes & Noble, but I was wondering if there were any
> that actual experienced COBOL programmers would recommend to a
> complete and utter novice.

I've *always* been a fan of the Murach books.  I own a bunch of them myself
and use
them for reference.

Check out:

    http://www.murach.com/books/mainframe.htm

While I have a few quibbles (mostly stylistic) with the presentation, the
Murach books always
give through coverage of what you need to know.

Just curious....

Why on earth are  you taking COBOL classes?  There are hardly any jobs out
there
for us experienced hands let alone someone fresh out of school.

Simeon (25 yrs on the mainframe) Nevel
```

<a id="t-fc2324663b"></a>
## Thread: convert ascii to ebcdic — 12 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### convert ascii to ebcdic

- **From:** lorigrenci@hotmail.com (lori)
- **Date:** 2003-09-23T12:19:16-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4dc738b.0309231119.7dc4809b@posting.google.com>`

```
in a mainframe COBOL II program, what's the best way to convert an
ASCII file to an EBCDIC file?  Is there a "clever" way, or is it just
read a translation table? TIA.
lorigrenci@hotmail.com
```

#### ↳ Re: convert ascii to ebcdic

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-23T22:31:26+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rq71nv01e6a4err09krsqg74fjko9cgiva@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4dc738b.0309231119.7dc4809b@posting.google.com>`

```
On 23 Sep 2003 12:19:16 -0700 lorigrenci@hotmail.com (lori) wrote:

:>in a mainframe COBOL II program, what's the best way to convert an
:>ASCII file to an EBCDIC file?  Is there a "clever" way, or is it just
:>read a translation table? TIA.

There is a DCB option (OPTCD=Q if I recall correctly) which does it
automagically.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: convert ascii to ebcdic

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-23T23:22:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MA4cb.1044$RW4.228@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<4dc738b.0309231119.7dc4809b@posting.google.com> <rq71nv01e6a4err09krsqg74fjko9cgiva@4ax.com>`

```
Yes, OPTCD=Q will work - but requires that the ASCII file be on TAPE.

However, as others have pointed out, there will be problems if you have any
non-display fields or numeric fields without sign is separate.

If the INTENT is to move from the mainframe to the PC (or Unix) then there
are tools to use a "record" layout for doing an intelligent conversion.  I
know that Micro Focus has such a tool - and I think some of the other PC
vendors do too.
```

#### ↳ Re: convert ascii to ebcdic

- **From:** "Simeon Nevel" <snevel@sonic.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-23T20:20:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iW1cb.23787$dk4.758782@typhoon.sonic.net>`
- **References:** `<4dc738b.0309231119.7dc4809b@posting.google.com>`

```

"lori" <lorigrenci@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:4dc738b.0309231119.7dc4809b@posting.google.com...
> in a mainframe COBOL II program, what's the best way to convert an
> ASCII file to an EBCDIC file?  Is there a "clever" way, or is it just
> read a translation table? TIA.

Conversion of signed numerics, floats and packed fields are a bit tricky

See:

http://www.discinterchange.com/TechTalk_signed_fields.html

Simeon
```

```
Real simple reason... both ASCII and EBCDIC are subject to local (well,
national) variations.

Besides, if there were such functions the tutorial I wrote on this subject
and make available (FREE) for all at my web site at...

http://www.talsystems.com/tsihome_html/downloads/C2IEEE.htm

.. would never have happened!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: convert ascii to ebcdic

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-24T21:52:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Fmocb.2159$NX3.124@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<4dc738b.0309231119.7dc4809b@posting.google.com> <i8icnfZpDpxAIO2iXTWQkQ@giganews.com> <Hy4cb.1043$RW4.252@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <l6-dnbukb8ERku-iXTWQlg@giganews.com>`

```
When/if IBM ever implements the entire 2002 COBOL Standard, this would be
quite easy to do with a "user-defined" function.  The user could do what the
sample (in the programming guide) does - but use your "intuitive" syntax.

Of course, they would have to know WHICH EBCDIC was in use (if you didn't
know - it varies from country to country - where alphabets have "additional"
characters)

P.S.  It is interesting to note that what IBM has implemented as intrinsic
functions for NATIONAL-OF and DISPLAY-OF is incompatible with the 2002
Standard.  Therefore, any applications that use the 2nd parameter of the
existing IBM implementations will have migration problems when/if IBM *does*
provide a fully conforming 2002 compiler.  I (personally) have never
understood WHY they made this "incompatible" decision, but they did - so
some day some "of us" can look forward to upward compatibility problems
and/or migration issues.
```
