<a id="t-3b72d7adbb"></a>
## Thread: cgi application, Microfocus vs Fujitsu — 4 this month / 5 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### cgi application, Microfocus vs Fujitsu

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWS0202@COMCAST.NET>
- **Date:** 2003-09-23T15:33:10-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GjKdnfitIPCyPe2iU-KYvg@comcast.com>`

```
    We have been working on a cgi application under Microfocus Cobol, Netx
3.1.

    We would like to switch to fujitsu, because of runtime prices.

    Does anyone have any experience in this area.  Which fujitsu compiler
should we work
with.  They have Netcobol and the .NET compiler.  The newest .Net compiler
does correct
many of the shortcomings of Version 1, such as no sort.  Extracting this
sort of data
from advertising copy is extremly difficult (they never come out and SAY
that they don't
support X Y and Z).

    How compatible is the cgi syntax in Microfocus and Fujitsu?

    They have dialect converters.  Do they handle CGI code?

    I am going to contact Fujitsu and see what they say, but I wanted some
outside opinions.
```

#### ↳ Re: cgi application, Microfocus vs Fujitsu

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-09-24T08:58:00+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f70d10e_5@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<GjKdnfitIPCyPe2iU-KYvg@comcast.com>`

```
Russell,

I have been using CGI/ISAPI with Fujitsu NetCOBOL for around 2 and a half
years now.

I am currently in Melbourne working with a team where we are using Fujitsu
CGI every day.

They have an excellent interface to CGI (provides a lot of environment stuff
like the TCP/IP address etc., you can "migrate" to ISAPI (multithreaded) if
the need arises, and they provide tools which are also good for generating
much of the code.

I found one area where their CGI stuff fell down and I was never able to get
it to work. (It concerned replacement of variables in Web Pages). I simply
wrote this function for myself and what I have works a treat. (I will gladly
let you have it if you go ahead, and strike the same problem I did.)

Can't comment on .Net compiler as I can't afford to buy it <G>.

Both NetCOBOL (I am using Version 6) and PowerCOBOL are EXCELLENT products.

Pete.


"Russell Styles" <RWS0202@COMCAST.NET> wrote in message
news:GjKdnfitIPCyPe2iU-KYvg@comcast.com...
>     We have been working on a cgi application under Microfocus Cobol, Netx
> 3.1.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: cgi application, Microfocus vs Fujitsu

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-09-23T22:39:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0309232139.6d456324@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<GjKdnfitIPCyPe2iU-KYvg@comcast.com>`

```
"Russell Styles" <RWS0202@COMCAST.NET> wrote 

>     Does anyone have any experience in this area.  Which fujitsu compiler
> should we work with.  

I have Cobol CGI programs that run with several combinations of
compiler, OS and Web Server including: MicroFocus, MicroSoft (eq MF
3.0), Fujitsu / DOS, OS/2, Windows 3.1 and Win32, SCO Unix, Linux /
Apache, Xitami, Netscape.

There are some minor differences between Windows start up and the rest
because Windows needs a message queue, but only a few lines. The code
uses standard CGI accessed using ACCEPT DISPLAY and does its own
templating.  The down side is that it doesn't (as yet) access any of
the various different fast-CGI mechanisms.

It isn't hard to access the standard CGI mechanism.
```

#### ↳ Re: cgi application, Microfocus vs Fujitsu

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-09-24T08:20:54-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0309240720.3a138578@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<GjKdnfitIPCyPe2iU-KYvg@comcast.com>`

```
Even using the NetCOBOL for .NET - Fujitsu DOES have runtime fees if
you are usign a server with multiple processors - so get prices before
you jump.

Using NetCOBOL for Windows - if you buy Enterprise edition the runtime
fee is waived for servers.

I have no experience using NetCOBOL for .NET with CGI but I do use the
NetCOBOL for Windows with Flexus Web client - which provides a Robust
and easy to use CGI environment.

"Russell Styles" <RWS0202@COMCAST.NET> wrote in message news:<GjKdnfitIPCyPe2iU-KYvg@comcast.com>...
> We have been working on a cgi application under Microfocus Cobol, Netx
> 3.1.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> outside opinions.
```

Hello everyone,

according to some articles from microsoft, buffer overflow is very risky
because it could allow a hacker to enter or control your system through
this flooded area in the memory.
can you please tell me why is that true??
I thought when a buffer becomes overflown with data, the data will simply
get truncated to fit in the receiving field?
for example:
01 text1   pic x(10) value spaces.
01 text2   pic x(80) value spaces.
open file.
move data from file (80 characters long) to text2.
move text2 to text1.
now, the data in text1 got truncated (70 characters lost). correct?
thanks a lot for your kind help. Kellie.

***
```

>

COBOL, but in many other languages, it is a pretty common attack.

See: http://www.home.linuxjournal.com/article.php?sid=6701
```

#### ↳ Re: Easy come easy go...

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-24T15:34:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VBmcnYvWJ4UhYuyiXTWJkQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309240827.3ec1300c@posting.google.com>`

```
KELLIE FITTON wrote:
> Hello everyone,
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> thanks a lot for your kind help. Kellie.

You're code is correct. But what if COBOL didn't do the bounds check for
you? That is, "text1" has the first 10 bytes from "text2" and whatever is
beyond "text1" in memory gets the remaining 70.

Regrettably, this is the case with C.

We often get C programmers here wanting to know the COBOL equivalent of a
"pointer." We don't really have one and virtually never run into problems
such as the one above. (A "pointer" points to a memory location and, for
example, you have commands in C like:
"Move a bucket-full of bytes to the location {pointer}" <== buffer overflow
if the bucket is too big.

A better question is why are not web browsers written in COBOL? There would
be no buffer overflow problems.
```

news:F2mdnZtFycR2w--iU-KYiA@giganews.com...
> Grinder wrote:
> >> A better question is why are not web browsers written in

>

written in COBOL.

Just curious if there is one.

> > Just an added tidbit as well -- most of those buffer
overflow
> > defects are in libraries used by browsers, not the browsers
> > themselves.
>
> Oh. You mean in the OOP parts. And since they are reusable,
one must not
> tamper with them because they're used in so many places.
Makes perfect sense
> to me.
>
> Explains a lot, really.

If you're using common code -- OOP or otherwise -- fixing it in
one place fixes all systems that use it.  Additionally, if that
code has a well-defined interface, you will be able to tell if
your changes are going to affect the linkages with those
systems.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Easy come easy go...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-25T06:54:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<I_CcnRYPnIzgSu-iXTWJkA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309240827.3ec1300c@posting.google.com> <VBmcnYvWJ4UhYuyiXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <bkt8ic013ep@enews1.newsguy.com> <F2mdnZtFycR2w--iU-KYiA@giganews.com> <bktt0i02eht@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```
Grinder wrote:
>>
>> I was being a tad silly. But there are COBOL compilers written in
>> COBOL.
>
> Just curious if there is one.

Sure.

>
> If you're using common code -- OOP or otherwise -- fixing it in
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> systems.

Ho, ho, ho. No, no, no.

Remember Y2K?

I, and everyone else, have seen "fixes" in nth-level subordinate programs
that hosed existing applications. Let me give you one example.

I was in a shop that had 4 subroutines: MOVEB, MOVEHW, MOVEW, & MOVEDW (move
some number of bytes/half-words/words/or double-words from one location to
another). Some bright programmer decided to combine them into one subprogram
with different entry points. The MOVHW entry took the number of half-words
to move, multiplied by two and branched to the MOVEB code. MOVEDW multiplied
by 8 and branched. Worked beautifully.

Until it was put into production.

The "new and improved" MOVEDW took 8 times longer to execute than the old
code (because in assembly you have primative instructions to move
half-words, words, and double-words - a move half-word is one instruction vs
two move-byte instructions).

The 1st step in the processing, after the above change, went from 4 minutes
per input reel to over 30. The largest job I saw at this shop had over 700
input tapes - the median was about 50.

We're talking pitchforks and torches, ripping the legs-off-bunnies,
reactions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Easy come easy go...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-25T13:16:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bkvbek0ll0@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309240827.3ec1300c@posting.google.com> <VBmcnYvWJ4UhYuyiXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <bkt8ic013ep@enews1.newsguy.com> <F2mdnZtFycR2w--iU-KYiA@giganews.com> <bktt0i02eht@enews1.newsguy.com> <I_CcnRYPnIzgSu-iXTWJkA@giganews.com>`

```
> > If you're using common code -- OOP or otherwise -- fixing
it in
> > one place fixes all systems that use it.  Additionally, if
that
> > code has a well-defined interface, you will be able to tell
if
> > your changes are going to affect the linkages with those
> > systems.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Remember Y2K?

Indeed.  I spent six months proofing a COBOL application that
extensively used 2-digit years.  If the application had been
designed around a common date-handling library, we could have
resolved the issue with fixes to that library, and a data
conversion utility.  As it was, however, we had to sift through
every line of the program to find the various implementations
and fix those.

> I, and everyone else, have seen "fixes" in nth-level
subordinate programs
> that hosed existing applications. Let me give you one
example.
>
> I was in a shop that had 4 subroutines: MOVEB, MOVEHW, MOVEW,
& MOVEDW (move
> some number of bytes/half-words/words/or double-words from
one location to
> another). Some bright programmer decided to combine them into
one subprogram
> with different entry points. The MOVHW entry took the number
of half-words
> to move, multiplied by two and branched to the MOVEB code.
MOVEDW multiplied
> by 8 and branched. Worked beautifully.
>
> Until it was put into production.
>
> The "new and improved" MOVEDW took 8 times longer to execute
than the old
> code (because in assembly you have primative instructions to
move
> half-words, words, and double-words - a move half-word is one
instruction vs
> two move-byte instructions).
>
> The 1st step in the processing, after the above change, went
from 4 minutes
> per input reel to over 30. The largest job I saw at this shop
had over 700
> input tapes - the median was about 50.
>
> We're talking pitchforks and torches, ripping the
legs-off-bunnies,
> reactions.

You don't have to incur the performance hit of using an
external library -- you can just use a code repository to
maintain a common block of logic that is compiled into each
application.  It is more difficult, in daily operation, to
achieve code reuse than to not.  This is an issue, however,
that existed before object-oriented programming.

OOP does suggest a component-based design, but there's no
requirement for you to put every distinct bit of logic into a
seperate library.  You have to balance the benefits of common
libraries (an easy equation if you believe there is *no*
benefit) against performance considerations.

In the case that you mention, the programmer clearly
implemented a bad design.  That poor judgement was further
compounded by not testing the restructured code *before* they
went into production.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Easy come easy go...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-25T13:35:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bkvchh0mh9@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309240827.3ec1300c@posting.google.com> <VBmcnYvWJ4UhYuyiXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <bkt8ic013ep@enews1.newsguy.com> <F2mdnZtFycR2w--iU-KYiA@giganews.com> <bktt0i02eht@enews1.newsguy.com> <I_CcnRYPnIzgSu-iXTWJkA@giganews.com>`

```
It occurs to me that you might think I'm arguing for the
adoption of OOD for all development.  This is not the case -- 
there are many situations where a "flat" design fits the
requirements well.  It entirely depends upon the project, but
generally, the benefits of OOD/OOP exceed the costs as the size
of the project increases.  When you have a lot of logic to
capture, encapsulation, inheritance and even componentization
can help to keep your algorithms concise.

A good example that comes to mind, is in handling Explanations
of Benefits (EOBs) provided by insurance carriers.  There are
hundreds (probably thousands) of formats that contain a
consistent subset of information that is of interest to a
medical claims application.

You can write all of these parsing/interpretation routines in a
piece of flat code, but it soon will become a behemoth.  Also,
the superposition of code can make these large "case statement"
routines difficult to analyze.

If you write it with OOD/OOP, you can devise a base class that
exposes the critical information you need, but leave the
details of that extraction to various subclasses.  The added
weight of that infrastructure will, at first, consume the
savings brought by inheritance.  As you add more carriers to
the mix, however, the design will help to keep your development
focussed and concise.

This may sound like an abstraction, but it's not -- I've done
this and am convinced it's a good idea.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Easy come easy go...

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-25T05:13:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KPucb.2743$NX3.2512@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309240827.3ec1300c@posting.google.com> <VBmcnYvWJ4UhYuyiXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <Hvucb.13538$eS5.2039@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
What USAGE POINTER is
   is an EXTENSION (non-portable)  to the '85 ANSI/ISO Standard and is
definitely NOT implemented the same in all compilers that currently support
it.

In fact, it was added to the 2002 Standard, but with allowable syntax that
does NOT match any existing compiler (that I know of) exactly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Easy come easy go...

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-09-27T14:01:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F75DE6C.6EF13AF6@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309240827.3ec1300c@posting.google.com> <VBmcnYvWJ4UhYuyiXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-ED96D6.08570926092003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger wrote:

> In article <VBmcnYvWJ4UhYuyiXTWJkQ@giganews.com>,
>  "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> browser program?

Well, when you realize that when you look far enough under the hood, ultimately
you're going to find machine language executing, the answer has to be yes. ML (or
assembly if you like) is purely sequential, procedural code, with no abstractions
or high-level concepts or anything other than the instructions the machine will
execute.  It stands to reason then that if you can do it at that low level than
you can do it in any language (bearing in mind that all languages have facilities
to access the operating system or assembly-language subroutines to do something
that the language doesn't have facilities for).  It wouldn't be easy, though!

All pages would have to be as the screen -handler defines. Nothing to do with the
COBOL pictures.

PL
```

#### ↳ Re: Easy come easy go...

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-09-24T14:46:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0309241346.6d1b590e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309240827.3ec1300c@posting.google.com>`

```
KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM (KELLIE FITTON) wrote

> according to some articles from microsoft, buffer overflow is very risky
> because it could allow a hacker to enter or control your system through
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> get truncated to fit in the receiving field?

That is true in Cobol (mostly) because Cobol does that truncate and/or
fill to try to ensure that there is no buffer overflow.

Other languages may not add code to check buffer lengths, relying on
the programmer to do this.

Of course it is entirely possible to get Cobol to overwrite things if
the BOUND checks are turned off:

         03 Item     PIC X(20)  OCCURS 10.

         MOVE 43         TO Item-No
         MOVE "XXX"      TO Item(Item-No)

The BOUND check code will pick this up, but not if the compile had
this turned off.

It is trivially easy to overwrite memory areas by misusing pointers.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Easy come easy go...

- **From:** charles.stevens@unisys.com (Chuck Stevens)
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T13:50:34-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c60a0b82.0309261250.4d2e8bbb@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309240827.3ec1300c@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0309241346.6d1b590e@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote in message news:<217e491a.0309241346.6d1b590e@posting.google.com>...

> Of course it is entirely possible to get Cobol to overwrite things if
> the BOUND checks are turned off:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>          MOVE "XXX"      TO Item(Item-No)

In some implementations, perhaps, but not in all.    

> The BOUND check code will pick this up, but not if the compile had
> this turned off.
> 
> It is trivially easy to overwrite memory areas by misusing pointers.

In some implementations, perhaps, but not in all.  

In implementations like Unisys MCP/AS (ex-Burroughs-large-System,
ex-Unisys-A-Series) that have hardware memory protection (and have had
for forty years and more) even if subscript and index range checking
is turned off you will still get "bitten" on any attempt to write
beyond the end of the allocated memory space, or on any attempt to
overwrite anything in memory that hasn't been predefined as data
(whether the particular program "owns" it or not).

What a subscript bounds check doesn't catch a fatal memory protection
interrupt of some sort will.

True, some of our compilers "pool" data for a given program instead of
allocating separate memory spaces for each 01-level record, so it's
possible for a program to reference outside of a particular *record*,
but getting beyond the data spaces as they were originally allocated
for the COBOL run unit is well-nigh impossible.  Code, descriptors,
program control words and the like require special machine
instructions not generated by the COBOL compilers for manipulation and
are thereby protected by the hardware as well.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Easy come easy go...

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T20:57:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VK1db.5439$NX3.881@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309240827.3ec1300c@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0309241346.6d1b590e@posting.google.com> <c60a0b82.0309261250.4d2e8bbb@posting.google.com>`

```
As a general rule, when something is documented as "results are undefined"
in "the Standard" ('85 - '74 or 2002), then that is EXACTLY what it means.

The same code may yield different results with different compilers - or even
with the same compiler with different options set.

Making "generalizations" about such things (as Richard did, not Chuck) is
BOUND <G> to be proven wrong for at least SOME compiler (probably one that
is in common usage).
```

#### ↳ Re: Easy come easy go...

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-09-25T22:31:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030925183135.27155.00000176@mb-m03.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309240827.3ec1300c@posting.google.com>`

```
The Great Leader of the Puppets asks about 
buffer overflow and codes a good first approximation as ...

<<
01 text1   pic x(10) value spaces.
01 text2   pic x(80) value spaces.
open file.
move data from file (80 characters long) to text2.
move text2 to text1.
now, the data in text1 got truncated (70 characters lost). correct?
thanks a lot for your kind help. Kellie.

>>

Kellie, it is you who are kind!

Permit me the privilege of commenting on two things, truncation and buffer
overflow.

If your text1 and my text1 both have the same definition, then truncation will
help us out.

The problem is when. for one reason or another. our definitions differ.  


If you call a submodule using text1 like this

Call 'goodcode' using text1.

And if program goodcode has 

LINKAGE SECTION
01 text1   pic x(10).

PROCEDURE DIVISION.

move 'really long text unexpected data' 
    to text1.

GOBACK.

The language definitions protects us.

Only 'really lon' gets moved. Truncation does occur.

But lets say we did not communicate well during program design and a submodule
has

LINKAGE SECTION
01 text1   pic x(35).

same procedure. The submodule now fills your text1 with the same 10 bytes, and
the next 25 bytes land in your next area text2, overrunning it.

Your text2 now might have some portion
of 'g text unexpected data' (there is a thing called boundary alignment that
may make two of the bytes land in slack space and text2 then 
would have 'text unexpected data', but that is platform specific, not to worry
... something will get clobber though.  Ask about SYNCHRONIZED if you care.).

The term  buffer overflow is generic. Anytime that I can overwirte your working
storage you are in some danger. This happens with flatlander COBOL and all
traditional programming methodologies.  There is simply nothing new about it at
all.  

ALL languages are vulnerable. ALL. 

What is new is that we do not have control over the submodules that are called
in the new open environments. Things like Microsoft's office suite have active
components, just like Java applets once did. They are effectively subroutines
and they can most definitely overwrite the  working areas of other programs.

If a buffer overflow damages some of your programs data, it may function poorly
or function in a way that damages your data or your systems current operating
state. 

A simple example, assume that your working storage was 

01 text1   pic x(10) value spaces.
01 loopControl   pic s9(8) value 3.


And you had a 

 perform something 
   varying myIndex from 1 by 1
   until myIndex is greater than loopControl

which should loop three times.


If you called

 CALL 'badguy' using text1

and badguy had 

01 IwillGetYou.
    05 text1   pic x(10) value spaces.
    05 surprise   pic s9(8).

procedure division using IwillGetYou.

move 'foolemgood' to text1
move  39876543. to surprise
goback.


If you execute your 
 perform something 
   varying myIndex from 1 by 1
   until myIndex is greater than loopControl
after the call to badguy, you may peg the CPU needle (call that denial of
service to other users of your system atleast for while), damage a table, or
get address exceptions, and you might not know why.

But, as you will come to learn all of that is trivial.

When programs are called, or traditional functions are invoked in procedural
languages, that call _might_ be achieved with code or hardware that uses a
stack data structure.

On Intel  PCs and IBMs Power PC the stack data structure is actually
implemented with a stack pointer.  On IBM mainframes there is a 
combination of a branch using one register as the forward pointer and another
register as the return (yellow ribbon) backward pointer, in combination with
saves of mulitple registers that achieves an application stack functionality.

When your submodule is called, the callee must be told how to goback to the
caller. That is the return address. Although it is not necessary to do things
in this fashion, the normal sequence is to push your interface data (the things
in your USING clause) onto the stack first, followed by a push of the return
address (there, exactly there, is the issue, the push of the return address).

So lets say you had

CALL 'malcode' USING text-1  text-2 .

This will create an 'activation record' that has your obvious things plus one
more. The activation record looks like

01 activation-record.
     05 pointer-to-text1 .....
     05 pointer-to-text2 .....
     05 return address .....

Now what that amounts to is a buffer that has pointers to what you are simply
thinking of as the buffer which is text1, and a pointer to a buffer that is
text2, and this same high level buffer contains the return address.

So think of the 
01 activation-record.
as a buffer. 

Now, overrun _THAT_.

Welcome to the new world. 

Now imagine that you do not even know the name of the submodule that will get
the address of the activation-record buffer. Imagine that you do not know what
nation the coder is located in.
Imagine that the coder would like to see your corporations fail, and would be
please if your economy was in the tank. Imagine that the coder is willing to
kill you in the office building where you work along with your family.

As a student of this important science, do you believe that Intel architecture
that places the reutrn address in the same space pointed to for data
interchange is a sound plan? It might have been okay for toasters controlled by
4004s, but I am not sure its a good idea for megabucks environments.

As a student of this important science, do you think that Microsoft office
suites that allow any attchment to become active the instant you touch them, is
a sound plan?

Read on good student, you are covering excellent ground. We need good minds
like yours!

Best Wishes,
Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Easy come easy go...

- **From:** clvrmnky <clvrmnky@coldmail.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T11:55:16-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EjZcb.75265$PD3.4793441@nnrp1.uunet.ca>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<41758a6b.0309240827.3ec1300c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309240827.3ec1300c@posting.google.com>`

```
KELLIE FITTON wrote:

> Hello everyone,
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
This might be obvious to everyone here, but this newbie just discovered 
something in his vendor's documentation.  My compiler has a CHECK 
run-time option that specifies if range checking is done on indices, 
subscripts and references.

By default it is ON, which means range-checking is enabled.  One could 
turn this off, and then you would be free to try all kinds of 
buffer-overflow and stack smashing tricks.
```

<a id="t-9d507d2d56"></a>
## Thread: COBOL syntax Q: Looking for an EXIT... — 28 this month / 29 total

### COBOL syntax Q: Looking for an EXIT...

- **From:** clvrmnky <clvrmnky@coldmail.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2003-09-24T13:17:10-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qkkcb.74528$PD3.4783075@nnrp1.uunet.ca>`

```
Greetings,

I've just come up against the fact that "EXIT" does not do what I 
thought it did.  Shame on me for not reading the Fine Manual a little 
closer.  It appears to be essentially a no-op under some circumstances.

Anyway, I'm trying to write code that calls several procedures in a row. 
  Within these procedures I'd like the ability to short-circuit 
execution and "return" early, possibly with some error condition set 
that I wish to handle.

This is a comman C-ism, I know, but this seems to be a foreign concept 
in COBOL.  That is, a procedure wants to run from beginning to end, no 
exceptions.  I'd like to handle some exceptions as they come up as I 
depend on a lot of CALLs to external routines that return/set an error flag.

I can redo my logic to make each procedure a number of nested IF...ELSE 
statements, but this is just not my style: too hard to read and 
understand.  Some broad exceptions I'd like to handle up front.  Not to 
mention that I'm going to want to indent my code a little too far to the 
right for my tastes.

Comments?  Questions?
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL syntax Q: Looking for an EXIT...

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-24T18:32:05+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dqk3nvc75j195cgl9hko2pvueqts7l8omc@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<qkkcb.74528$PD3.4783075@nnrp1.uunet.ca>`

```
On Wed, 24 Sep 2003 13:17:10 -0400, clvrmnky
<clvrmnky@coldmail.com.invalid> wrote:

>Greetings,
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>Comments?  Questions?
At present the only options are

1- If your compiler vendor has implemented the new standard (partially
or fully) then you have a "exit paragraph" instruction which does

"If an EXIT statement with the PARAGRAPH phrase is executed, the EXIT
statement causes control to be passed to an implicit CONTINUE
statement immediately following the last statement in the current
paragraph."


2- Use if's

  if not-error
     call xx
     if ret-code = "bad" set erro-found to true.
  end-if
  if not-error
     call yy
  end-if

3- Use perform thru with GOTO paragraph exit
E.G.
perform aa thru aa-exit.

aa.

  call xx
  if ret-code = "bad" go to aa-exit.
  call yy
aa-exit.
  exit.



Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax Q: Looking for an EXIT...

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-09-24T15:12:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0309241412.452869d1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<qkkcb.74528$PD3.4783075@nnrp1.uunet.ca> <dqk3nvc75j195cgl9hko2pvueqts7l8omc@4ax.com>`

```
> >I've just come up against the fact that "EXIT" does not do what I 
> >thought it did.  Shame on me for not reading the Fine Manual a little 
> >closer.  It appears to be essentially a no-op under some circumstances.

"EXIT" by itself should _always_ be a no-op. As this must be in its
own paragraph alone, this should not be an issue.  It is a compiler
extension that allows this to be used in a way that its no-op status
becomes a problem.

> >This is a comman C-ism, 

If you want to write C programs then don't use PERFORM which is very
non-C.

In C you would use a procedure call and this would contain return()s. 
To do this in Cobol you use a procedure call which would contain the
equivalent of return: 'exit program'.

Put your paragraph in a nested program and CALL it, then it can do an
EXIT PROGRAM wherever required.

Of course you may come up with several reasons why the wholly non-C
PERFORM of a paragraph is preferable, in which case don't try to mix
C-isms and non-C-isms, they don't match.






 
> 1- If your compiler vendor has implemented the new standard (partially
> or fully) then you have a "exit paragraph" instruction which does
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

PERFORM is sufficiently like a simple by-reference function call that I 
can tweak the logic to handle things, and have a common exit procedure I 
can jump to via GO TO.  Since most of the procedures are similar 
(usually only one PERFORMed via an EVALUATE case-like statement), I just 
have to handle the exceptions in the 3 helper procedures each one of 
these calls.

I've probably deemed this Good Enough and moved on.

#### ↳ Re: COBOL syntax Q: Looking for an EXIT...

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-24T16:51:22-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bkt04k$vv8$1@news.btv.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<qkkcb.74528$PD3.4783075@nnrp1.uunet.ca>`

```
In my time I have met people who HATE HATE HATE GOTO and make it a goal to
remove them all in COBOL....then will go into a C/Java program and routinely
type return write in the middle of something.
I've never understood why it is "acceptable" in one and not the other....

That said...

EXIT might not work, but GOTO EXIT could :-) You would risk the flame war
that ensues (search google groups) if I were to suggest this but what the
heck.  Of course I would also suffer the heat from suggesting that you put
the word THRU in your code....

As in:

PERFORM A THRU A-EXIT

A.
    do something
    if x
        goto A-EXIT.
    do something
    if x
        goto A-EXIT.
 A-EXIT.

I *PERSONALLY* think that if your paragraph A is very clear and then this is
acceptable. It's a style thing and I was brought up on C so what do I know.
I *PERSONALLY* think that it is very easy to get carried away and do this
when there are better alternatives.
I *PERSONALLY* think that if someone takes offense to this, you should ask
them if they could understand it.  If they could read it. If they could
maintain it. If the answer is yes to these three items then I suggest they
find three equally compelling reasons why you shouldn't do it.

That said - you should be able to decompose easily enough that would reduce
the number of nested IFs but increase the number of paragraphs...naming them
well (not A and B) will result in suprisingly readable code. It will
encourage you to logically follow through with your design and group code
accordingly.  This will in the long run more often than not be preferable to
the first solutio as it will in my *PERSONAL* opinion create a more simple
flowing code that is more obvious.  Each paragraph can have a specific
function (so OPEN-FILES is not open files and initiate variables - that
would be OPEN-FILES-AND-INITIALIZE-DATA or OPEN-FILES and INITIALIZE-DATA)
that will be very clear to follow.

Perform A

A.
    if x
        Perform B
    if x
        Perform C
    if x
        Perform D

B.
    if x
        Perform E
    if x
        Perform C
    if x
        Perform D

Possibly what you want is a combination of the second and the evaluate.  The
decomposition will group logically those operations that belong together and
the evaluate will coherently organize the conditions with an explicit
default where necessary.
Whether you use 88 levels for the conditions in the evaluate are up to you,
but don't mix your animals and colours..and stick to simple states :-)

A.
EVALUATE TRUE
WHEN X
    CONTINUE
WHEN B
    PERFORM B
WHEN C
    PERFORM C
WHEN OTHER
    CONTINUE
END-EVALUATE

B.
EVALUATE TRUE
WHEN X
    CONTINUE
WHEN B
    PERFORM B
END-EVALUATE


Methods do have an EXIT-METHOD but you appear to be procedural.

JCE

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax Q: Looking for an EXIT...

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-09-25T14:52:53-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0309251352.20d59b08@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<qkkcb.74528$PD3.4783075@nnrp1.uunet.ca> <bkt04k$vv8$1@news.btv.ibm.com>`

```
"jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote

> In my time I have met people who HATE HATE HATE GOTO and make it a goal to
> remove them all in COBOL....then will go into a C/Java program and routinely
> type return write in the middle of something.
> I've never understood why it is "acceptable" in one and not the other....

Because it isn't the 'go to' that is the problem.  A 'go to' or a
'return()' at the point they are written is no problem at all. They
clearly indicate what will happen.

The problem occurs because the go to must have a label as the target.

When inspecting a program it must be viewed in small pieces at a time.
 A properly structured program aims to have the essential parts of the
program correctly understandable by looking at the minimum amount of
code, thus being maintainable in the minimum of time.

A label can have several types of logic flow around it:  It can be the
target of a PERFORM, it can terminate a PERFORM or ~THRU, it can be
dropped through or it can be the target of a GO TO.  Because labels
can be paragraph names or section names there is an added complexity
if both types occur.

When inspecting a program and we want to understand a piece of code
that contains a label, we must determine all the ways that the logic
flows through that label.  In the case where the program contains GO
TOs or THRU then we must examine the _whole_ program to determine how
the logic flows around each label.

In a program which has no GO TO, no THRU, no SECTION labels and only
does PERFORM paragraph-label, then it is only necessary to ensure that
the initial paragraph does an EXIT PROGRAM/STOP RUN/GOBACK and then
_every_ label in the program can do just one thing.  It cannot be the
target of a GO TO, it cannot be a dropthru, it can only stop the
perform of the paragraph above and be a PERFORM of the code below
until the very next label.

Given those conditions then inspecting small parts of the program is
sufficient because each part is isolated.

Other languages achieve this by having different types of labels which
can only be used in a single way.  You cannot 'drop into' or 'drop
out' of a C function, or 'goto' it.  You cannot perform or call a C
label, it cannot act as a silent return().

Getting rid of the GO TO isn't about the GO TO verb, it is about the
labels and how they are used.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax Q: Looking for an EXIT...

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-09-25T15:03:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0309251403.7f322a89@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<qkkcb.74528$PD3.4783075@nnrp1.uunet.ca> <rUFcb.431109$cF.134450@rwcrnsc53>`

```
"Paul Hoberg" <nospamphoberg@att.net> wrote 
>...
> Would "NEXT SENTENCE" accomplish what you want?

No.  There is no _sensible_ way that NEXT SENTENCE can be used where
it wouldn't do the same as a null statement.

Any attempt to use NEXT SENTENCE as not being equivalent to CONTINUE
relies on abuse of the standards and is a bug trap of the worst kind.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax Q: Looking for an EXIT...

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-25T22:50:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yiKcb.3309$RW4.1420@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<qkkcb.74528$PD3.4783075@nnrp1.uunet.ca> <rUFcb.431109$cF.134450@rwcrnsc53> <217e491a.0309251403.7f322a89@posting.google.com>`

```
Or from another perspective,

  Any COBOL programmer who thinks that a CONTINUE is equivalent to NEXT
SENTENCE in any statement where both may be used, really is "stuck" in 1984
and doesn't understand one of the basic distinction in COBOL (between a
sentence and a statement).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax Q: Looking for an EXIT...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T00:12:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030925201240.24071.00000153@mb-m14.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<yiKcb.3309$RW4.1420@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```
Yep that's right.

And truth is the NEXT SENTENCE really is the elegant solution the original
poster asked for, it just flew a wee bit over the top level there.

Yet, help me with something here, all you standards gurus.  Is NEXT SENTENCE
supposed to go away? Is it deprecated?

Actually, it could be deprecated and there is still so much legacy code with
NEXT SENTENCE, that it wouldn't matter, it will get grandfathered in to a
number of new compiler products.  But I thought that the future of COBOL was
this free format thing that was supposed to not have periods, really, and that
we would all get over to the new way of thinking NEXT STATEMENT. Did that not
happen?

My point is really only a small one.  If NEXT SENTENCE is supposed to fall off,
should we lead people to that as a solution, for coding requirements?

Truth is, in COBOL 85 big lengthy things with all manner of nested
whatchamacallits terminated only when we have all turned blue, became common in
some shops.  It took me a while to accept it.  It was as though I could deal
with explicit scope terminators but I could not work in the requisite
period-less-think genre, at first

I agree that it is not an abuse of standards to rely on the period as the
common terminus of a number of aborting points across a whole nest of depths.
It is ordinary COBOL, and it is elegant.  If you understand Dijkstra point, it
is quite clear in such code exactly where you are and where you are going.
Hopping down to that period is just a language facilitiy. Real simple actually,
somewhat elegant, and responsive to the original call.

But you have to get all the way into the explicit scope terminators to make it
viable, and for any one flabby old nest you only get one hop-to 'point'.

But really, I wonder, isn't this passing.  Isn't the standard trying to move to
NEXT STATEMENT and deprecate NEXT SENTENCE? Are we leading serious coders
astray if we encourage this particular technique?

Best Wishes,
Bob Rayhawk
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax Q: Looking for an EXIT...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T00:30:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pMLcb.3466$RW4.411@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<yiKcb.3309$RW4.1420@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <20030925201240.24071.00000153@mb-m14.aol.com>`

```
NEXT SENTENCE is in the ARCHAIC (not the OBSOLETE) category in the 2002
Standard, that means that at MOST it *might* (I strongly doubt it) be placed
in the OBSOLETE category in the NEXT Standard and removed in the Standard
after that.

FYI, the following is the "relevant" information from the 2002 Standard

"The purpose of the archaic language element designation is to discourage
the use in new programs of some features that are unreliable, poor
programming practice, or ill-defined -- where better programming techniques
are available in standard COBOL. These elements are classified as archaic
rather than obsolete because their use in existing programs might be too
extensive to warrant removal in the next revision of standard COBOL.

Archaic language elements are intended to remain in the next revision of
standard COBOL. There is no plan for the removal of archaic elements;
however, should their use in program inventories become insignificant, they
may be considered for designation as obsolete by future architects of
standard COBOL.
   ...

3) NEXT SENTENCE phrase in the IF and SEARCH statements. This phrase can be
confusing, especially when specified in a delimited-scope statement. It is a
common belief among users that control is transferred to a position after
the scope delimiter rather than to a separator period that follows it
somewhere. In addition, it is a common source of errors, especially for
maintenance programmers who inadvertently insert a period somewhere before
the actual terminating separator period. The CONTINUE statement and scope
delimiters can be used to accomplish the same functionality and such
constructs are clearer and less prone to error."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax Q: Looking for an EXIT...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T00:31:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0309252331.7f59bc37@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<yiKcb.3309$RW4.1420@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <20030925201240.24071.00000153@mb-m14.aol.com>`

```
rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk) wrote

> And truth is the NEXT SENTENCE really is the elegant solution the original
> poster asked for, it just flew a wee bit over the top level there.

There is nothing elegent about 'NEXT SENTENCE', it is a GO TO in
disguise to an _anonymous_ label.  Used in '74 Cobol style it is
innocuous.

The standard disallows NEXT SENTENCE and scope terminators being used
together. This means that it is perfectly valid to use NEXT SENTENCE
in any structure that could be written in '74 Cobol, but disallows it
in any way that would work differently.

In order to use it as a 'solution' in a non-standard way would have it
in a structure that relied on the full stop(s) being specifically and
unmovably placed and without any other full stops being added.

Think of it as a fragile crytal flower and that anyone who has any
opinions on whether full stops should be used or eliminated, or anyone
who thinks that they can be added or discarded as a matter of style is
in hob-nail boots stamping around.

If I saw anyone using a NEXT SENTENCE in '85 Cobol I would fire them
as being too dangerous to have anywhere near production code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax Q: Looking for an EXIT...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T16:15:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jCZcb.5072$NX3.3917@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<yiKcb.3309$RW4.1420@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <20030925201240.24071.00000153@mb-m14.aol.com> <217e491a.0309252331.7f59bc37@posting.google.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0309252331.7f59bc37@posting.google.com...
> rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk) wrote
>
<snip>
> The standard disallows NEXT SENTENCE and scope terminators being used
> together. This means that it is perfectly valid to use NEXT SENTENCE
> in any structure that could be written in '74 Cobol, but disallows it
> in any way that would work differently.
>

Should anyone be "confused" by Richard's statement, it is simply a "common
misconception".  The '85 Standard *only* disallows Next Sentence in the
immediately containing IF or SEARCH statement.  As frequently reported in
this newsgroup, the Standards committees have fully confirmed that the '85
Standard *does* provide full support for a NEXT SENTENCE with an IF
statement terminated by a containing IF statements ELSE phrase, e.g.

Read Whatever
    At End
         Perform Some-procedure
         If This = "This"
              If That = "That"
                     Next Sentence
               Else
                      Continue
         Else
              Performe  Other-procedure
          End-If
          Peform Either-This-or-not-This
     Not At End
          Perform At-End
 End-Read
 Perform Still-with-continue
     .
 Perform Next-Sentence-Goes-Here
     .

Is fully conforming '85 Standard Code
```

> Should anyone be "confused" by Richard's statement, it is simply a "common

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> statement terminated by a containing IF statements ELSE phrase, e.g.

While it is true that the committe have confirmed that this is not

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax Q: Looking for an EXIT...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** charles.stevens@unisys.com (Chuck Stevens)
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T11:21:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c60a0b82.0309261021.1fc4226b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<yiKcb.3309$RW4.1420@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <20030925201240.24071.00000153@mb-m14.aol.com>`

```
rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk) wrote in message news:<20030925201240.24071.00000153@mb-m14.aol.com>...

> Yet, help me with something here, all you standards gurus.  Is NEXT SENTENCE
> supposed to go away? Is it deprecated?

As Bill has pointed out, its use is deprecated, but until everybody
stops using it it's not going away, because ...

> ... there is still so much legacy code with
> NEXT SENTENCE, that it wouldn't matter, it will get grandfathered in to a
> number of new compiler products.  

Not just that.  Many COBOL users take existing COBOL code and "adjust"
it just enough so that it will compile with later compilers (and with
compilers compliant with later standards).  The standards committee
works hard to keep the later standard compatible with earlier ones.

> But I thought that the future of COBOL was
> this free format thing that was supposed to not have periods, really, and that
> we would all get over to the new way of thinking NEXT STATEMENT. Did that not
> happen?

Two different issues, in my view unrelated.   

Free format is available in 2002 standard COBOL.  

The avoidance of periods is a STYLE ISSUE.  

So far as I know *scope terminators* are available for every statement
that might need them in 2002 COBOL, so there is an *alternative* to
using periods.

But until periods are *no longer used* -- not only in NEW code but in
ANY code that somebody might want to compile using a latest-standard
COBOL compiler -- I would not expect any future standard to prohibit
their use, any more than I would expect any program that's been
compiling and running for decades suddenly to receive syntax errors
based solely on the presence of a GO TO.

> But really, I wonder, isn't this passing.  Isn't the standard trying to move to
> NEXT STATEMENT and deprecate NEXT SENTENCE? 

I'd say the newer standards encourage the use of scope terminators
over NEXT SENTENCE.  As part of that they've also added EXIT PARAGRAPH
and EXIT SECTION so that it's *unnecessary* to code the likes of GO TO
PARAGRAPH-NAME-EXIT.  But there is no explicit NEXT STATEMENT in COBOL
for users to prefer.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax Q: Looking for an EXIT...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T19:04:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<I40db.4230$RW4.2309@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<yiKcb.3309$RW4.1420@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <20030925201240.24071.00000153@mb-m14.aol.com> <c60a0b82.0309261021.1fc4226b@posting.google.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
news:c60a0b82.0309261021.1fc4226b@posting.google.com...
> rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk) wrote in message
news:<20030925201240.24071.00000153@mb-m14.aol.com>...
>
<snip>
>
> The avoidance of periods is a STYLE ISSUE.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

One minor clarification.

The 2002 Standard still requires a period at the end of every paragraph.  In
FACT, with the removal of A-/B-margin in the 2002 Standard, it is the ONLY
way to tell where one paragraph ends and a new paragraph header begins.

Consider the following with the 2002 Standard:

Move A to B C Display "X".
    which is semantically the same as
Move A to B C. Display "X".
    versus
Move A to B. C. Display "X".

Without the "correct" interpretation of  "periods", different pieces of code
would be "confused".

I think (but am not positive) that there was a BRIEF discussion of adding a
new "END-PROCEDURE" marker in a futute COBOL Standard, but I know it is NOT
in the plans for the next Standard, and I think (but again am not positive)
that it received little if any "support" among the vendors or users on the
Standards committees.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax Q: Looking for an EXIT...

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bl250m$nl0$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<yiKcb.3309$RW4.1420@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <20030925201240.24071.00000153@mb-m14.aol.com> <c60a0b82.0309261021.1fc4226b@posting.google.com> <I40db.4230$RW4.2309@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```

On 26-Sep-2003, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

> The 2002 Standard still requires a period at the end of every paragraph.  In
> FACT, with the removal of A-/B-margin in the 2002 Standard, it is the ONLY
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> would be "confused".

I'm curious.   What were the arguments in favor of allowing paragraph names on
the same lines as data?   Did anybody propose that there your third example
above is worth doing?   Or was it that margins were seen as not modern and that
we should give up clarity to be modern and save CoBOL by taking on superficial
resemblances to modern languages?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax Q: Looking for an EXIT...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T20:49:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wD1db.5429$NX3.577@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<yiKcb.3309$RW4.1420@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <20030925201240.24071.00000153@mb-m14.aol.com> <c60a0b82.0309261021.1fc4226b@posting.google.com> <I40db.4230$RW4.2309@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net> <bl250m$nl0$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:bl250m$nl0$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 26-Sep-2003, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>
<snip>
> I'm curious.   What were the arguments in favor of allowing paragraph
names on
> the same lines as data?   Did anybody propose that there your third
example
> above is worth doing?   Or was it that margins were seen as not modern and
that
> we should give up clarity to be modern and save CoBOL by taking on
superficial
> resemblances to modern languages?

The "desire" to have completely free-form reference format was expressed by
SOME users.  As far as I can tell, its only "major" advantage is for machine
generated COBOL source code, i.e. code that comes out of a "precompiler" or
similar software.  It should be noted that there are MANY existing
implementations of the '85 Standard that do not enforce A-/B-margin
restrictions (as an extension to the '85 Standard).  I think (but am not
certain) that they already allow paragraph headers to start on the same line
as a preceding procedure division statement.

The "necessity" for free-form (no fixed R-margin) came from problems with
"DBCS" (multi-octet, or whatever) literals and the way that differences
between systems with and without SHIFT-IN/-OUT characters worked.  However,
if the "new style" continuation system had been used, even this could have
been avoided - but new style continuation was added to the 2002 Standard
AFTER free-form reference format.

Bottom-Line:
  I (personally) see little or no motivation for allowing "headers" to
appear AFTER other data on the same source code line.  However,  I think
that this was felt to be a "style" issue with little reason NOT to allow it.

If Chuck and Thane are "watching", they may have other reasons (historical
or current) for why this "enhancement" was made to the language.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax Q: Looking for an EXIT...

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T14:36:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bl1itt$6rv$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<qkkcb.74528$PD3.4783075@nnrp1.uunet.ca> <rUFcb.431109$cF.134450@rwcrnsc53>`

```

On 25-Sep-2003, "Paul Hoberg" <nospamphoberg@att.net> wrote:

> Would "NEXT SENTENCE" accomplish what you want?
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> circumstances.

I no longer use EXIT nor NEXT SENTENCE.

There are two reasons I don't care for EXIT.   One of these is because my style
doesn't have exit paragraphs.   But the more inclusive reason is that CONTINUE
works just as well, with more flexibility.   For instance the following is valid
whether or not DEBUGING MODE is on:

 MY-READ-EXIT.
        CONTINUE.
D      DISPLAY "Debug test, reached MY-READ-EXIT".

NEXT SENTENCE is dangerous because it is a command that says "Look for the next
period and perform a go to as if you dropped through that period".     A
maintenance programmer might add or delete periods without noticing this,
changing where the drop through ends up.

Again CONTINUE is less likely to be dependent upon style.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL syntax Q: Looking for an EXIT...

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T03:47:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030925234752.24044.00000164@mb-m14.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<qkkcb.74528$PD3.4783075@nnrp1.uunet.ca>`

```

* DA SHORT CIRCUIT ALGORIDM


* Do an 88 in ws 


01  OUTTA-HERE-SW   PIC X(1) VALUE '1'.
    88 KEEP-ON-TRUCKING VAUE '1'.
    88 SHORT-CIRCUIT VAUE '0'.



*Den down in da procedure dibision * (Reaganesque, ya seez).

SET KEEP-ON-TRUCKING TO TRUE.

PERFORM PARAGRAPH-A
  WITH TEST BEFORE
  VARYING NADA FROM 1 BY 1 
   UNTIL NADA GREATER THAN 1
          OR SHORT-CICUIT.

PERFORM PARAGRAPH-B
  WITH TEST BEFORE
  VARYING NADA FROM 1 BY 1 
   UNTIL NADA GREATER THAN 1
          OR SHORT-CICUIT.

-----
PERFORM PARAGRAPH-AD-INFINITUM
  WITH TEST BEFORE
  VARYING NADA FROM 1 BY 1 
   UNTIL NADA GREATER THAN 1
          OR SHORT-CICUIT.




anywhere in the several paragraphs you would like to cut this short just

   SET SHORT-CIRCUIT TO TRUE


Hope you are still tuned in.


All of that can done with  in-line performs if you do not want separate
paragraphs 



BIG-OLE-PARAGRAPH.

* this is segment A of my masterpiece
PERFORM 
  WITH TEST BEFORE
  VARYING NADA FROM 1 BY 1 
   UNTIL NADA GREATER THAN 1
          OR SHORT-CICUIT
*   * put a bunch of code here with
*   * the occassional short-circuit    
END-PERFORM.

* this is segment B of my masterpiece
PERFORM 
  WITH TEST BEFORE
  VARYING NADA FROM 1 BY 1 
   UNTIL NADA GREATER THAN 1
          OR SHORT-CICUIT
*   * put a bunch of code here with
*   * the occassional short-circuit    
END-PERFORM.

-----

* this is segment ad infinitum of my masterpiece
PERFORM 
  WITH TEST BEFORE
  VARYING NADA FROM 1 BY 1 
   UNTIL NADA GREATER THAN 1
          OR SHORT-CICUIT
*   * put a bunch of code here with
*   * the occassional short-circuit    
END-PERFORM.

So da principle of dis ting is that just as exiting of a paragraph or a
paragraph sequence is not controled by da EXIT statement, but instead is under
the aegis of a simple PERFORM or indeed the THRU clause; so it is that an UNTIL
clause is a GOTO statement of the variety called an abort goto. It is
sanctioned even by extremist goto-less-establishmentarians.

An UNTIL clause is like a GOTO up at the begining of a coding stucture that
does a goto to the bottom of the sturcture. An UNTIL clause is a conditional
GOTO. To put it into Java parlance, it is like a label-less goto. Akin, but
only akin to a break.

Actually UNTIL clauses automate technical workers, and are one of the many very
nice things about COBOL. THe coder does not have to invent a label, nor the a
go verv of any kind, just the conditionals.  UNTIL clause are like Dijkstra's
'guards'.

But that only solves 1/2 of your posted problem; to wit how to by pass major
segments of code once the short-circuit sparks.  You still need to get out of
the code nest where you are, and for that you cannot be reluctant to nest, the
nesting will automate your exiting from the current segment.  Nest, nest, nest,
don't worry about indentation in the begining, who cares! Nest! Explicitly
terminate scope with explicit scope terminators. Nest!

But if you are dealing with a desired side-effect from a return value, you
cannot pull up short without an actual GOTO or a conditional that hides a GOTO
in NEXT SENTENCE or the like.

The general guide is do not have code that you would fallthru if you don't want
to sometimes. Put that lower code off in a paragraph and perfrom that only if
affirmative conditions still obtain.

Structure your code, forget about the indentation. Nest with heavy use of
explicit scope terminators, and put code that you may not want to fallthru into
isolated paragraphs conditionally performed.

But you have learned a good lesson if you now know that EXIT does nothing. Your
next lesson is that the yada-yada-yada-EXIT paragraphs are usually useless, and
that if you take anytime at all to play your program the THRU clauses disappear
(because you nest and isolate). 

If you have code that you are worried about falling thru when you do not want
to then you have not structured the program enough. The fact that the
discriminant is the return code from called submodules is just a distraction.
Really.

Structure, structure and structure.  If you are falling through and that
bothers you, then you are looking at you, ... the you that has not yet
structured that program.  It's just a mirror.


Best Wishes, 
Bob Rayhawk
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax Q: Looking for an EXIT...

- **From:** clvrmnky <clvrmnky@coldmail.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T11:49:43-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<reZcb.75260$PD3.4793825@nnrp1.uunet.ca>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<20030925234752.24044.00000164@mb-m14.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<qkkcb.74528$PD3.4783075@nnrp1.uunet.ca> <20030925234752.24044.00000164@mb-m14.aol.com>`

```
RKRayhawk wrote:
[...]
> But that only solves 1/2 of your posted problem; to wit how to by pass major
> segments of code once the short-circuit sparks.  You still need to get out of
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Oh, I'm nesting like a bird.  However, my style is to handle exceptions 
up front, and to groups nests logically.  That is, if I have a single 
thing that is not related to a group of logical decisions, I tend not to 
  combine that in with them.  I prefer to handle it as a separate 
connection.

> But if you are dealing with a desired side-effect from a return value, you
> cannot pull up short without an actual GOTO or a conditional that hides a GOTO
> in NEXT SENTENCE or the like.
>
Unfortunately, yes.  As far as "structured" goes, I'm comfortable with a 
section of code having more than one exit point: a few (where few >= 0) 
for obvious exceptions, and one at the end for "normal" processing.

Anyway, in some cases I've added a new layer to the main "normal" 
processing logic that asks "did all the hairy stuff at the beginning 
work out?".  A false answer just puts me at the end of the procedure.

I've also tught myself the magic of subprograms, and split some of my 
workhorse blocks into proper functions.
```

Do you have access to a relational database?  Some include this as a 
function, so you could do something like...

Exec SQL
     Select MD5(:My-Hashable-Text)
       Into :My-Hash
       From [Any_Table_Name]
End-Exec

But then again, can't you call .NET stuff from NetExpress?

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Link to CMD.EXE or NET.EXE on Win

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T00:22:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030925202207.24071.00000156@mb-m14.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<e6278d73.0309241137.111369fc@posting.google.com>`

```
Tell us more about your 'normal tricks'.

Actually, you are not calling other COBOL porgrams in the cases where you are
succeeding. Instead you are calling submodules, the interface into these
matches the expectation of the CALL.

If you can infact call other languages from your development environment, then
create a C language object module (one with just the function you can call from
COBOL, no main() in the C routine). Have that intermediate do a C
  exec() 
or 
  spawn()

Some COBOLs may not have that facility built-in. You will need to learn enough
about calling submodules across languages to get your COBOL parms down to the C
intermediary, so that it can do the exec() or spawn() you want dynamically.

There may be system calls or execute verbs in some COBOLs, and maybe someone
knows that particular in VACOBOL.  But the various *.EXE files you name are not
subroutines so the bindings to those do not look at all like a COBOL sub
program.  

Tell us more about your 'tricks.'
```

<a id="t-aa10bcbab3"></a>
## Thread: from Martin A. Goetz with love... — 33 this month / 35 total

### from Martin A. Goetz with love...

- **From:** KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM (KELLIE FITTON)
- **Date:** 2003-09-24T15:17:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41758a6b.0309241417.79dde1d9@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello everyone,

here is a great article from Martin A. Goetz regarding OOP programming.
Kellie.

Futile search for the fountain of reuse

Martin A. Goetz
Goetz, a software industry pioneer who received the first U.S. software patent
(in 1968), is a management consultant in Teaneck, N.J.
592 words
20 January 1997
ComputerWorld
33,b
English
(Copyright 1997 by Computerworld, Inc. All rights reserved.)

A recent Computerworld article [``Reuse revealed,'' Oct. 14, 1996] correctly
identified the risks of chasing the dream of object-oriented, reusable code.
They include ``analysis paralysis,'' high up-front investments in time and
money and the chance that reuse will never spread across the organization.

But the article suggests these are just hurdles to be overcome. Not so.
Attempts at reusing software objects have been failing for years. Reusability
will never become widespread.

For starters, object-oriented programming (OOP) is difficult to learn. Cobol
and PL/1 were successful largely because their English-like, procedural
statements made them very easy to learn. Programmers could write application
logic in a straightforward, easily understood and easily maintainable manner.
By contrast, OOP demands that programmers and designers learn an entirely new
and unnatural way of looking at the application as they search for reusable
objects.

Don't be fooled by the steady stream of positive verbiage about the theoretical
potential of OOP and reusable code. In the real IS world, reuse doesn't work.
Study after study shows that most companies that tried OOP have abandoned it.
And those that haven't have yet to develop a significant number of reusable
objects.

Why? The simple truth is that trying to find and maintain reusable code for
enterprise applications just isn't cost-effective or practical. IS directors
should ask themselves why there are so few successes and so many failures in
companies that attempted to develop reusable code.

Of course, all IS organizations want to increase productivity and reduce costs
and reusing code promises that. But it doesn't deliver. Most enterprise
applications are constantly changing to reflect new company policies, practices
and procedures. In a large organization, there are complex interactions among
these applications. It may be possible to find reusable objects in this
environment, but it will be difficult and very time-consuming.

The applications of midsize organizations typically change even faster. Even if
reusable objects can be identified, they probably won't be reusable over a long
period of time. Smaller organizations may be the best candidates for
developing reusable objects, but the key test is whether the objects remain
viable over the life of the application. Today's ``success'' might be tomorrow's
software-maintenance nightmare. Although experts have strategies for
identifying reusable code, they haven't solved the problem of changing the
embedded objects to reflect organizational changes, business mergers or new
government regulations.

History shows that the time-tested way to improve on third-generation languages
such as Cobol which has seen better days is through enterprise fourth-generation
languages (4GL). They are effective for the life of applications and have the
benefit of being as easy to learn as Cobol. Why wade through the swamp trying
to find reusable code when 4GLs are a proven alternative to Cobol for improving
productivity?

OOP is at least 10 years old. It's been ``successful'' in a few small IS shops
with a handful of OOP gurus. But these are isolated cases. Analysts at firms
such as Gartner Group caution that there is no theoretical support for OOP's
claims, that OOP is incompatible with relational database technology and that
there have been relatively few success stories.

The seductive ``benefits'' of reusability may seem heaven-sent, but the devil
is in the details.

Martin A. Goetz
```

#### ↳ Re: from Martin A. Goetz with love...

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-09-25T11:53:13+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f724ba1_5@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309241417.79dde1d9@posting.google.com>`

```
Seven years later, he is probably writhing with embarrassment at the success
of OO and components.

His 4GL solution has not come to pass in any significant way, Java (a
completely OO based language) has taken the World by storm, and ALL of the
new languages appearing are Object Oriented.

(The only resistance has been from Fortress COBOL...)

Bet he wishes he'd never written it...<G>

Pete.

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3f724ba1_5@news.athenanews.com...
> Seven years later, he is probably writhing with embarrassment at the
success
> of OO and components.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

All languages in the domains in which you are working.

QCL (Quantum Computation Language) tries to fill this gap: QCL is a high
level, architecture independent programming language for quantum computers,
with a syntax derived from classical procedural languages like C or Pascal.
This allows for the complete implementation and simulation of quantum
algorithms (including classical components) in one consistent formalism.

XPL - Our task is to create an eXtensible Programming Language framework, in
which XML documents will be source code, processed by XSLT and by our own
tree operations.  (More debatable as it will could be argued that much XML
has an OO premise).

I'm sure he's not embarrassed - he's been in the business longer than you
have - the older he gets the more errors in judgment are forgiven :-)

SQL is a very pervasive 4GL.

Also - if one expands the definition of 4GL a little and moves it in to the
21st century...we could argue that XML/UML etc are 4GL. TogetherJ/XDE etc
all *generate* code based on something more "user friendly".

Check out www.asksam.com for a little known (very successful) company in a
FLorida backwater with a free form database....now this is what...a 5GL?


JCE
```

Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:

> Seven years later, he is probably writhing with embarrassment at the success
> of OO and components.
>
Heh.  I'm surprised when folks commit to what they see in their crystal 
balls.  OO has certainly been more successful than this article would 
want to believe.

> His 4GL solution has not come to pass in any significant way, Java (a
> completely OO based language) has taken the World by storm, and ALL of the
> new languages appearing are Object Oriented.
> 
Java is not _completely_ object-oriented.  Sun made some design choices 
that were specifically non-OO for various reasons.    By most 
definitions, Java is a bit of a hybrid language due to the varied 
requirements.

For example, Java classes themselves are not strictly objects.  Not 
everything can be overloaded.

Anyway, that's what my hair-splitting machine suggests...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: from Martin A. Goetz with love...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** clvrmnky <clvrmnky@coldmail.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T11:40:09-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<t5Zcb.75256$PD3.4793877@nnrp1.uunet.ca>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3f740af7_9@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309241417.79dde1d9@posting.google.com> <3f724ba1_5@news.athenanews.com> <q6Jcb.75007$PD3.4790143@nnrp1.uunet.ca> <3f740af7_9@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
[...]
> As I am now becoming more follicularly challenged each day, I can't afford
> to split hairs...<G>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
It's an important distinction for some.  BTW, it is not a criticism of 
Java when I say it isn't completely object-oriented when compared with 
many other languages.

Sun made some sensible choices to bring OO to a world that needed it, 
but was not quite ready.  Regardless of what people say, Java is 
intended to be a everyday general-purpose development tool, and the 
behaviour an syntax needed to mirror some aspects of well-known (and 
well-loved) tools like C.

As long as you stick closely to good OO practices with Java, you can 
consider it "pure" enough.  However, things like primitives such as int 
(with it's Integer wrapper) and the fact that each class you create is 
given a special static method to make things work makes it not as pure 
as it could be.

The real deciding factor is that it is possible to write a functional 
program in Java, whereas this is not possible in Smalltalk or 
Objective-C (perhaps Eiffel, as well?).

Java is certainly a practical implementation of a pure OO model, and one 
that is pretty close to Smalltalk.  Objective-C is a _little_ bit 
closer.  Since no one is handing out prizes for this distinction, 
however, this is only a matter of interest to those who care.
```

 clvrmnky clvrmnky@coldmail.com.invalid

>>

If a COBOL compiler then allows FACTORY attributes and methods, is it not
'pure' Object Oriented Programming?

understand it better as principle. What are we gaining if we limit the
development environment to 'pure',

I am going to have to backtack a bit here because of you, daw gone it, now I
need to glance back at Simula to see if it had class statics.... If it did then
the notion of 'pure' object orientation is later and must be considered an
adulteration of the base strategies.  But maybe not.  Maybe even Simula was
'pure' and had no FACTORY capabilities.

Darn, now I have to do some work.

But anyway, what are we to gain from your distinction?  Is our code more
reliable? Are workers more efficient? 

If there is something to gain, then we could just have a shop standard that
smacks you when you code Java static or COBOL FACTORY.
Surely I am not understanding you. Tell us more.

Best Wishes,
Bob Rayhawk

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: from Martin A. Goetz with love...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-09-27T00:26:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030926202623.12504.00000187@mb-m04.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<20030926124157.29125.00000178@mb-m22.aol.com>`

```
I have done one half of my homework in reviewing the basics of ALGOL and SIMULA
grammar, and am just amazed at how much a little Pascal experience allows me
the 'see' the code of the earlier compiler technologies.

I still have to get more solidly with the later SIMULA syntax to know if that
had statics. But I already see in anything Pascal like that a basic program has
variables that can be passsed BY NAME as it is said in that genre, .... it is a
fact that that is not the exact same thing as by reference. But such vars are
static in the Java sense, atleast to the extent that all PROCEDURES invoked in
the pre-Smalltalk compilers had local vars (_almost_ instance attributes) and
the input parms that were BY NAME (effectively by reference) allowed the
invokee to get to the invoker's vars.

This foundation makes me think that the later SIMULA compiler probably had
statics in the CLASS as well. Yet I still must get to understand what Ole-Johan
Dahl did with his memory model entirely. Obviously he achieved instantiation of
multiple objects of a single class. But did they or did they not let those
objects get to class static vars?  I bet they did.  

So for me the question is still outstanding (though really ansered long ago),
if the original stuff had statics, then isn't that 'pure', and any
interpretation or simplification that left that out is just confused?!

I believe I will find COBOL FACTORY in Dahl's original work on a memory model
beyound simple ALGOL stacks. But I'll have to do the other half of my homework.

Best Wishes to All
Bob Rayhawk
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: from Martin A. Goetz with love...

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T10:23:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0309260923.140ead84@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309241417.79dde1d9@posting.google.com> <3f724ba1_5@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3f724ba1_5@news.athenanews.com>...
> Seven years later, he is probably writhing with embarrassment at the success
> of OO and components.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This seems like as good a place as any to add my comments.

There are several issues here in this mish mash.

1 - Failure or success of OO specifically the REUSE area
2 - Components
3 - New languages

Let's take #1 - the purported value of reuse is in the fact that it is
SUPPOSED to reduce the testing cycle.  OO Adoption has (reportedly at
least here) not resulted in that - in fact an underlying change in an
object causes a cascade of required testing.

My take on this is three fold.  First - If one had PROPERLY reused the
object one would NOT be changing the underlying object at all - one
would be using the things in the object that are pertinent and where
change is necessary using inheritence to override or expand with a new
object based on the reused object.

Second - It all depends on WHERE you use the object. Objects SHOULD
operate in isolation - having  minimim of dependencies.  They should
be self contained.  Makign them such makes for much more stable reuse
and does gain from the benefits of such.

Third - CAREFUL control measures should be in place such that
underlying objects ARE NOT CHANGED - it's the failure to manage the
code that leads to the perception of OO failing to deliver on the
promise - not OO or the concepts behind it.

OO is a different way of thinking.  Once you get it - you won't go
back.

Now #2 - Components - These are king.  It is the OO style COMMON
interface that makes components more ubiquitous today.  Components owe
a lot to OO - even if the compoents themselves are not specifically
OO!

And finally #3 - the newest language I have been using is Actionscript
2.0 from Macromedia in Flash MX 2004 - this is most OO - and in fact
as I see new languages introduces I have yet to see a NON OO language
NEWLY INTRODUCED.  I agree with Pete here - the advantage is that once
you know one of these generally ECMA script style OO languages -
others are very very easy to pick up.

To sum up - the success in Reuse is not going to be OO in general -
the REAL success of reuse is in Components.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: from Martin A. Goetz with love...

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

Very difficult to do when the component is a business rule.


> Now #2 - Components - These are king.  It is the OO style COMMON
> interface that makes components more ubiquitous today.  Components owe
> a lot to OO - even if the compoents themselves are not specifically
> OO!

Could you explain this further?    What is a component and what is an object?  
Can we use these for business rules that are subject for change?

(I think we all understand the advantage of using components/objects to do such
things as draw a box or FTP a file).


> And finally #3 - the newest language I have been using is Actionscript
> 2.0 from Macromedia in Flash MX 2004 - this is most OO - and in fact
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

languages and they all work together.
