###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: from Martin A. Goetz with love...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-09-30T00:59:58+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f784a0d_8@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309241417.79dde1d9@posting.google.com> <3f724ba1_5@news.athenanews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0309260923.140ead84@posting.google.com> <bl20ne$cb9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:bl20ne$cb9$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 26-Sep-2003, thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No it isn't Howard. It WOULD be if you tried to encapsulate a particular
business rule statically in a component, but you don't do that. (Well, you
don't if you are wise...)

I'll try and demonstrate with an example...

BUSINESS RULE: (For purchase of Widgets)

1. People can only buy Widgets on a Thursday, unless they buy blue ones.

2. They MUST buy a minimum of 20 and they cannot have them delivered.

3. They are to be encouraged to buy blue ones as we have 2,000,000 excess of
them. We therefore offer a discount on Blue ones, depending on the quantity
they intend to purchase.

Now, you could build a Widget Purchase Checking component that would
encompass all those rules (just as you could write the same thing in
Standard COBOL). But you just KNOW it is going to change...

So... you start thinking about processes and properties...

What properties affect the transaction?

01 MoveThisColour  pic x.
01 MinimumQuantity pic s9(3) comp.
01 Delivery               pic x.

What about a scale of  Discounts in a DB table somewhere?  (Based on
Quantity or any other criterion you like...)

01 ApplyDiscount     pic x.

Now, the Methods...

1. Validate (Returns OK or NOT OK)
2. BestDeal (suggests the most advantageous transaction to the customer...)
3. Show all properties.

I decide that this will be an Automation Server component (COM), so it will
have automatic GET and SET methods that allow individual Properties to be
adjusted and retrieved.(I don't have to write these... )

You use it by setting the properties you want and invoking its methods.
Start by getting an instance and setting the baselines (ONCE in the
process)...

Like this...

invoke WidgetClass "CREATE-OBJECT" using        WidgetCOMProgID
                                                                 returning
widgetObj
end-invoke

invoke widgetObj "SET-MoveThisColour"  using  "Blue" end-invoke
invoke widgetObj "SET-MinimumQuantity" using  20      end-invoke

[The above has been amended slightly for the sake of clarity, it cannot be
implemented as written, but the changes to make it viable are trivial.]

Now it is ready to process transactions...

Tran1:

(Today is a Thursday...)

Quantity = 50
Unit Price= $4.00
Colour =  Red

invoke widgetObj "Validate"   using transaction-data
                                              returning  yes-or-no
end-invoke

(The validate function will return OK  and set the Delivery Property to
FALSE)

Tran2:

(Today is Friday...)

Quantity = 50
Unit Price= $4.00
Colour =  Red

invoke widgetObj "Validate"   using transaction-data
                                              returning  yes-or-no
end-invoke

(The validate function will return  NOT OK  and set the Delivery Property to
FALSE)

The Customer is informed that due to the Business Rules he cannot make the
purchase, and the system invokes the best deal available Method.

invoke widgetObj "BestDeal"   using transaction-data
                                                returning  suggestion-data
end-invoke

The suggestion-data might be:

Quantity = 50
Unit Price= $3.90
Colour =  Blue

With delivery tossed in...

(This Method will assess the colour we are trying to move and the discounts
available, and measure the cost of delivery. At some later time when the
rules change we may decide to do this differently or manually. The nice
thing about components is that you don't HAVE to invoke the methods.)

The point about all the above is that the component is flexible. If the
rules change it still does its job. It is not intended to be amended or
maintained

If you wanted to print a sales docket it would be tempting to add this
functionality as another Method. But it would be wrong.Instead you would
produce another component that might well use some of the properties of this
one (retrieved by GET- as shown above) and would utilise the transaction
data also, to produce a docket for the sale. You then use the two components
in unison. If the validation fails you don't print the docket. The
functionality is simple and encapsulated.

In the time I have available I have not attempted to make this
comprehensive. It is flawed and those who are so inclined will find many
faults with it. But it will hopefully give an inkling of the way reusable
components can be designed and built.


>
> > Now #2 - Components - These are king.  It is the OO style COMMON
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Could you explain this further?    What is a component and what is an
object?
> Can we use these for business rules that are subject for change?
>

There are many definitions as you well know, Howard.

I use "component" in the sense of  a piece of encapsulated functionality
that can be dropped on a web page or placed on a desk top or invoked from a
program, due to its wondrous interface. I also see them as the building
blocks of systems.

You can certainly use them for business rules that are subject to change as
described above.


> (I think we all understand the advantage of using components/objects to do
such
> things as draw a box or FTP a file).
>

How can you use the terms if you profess to have no definition of them <G>?
You already have a concept.

>
> > And finally #3 - the newest language I have been using is Actionscript
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Certainly a huge advantage of OO is that we can pick and choose from
different
> languages and they all work together.

Pete.
```

```

On 29-Sep-2003, "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> No it isn't Howard. It WOULD be if you tried to encapsulate a particular
> business rule statically in a component, but you don't do that. (Well, you

>
> I'll try and demonstrate with an example...

Read, printed and saved.    Thanks.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: from Martin A. Goetz with love...

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-09-27T04:26:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c384af$79634d00$0595f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309241417.79dde1d9@posting.google.com> <3f724ba1_5@news.athenanews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0309260923.140ead84@posting.google.com>`

```
Please see comment below.

Thane Hubbell <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in article
<bfdfc3e8.0309260923.140ead84@posting.google.com>...

<snip>
> 
> My take on this is three fold.  First - If one had PROPERLY reused the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> object based on the reused object.

I thought that encapsulation and data hiding were supposed to be advantages
of objects and components.

As long as the interface to them does not change, you should be able to
change the underlying implementation without any users noticing.

For example I could implement a stack as a table and then later change the
implementation to a linked list and as long as the interface did not change
the user would not know the difference, unless there was some other
critical implied dependancy, such as the amount of storage used, or the
amount of time used.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: from Martin A. Goetz with love...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-09-28T07:52:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0309280652.7b818d47@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309241417.79dde1d9@posting.google.com> <3f724ba1_5@news.athenanews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0309260923.140ead84@posting.google.com> <01c384af$79634d00$0595f243@chottel>`

```
Sure - this is possible - but it also take the chance of introducing
broken code.  Better to create a new object - overriding the existing
method with your own - fully parallell test - then update the
original.  It's this testing requirement tht's the bugaboo - how do
you know you got it right unless you parallel test everything that
used the class?


"Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in message news:<01c384af$79634d00$0595f243@chottel>...

> 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: from Martin A. Goetz with love...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-09-28T20:17:23-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0309281917.4fafee73@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309241417.79dde1d9@posting.google.com> <3f724ba1_5@news.athenanews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0309260923.140ead84@posting.google.com> <01c384af$79634d00$0595f243@chottel> <bfdfc3e8.0309280652.7b818d47@posting.google.com> <y1Ddb.80$Tu2.33377@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald,

I think here we may be mixing "components" vs "objects".  Only in the
.NET environment am I aware that one could use COBOL to override a VB
method - or C# to override a COBOL method - and in that environment,
the source code is not necessary - well, that's not STRICTLY true -
the Programmer created source code is not necessary - IL source code
still is.  (Intermediate Language that all source is "compiled"
(translated might be better) into.

I was speaking strictly in the realm of a contained
development/operating environment - not in the world of exchangable
components.



"Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message news:<y1Ddb.80$Tu2.33377@news20.bellglobal.com>...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: from Martin A. Goetz with love...

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-09-29T04:07:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030929000724.24316.00000604@mb-m03.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0309281917.4fafee73@posting.google.com>`

```
clvrmnky (clvrmnky@coldmail.com.invalid)
Date: 2003-09-26 08:40:15 PST 

Was complementary about Java and commented

<<
As long as you stick closely to good OO practices with Java, you can 
consider it "pure" enough.  However, things like primitives such as int 
(with it's Integer wrapper) and the fact that each class you create is 
given a special static method to make things work makes it not as pure 
as it could be.
>>

I had asked some questions about that view, and think that I went off
in the wrong direction. I am sorry for that.  Java does have some tricks
at the base level, and it is using static methods to get certain behaviors.

I had misunderstood clvrmnky's comment, thinking that there was an assertion
that pure OOP tools do not have statics. Having reviewed his original posting 
more carefully, I now see the need to correct my post.

Best Wishes
Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: from Martin A. Goetz with love...

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-29T05:11:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<I9Pdb.7544$RW4.1540@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309241417.79dde1d9@posting.google.com> <3f724ba1_5@news.athenanews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0309260923.140ead84@posting.google.com> <01c384af$79634d00$0595f243@chottel> <bfdfc3e8.0309280652.7b818d47@posting.google.com> <y1Ddb.80$Tu2.33377@news20.bellglobal.com> <bfdfc3e8.0309281917.4fafee73@posting.google.com>`

```
I won't swear to C#, but it certainly possible for COBOL to override a JAVA
method (as long as it isn't static) in an IBM mainframe environment. See:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/5.1.3.5

what that has in "common" with .NET is that there is a "common run-time" (LE
on the mainframe). My guess is that wherever multiple OO languages "share a
run-time environment" that having multi-language method overriding MAY be
available.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: from Martin A. Goetz with love...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-30T08:59:25-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-CBE055.08592530092003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309241417.79dde1d9@posting.google.com> <3f724ba1_5@news.athenanews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0309260923.140ead84@posting.google.com> <01c384af$79634d00$0595f243@chottel> <bfdfc3e8.0309280652.7b818d47@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <bfdfc3e8.0309280652.7b818d47@posting.google.com>,
 thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote:

> Sure - this is possible - but it also take the chance of introducing
> broken code.  Better to create a new object - overriding the existing
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Not everything -- only the public interface.  The implementation can 
radically change as long as that stays the same.  This is usually a much 
smaller set of tests than the full object.

This reminds me of the Engineers and Mathematicians on the dance floor 
story.  After testing some large quantity of products using the public 
interface you allow for the slight, but still non-zero, risk that some 
other product might break -- then you take that risk.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: from Martin A. Goetz with love...

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-09-29T23:26:40+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f783433_4@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309241417.79dde1d9@posting.google.com> <3f724ba1_5@news.athenanews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0309260923.140ead84@posting.google.com>`

```

"Thane Hubbell" <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:bfdfc3e8.0309260923.140ead84@posting.google.com...
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:<3f724ba1_5@news.athenanews.com>...
> > Seven years later, he is probably writhing with embarrassment at the
success
> > of OO and components.
> >
> > His 4GL solution has not come to pass in any significant way, Java (a
> > completely OO based language) has taken the World by storm, and ALL of
the
> > new languages appearing are Object Oriented.
> >
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> object based on the reused object.

ABSOLUTELY!!!

I learned this when I first started extending OO into Components. Nowadays,
I don't even add Methods or Properties to existing components. Instead, I
spend more time on design and utilise imagination and insight to envision
EVERYTHING that particular component is going to do. If it turns out I
missed something (fortunately, this doesn't happen often) I penalise myself
by having to write a new component with the extra functionality. (Of course,

 I use Inheritance from the underlying class that the component was based
on).

Can't emphasise enough my agreement with your position on this, Thane.

Components are designed and written ONCE. After that it is Reuse.
>
> Second - It all depends on WHERE you use the object. Objects SHOULD
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Vehemently agree again...

> Third - CAREFUL control measures should be in place such that
> underlying objects ARE NOT CHANGED - it's the failure to manage the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You said it all <G>!!!

> Now #2 - Components - These are king.  It is the OO style COMMON
> interface that makes components more ubiquitous today.  Components owe
> a lot to OO - even if the compoents themselves are not specifically
> OO!
>

Agreed. But by far the majority of Components ARE OO... I would go so far as
to say that if it weren't for OO we wouldn't have components. For thirty
years before OO, nobody considered wrapping called modules so they could be
used anywhere... It was the advent of OO that gave the necessary impetus. I
have seen Components (particularly COM, and lately, COM+ and DCOM) as being
the logical extension of OO, and, for me, the whole point of learning OO has
been to attain the ability to create components. If OO COBOL had not been
provided, I'd be doing it with Java Beans <G>

> And finally #3 - the newest language I have been using is Actionscript
> 2.0 from Macromedia in Flash MX 2004 - this is most OO - and in fact
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> the REAL success of reuse is in Components.

I can't add anything to this except that I agree wholeheartedly.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: from Martin A. Goetz with love...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-29T15:49:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NvYdb.31480$eS5.24213@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309241417.79dde1d9@posting.google.com> <3f724ba1_5@news.athenanews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0309260923.140ead84@posting.google.com> <3f783433_4@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3f783433_4@news.athenanews.com...
> "Thane Hubbell" <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
> news:bfdfc3e8.0309260923.140ead84@posting.google.com...
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

Explain this to people who setup EJBs and XML parsers in the last 4-5 years
and were surprised to find some changes when they upgraded to the new
Application Server.....

Remember when JDBC was king...now if you don't use the DataSource you messed
up....

The "fundamental" APIs for a language as implement as Java, and as common
place as XML has changed repeatedly in the last few years.

Have you ever had someone install a JDK 1.4 to do you a favour and "break"
your 1.3 code?

Have you ever run into a component that used "deprecated" methods? Would you
even know? Would you care? I think you would when they take it out of the
API.

The component you design may be rock solid....the underlying component may
not be. It is as rock solid as the base it was built on.
XML is not rock solid, DBMS's are not rock solid.

Have you ever used something like log4j and then have them tell you that
....well we're changing the package name and the main class names? Did it
impact? Not really as they were kind enough to support an intermediary
stage....does it require retesting...(btw this is intended to knock log4j
...it's an "excellent" project)

Say, for example, you Create a component that uses another component to READ
a RULE from a DB2 table.  You implement that rule - you are thinking ahead,
you will never have to change - you are "protected" by the
unchangeable...unvariable component model.

Now the DB2 table has changed to a SQL Server table....what changed here:
your component/their component?  what is impacted: your component/their
component?
What are the differences if you inherited versus implemented?

Say the component you used was thoughtful enough to abstract away from DB2
and could just drop into an XML interface...this is still a change to your
environment.
Do you need to retest now that you've changed the environment - probably
not....should you? depends on the business you are in.  Try telling a bank
transfer system you just changed their transfer system without testing it
because they went from FTP to MQSeries...but it's a reusable component.
Your manager may be ok with it, but any system auditor will not.

There is *IMHO* NO SUCH THING AS A PROPERLY DEFINED UNCHANGING UNDERLYING
OBJECT unless that object just wraps a primitive - and even then you may
have a hardware dependency - say they switch from machine (a) to machine (b)
and your underlying component uses some kind of byte order...
(You don't know that they don't because you only know the interface and not
the internals).

I'm being somewhat contentious but that's only because I'm a fence sitter.
I don't discard the concepts of "perfect" design; however, I just don't
believe we'll ever see anyone get beyond "imperfect" design...maybe some
smart ones will get to "almost perfect" design.  Not that I don't think it's
worth aspiring to perfection....

JCE
```

```
of

> Explain this to people who setup EJBs and XML parsers in the last 4-5
years
> and were surprised to find some changes when they upgraded to the new
> Application Server.....
>
> Remember when JDBC was king...now if you don't use the DataSource you
messed
> up....
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
No, but I'm sure it could happen...

> Have you ever run into a component that used "deprecated" methods? Would
you
> even know? Would you care? I think you would when they take it out of the
> API.

None of mine do, but I accept that others might. As I only have object code
of third party components it isn't a problem...

>
> The component you design may be rock solid....the underlying component may
> not be. It is as rock solid as the base it was built on.

Mine are built on COBOL (mostly). I don't care what others are built on. So,
far, (3 years now) I have never had a third party component fail, and my own
are pretty stable also.

> XML is not rock solid, DBMS's are not rock solid.
>

I don't build components on either of them. All my RDB access is via ODBC
and I DON'T use DB Procedures (I am reviewing this last at the moment...)

> Have you ever used something like log4j and then have them tell you that
> ....well we're changing the package name and the main class names? Did it
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
No.

> Say, for example, you Create a component that uses another component to
READ
> a RULE from a DB2 table.  You implement that rule - you are thinking
ahead,
> you will never have to change - you are "protected" by the
> unchangeable...unvariable component model.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
No problem. I have a separation layer from the DB.

> Say the component you used was thoughtful enough to abstract away from DB2
> and could just drop into an XML interface...this is still a change to your
> environment.

Only if I allow it <G>...

> Do you need to retest now that you've changed the environment - probably
> not....should you? depends on the business you are in.  Try telling a bank
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> have a hardware dependency - say they switch from machine (a) to machine
(b)
> and your underlying component uses some kind of byte order...
> (You don't know that they don't because you only know the interface and
not
> the internals).

Then here we differ. I believe functionality CAN be encapsulated into a
component and it won't change. I have a date component, for instance, that
validates dates in any format and gives me them in 9 other formats. It won't
change. (Unless the whole world decides to leave the Gregorian Calendar, in
which case, I'll simply write a new component; would NOT maintain the old
one...) This same date functionality would slot seamlessly into my
applications and the impact would be minimal or zero.

I think the difference in our positions arises because (I see from your
post) that you are very concerned with the nitty gritty of technology and
your components tend to be pieces of a given technology encapsulated and
wrapped. I don't do that. My components are encapsulated pieces of
FUNCTIONALITY. Most of them are written in OO COBOL. Even if they weren't,
they would still be transportable to THE ENVIRONMENTS WHICH I WISH TO USE...
(namely, the Web and the Windows desktop.)

>
> I'm being somewhat contentious but that's only because I'm a fence sitter.
> I don't discard the concepts of "perfect" design; however, I just don't
> believe we'll ever see anyone get beyond "imperfect" design...maybe some
> smart ones will get to "almost perfect" design.  Not that I don't think
it's
> worth aspiring to perfection....
>
I believe this can be attained by iteration and interaction as I have said
here on numerous occasions. It isn't "perfect design" that is the goal; it
is "delivery of functionality that does what we want".

I really have no issues with your post and we are probably closer on this
than you might think.

As to being perfect...<G>

"Heaven is being perfect. Perfect speed is just being there." - JLS

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: from Martin A. Goetz with love...

```
code
> of third party components it isn't a problem...

You always are telling us this about object vs source.  You know what? I
think this time it sunk in.  Still not sure I buy it...but I understand
better now.

> I don't build components on either of them. All my RDB access is via ODBC
> and I DON'T use DB Procedures (I am reviewing this last at the moment...)

From MS.
Win32::ODBC was written to conform to the ODBC API 2.0 specification. Since
ODBC 3.0 is backward compatible it should not cause any compatibility
problems. To borrow from Microsoft's web pages...

Notice the word "should" :-)

> > Now the DB2 table has changed to a SQL Server table....what changed
here:
> > your component/their component?  what is impacted: your component/their
> > component?
> > What are the differences if you inherited versus implemented?
> No problem. I have a separation layer from the DB.
But you assume the separation layer is not changing..see above.

> > Say the component you used was thoughtful enough to abstract away from
DB2
> > and could just drop into an XML interface...this is still a change to
your
> > environment.
> Only if I allow it <G>...
I term environment much more loosely.  If you change from Gregorian to
Darmorian, you switch components, your environment has changed (maybe
seamlessly).
In financial worlds, that still mean retest :-)

> > Do you need to retest now that you've changed the environment - probably
> > not....should you? depends on the business you are in.  Try telling a
bank
> > transfer system you just changed their transfer system without testing
it
> > because they went from FTP to MQSeries...but it's a reusable component.
> > Your manager may be ok with it, but any system auditor will not.
> >
> > There is *IMHO* NO SUCH THING AS A PROPERLY DEFINED UNCHANGING
UNDERLYING
> > OBJECT unless that object just wraps a primitive - and even then you may
> > have a hardware dependency - say they switch from machine (a) to machine
(b)
> > and your underlying component uses some kind of byte order...
> > (You don't know that they don't because you only know the interface and
not the internals).
>
> Then here we differ. I believe functionality CAN be encapsulated into a
> component and it won't change. I have a date component, for instance, that
> validates dates in any format and gives me them in 9 other formats. It
won't
> change. (Unless the whole world decides to leave the Gregorian Calendar,
in
> which case, I'll simply write a new component; would NOT maintain the old
> one...) This same date functionality would slot seamlessly into my
> applications and the impact would be minimal or zero.
This is a very simple case, but I give you points for proving your point
with this case<G>

> I think the difference in our positions arises because (I see from your
> post) that you are very concerned with the nitty gritty of technology and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> they would still be transportable to THE ENVIRONMENTS WHICH I WISH TO
USE...
> (namely, the Web and the Windows desktop.)
You're probably right.  I would go further and say that you view components
as fully finished, polished units of work whereas as I see them purely as an
API -and oftentimes that API is based on new technology and is not fixed or
finite - yet.   I'm like the the guy fixing up old beat up cars for a
living....I know the money is in the newer models, but I just don't like
sticking an electronic device and having it tell me what's wrong.  I want to
*know* why it's not working.  Call me a source code junkie.
I think probably more at issue is that I still treat coding as a
creative/analytic thing.  There's a craft and only by seeing others work can
you get better....one day - you think - I will be nothing more than a
technical artisan...a relic :-)

> > I'm being somewhat contentious but that's only because I'm a fence
sitter.
> > I don't discard the concepts of "perfect" design; however, I just don't
> > believe we'll ever see anyone get beyond "imperfect" design...maybe some
> > smart ones will get to "almost perfect" design.  Not that I don't think
it's worth aspiring to perfection....
> I believe this can be attained by iteration and interaction as I have said
> here on numerous occasions. It isn't "perfect design" that is the goal; it
> is "delivery of functionality that does what we want".
So are you a proponent of "just good enough code", and if so, does that fit
in this model?
I find that is how I spend most of my time operating.

> I really have no issues with your post and we are probably closer on this
> than you might think.
I don't think we're far apart at all.  I'm probably too cynical for my age.

> As to being perfect...<G>
>
> "Heaven is being perfect. Perfect speed is just being there." - JLS
>
> Pete.

JCE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: from Martin A. Goetz with love...

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-09-30T20:10:07+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f79579c_5@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0309241417.79dde1d9@posting.google.com> <3f724ba1_5@news.athenanews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0309260923.140ead84@posting.google.com> <3f783433_4@news.athenanews.com> <NvYdb.31480$eS5.24213@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <3f78bcfa_9@news.athenanews.com> <H98eb.45173$Ci5.1313543@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```

"jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:H98eb.45173$Ci5.1313543@twister.tampabay.rr.com...
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
> news:3f78bcfa_9@news.athenanews.com...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
That's great! I don't expect or need acquiescence ('though it's always nice
when it happens <G>), but understanding is essential...

If you view components as encapsulated functionality, then the fact that you
can't/won't maintain them is really unimportant.

>
> From MS.
> Win32::ODBC was written to conform to the ODBC API 2.0 specification.
Since
> ODBC 3.0 is backward compatible it should not cause any compatibility
> problems. To borrow from Microsoft's web pages...
>
> Notice the word "should" :-)
>
Yes, but I won't be losing any sleep over this. The reason is that I view
RDBs purely as repositories for data, and avoid anything that is specific to
a particular RDB system.The "intelligence" for my Data is encapsulated in
components, NOT on the database... I have had numerous arguments about this
with people I respect very much, and there has been some movement on both
sides <G>.

For example, I will now define dates on RDB using the intrinsic DATE type,
whereas for a long time I fiercely resisted this, preferring to store them
in a simple CCYYMMDD format as strings, and let my date component deal with
converting them on retrieval, presentation, and storage, as required. I  was
persuaded by some posters here and some of the SQL Server guys I was
managing, that it isn't really a big deal and there are advantages in
letting SQL do some date arithmetic. It isn't as pure as it was, but I admit
the benefits outweigh the disadvantages ad, so far, I have had no problems
with it. Of course, if I had to change to a RDB that didn't support the DATE
type, I'd be in trouble, but this is very unlikely...

I'm still thinking over the implications of DB Stored Procedures and I can
see pros and cons. So far I haven't moved that way, but it is 50:50 that I
will soon...

<snip>

> I term environment much more loosely.  If you change from Gregorian to
> Darmorian, you switch components, your environment has changed (maybe
> seamlessly).
> In financial worlds, that still mean retest :-)
>

Yes, we see a lot of discussion about this.

Everyone HATES regression testing (although it is a NECESSARY hardship...)
because it is expensive, boring, and tedious.

I'm not sure that if one component was exchanged for another, and they both
had the same Methods (one Gegorian and one Darmorian, to stay with the
analogy), it is essential to test EVERY single module that invokes them. The
interface hasn't changed... More importantly, the FUNCTIONALITY hasn't
changed (presumably, the new component's Methods provide the same
functionality as the old one's did... services like converting date formats
and doing date arithmetic), all that has changed are the formats of the
properties... I reckon it would be justifiable to select say, 10% of the
modules that use the component (assuming there were hundreds...) and show
that these function exactly the same with the new component as they did with
the old.

(Imagine someone who had built their system on such a reusable component,
approaching the Y2K Millennium... they would be laughing. No problems with
date conversions or arithmetic, just extending the date fields in the COPY
books, recompiling, and away they go...The component would do the same
actions on the new date format that it did on the old.)

<snip>
>
> > I think the difference in our positions arises because (I see from your
> > post) that you are very concerned with the nitty gritty of technology
and
> > your components tend to be pieces of a given technology encapsulated and
> > wrapped. I don't do that. My components are encapsulated pieces of
> > FUNCTIONALITY. Most of them are written in OO COBOL. Even if they
weren't,
> > they would still be transportable to THE ENVIRONMENTS WHICH I WISH TO
> USE...
> > (namely, the Web and the Windows desktop.)
> You're probably right.  I would go further and say that you view
components
> as fully finished, polished units of work

...exactly!...

> whereas as I see them purely as an
> API -and oftentimes that API is based on new technology and is not fixed
or
> finite - yet.   I'm like the the guy fixing up old beat up cars for a
> living....I know the money is in the newer models, but I just don't like
> sticking an electronic device and having it tell me what's wrong.  I want
to
> *know* why it's not working.  Call me a source code junkie.
> I think probably more at issue is that I still treat coding as a
> creative/analytic thing.  There's a craft and only by seeing others work
can
> you get better....one day - you think - I will be nothing more than a
> technical artisan...a relic :-)

You won't be alone, and in the meantime you are deriving real pleasure from
your work. That can't be bad <G>....

>
> > > I don't discard the concepts of "perfect" design; however, I just
don't
> > > believe we'll ever see anyone get beyond "imperfect" design...maybe
some
> > > smart ones will get to "almost perfect" design.  Not that I don't
think
> it's worth aspiring to perfection....

> > I believe this can be attained by iteration and interaction as I have
said
> > here on numerous occasions. It isn't "perfect design" that is the goal;
it
> > is "delivery of functionality that does what we want".
> So are you a proponent of "just good enough code", and if so, does that
fit
> in this model?

It is very hard for me to say this next, but your question made me stop and
consider and I am sure that it is what I really believe:

"I don't care."

I did. For many years. Source code and the elegance of it was extremely
important to me. I always strove to make each program I wrote better than
the last one I wrote, using the full experience being gained along the way.
(Actually, I still do that, but now it is just pride and the habit of years,
not because I think it is really important.)

I honestly believe the days of source code forged by humans, are coming to
their twilight. Therefore the quality (in pure programming terms) of said
code is no longer relevant. (As long as it works, of course...<G>).

If I'm not going to maintain it, why would I care what it's like?

Having said that, I still get a buzz from seeing some innovative or novel
use of code, and I can still appreciate good code when I see it. It just
isn't important in the scheme of things because we are not going to be
maintaining it. (I already have a number of third party components that I
couldn't maintain (even if I wanted to), because I don't have the source
code. It isn't a problem. They do what they are documented to do, and I
design my systems around required functionality, then select (or write)
components that match that functionality.

> I find that is how I spend most of my time operating.
>
Ah, get over it...<G>

Pete.
```

## Thread: Deleting "invalid" character from a file — 2 this month / 11 total

_Thread started [2003-05](2003-05.md#t-8ef9dd2cc6)._

#### ↳ Re: Deleting "invalid" character from a file

- **From:** weberm@polaris.net (Ubiquitous)
- **Date:** 2003-09-24T19:07:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LXydnTCJb8M2rO-iU-KYgA@comcast.com>`
- **References:** `<be9d5d6a.0305030456.23e527e9@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <be9d5d6a.0305030456.23e527e9@posting.google.com>, 
datacraft@netzero.net wrote:

>I support an old PC application developed under Ryan Macfarland COBOL
>vers 6.10. I am having a problem processing external input files that
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>is not an option.

Perhaps you'd be better off investigating why that non-ASCII character
appears in the data file than adjusting your program to deal with it.
If you can't fix the file creation, perhaps an INSPECT REPLACING 
LOW_VALUES with SPACE on each record would do the trick?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Deleting "invalid" character from a file

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-09-24T22:39:11-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0309242139.39dfb3d1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<be9d5d6a.0305030456.23e527e9@posting.google.com> <LXydnTCJb8M2rO-iU-KYgA@comcast.com>`

```
weberm@polaris.net (Ubiquitous) wrote 

> >I support an old PC application developed under Ryan Macfarland COBOL
> >vers 6.10. I am having a problem processing external input files that
> >contain the character hex 1A (decimal 26) ASCII right arrow.
> >As far as I can tell, RM/COBOL treats this character as an End-of-File
> >marker, and does not read beyond it.

hex 1A, Ctrl-Z, is the end of file marker.  This started in CP/M in
the mid 70s and was kept when MS bought a clone of CP/M from SCP which
they called MS-DOS.  While later versions of MS-DOS may not need this
file terminator many programs still recognise it.

> >The characted appears once every 15 records or so.
> >I would appreciate any suggestions on dealing programatically with
> >this problem. Manually editing the file to delete the offending char
> >is not an option.

You may need to run a filter on the file before Cobol gets to read it.
 If you are reading the file as LINE SEQUENTIAL then it should not
contain control characters other than tab, lf, cr.  ie if it has hex
1A in it and this is not eon of file then it is _NOT_ a line
sequential file.
 
If you read it as a binary sequential file with a one byte record file
you can deal with any byte values in the file and can assemble the
data using cr, lf to signal when a 'record' is complete.

> Perhaps you'd be better off investigating why that non-ASCII character
> appears in the data file than adjusting your program to deal with it.
> If you can't fix the file creation, perhaps an INSPECT REPLACING 
> LOW_VALUES with SPACE on each record would do the trick?

hex 1A is not low-values.  If it is treating hex 1A as end of file
then the program doesn't get to see the byte, so thinking they can be
replaced in the program is fruitless.
```

## Thread: MF use skeletons — 7 msgs

### MF use skeletons

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-09-25T21:20:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030925172026.27155.00000170@mb-m03.aol.com>`

```

I am reading a 1998 copy of "COBOL Unleashed,"
by Jon Wessler, et al.; a good book, and useful
for keeping furniture in place during high winds, as well.

He refers to Micro-Focus use skeletons.

I am interested in knowing if such things are still in their (MF's)
current product. And would like to know how much of that thinking
has made it into COBOL 2002. 

Citations to technical material online from _any_vendor_ would be 
helpful to my further studies.  Online examples would also be useful.

If there is alternative vocabulary from other vendors, please guide me 
to their terms, and any terms that have become the norm in the standard
that might relate to use skeletons.

Thanking you in advance for your troubles, ...

Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

```
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF use skeletons

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T16:30:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2RZcb.5119$NX3.1670@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<20030925172026.27155.00000170@mb-m03.aol.com> <3F746922.EDB5A194@shaw.ca>`

```
Look at the chapter

   "Chapter 13: Requirements-based Vocabulary"

in
http://supportline.microfocus.com/supportline/documentation/books/nx40/nx40indx.htm

For an example, they have

method-id. credit is method
    ...
procedure division using lnkAmount
    INVOKED AS ==Credit <self> [with] <lnkAmount> ==
    OR AS ==deposit <lnkAmount> [in] <self> ==

which allows your program to "use" CREDIT as a COBOL "verb".

(I assume this is what RKRayhawk was referring to)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF use skeletons

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T17:40:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F747B24.2D8247B5@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<20030925172026.27155.00000170@mb-m03.aol.com> <3F746922.EDB5A194@shaw.ca> <2RZcb.5119$NX3.1670@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> Look at the chapter
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If your assumption is correct, about 'Vocabulary', then it's probably a fair guess that
this is a feature that J4 wont pick up on. I'm not aware of any N/E people using this
OO feature extensively. (Nobody has publicly 'enthused' about it).

For Ray's info there is a Vocabulary class, (included under the 'Base' set), which lets
you make the kind of change BIll illustrated.

In contradiction of what I've written above, a GUI vocabulary file, 'guibase.if', is
used extensively by people creating GUIs :-

copy "guibase.if".
*>-------------------------------------------------
$set hidemessage(731)
 class-id. guibase is external.
  object section.
  class-control.
     Dependent is class "dependnt"
     guibase is class "guibase".
 object.
 method-id. "setEvent".
 linkage section.
 01 lnkeventindex          pic s9(9) comp-5.
 01 lnkeventhandler        usage object reference.
 procedure division using lnkeventindex lnkeventhandler
*> vocabulary
 INVOKED as
 == setEvent
   [using] ([<self>] ,
             <lnkeventindex> ,
             <lnkeventhandler> )
 ==.
 end method "setEvent".
 method-id. "translateEvent".
 linkage section.
 01 lnkeventindex          pic s9(9) comp-5.
 01 lnkTranslatedEvent     pic s9(9) comp-5.
 01 lnkReceiver            usage object reference.
 01 lnkmethodName          usage object reference.
 procedure division using lnkeventindex lnkReceiver
                          lnkTranslatedEvent
*> vocabulary
 INVOKED as
 == map event <lnkeventindex> upon <self>
        to logical event <lnkTranslatedEvent> upon <lnkReceiver>
 ==.
 end method "translateEvent".
 method-id. "setEventTo".
 linkage section.
 01 lnkeventindex        pic s9(9) comp-5.
 01 lnkReceiver          usage object reference.
 01 lnkmethodName        pic x.
 01 lnkParam1            pic x(4).
 01 lnkParam2            pic x(4).
 procedure division using lnkeventindex lnkReceiver lnkMethodName
                          lnkParam1 lnkParam2
*> vocabulary
 INVOKED as
 == map event <lnkeventindex> upon <self>
        to method <lnkMethodName> upon  <lnkReceiver>
             [<lnkParam1>] [<lnkParam2>]
 ==.
 end method "setEventTo".
 end object.
 end class guibase.
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

As yet I haven't found a need to create my own 'Vocabularies' - but I'll keep my
options open - the penny might drop some time in the future.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF use skeletons

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-09-27T20:21:38+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3F75F27C.634EC8BD@shaw.ca>`

```
Richard wrote:

> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> (see other discusion on 'all manner of searching').

Basically the reason that I don't use vocabularies. However, following up
on your comments I had a look at the on-line manual for N/E V4.0.  (V.3.1
is 'poor' on OO in the LRMs). - this piqued my interest :-
---------------------------------------------------------
User-defined Functions

You can also create new functions with user vocabularies. The new
functions look like
COBOL intrinsic functions when used in a program.

For example, you could code:

compute interest = .06 * function balance (thisBankAccount)

You can omit the keyword function, so you could also write:

compute interest = .06 * balance (thisBankAccount)

The definition of function balance looks like this:

 method-id. "getBalance".
 linkage section.
 01 lsAmount pic S9(10)v99.

 procedure division returning lsAmount
    INVOKED AS FUNCTION == Balance ( <self> ) ==.
 end method "getbalance".
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

Now I'm not about to rush off and start coding that way - but it does have
possibilities.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL to Java Conversion

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T13:33:37-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0309261233.725e5777@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ybPcb.30593$Ci5.680950@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
"RGonzalez" <rgonzalez@f3rsolutions.com.removethis> wrote

> Is there any businesses that perform COBOL to Java conversions?
> Does anyone know what their going rates are (more or less)?

You say 'conversion' rather than 'reimplementation'.  Any code that is
converted is likely to retain much of the structure and style of the
original.  The programs won't be 'Java programs', but will be 'Cobol
programs in the syntax of Java'.  In order to produce the same results
they will have to emulate Cobol's semantics.

From working with Fortran and Cobol programs converted to C I would
suggest that the results would need programmers who are fluent in both
Cobol (for the style and semantics) and Java (for the syntax).  If
such people were actually available then they could be employed much
more productively working on the Cobol code.

There are, however, Cobol to Java translators which will produce Java
executables from Cobol source code.  If, for example, you want to
write Cobol to run in a Java VM in WebSphere then this may be viable -
just don't even look at the java code.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL to Java Conversion

- **From:** bsulliv@legacyj.com (Brian Sullivan)
- **Date:** 2003-09-28T21:51:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f8041505.0309282051.6cbe7d4a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ybPcb.30593$Ci5.680950@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
"RGonzalez" <rgonzalez@f3rsolutions.com.removethis> wrote in message news:<ybPcb.30593$Ci5.680950@twister.tampabay.rr.com>...
> Hello,
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> F3RSolutions.com

Hello,

Check out LegacyJ (http://www.legacyj.com) for PERCobol.

It's not COBOL to Java conversion, but rather compiling COBOL to work
in the Java environment with extremely simple integration between the
two.  We believe there's a number of accuracy, performance, and
maintainance tradeoffs in translation, so we encourage people to keep
the code in COBOL source for maintainance so we can have accuracy and
continue to upgrade our compiler/runtime's performance over time.

There's a download available for evaluation.  The IDE is Eclipse,
allowing both COBOL and Java development.  If wanting to do new work
in Java, check out the Cobol calling Java and Java calling Cobol
samples in particular.  With our LTP product, even CICS API code is
now supported directly as EJBs.

Every Cobol program is an object, so calling:

PROGRAM-ID. MYCOBOL.
DATA DIVISION.
LINKAGE SECTION.
01 A PIC X(10).
01 B PIC 9(3).
01 C PIC 9(5)V99 COMP-2.
PROCEDURE DIVISION USING A B C.

is as direct as:

mycobol instance=new mycobol();
instance.call(null,new Object[] {"Hello",new Integer(123),new
Double(456.78)});

(Note, multiple instances of the same Cobol program are permitted,
with each 'context' following standard COBOL-85 calling rules, so
technologies such as servlets and EJBs do work as expected.  Beware
any Java translation that makes data static, it's a dead-end.)

And calling from Cobol into Java is just as simple as implementing a
class with one method, call.  Each parameter implements a Datatype
interface with methods such as toInt, toText, toDouble, etc. for
direct usage of elementary types, and getElements() for breaking down
groups and tables.

Objects are supported directly from Cobol, so the following is valid
(using custom C"" class literals for brevity):

INVOKE C"java.lang.Thread" "sleep" USING BY VALUE 1000

(Or you could use one of the numerous extensions, such as DELAY 1.0
from Tandem syntax.)

Regards,
Brian Sullivan
LegacyJ Corp.
```

<a id="t-e32679c0c0"></a>
## Thread: XML and COBOL — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### XML and COBOL

- **From:** "Johan den Boer" <me@knoware.nl>
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T08:05:23+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vn7lp16dr3nge1@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Hi,

I want to know if there is a tool / sources available to write XML documents
for cobol ?

regards

Johan
```

#### ↳ Re: XML and COBOL

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T12:42:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030926084208.06460.00000196@mb-m26.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<vn7lp16dr3nge1@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Johan writes ...

>Hi,
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>regards

Well, I don't understand your question. XML is XML. What would XML for COBOL
look like?

On the other hand, if you want to write XML documents _in_ a COBOL program,
it's just like any other output file: you put out records satisfying the XML
formmating rules. So you have to know your data. Initially put out a header
line (<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?> then build elements as you get the
data. An element would consist of an start tag, the data piece you want, and an
end tag. Such a program would, of necessity, be a different program for each
set of differently-structured input data.

If you're on a mainframe using the current IBM Enterprise COBOL compiler, you
can even translate the EBCDIC records to UTF-8 before writing them out, since
XML is supposed to be coded in Unicode.

Of course if you're on Windows or UNIX and not using large character sets you
are already using ASCII which is effectively UTF-8.


Kind regards,



-Steve Comstock
800-993-9716
303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-BE6C46.09074326092003@corp.supernews.com>`

```

> Hi,
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

To write XML documents there is an open standard suggestion that can be 
found at www.saxproject.org called the XMLDataWriter.  It is a Java 
interface, but it covers all the bases (methods and variables) needed to 
generate XML from any language.

Filling in the interface blanks in Cobol takes perhaps a few days.  
Nothing tricky, nothing unusual.  OTOH, reading XML in Cobol using SAX 
is much more interesting...
```

#### ↳ Re: XML and COBOL

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T13:07:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0309261207.5f5c85e4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<vn7lp16dr3nge1@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Johan den Boer" <me@knoware.nl> wrote 

> I want to know if there is a tool / sources available to write XML documents
> for cobol ?

Writing XML is rather easy, it is just a text file with the data items
with tags and attribute names surrounding it.  You could code the
formatting into the program or, much better, have a template file that
has all the tags and attribute names that are required and also has
markers where the data items are to be put.

For repeating groups you could have markers that indicate the start
and end of the group, and these could be nested, or simply divide the
template up into blocks and have the program repeat the blocks the
number of times that the data item occurs.

If you wrote a callable module (or class) that could be called with
sets of data items then this could be used to write out many different
XML documents using appropriate templates and data items.

Change the template and it will write HTML, or EDIFACT, or CSV, or
Postscript, or anything really.

-------- template order.tem ----------------------------
*PACKED
*LINES 0
*OUTPUT directory/filename
:HEAD
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="ISO-8859-1"?>
<despatch>
   <shipment>
     <order><!%ORDER%></order>
     <reference><!%REF%></reference>
     ...
     <lines count="<!%LINES%>">
:LINE
     <line number="<!%LINE%>">
         <product><!%PCODE%></product>
         <ordered><!%QORDERED%></ordered>
         <sent><!%QSENT%></sent>
         ....
     </line>
:TRAILER
     </lines>
  </shipment>
</despatch>

I would have an array as the interface to the template routine that
is, say:

      01 Template-interface.
         03  Template-name     PIC ..
         03  Template-function PIC ..
             (Open, print section, close, etc)
         03  Template-Response PIC ...
         03  Template-section  PIC X(8).
             (HEAD, LINE, TRAILER in this example)
         03  Data-I            PIC S9(4).
         03  Data-items OCCURS 1 TO 100 DEPENDING ON Data-I.
             05  Data-name          PIC X(12).
             05  Data-Value         PIC X(40).

       MOVE ZERO    TO Data-I
       MOVE "ORDEROUT"  TO Template-Name
       MOVE "HEAD"      TO Template-Section
       MOVE "S"         TO Template-Function
       
       ADD 1        TO Data-I     
       MOVE "ORDER"     TO Data-Name(Data-I)
       MOVE ON-Number   TO Data-Value(Data-I)
       ADD 1        TO Data-I
       MOVE "REF"       TO Data-Name(Data-I)
       MOVE ON-Ref      TO Data-Value(Data-I)
       ...
       CALL "TEMPLATE" USING Template-Interface

The template program then has to read all the template file into an
array of text lines.  Process '*' commands to set options, process ':'
section names into an array of section names recording the line
numbers, ignore comment lines.

For each section print do a lot of INSPECT and UNSTRING/STRING on each
line.
```

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

- **References:** `<dcb682e3.0309260759.5f266a77@posting.google.com> <Xs_cb.153990$0v4.11492191@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <dcb682e3.0309261418.714eb48d@posting.google.com>`

```
On 26 Sep 2003 15:18:12 -0700, mr.kyle@prodigy.net (Mr. Kyle) wrote:

>"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote in message news:<Xs_cb.153990$0v4.11492191@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>...
>> Mr. Kyle <mr.kyle@prodigy.net> wrote in message news:dcb682e3.0309260759.5f266a77@posting.google.com...
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>receive that "start in" value in my application.
The "start in" value just means that before the application is started
the OS is going to issue a "change dir" to that particular directory.

So you just need to get the "current directory" as of the start of
your app.

Using the CBL routines

 ENVIRONMENT     DIVISION.
 DATA            DIVISION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01 path-name PIC X(200).
 01 path-name-length PIC 9(4) BINARY value 200.
 01 status-code PIC S9(4) COMP-5.
 01 drive PIC X.
 01 path1 pic x(203).
 PROCEDURE       DIVISION.
     CALL "PC_READ_DRIVE"
     USING drive
     RETURNING status-code.
     CALL "CBL_READ_DIR"
      USING path-name
      path-name-length

      RETURNING status-code.
      move spaces to path1.
      string drive ":\"
             path-name delimited by size
             into path1.
      Display "Start path = " path1.




Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol / using Windows "Start in" property

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T18:07:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<byadnev6gN0GW-miU-KYgA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<dcb682e3.0309260759.5f266a77@posting.google.com>`

```
Mr. Kyle wrote:
> I've been searching manuals, web pages and adtools.com for a way to
> use Window's desktop property "start in" to pass my initial file
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks.  Kyle

Sure, we do it all the time.

In PowerCOBOL it's a property of POW-SELF (CommandLine).

In COBOL97, from one of FJ's samples:

---- begin

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID. commandline.

 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
 SPECIAL-NAMES.
     console is crt
     ARGUMENT-NUMBER   IS COMMAND-LINE-NUMBER
     ARGUMENT-VALUE    IS COMMAND-LINE-VALUE.

 DATA DIVISION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01 number-of-arguments   pic 99    value 0.
 01  command-line-arguments.
  05 a-command-line-argument pic x(80) occurs 1 to 99 depending on
number-of-arguments.
 01 arg-no       pic 99    value 0.
 01 line-no      pic 999   comp  value 3.
 01 output-line     pic x(80).

 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
   display spaces upon crt.
*
* Get the number of arguments passed to the program
*
  accept number-of-arguments from command-line-number
  move spaces to output-line
  string number-of-arguments delimited by size
      " Arguments were passed to this program" delimited by size
   into output-line

  display output-line at 0101
*
* Now, let's show the arguments to the user
*
  display " ".

  perform varying arg-no from 1 by 1 until arg-no > number-of-arguments
   accept a-command-line-argument (arg-no) from command-line-value
   move spaces to output-line
   string "Argument Number " delimited by size
        arg-no delimited by size
        " = " delimited by size
        a-command-line-argument (arg-no) delimited by size
    into output-line
   display output-line at line line-no column 1
   add 1 to line-no
  end-perform.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol / using Windows "Start in" property

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T16:16:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0309261516.52efae4e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<dcb682e3.0309260759.5f266a77@posting.google.com>`

```
mr.kyle@prodigy.net (Mr. Kyle) wrote

> I've been searching manuals, web pages and adtools.com for a way to
> use Window's desktop property "start in" to pass my initial file
> directory location to my application.  Can't find a thing about it and
> I'm guessing it can't be done (or it's right under my nose and I can't
> see it).

The 'start in' directory is simply the directory that will be made
'current' _before_ your program is started.  It is not 'passed' to
your program.  The equivalent DOS commands are:

      cd startdirectory
      yourprog parameters
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol / using Windows "Start in" property

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T16:28:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0309261528.68fdfd1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<dcb682e3.0309260759.5f266a77@posting.google.com>`

```
mr.kyle@prodigy.net (Mr. Kyle) wrote

> I've been searching manuals, web pages and adtools.com for a way to
> use Window's desktop property "start in" to pass my initial file
> directory location to my application.  Can't find a thing about it and
> I'm guessing it can't be done (or it's right under my nose and I can't
> see it).

If you want to know where this has placed you then get the current directory with 
   
     CALL "GetCurrentDirectoryA" USING ..

See samples on Fujitsu site 'GETDIR.ZIP'.

or:
     CALL "CBL_READ_DIR" USING ...

See samples on Fujitsu site for CBL routines.
```

## Thread: The Manhattan Benchmark (spin-off from Benchmark Java vs. COBOL) — 28 this month / 35 total

### The Manhattan Benchmark (spin-off from Benchmark Java vs. COBOL)

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T14:42:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bl24r10a9o@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```
Steve Thompson mention the "Indian Problem" -- How much would
12.00 be today if it was put in the bank in 1626, when
Manhattan was purchased?

Benchmarking this simple logic likely proves nothing, but it is
yield some interesting results:

mpb Visual COBOL 3.6.0.8
  Test Count:           100000
Init -------------------------
  Year:                   1626
  Principle:           12.0000
  Rate:                 0.0400
Results ----------------------
  Principle:     31674824.8227
  Elapsed:               52850

Borland Delphi 5.0
  Test Count:           100000
Init -------------------------
  Year:                   1626
  Principle:           12.0000
  Rate:                 0.0400
Results ----------------------
  Principle:     31677245.6417
  Elapsed:                1783

Microsoft Visual Basic 6.0
  Test Count:           100000
Init -------------------------
  Year:                   1626
  Principle:           12.0000
  Rate:                 0.0400
Results ----------------------
  Principle:     31677245.6417
  Elapsed:                3505

I'll post the code if anyone is interested.
```

#### ↳ Re: The Manhattan Benchmark (spin-off from Benchmark Java vs. COBOL)

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-26T15:46:06-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bl28if0ji6@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<bl24r10a9o@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```
Here's another one:

Microsoft C#.net 1.0
  Test Count:           100000
Init -------------------------
  Year:                   1626
  Principle:           12.0000
  Rate:                 0.0400
Results ----------------------
  Principle:     31677446.1774
  Year:                  13830

The difference in resultant principle is pretty interesting.

31674824.8227 (mbp COBOL)
31677245.6417 (VB)
31677245.6417 (Delphi)
31677446.1774 (C#)

A tentative explanation:

mbp COBOL -- truncates digits beyond those defined in the PIC.
(Is that right?)

VB, Delphi -- rounds digits beyond the 4th digit after the
decimal (Currency types are 64-bit integers with a presumed
numeric scale of 4.)

C#.net -- I used the "decimal" type which a 128-bit integer
scale by 10, but it keeps more digits past the decimal point
than 1.  It has a precision of about 28, but it's unclear to me
how it determines numeric scale.  It coerces to and from
integer types implicitly, so it's apparently not a floating
point type.
```

```
FWIW, here's a REXX program and its result to a ridiculous number of
decimal places.

/* REXX */
Numeric digits 1000
Amount = 12
Rate = 1.04
do i = 1627 to 2003
   Amount = Amount * Rate
end
say Amount
exit


31677446.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Manhattan Benchmark (spin-off from Benchmark Java vs. COBOL)

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-28T17:26:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bl7n6901nlr@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<bl24r10a9o@enews1.newsguy.com> <bl28if0ji6@enews1.newsguy.com> <b2ocnvo4n8nttt96n70jc5c0ujivpca511@4ax.com>`

```
I added to you code in an attempt to match the other
implementation -- it's attached at the end.  I hope I haven't
introduced any idiocy in the process.

Here's some output:

Regina REXX 3.2
  Test Count:           100000
Init -------------------------
  Year:                   1626
  Principle:           12.0000
  Rate:                 0.0400
Results ----------------------
  Principle:     31677446.1785
  Elapsed (ms):          75000 +/- 1000

From what I've learned of REXX in the last 10 seconds, it
appears to be doing string-based long arithmetic.  This is
reasonable given its goals, but that and probably the
interpretation, adds a lot to the elapsed time.  In particular,
using "12.00" and "0.04" for principle and rate respectively,
takes off about 10 seconds.

I see there are ways to store numerics as binaries, so I'll
investigate that.  Also, there's probably some way to compile
REXX.

_____________

/* REXX */
Numeric digits 12

TestCount     = 100000
InitPrinciple = 12.0000
InitYear      = 1626
Rate          = 0.0400

Multiplier    = 1 + Rate
ThisYear      = 2003

say
say "Regina REXX 3.2"
say "  Test Count:          " TestCount
say "Init -------------------------"
say "  Year:                  " InitYear
say "  Principle:          " InitPrinciple
say "  Rate:                " Rate

/* start the clock */
/* Any ideas on how to get fractional seconds? */
Elapsed = DATE("T")

do Test = 1 to TestCount
  Principle = InitPrinciple
  do Year = InitYear to ThisYear - 1
    Principle = Principle * Multiplier
  end
end

/* stop the clock */
/* Any ideas on how to get fractional seconds? */
Elapsed = 1000 * (DATE("T") - Elapsed)

say "Results ----------------------"
say "  Principle:    " Principle
say "  Elapsed (ms):         " Elapsed "+/- 1000"

exit
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Manhattan Benchmark (spin-off from Benchmark Java vs. COBOL)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-09-28T18:56:32-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1HJdb.1117$op2.142741@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<bl24r10a9o@enews1.newsguy.com> <bl28if0ji6@enews1.newsguy.com> <bl6dec$fvg$05$1@news.t-online.com> <bl7hc3013mr@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```
"Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote in message
news:bl7hc3013mr@enews1.newsguy.com...
>
> "Alfred Cleve" <alfred.cleve@t-online.de> wrote in message
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It would be interesting to see if how a cobol program that gave the correct
answer using bussiness rules, and how fast the other languages gave the
correct answer each benchmarked.  To find out, just change your picture
clause to 9(8)v9(2) rounded. That will give the correct answer.  Then adjust
the other routines to fit.

To check the answers and debug, just print a balance sheet for each year.
Three or four columns will do. It is only going to be a dozen pages long,
and you can compare the entire thing in each of the languages as a test.

Progressive errors should be very easy to spot.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Manhattan Benchmark (spin-off from Benchmark Java vs. COBOL)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-28T20:20:38-05:00

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<bl85od02pj3@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<bl24r10a9o@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```
I've taken Donald Tees' advice, and now the resultant principle
values are in agreement.  This generally increased computation
time, but that has been offset by cutting the size of the local
variables -- slightly for REXX and COBOL, but significantly for
the others.

REXX gets pounded by the 40 million rounding operations it must
now make, but that may be because I don't know the optimal
path -- are there any rexxperts who can assist?  (That code
posted as a reply to this note.)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Latest REXX code

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-09-28T21:45:17-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bl86bu02qc7@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<bl24r10a9o@enews1.newsguy.com> <bl85od02pj3@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```
/* REXX */
Numeric digits 12

TestCount     = 100000
InitPrinciple = 12.00
InitYear      = 1626
Rate          = 0.04

Multiplier    = 1 + Rate
ThisYear      = 2003

say
say "Regina REXX 3.2"
say "  Test Count:        " FORMAT(TestCount, 8, 0)
say "Init -------------------------"
say "  Year:                  " FORMAT(InitYear, 4, 0)
say "  Principle:  " FORMAT(InitPrinciple, 12, 2)
say "  Rate:                " FORMAT(Rate, 3, 2)

/* start the clock */
/* Any ideas on how to get fractional seconds? */
Elapsed = DATE("T")

do Test = 1 to TestCount
  Principle = InitPrinciple
  do Year = InitYear to ThisYear - 1
    Principle = FORMAT(Principle * Multiplier, , 2)
  end
end

/* stop the clock */
/* Any ideas on how to get fractional seconds? */
Elapsed = 1000 * (DATE("T") - Elapsed)

say "Results ----------------------"
say "  Principle:  " FORMAT(Principle, 12, 2)
say "  Elapsed (ms):        " FORMAT(Elapsed, 6) "+/- 1000"


exit
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Manhattan Benchmark (spin-off from Benchmark Java vs. COBOL)

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<bl24r10a9o@enews1.newsguy.com> <bl85od02pj3@enews1.newsguy.com> <qcPdb.7546$RW4.2514@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:qcPdb.7546$RW4.2514@newsread4.news.pas.earthlink.net...
> Do you have a compiler with a full set of COBOL '89 Intrinsic
Functions?  If
> so, I know it wouldn't be a "fair" test of the compiler
(itself), but I
> would be interested in the RESULTS obtained using the
FUNCTION Annuity
> feature (if that is the right function).

If you show me where and how it should be applied, I'll run it
through a compile and test.  Here's the current COBOL code:

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. MANHAT.

       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
      * Arguments
       01  WS-TEST-COUNT            PIC 9(7)      COMP VALUE
100000.
       01  WS-INIT-YEAR             PIC 9999      COMP VALUE
1626.
       01  WS-INIT-PRINCIPLE        PIC 9(8)v9(2)      VALUE
12.00.
       01  WS-INTEREST-RATE         PIC 9v9(2)    COMP VALUE
0.04.
      * Computed initial conditions
       01  WS-MULTIPLIER            PIC 9v9(2)    COMP VALUE
ZEROS.
      * State variables
       01  WS-TEST                  PIC 9(7)      COMP VALUE
ZEROS.
       01  WS-PRINCIPLE             PIC 9(8)v9(2) COMP VALUE
ZEROS.
       01  WS-YEAR                  PIC 9(4)      COMP VALUE
ZEROS.
      * Statistics
       01  WS-ZERO-DATETIME.
           05  WS-ZERO-YEAR         PIC 9(4)      VALUE ZEROS.
           05  WS-ZERO-MONTH        PIC 99        VALUE ZEROS.
           05  WS-ZERO-DAY          PIC 99        VALUE ZEROS.
           05  WS-ZERO-HOUR         PIC 99        VALUE ZEROS.
           05  WS-ZERO-MINUTE       PIC 99        VALUE ZEROS.
           05  WS-ZERO-SECOND       PIC 99        VALUE ZEROS.
           05  WS-ZERO-HS           PIC 99        VALUE ZEROS.
           05  WS-ZERO-ZONE         PIC XXXXX.
       01  WS-FINAL-DATETIME.
           05  WS-FINAL-YEAR        PIC 9(4)      VALUE ZEROS.
           05  WS-FINAL-MONTH       PIC 99        VALUE ZEROS.
           05  WS-FINAL-DAY         PIC 99        VALUE ZEROS.
           05  WS-FINAL-HOUR        PIC 99        VALUE ZEROS.
           05  WS-FINAL-MINUTE      PIC 99        VALUE ZEROS.
           05  WS-FINAL-SECOND      PIC 99        VALUE ZEROS.
           05  WS-FINAL-HS          PIC 99        VALUE ZEROS.
           05  WS-FINAL-ZONE        PIC XXXXX.
       01  WS-ELAPSED               PIC S9(9)     VALUE ZEROS.
      * Scratch variables
       01  WS-TIME-DIFF             PIC S9(9)     VALUE ZEROS.
       01  WS-SOME-DATE             PIC 9(8)      .

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.

       MAIN.

      * Compute some initial conditions
           ADD 1.0 TO WS-INTEREST-RATE GIVING WS-MULTIPLIER.

      * Output initial conditions
           DISPLAY " ".
           DISPLAY "mbp Visual COBOL 3.6.0.8".
           DISPLAY "  Test Count:          " WS-TEST-COUNT.
           DISPLAY "Init -------------------------".
           DISPLAY "  Year:                   " WS-INIT-YEAR.
           DISPLAY "  Principle:       "
WS-INIT-PRINCIPLE.
           DISPLAY "  Rate:                   "
WS-INTEREST-RATE.

      * Start the clock
           MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE TO WS-ZERO-DATETIME.

           PERFORM ITERATE-TEST
               VARYING WS-TEST FROM 1 BY 1
               UNTIL WS-TEST > WS-TEST-COUNT.

      * Stop the clock
           MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE TO WS-FINAL-DATETIME.
           PERFORM COMPUTE-ELAPSED.

      * Kick results
           DISPLAY "Results ----------------------".
           DISPLAY "  Principle:       "      WS-PRINCIPLE.
           DISPLAY "  Elapsed (ms):      "      WS-ELAPSED.
      *    DISPLAY "  Time Zero:  " WS-ZERO-DATETIME.
      *    DISPLAY "  Time Final: " WS-FINAL-DATETIME.

           STOP RUN.

       ITERATE-TEST.
      * Run a test
           MOVE WS-INIT-PRINCIPLE TO WS-PRINCIPLE.
           PERFORM COMPOUND-INTEREST
               VARYING WS-YEAR FROM WS-INIT-YEAR BY 1
               UNTIL WS-YEAR >= WS-ZERO-YEAR.

       COMPOUND-INTEREST.
           MULTIPLY WS-MULTIPLIER BY WS-PRINCIPLE ROUNDED.

       COMPUTE-ELAPSED.
      * Determine elapsed time (in milliseconds)
           MOVE ZEROS TO WS-ELAPSED.

           MOVE WS-FINAL-DATETIME(1:8) TO WS-SOME-DATE.
           MOVE FUNCTION INTEGER-OF-DATE(WS-SOME-DATE)
               TO WS-ELAPSED.

           MOVE WS-ZERO-DATETIME(1:8) TO WS-SOME-DATE.
           MOVE FUNCTION INTEGER-OF-DATE(WS-SOME-DATE)
               TO WS-TIME-DIFF.
           SUBTRACT WS-TIME-DIFF FROM WS-ELAPSED.
           MULTIPLY 86400000 BY WS-ELAPSED.

           SUBTRACT WS-ZERO-HOUR FROM WS-FINAL-HOUR
               GIVING WS-TIME-DIFF.
           MULTIPLY 3600000 BY WS-TIME-DIFF.
           ADD WS-TIME-DIFF TO WS-ELAPSED.

           SUBTRACT WS-ZERO-MINUTE FROM WS-FINAL-MINUTE
               GIVING WS-TIME-DIFF.
           MULTIPLY 60000 BY WS-TIME-DIFF.
           ADD WS-TIME-DIFF TO WS-ELAPSED.

           SUBTRACT WS-ZERO-SECOND FROM WS-FINAL-SECOND
               GIVING WS-TIME-DIFF.
           MULTIPLY 1000 BY WS-TIME-DIFF.
           ADD WS-TIME-DIFF TO WS-ELAPSED.

           SUBTRACT WS-ZERO-HS FROM WS-FINAL-HS
               GIVING WS-TIME-DIFF.
           MULTIPLY 10 BY WS-TIME-DIFF.
           ADD WS-TIME-DIFF TO WS-ELAPSED.

       END-OF-SOURCE.
```

#### ↳ Truncation (instead of Rounding) give mbp COBOL a boost

```
method is more "correct" -- truncation or rounding?
```
